Halfway down the Rue Saint-Denis, near the Rue du Petit-Lion, stood a charming old house that let historians reconstruct old Paris. Dilapidated walls bore hieroglyphic patterns of timbers and plaster cracks. The triangular roof, warped by weather, jutted over the street, sheltering the entrance and dormer. The upper story used overlapping planks to avoid overburdening the fragile structure.

On a rainy March morning a young man in a cloak stood beneath a shop awning across from the house, studying it with the zeal of an antiquary. The relic of sixteenth-century civic life displayed many peculiarities. He ignored the first floor’s narrow, boarded windows and focused on the third floor’s modest sash windows, poorly made and dimmed by green-tinted glass, occasionally glancing down at the shop where small details amused him.

A large wooden beam, supported by four pillars, bore layers of paint and a comical depiction of a cat playing racket, which amused the young man. The caricatured cat, racket in paw with an oversized ball, had grown more absurd with age. A faded sign above it read “Guillaume, Successor to Master Chevrel,” a remnant of a whimsical past when such signs lured customers.

The young man, in a stylish cloak and white silk stockings, looked as if he had just come from a wedding or ball; white gloves and dishevelled hair hinted at recent festivities. Despite market gardeners hurrying by, the street held a rare stillness. His striking features — a blend of passion and calm — drew the attention of the shopkeepers at the “Cat and Racket.” His pale face set off a dazzling white cravat, and his expressive eyes suggested many moods.

Suddenly three apprentices appeared at the dormer window, laughing and teasing him; they playfully sprinkled him with shaving cream and he shook it off with indifference. Then a young girl’s face appeared at the window, her beauty like a Raphael Virgin — innocent and unembarrassed. On noticing the young man she withdrew and the window slammed shut, leaving him disappointed.

Meanwhile the shutters of the “Cat and Racket” opened and a manservant hung a cloth sign repeating “Guillaume, successor to Chevrel.” Behind iron bars the shop’s exterior concealed packages wrapped in brown linen. Though old-fashioned in appearance, Monsieur Guillaume was known for having the best-stocked establishment among Parisian cloth merchants and a reputation for honesty. He knew how to secure profits without flattery or costly gifts, yet fellow traders often warned against dealing with his notary, notorious for heavy discounts.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The old merchant stood at his shop threshold, watching the Rue Saint-Denis as if waking from slumber. Monsieur Guillaume, in traditional attire, scrutinized a stranger overly curious about his business. His patient, shrewd face reflected years of experience. A staunch traditionalist, he lamented lost customs and kept a strict regime for his three apprentices, who feared his vigilance.

As the apprentices began the day, Guillaume noticed the stranger's anxious glances and suspected ulterior motives. The eldest stepped outside and thought he saw Mademoiselle Augustine leaving hastily; he hailed a cab, easing the apprentices' worries about their prank on Augustine.

Guillaume reprimanded them for idleness, recalling how he would have worked far harder in his youth. Though the apprentices came from reasonably comfortable backgrounds, he demanded rigorous industry, contrasting his order and economy with modern shops where apprentices expected early wealth. The shop itself was meticulously kept, a testament to his values.

Although the apprentices paid for their board, they were not allowed to join the family at dessert—an indication of Madame Guillaume's frugality. They attended Mass with the family, and the eldest, Joseph Lebas, earned a modest salary after years of service. Guillaume regarded him as a fitting husband for his elder daughter, Virginie, though Joseph secretly loved the younger daughter, Augustine.

The household was governed by strict traditions. Virginie and Augustine had been raised under their mother's austere rule. Virginie resembled her mother in piety and sternness; Augustine, eighteen, was delicate and charming but often overshadowed by maternal expectations. Their lives centered on domestic duties with little time outside, so they took pleasure in family gatherings and rare visitors.

Madame Guillaume's rigid schedule dictated their days: they dressed early and followed routine. Augustine, however, yearned for more than their cloistered existence, often lost in thoughts of a more passionate life; secret readings of romantic novels fed her longing. That longing deepened Joseph's affection for Augustine while Virginie found herself drawn to him. Their feelings developed quietly amid the shop's monotony, and Monsieur Guillaume, aware of shifting dynamics, mused about his daughters' futures in a world where suitors might be scarce.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
From that day the old merchant, grieving his eldest daughter’s decline, remembered marrying Mademoiselle Chevrel under circumstances like Joseph Lebas and Virginie. Marrying his daughter would settle a sacred debt—repaying an orphan for a past kindness. Joseph Lebas, thirty-three, noted the fifteen-year age gap with Augustine and Monsieur Guillaume’s strict rule that the elder daughter marry first. The lovesick assistant, warm-hearted and tall, suffered in silence.

To understand, rewind months before. One evening a young man paused outside the darkened Cat and Racket shop, captivated by a scene any painter would prize. The unlit shop resembled a cave; a dining room, lit by a hanging lamp, cast a warm, Dutch-like glow. The family—Augustine and a chubby maid among them—made a modest, striking tableau. The painter, just returned from Rome, hungered for simple nature after Italian grandeur. His heart, filled with Italian passion, sought the meditative maiden he had only seen in paintings. The living scene stirred deep admiration: Augustine, pensive and scarcely touched by food, looked almost angelic in the lamp’s light. Overwhelmed, he left unable to eat or sleep.

The next day he retreated to his studio to capture that light. He visited the Cat and Racket repeatedly, sometimes in disguise, studying Augustine closely. For eight months he devoted himself to love and art, neglecting friends and former pursuits. One morning his friend Girodet pierced his isolation to ask about his Salon submission. The artist revealed two masterpieces; Girodet embraced him, recognizing the depth of his friend’s feeling.

“You are in love?” Girodet asked; the artist nodded. They agreed great work springs from such passion. Girodet warned against showing the pieces at the Salon; he doubted the public would grasp their depth. Still, the works were exhibited and caused a sensation. The Interior sparked a genre shift, while the portrait—Augustine—became unforgettable, earning acclaim and offers the artist refused.

These events, though public, felt remote from the monastic life on Rue Saint-Denis. During a visit to Madame Guillaume, the notary’s wife mentioned the exhibition, arousing Augustine’s curiosity. With her cousin’s help she obtained permission to visit the Louvre. Seeing the portrait, she trembled: it was herself; terror mixed with excitement. The artist stood nearby and whispered, “You see how love has inspired me,” leaving her stunned.

Desperate to escape the crowd, Augustine urged her cousin to leave, but they were carried toward the celebrated painting. Overcome, she wept. In a brief silent exchange she gestured to the artist; he acknowledged her with a nod. That moment ignited both guilt and exhilaration. The Salon’s chaos and the painter’s gaze intoxicated her; new emotions surged where none had been before.

As they left, the painter glowed with joy; Augustine, caught in the whirlwind, realized she was loved. His words echoed in her mind, intensifying her turmoil. She feigned a headache to avoid discussing the exhibition, yet her mother’s plans to visit the Salon filled her with dread. That night she reflected, feeling a void in her mundane life and longing for the elegance she had always desired. The thought of being the wife of a genius filled her with hope despite her modest upbringing. The next day her parents returned from the Salon disappointed: the painter had removed the two pictures, and Madame Guillaume had lost her shawl. Still, news of the paintings’ disappearance and the lost shawl revealed to Augustine a sensitivity she had not known she possessed.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On the morning after a ball, Theodore de Sommervieux, who had won Augustine’s heart, found it hard to declare his love because the household kept her under close watch. Seated in the counting-house between Mademoiselle Virginie and Madame Guillaume, Augustine was constantly observed by her mother, which stifled Theodore’s approach. Fearing a rival among the assistants, he bribed a maid to facilitate secret exchanges and arranged to meet Augustine discreetly during Mass at Saint-Leu.

Meanwhile the Cat and Racket household was absorbed in stock-taking, with Monsieur Guillaume supervising inventory and accounts while the family verified goods, oblivious to Augustine’s turmoil. After weeks of labor the inventory ended, and the family enjoyed a rare evening out to see Cendrillon while the apprentices celebrated elsewhere.

The next morning, Monsieur Guillaume resumed business as usual and summoned his assistant Joseph Lebas to review the profitable results. Guillaume proposed making Joseph a partner to reward his work. Overwhelmed, Joseph confessed his love for Augustine; Guillaume initially misunderstood, thinking Joseph meant Virginie. Once corrected, Guillaume agreed to consider Joseph’s attachment and suggested he accompany Augustine to Mass.

At breakfast tension rose as Madame Guillaume and Virginie exchanged glances at a nervous Joseph. Madame, unaware of Augustine’s romance, reproached her for inattentiveness in church and sent her to her room, prompting a heated discussion between the parents about their daughter’s future.

Summoned before them, Augustine declared her love for Theodore and emphasized his respectable background. Monsieur Guillaume voiced the usual doubts about artists, but Augustine defended Theodore as well-off and from a good family, which piqued her father’s interest. Madame Roguin then arrived with news of Theodore’s recent honors and fortune and advocated the match, beginning to sway the parents who had been resistant to their daughter marrying above her station.

As the day progressed the mood shifted from conflict to acceptance. Monsieur Guillaume informed Joseph of the developments, and by evening the family gathered to celebrate the prospective union. Theodore presented a painting of the Cat and Racket as a token of shared happiness. Guillaume, still cautious, consented to the marriage while stressing financial prudence and advising Augustine to consult him on money matters. In the end the lovers found solace in mutual affection, and the household, once tense, embraced a hopeful future together.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A few months after a memorable Sunday, the high altar of Saint-Leu saw two contrasting weddings. Augustine and Theodore were radiant; Virginie, simply dressed, followed her sister. Monsieur Guillaume had tried to have Virginie married before Augustine, but the priests favored the more elegant bride. Neighbors commended Virginie for a sensible match and envied Augustine for marrying an artist of rank. An old fan-maker warned that such extravagance could bring ruin, and Monsieur Guillaume felt justified in his cautious approach to settlements. After the ball and supper, the Guillaumes returned home, the Lebas family to their old residence, and the joyful artist carried Augustine to their well-adorned apartment.

Their first year together was blissful. Theodore adored Augustine, who, lost in love, believed her devotion would always charm him; she spoke the language of love and revealed a naive simplicity. But as a child approached, Theodore had to return to work. He reconnected with friends and began frequenting the Duchesse de Carigliano’s gatherings, seeking the social validation of being seen with a beautiful wife.

Augustine enjoyed society but soon realized her lack of sophistication compared to Theodore’s artistic circle. Her admiration for his work was sincere yet superficial, and a growing disconnect emerged. Theodore, once captivated, felt the strain of their different intellectual worlds. He retreated to his studio, hoping the environment would inspire Augustine, but she struggled to adapt to the lifestyle. At gatherings her innocence clashed with the freer attitudes of artists, and her discomfort showed.

Over time Theodore’s affection cooled, and Augustine felt the weight of his indifference. Despite trying to meet her duties as a wife and rekindle his interest, she sensed a widening distance. Three years into their marriage, vibrant Augustine was consumed by sorrow. The artist’s enthusiasm for the Duchesse de Carigliano and for more experienced women deepened her despair; she feared being replaced.

Her heartache prompted Augustine to examine her shortcomings and the barriers between them. She resolved to educate herself, believing that understanding poetry and art would bridge the gap. Yet her attempts to change only brought frustration: she could not manufacture the natural wit and charm Theodore admired, and her earnest efforts often backfired, making her feel inadequate in his presence.

In desperation, Augustine sought refuge with her family, returning to her childhood home. There she found Virginie and brother-in-law Joseph Lebas managing their drapery business with a practical stability that contrasted sharply with her turmoil. Joseph offered straightforward, businesslike counsel, but it left her more conflicted than comforted.

Ultimately Augustine returned home burdened by uncertainty and longing for the love she once shared with Theodore, like a desperate patient trying every remedy to heal a fractured marriage.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The elderly couple welcomed their daughter warmly; her visit brightened their monotonous lives. For four years they had drifted, dwelling on past business successes and failures, notably the Lecocq bankruptcy. Monsieur Guillaume checked on his shop, the Cat and Racket, and traded memories with former competitors, while Madame Guillaume, now on the Army's clothing consulting board thanks to her son-in-law, filled their home with extravagant, often tasteless decor. Their nostalgia and stagnation struck Augustine as a stark contrast to her own turbulent experiences, leaving her both proud and appalled at their vacuous existence.

Despite her inner turmoil, Augustine displayed her recent achievements and aired marital grievances, which her parents eagerly absorbed. Madame Guillaume was especially curious about life with Theodore, Augustine's husband, a painter. Augustine mentioned his late nights and unconventional habits, prompting her mother’s disapproval of his lifestyle and profession. The couple’s incredulity grew as Augustine defended Theodore, sparking a heated debate about genius and marital responsibility.

When Augustine hinted at divorce, her parents were shocked into action; Monsieur Guillaume was ready to support her. Overwhelmed by their concern and unable to convey the depth of her suffering, Augustine rejected separation, realizing her pain could scarcely be understood by them. She felt isolated, convinced that only those who had shared similar experiences could truly empathize.

Resolved to win back Theodore’s affection by other means, Augustine decided to consult the Duchess de Carigliano—not to reclaim his heart outright but to learn how to captivate him. Approaching the Duchess’s opulent home, she felt both envy and admiration for its elegance. Meeting the Duchess, Augustine was struck by the woman’s beauty and charm but also sensed rivalry.

The Duchess responded with a mix of sympathy and shrewd counsel, stressing the importance of a composed facade and control when dealing with men of genius. She advised cultivating mystery and not revealing the full measure of one’s affections, framing such guardedness as strategy rather than mere manipulation. Initially disheartened, Augustine absorbed the Duchess’s lessons, recognizing that her love for Theodore might require a different approach.

The Duchess gave Augustine a portrait of herself painted by Theodore, believing it could rekindle his interest. Filled with hope, Augustine resolved to embody the image in the painting. When Theodore returned, she presented the portrait; his reaction was fury and jealousy, igniting a tumultuous confrontation.

The quarrel escalated, exposing the depths of Theodore’s passion and insecurity and culminating in a violent outburst. The aftermath left Augustine shattered; her dreams of mending their marriage lay in ruins. The next morning, her mother found her consumed by despair amid the remnants of the confrontation.

Augustine’s tragic fate was sealed as she succumbed to the pressures of her turbulent life, dying young. Her story stands as a poignant reminder of the struggles women face pursuing love and fulfillment, especially when entangled with the complexities of artistic genius. The inscription on her grave reflects that tragedy, prompting those who knew her to ponder the challenges of balancing passion and stability in relationships.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Middle of the story: tension builds, characters shift, conflicts deepen, and the plot advances.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Comte de Fontaine, a loyal royalist from Poitou, had served the Bourbons during the La Vendée conflict. Despite noble lineage and past sacrifices, he was financially ruined after his estate was confiscated. He married Mademoiselle de Kergarouet, a noblewoman without fortune. After the second revolution he moved to Paris with his large family seeking better prospects. Initially disillusioned, he received unexpected honors—a military promotion and royal decorations—that rekindled hope for favor.

He soon found his sacrifices little recognized and felt marginalized as new aristocrats rose. Though he considered returning to his estate, the turmoil of 1815 drew him back into royal service. His loyalty was rewarded when he became a trusted adviser to Louis XVIII, who valued his wit and political acumen. The Fontaines then began to prosper: his sons secured important posts and his daughters made advantageous marriages, if to men of lesser nobility.

Emilie, the youngest, was spoiled and the focus of Parisian attention. Her beauty and charm made her desirable, yet she remained indifferent, asserting superiority over suitors of lower birth. Despite her father’s efforts to arrange suitable matches, Emilie’s exacting standards and caprice provoked family conflict; she dismissed candidates for trivial flaws and repeatedly frustrated his plans.

As politics shifted and the King’s health declined, the Comte intensified his efforts to marry Emilie off, hoping a good match would cement the family’s status and justify his sacrifices. Her unreasonable expectations yielded a string of rejections that left him increasingly desperate. Family dynamics grew strained: Emilie’s arrogance created friction with siblings and their spouses, who themselves navigated the complexities of newfound standing.

Through the turmoil the Comte clung to hope that royal favor and a proper marriage for Emilie would restore the family’s honor. Yet as the winter social season brought new prospects, her dismissive attitude persisted, leaving the Fontaine family’s future uncertain.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Nature had shaped Emilie de Fontaine to be the perfect Célimène. Tall and slender, she could assume dignity or coquettishness at will. Her long neck expressed scorn and impertinence; fine black hair and arched brows lent prideful air. She had mastered feminine gestures that could convey contempt or charm; her melodious voice could win a heart or, used sharply, silence indiscretion. Her alabaster complexion resembled a tranquil lake, stirred only by fleeting emotion. Many young men whom she scorned accused her of merely acting; she answered that she inspired their desire and then subjected them to her whims.

Monsieur de Fontaine saw too late that adulation had spoiled his daughter. Praise had swelled her pride and self-confidence, nurturing the selfishness of a pampered child. Her youthful charms masked these defects, but he perceived them and tried to teach practical wisdom. His efforts met caprice and ironic shrewdness; his gentle advice seemed to glide off her heart like water from marble. He came to believe her affection was often condescending, like a child demanding attention before returning to play.

Emilie sometimes showed fondness for her parents but often withdrew, jealous of their shared love with others, including her siblings. She blamed solitude on fate, failing to see happiness comes from within. Determined to resist her sisters’ marriages, she nonetheless envied their contentment. Her unpredictability led her mother to suspect she might be slightly mad.

This pride is common among beautiful young women of wealth, who often imagine their mothers cannot understand youthful dreams and suspect maternal envy. These struggles produce secret tears and revolts against perceived tyranny, and elaborate life plans that picture a perfect partner who must meet exacting criteria. With experience many find happiness unexpectedly and learn ideals may not match reality.

Emilie’s terms for a suitor were strict: he must be a peer of France, a soldier, and possess an Order so they could be treated with respect. He also had to be slender; she considered corpulence a flaw in men, believing only the slim could be romantic. Her disdain for those who failed her standards was clear, and Monsieur de Fontaine feared her eccentricities would invite ridicule and that indecision would harm her prospects.

During the winter of Charles X’s reign Monsieur de Fontaine intensified efforts to find matches, hosting gatherings to attract suitors. His reputation as a corrupter of legislative integrity grew, yet he remained earnest. After a season of matchmaking he felt hopeful, only to see Emilie’s whims and standards unyielding.

One morning he summoned her to discuss her future. He arranged his study meticulously to present a dignified front. Emilie greeted him with a light, perfunctory kiss. He broached her marriage prospects and stressed the importance of a wise choice for her happiness.

She interrupted, reminding him of their prior agreement about suitors. He urged her to take the matter seriously, noting the family’s efforts to secure a suitable match. She dismissed his concerns, mocking the idea of settling for anything less than a peer and finding fault with the candidates he proposed.

Exasperated, Monsieur de Fontaine declared he had fulfilled his duty and that the choice now lay with her. He warned that marital happiness depends on mutual respect rather than mere wealth or status. Emilie, moved by his sincerity, feigned warmth to coax him, yet her pride remained intact.

At a family gathering she reiterated her refusal to consider marriage, insisting she would only wed a peer. Her uncle joked that she was waiting for the Duc de Bordeaux to come of age, provoking laughter, but Emilie remained resolute and forbade further talk of marriage.

As summer neared the family retired to the country house. Emilie wished to attend the Bal de Sceaux, drawn by the crowd and the anonymity it offered. The ball, known for its lively atmosphere, drew a mix of social classes.

There she was surprised to find upper-class attendees mingling with the rest. While observing the festivities she noticed a striking young man who stood apart, exuding elegance and dignity. His appearance and manner stirred her imagination and she felt instant attraction.

Determined to learn more, Emilie approached him, but he remained aloof. She tried to attract his attention with exaggerated gestures and laughter, but he seemed indifferent. Frustrated, she followed his gaze and found him watching another dancer, a pale girl named Clara.

Intrigued, Emilie decided to follow the pair as they left the ball. The young man, attentive to Clara, nonetheless caught Emilie’s eye as they departed; she felt a mix of jealousy and curiosity, eager to discover his identity.

Days later, while riding with her uncle, Emilie encountered Lady Dudley and Monsieur Vandenesse and realized the young man she admired was not what she had hoped. Disappointed, she tried to conceal her feelings but remained drawn to him.

Her uncle, noticing her agitation, speculated about her feelings for the young man. Emilie insisted he must be of noble birth; her uncle promised an introduction. This intensified Emilie’s interest and she began to fantasize about a future with him.

As days passed, Emilie’s infatuation grew, fueled by imagination and the romance of the unknown. She remained unaware of the complexities and challenges that love and marriage would bring, blinded by youthful ideals.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The next morning, before Emilie woke, her uncle hurried to Chevreuse and, recognizing the young man he'd insulted the day before, greeted him with the polished courtesy of the old court.

“Who would have guessed I’d meet the son or grandson of one of my best friends at seventy-three? I’m a vice‑admiral; dueling’s no matter to me. I had too much grog and ran you down. Shake hands; I’d sooner endure a hundred rebuffs from a Longueville than bring his family regret.”

The young man, reserved but cordial, accepted the Count’s apology and they rode together. The admiral joked about duels, fired a pistol into a birch to show his fearlessness, and praised the young man’s shooting. He quizzed him on matters that marked a gentleman—debts, pleasures, heraldry—teasing about mistresses and youthful extravagance. Longueville answered with composed manners: he paid punctually, lived independently, and showed the polish of a fine education. The Count resolved to introduce him to society at the Pavillon Planat.

At the Villa Planat, when a servant announced “Monsieur de Longueville,” curiosity became attention. Longueville arrived simply but elegantly, at ease in the Receiver‑General’s mansion, well‑spoken and knowledgeable—even about naval architecture, which the admiral broached. His answers were discreet; his manner suggested training like that of the École Polytechnique. When pressed on his profession, he said he had renounced the Engineers to preserve his independence. He declined dinner courteously, saying he cared for a delicate sister.

The family was struck. He charmed without ostentation, and though he aroused Mademoiselle de Fontaine’s interest—Emilie had dressed to attract him—his measured attention left her pleased and wounded. She usually displayed her charms for newcomers, but now seemed simpler, almost natural; she spoke little and, when roused by her uncle, quipped that perfection often hid defects—“Those who please everybody please nobody”—a remark revealing caution and pride.

Over the next fortnight Longueville called repeatedly. Emilie, accustomed to being the center of attention, found herself drawn outward by his reserve. He was modest and discreet: he never spoke of his family and skillfully evaded her probes. He accepted what she offered—conversation about painting, piano and song—without revealing his social standing. Once they sang a duet and those present could not tell whether he was professional. His silence both tormented Emilie and deepened her interest.

The family wished to meet Clara Longueville, and an informal dance was arranged to welcome her. When Clara and her brother arrived, Emilie felt genuine pleasure for Clara and lavished her with attention, though tinged by envy: Clara combined ease with modesty and, while amiable, gently reproved Emilie’s earlier disdain for commoners. Clara said she had come out of affection for her brother, and her frank courtesy reassured Emilie. The exchange—Clara’s gentle rebuke and Emilie’s relieved response—brought the two girls closer.

With autumn the attachment between Emilie and Longueville quietly deepened. Their life became a series of small intimacies: aimless conversations that skirted deeper feeling, shared sunsets, gatherings of wildflowers, and passionate duets where music expressed what words did not. They learned each other’s ways as lovers do, testing limits, revealing little and withholding more. Longueville’s silence remained both comfort and barrier; Emilie cherished his attention and resented the mystery, yet the bond made her gentler, more indulgent, and less self‑centered.

Throughout, the Count and household watched and speculated, charmed by Longueville’s worldliness and by Emilie’s changing demeanor. His discretion, talent, and reserve left his background uncertain and admirers hopeful. Emilie surrendered to the tide of feeling, delighting in their private intimacies and the new inward life he awakened, even as questions about his rank and history lingered at the edges of their romance.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
MIDDLE OF THE STORY — midpoint, turning point.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For effusive souls, twilight — that soft hour between day and night — invites contemplation on art. During such an hour a young painter, absorbed in finishing a large canvas, falls from his ladder, strikes his head, and loses consciousness.

He wakes to the gentle voice of a young woman and her mother, who had rushed to his aid after hearing the fall. As he regains his senses he is struck by the girl's beauty, matching the ideal of feminine grace he has long imagined. The older woman, resembling a refined marquise, tends him kindly and advises on his recovery. The painter thanks them, but the two women, naturally reserved, depart without pressing for further acquaintance.

After they leave, the porter’s wife tells him the visitors are Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, long-time residents of the building. The painter, Hippolyte Schinner, has recently moved into the attic studio, having won some recognition. He is determined to provide better for his mother, who sacrificed much for him. Despite growing success he remains reserved and does not easily share his private life.

During his convalescence Hippolyte keeps returning in thought to the two women, especially the young girl's image, which lingers. When well enough to return to his studio he is eager to visit them, feeling a bond with their situation; he imagines they share a kind of misfortune that draws him to them.

At their modest home he notes signs of poverty but feels no contempt, only empathy. Mademoiselle Leseigneur greets him politely, and he is struck by the contrast between their humble surroundings and the elegance they retain. The room contains remnants of past luxury — faded and worn — yet everything is meticulously kept.

Conversation begins stiffly but soon turns to art, allowing Hippolyte to speak openly about his thoughts and feelings. Madame Leseigneur reveals how dear a portrait of her late husband, a naval officer, is to her, though its condition is deteriorating. Hippolyte offers to paint a new portrait, hoping to forge a closer connection; the offer is accepted with graceful gratitude, and he senses the emotional weight behind it.

As they talk, the young woman, Adelaide, steps out briefly and returns with candles to brighten the room. The atmosphere shifts when an unexpected visitor arrives — a man acquainted with Adelaide. His presence stirs jealousy in Hippolyte, who wants to know the nature of their relationship.

The newcomer is an older gentleman in a coat whose style recalls a bygone era; he carries an air of confidence that blends nobility and libertinism, suggesting a complicated past. Their exchange reveals layers of the women's lives, leaving Hippolyte to ponder the dynamics at play and the histories hinted at by their manner and circumstances.

Throughout the evening Hippolyte struggles with his feelings for Adelaide — admiration tangled with uncertainty. The contrast in their social standings weighs on him, yet he is drawn to her spirit and grace. As night unfolds he becomes increasingly aware of the delicate balance between their worlds and of the slender hope for connection amid the shadows of their respective circumstances.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A remarkable figure stood beside the “Louis XIV’s light infantry” specimen — Bonapartists’ nickname for those remnants of the Monarchy. A lean, aged man, he was a faded echo of the first: a worn coat, duller hair powder, a feebler intellect. He seemed the faded reflection Rivarol’s quip on Champcenetz suggested.

This silent old man puzzled the painter, the Chevalier. He neither spoke nor was spoken to; his relation to the gallant man was unclear—friend, relative, or mere companion? His presence invited curiosity but offered no answers, especially about any past connection to the gallant’s deceased wife.

The least dilapidated of the group approached Madame de Rouville, kissed her hand, and sat beside her. The other man bowed and took a seat nearby. Adelaide leaned over the old gentleman’s chair, echoing a famous painting’s pose, though the gentleman’s familiarity seemed to irritate her.

“Are you sulky with me?” he asked, casting a shrewd glance at Schinner, the painter, as if to judge his standing.

“This is our neighbor,” Madame de Rouville introduced Hippolyte Schinner, the celebrated painter. The old man acknowledged Hippolyte, noting the artist’s red ribbon of distinction against the backdrop of his own hard-earned honors.

Hippolyte, captivated by Adelaide’s beauty, grew lost in admiration, momentarily forgetting their squalor. Absorbed, he answered briefly, his mind elsewhere. The old gentleman lamented his card losses; Adelaide offered help, but he insisted she remain where he could see her.

As the game ended, the gentleman complained about his bad luck. The silent figure echoed the time, prompting Hippolyte to leave, still entranced by the first stirrings of love.

The next day Hippolyte felt an overwhelming urge to see Adelaide. He hesitated until afternoon before visiting Madame de Rouville and awkwardly requested the Baron’s portrait. Adelaide, alone, asked him to fetch it, revealing her modesty. Their shared glance conveyed more than words, and Hippolyte took the portrait, promising its quick return.

In the following days their relationship blossomed with laughter and shared thought. Schinner, eager to learn piquet, often lost, while an unspoken bond between him and Adelaide deepened. They became inseparable; Adelaide’s affection grew, and Hippolyte’s jealousy flared when the old Count showed undue familiarity.

Adelaide’s hopes for Hippolyte expanded; she worried his social life harmed his health. Their love matured without grand gestures, built on mutual trust and delicate exchanges. Within two months their lives were intertwined: Hippolyte visited daily, and their connection deepened.

The old gentleman’s visits waned as Hippolyte took his place at the card table, sharing in the losses. Yet Hippolyte couldn’t shake concerns over Madame de Rouville’s finances. He spent two months on the portrait, calling it one of his best, but the Baroness’s silence puzzled him.

One day, Adelaide visited his studio and was awestruck by the portrait; their charged moment overflowed with unspoken feeling. Hippolyte, overwhelmed, declared his desire to marry her, but the old vice-admiral arrived, praised the work, and hinted at funds to pay for both portraits.

As the evening went on, Hippolyte noticed Madame de Rouville’s unusual excitement during cards and suspected a gambling habit. He began to question their relationship’s nature, wondering if deception was afoot. After a night of turmoil, he discovered his purse missing and suspected Adelaide and her mother of theft.

Despite heartbreak, he clung to hope of misunderstanding. The next day a friend mentioned rumors about Adelaide and her mother, unsettling him further. Torn between love and doubt, he resolved to confront his feelings and visit Madame de Rouville.

Seeing Adelaide, pale and sad, he felt ashamed of his earlier suspicions. Their talk revealed mutual longing, and he realized his mistake. The evening turned sweet as they played cards, but his fears returned when he noticed Adelaide’s secretive glances at her mother.

During the game, Adelaide discreetly placed a new purse before Hippolyte, revealing her thoughtful attempt to replace the lost one. Overwhelmed, he grasped the depth of their bond and impulsively declared his intention to marry her.

Just then the old vice-admiral arrived, announcing his promotion and expressing support for the young couple. The evening ended with a sense of unity as the families came together, dispelling lingering doubts.

This tale of love, trust, and misunderstanding follows characters navigating complex relations and ultimately finding solace in shared bonds and the promise of a brighter future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

In October 1800, a foreigner named Bartolomeo di Piombo stood outside the Tuileries Palace with his wife and young daughter. In his sixties and grave, he was deeply troubled; his wife kept a calm face for their child. Passersby noticed their despair, but his fierce gaze deterred sympathy.

After a moment’s thought he handed a dagger to his wife and declared he would confront the Bonapartes. He approached the palace but was stopped by a soldier who refused entry without a written request. Bartolomeo persisted in a tense standoff until Lucien Bonaparte, minister of the interior, arrived, recognized him, and took him inside to meet his brother, Napoleon.

Inside, Napoleon dismissed his aides to speak privately with Bartolomeo. The Corsican sought asylum and confessed he had killed the Portas—who had burned his vineyard and killed his son—and had avenged his son's death by killing all seven members of the family. The revelation shocked Napoleon. Bartolomeo complained of the Bonaparte brothers' indifference, reminding them he had once protected their family.

Napoleon, now leader of a nation, felt bound by law and could not openly endorse private vengeance. Still, he acknowledged the Corsican tradition of vendetta and offered Bartolomeo a chance to live in Paris with promised financial assistance, warning that the law would not tolerate private justice in the city.

Bartolomeo accepted, pledged loyalty to the Bonapartes, and expressed gratitude for their recognition of his misfortune, appearing hopeful about life in Paris. Napoleon, seeing a possible ally, hinted he might need trustworthy friends in the future.

After the meeting, Lucien learned the family had arrived in Paris with nothing. He gave them money and promised to help secure their comfort, noting that Bartolomeo’s Corsican property would not suffice to support them in Paris.

Fifteen years later, events unfolded that required the context of these earlier struggles and decisions.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Servin, a distinguished artist, was the first to establish a studio for young girls wishing to paint. About forty devoted pupils attended; he was married to a general’s dowerless daughter. At first mothers accompanied their daughters, but they soon trusted Servin’s principles enough to send them alone. He accepted only young ladies from wealthy families to avoid class complaints and refused aspiring professionals who would demand more thorough instruction. His carefulness and the quality of his teaching won him a stellar reputation among the aristocracy, so that mothers recommended him without hesitation.

Servin became synonymous with feminine art, as Herbault was with bonnets and Leroy with gowns. A young woman trained by him was presumed able to critique museum paintings, paint striking portraits, or produce genre scenes. Despite his Parisian connections, Servin maintained an independent, patriotic stance, often expressed with a witty tone.

The studio itself was arranged for the pupils. The attic entrance above his apartment was sealed and reached by a small spiral staircase. The studio was large, lit by panes veiled with green linen shades; dark-gray walls were hung with caricatures and plaster models, a blend of ordered instruction and artistic chaos.

On a bright July day the studio buzzed with young girls at easels, each with distinct expressions and styles. Among them sat Ginevra Piombo, modestly dressed and apart, anxious yet quietly intent. The group was split into two cliques: daughters of wealthy merchants and aristocrats led by Amelie Thirion, vain and pretentious. The room was lively with work, laughter, and chatter, but under that surface simmered rivalry. Ginevra, the most talented and beautiful, drew jealousy from the aristocratic circle. Amelie, intent on ostracizing her, moved Ginevra’s easel away from the others, provoking whispers about the slight.

The political climate after the Bourbons’ second return intensified tensions. Ginevra, a devoted Bonapartist, faced disdain from royalist peers; Amelie’s action was thus both personal and political. When Ginevra arrived she encountered cold silence where she had expected warmth. Her beauty and grace still commanded attention, but her politics made her a target. She remained focused on her work, apparently unaware of the growing hostility.

During the session a mysterious sound behind a partition drew Ginevra’s attention. She discovered Servin was hiding a political fugitive: an officer of the Imperial Guard. The revelation brought excitement and concern for the man’s safety. Ginevra resolved to protect him, preferring to be near him rather than risk his exposure. Her indifference to Amelie’s insult only intensified the other girls’ jealousy, who were baffled by her calm.

Madame Servin’s arrival interrupted the brewing conflict. She announced her husband could not attend that day, and her manner suggested she knew of the hidden man. Sensing the tension, Ginevra began humming a Corsican song to mask the sound of the fugitive’s breathing. Throughout the session she stayed absorbed in her work, sketching the officer’s head with a fervor that surprised even herself. Servin praised her talent; others, including Amelie, were drawn to her sheet. Ginevra’s determination to keep the man’s presence secret became evident as she deftly deflected attention from her drawing.

Amelie, suspicious, tried to uncover the mystery but was foiled by Ginevra’s quick thinking. Servin, aware of the undercurrent, engaged each pupil while keeping an eye on Ginevra. Their shared understanding deepened a tacit bond; Ginevra felt empowered to help protect the fugitive. As the day ended, her resolve to keep the secret and aid the officer’s escape grew. Political turmoil loomed outside, but within the studio the girls navigated rivalries and friendships, united by their art.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the painter Servin and Ginevra believed they were alone, Servin knocked and admitted a tall young officer in an Imperial uniform, his arm in a sling and his face pale with pain. He recoiled at seeing Ginevra, but Servin calmed him, introducing her as the daughter of a loyal friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo. Recognizing her patriotism, the soldier relaxed.

"You are wounded," Ginevra observed.

"It's nothing, mademoiselle," he replied. "The wound is healing."

Cries from the street announced a death sentence: "Condemned to death!" The officer went pale at the name Labedoyere and collapsed onto a stool, anguished that while Labedoyere faced execution for their cause, he was hiding. He felt abandoned after losing his protector and spoke of dying rather than betraying his ideals, even contemplating selling his life to the executioner.

Ginevra blocked his path when he tried to leave. "Can you restore the Emperor?" she asked. "Do you expect to raise that giant who could not maintain himself?"

Alarmed, she offered him her savings, explaining her father was wealthy and would not blame her for helping a soldier. She urged him to wait until the storm passed, suggesting he could escape and serve elsewhere. Her words, filled with maternal compassion and hope, moved him. He admired her goodness and beauty, then his thoughts returned to Labedoyere. In silence they shared a moment of understanding; their acquaintance deepened into a bond. Servin asked if he could save Labedoyere; the officer said he could only avenge him. Ginevra felt a surge at the thought of vengeance and recognized the officer's devotion to Napoleon.

As evening fell, Ginevra felt a strange connection, recalling childhood memories. Captivated, she smiled at him as she left, igniting his hope.

The next day she returned to the studio. The officer, now called Luigi, told stories of the retreat from Moscow and the disaster at Waterloo. Ginevra listened, admiration growing; speaking Corsican together deepened their bond.

Days passed and Ginevra's classmates noticed her absence from social gatherings, blaming her relationship with Luigi. Mademoiselle Thirion, worried by the rumors, withdrew from class, and attendance declined. Ginevra, lost in thoughts of Luigi, remained oblivious.

One day Laure, a fellow student, confided that her mother insisted she leave the studio because of gossip about Ginevra and Luigi. Shocked, Ginevra reassured Laure they would meet again. Laure, sad to go, left, and Ginevra felt a pang of loss.

As Ginevra and Luigi's bond deepened, they shared an unspoken love and mutual recognition of worth. One evening Luigi signaled to Ginevra but, seeing Laure, retreated. Unaware of the tension, Ginevra spoke with Laure and learned the others had left because of scandal.

Ginevra confronted Servin, revealing that the young ladies had spread falsehoods about her relationship with Luigi. Servin dismissed the rumors, focused on his exhibition. Luigi entered, jokingly claiming responsibility for Servin's lost students; the painter playfully suggested marriage. Their silence then became the first acknowledgment of their love. Servin encouraged their union and celebrated the joy of love. Ginevra proposed to help Luigi secure a pardon, confident in her father's influence.

Meanwhile her parents grew anxious about her late returns. Bartolomeo paced, concerned for his daughter. Though used to her late hours, he and his wife felt unease. Bartolomeo, a man of integrity who prioritized his family's well-being over appearances, reflected on their close-knit dynamic built on love and mutual respect. Ginevra's upbringing had instilled loyalty and passion, shared with her father and their political commitment to the Emperor.

When Ginevra finally arrived home, her father embraced her, relieved. The family shared a warm moment; their love was evident. Ginevra's heart swelled with affection for her parents, knowing they were her greatest supporters.

In the studio, Ginevra and Luigi's relationship blossomed amid challenges. Their love, forged in adversity, became a source of strength that united them against the world. As they navigated complexities, they remained steadfast in their commitment to one another, embodying the resilience of their shared ideals.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old man.

“Oh, father, you hurt me!” Ginevra exclaimed, but he set her down gently. She reassured her mother it had been a trick to escape. The baroness recovered her composure, and Piombo, rubbing his hands, showed courtly pleasure. The two old parents seemed revived.

“Now, to dinner!” Piombo called, offering his hand; Ginevra smiled.

“Your mother says you’ve been spending more time at the studio. Painting seems more important to you than us,” he remarked.

“Oh, father!” she protested.

“Ginevra is preparing a surprise for us,” her mother suggested.

“A picture of your own! Will you show us?” Piombo asked.

“Yes, I am busy at the studio,” Ginevra answered slowly.

“You look pale!” her mother exclaimed.

“No! It shall never be said that Ginevra Piombo acted a lie,” she declared.

Her parents exchanged astonished looks. “I love a young man,” she confessed, lowering her eyelids.

“Is he a prince?” Piombo asked ironically, making his wife tremble.

“No, father, he is a young man without fortune,” she replied gently.

“Is he handsome?” he pressed.

“He is very unfortunate,” she said.

“What is he?” Piombo inquired.

“Labedoyere’s comrade; he was proscribed and hidden by Servin,” she explained.

“Servin is a good fellow,” Piombo acknowledged, “but you wrong to love any man but your father.”

“It does not depend on me to love or not love,” Ginevra replied softly.

“I thought my Ginevra would remain faithful to me until I die,” Piombo lamented. “I did not expect our love to find a rival.”

“Did I ever reproach you for your devotion to Napoleon?” Ginevra countered. “You left me for months on missions. I endured your absence. Life has necessities.”

“Ginevra!” Piombo exclaimed.

“You don’t love me for myself; your reproaches show your egotism,” she said.

“Dare you blame your father’s love?” he shouted.

“Father, I don’t blame you,” she replied gently. “You have grounds for your egotism, as I have for my love. I have never left your side for fifteen years; it has been my pleasure to charm your life. Is it ingratitude to desire a husband who will protect me?”

“What! You reckon benefits with your father?” Piombo asked dangerously.

A dreadful silence followed. Bartolomeo cried out, “Oh! stay with us! I cannot bear to have you love another man. Ginevra, you will not have long to await your liberty.”

“But, father, I need not leave you; we will both love you. You will come to know the man to whom you bequeath me,” she said.

“Oh! Ginevra, why did you not marry when Napoleon offered?” Piombo lamented.

“They loved me to order,” she replied. “I did not wish to leave you alone.”

“You don’t wish to leave me alone, yet you marry! I know you, my daughter; you would cease to love us,” he said.

Elisa, the baroness, remained motionless, stunned. “We have no longer a daughter; she wishes to marry,” Piombo declared, then bowed, hands raised, bowed by sorrow. Ginevra, touched by his restraint, expected a violent outburst but met gentleness.

“Father,” she said tenderly, “you shall never be abandoned by your Ginevra. Love me for my own sake. If you knew how he loves me! He would never make me unhappy!”

“Comparisons already!” Piombo shouted. “I cannot endure the thought of your marriage. If he loved you as you deserve, he would kill me; if he does not love you, I would kill him.”

Ginevra alone could meet his gaze, her eyes aflame. “Oh! to love you! What man is worthy of such a life?” Piombo continued. “Who is worthy to be your husband?”

“He,” Ginevra replied, “he of whom I am not worthy.”

“He?” Piombo repeated. “Who is he?”

“He whom I love,” she said.

“How can he know you enough to love you?” he asked.

“Father,” she said impatiently, “whether he loves me or not, if I love him—”

“You love him?” Piombo interrupted. Ginevra nodded.

“You love him more than you love us?” he pressed.

“The two feelings cannot be compared,” she replied.

“Is one stronger than the other?” he asked.

“I think it is,” she admitted.

“You shall not marry him,” Piombo declared, shaking the window panes with his voice.

“I shall marry him,” Ginevra replied calmly.

“Oh, God!” cried her mother. “How will this quarrel end?”

Piombo, now seated, wore an icy sternness. He looked at Ginevra and said gently, “No! You will not marry him. Say nothing more tonight—let me think otherwise. Do you wish to see your father on his knees, his white hairs prostrate before you? I supplicate you—”

“Ginevra Piombo does not break her word,” she replied. “I am your daughter.”

“She is right,” the baroness said. “We are sent into the world to marry.”

“Do you encourage her in disobedience?” Piombo asked his petrified wife.

“Refusing to obey an unjust order is not disobedience,” Ginevra asserted.

“No order can be unjust from your father’s lips,” Piombo replied. “Why do you judge my actions? My repugnance is counsel from on high, perhaps to protect you from great evil.”

“The only evil would be if he did not love me.”

“Always he!” Piombo exclaimed.

“Yes, always,” she affirmed. “He is my life, my good, my thought. Even if I obeyed you, he would remain in my soul. To forbid me to marry him is to make me hate you.”

“You love us not!” Piombo shouted.

“Oh!” Ginevra shook her head.

“Then forget him; be faithful to us. After we are gone—you understand?”

“Father, do you wish me to long for your death?” she asked.

“I shall outlive you. Children who do not honor their parents die early,” he warned.

“All the more reason I should marry and be happy,” she replied.

This cool reasoning troubled Piombo. Ginevra shuddered, sprang onto his knee, and embraced him. “Oh, yes, yes, let me die first! I could never survive you, my father!”

“Oh! my Ginevra, my own Ginevra!” Piombo replied, his anger melting under her affection.

“It was time you ceased,” the baroness said, trembling.

“Poor mother!” Ginevra said.

“Ah! Ginevretta! mia bella Ginevra!” Piombo exclaimed, playing with her as if she were a child. They had moments of joy, but Ginevra’s attempts to introduce Luigi met with playful refusals from her father.

The next day Ginevra was more affectionate, as if to thank him for apparent consent. That evening she sang and played for him, claiming they needed a man’s voice for the nocturne.

A week later, her mother signaled her to come closer. “I have persuaded your father to receive him,” Elisa whispered.

“Oh! mother, how happy you have made me!” Ginevra replied.

That day Ginevra returned home on Luigi’s arm. The officer emerged from hiding for the second time. Ginevra’s appeals to the Duc de Feltre had succeeded; Luigi’s name was restored on the officer’s list. Warned of her father’s potential disapproval, Luigi felt anxious; Ginevra’s awareness of his fear deepened her love.

“How pale you are!” she remarked as they reached home.

“Oh! Ginevra, if it concerned my life only—”

Though Bartolomeo had been informed of the formal introduction, he remained seated, his stern expression intimidating.

“Father,” Ginevra said, “I bring you someone you will be pleased to see—a soldier who fought beside the Emperor at Mont-Saint-Jean.”

Piombo rose, casting a sidelong glance at Luigi. “Monsieur is not decorated,” he remarked sarcastically.

“I no longer wear the Legion of Honor,” Luigi replied timidly.

Embarrassed by her father’s rudeness, Ginevra pulled out a chair for Luigi. That simple civility seemed to satisfy Piombo. Madame Piombo, noticing her husband soften, tried to engage him.

“Monsieur’s resemblance to someone we knew in Corsica, Nina Porta, is surprising,” she noted.

“Nothing could be more natural,” Luigi replied. “Nina was my sister.”

“Are you Luigi Porta?” Piombo asked.

“Yes,” Luigi confirmed.

Bartolomeo staggered, needing support from his wife. The two old parents left the room in silence, abandoning their daughter in horror.

Luigi, bewildered, looked at Ginevra, who had turned pale, staring at the door through which her parents had exited. The solemnity of the moment filled her with fear. “What misery in a word!” she exclaimed.

“In the name of our love, what have I said?” Luigi asked.

“My father never spoke of our tragic history, and I was too young to know,” Ginevra explained.

“Are we in vendetta?” Luigi asked, trembling.

“Yes. My mother said the Portas killed my brother and burned our house. My father then massacred their family. How did you survive? You were tied to the bed before they set fire to the house.”

“I do not know,” Luigi replied. “I was taken to Genoa at six and raised by an old man named Colonna. I was told I had enemies and advised to take Luigi as my surname to evade them.”

“Go, go, Luigi!” Ginevra urged. “Stay here; you are safe in my father’s house, but once you leave, danger awaits! My father has two Corsicans in his service, and they will hunt you.”

“Ginevra,” he said, “does this vendetta exist between us?”

She smiled sadly and nodded. “Oh, Luigi, our love must be pure and sincere to give me strength for the path ahead. But it promises happiness for our lives, does it not?”

Luigi smiled and squeezed her hand. Ginevra realized true love transcends superficial expressions. Their destiny was sealed; she foresaw a difficult struggle but felt no inclination to abandon him. With resolve, she helped him reach his modest lodging.

Returning home, Ginevra felt a calm resolve. She greeted her parents, who were preparing dinner, with gentle eyes, noticing her mother’s tear-stained face. They rose from the table in silence, unable to speak.

In the gloomy salon, Piombo attempted to speak but faltered, his strength failing him. He rang for a servant. “Pietro, light the fire; I am cold,” he ordered.

Ginevra trembled, watching her father’s internal struggle. Both suffered in silence, nursing pride and anger. The love that once brought them joy now felt like a burden. Ginevra’s gaze at her father was full of reproach, while Piombo’s eyes reflected deep pain.

On Ginevra’s birthday, her mother, desperate to mend the rift, gathered them in Bartolomeo’s study. Ginevra sensed her mother’s intentions and smiled sadly.

At that moment, two notaries arrived with witnesses. Bartolomeo eyed them warily. The older notary introduced himself and began to discuss Ginevra’s marriage to Luigi Porta.

“Monsieur le baron, we have come to fulfill the law regarding your daughter’s marriage,” he stated.

“Are there laws in France that destroy paternal authority?” Piombo demanded.

“Monsieur—” the notary began.

“Which tear a daughter from her father?” Piombo pressed.

“Monsieur—”

“Which deprive an old man of his last consolation?” he continued.

“Monsieur, your daughter only belongs to you if—”

“And kill him?” Piombo shouted.

“Monsieur, permit me—”

The notary’s calm only fueled Piombo’s rage. In a sudden movement he grabbed a dagger from the wall and rushed toward Ginevra. Witnesses intervened, but Piombo tossed them aside, fury raw.

When Ginevra saw him approach, she knelt before him. “No, no! I cannot!” he cried, throwing the dagger away. “Have mercy! Give me Luigi! I ask for your consent upon my knees,” she pleaded, voice trembling.

Bartolomeo pushed her away. “Go. The wife of Luigi Porta cannot be a Piombo. I have no daughter. My Ginevra Piombo is buried here,” he said, pressing his heart. “Leave my house, unhappy girl,” he added, ushering her out.

“Luigi!” Ginevra cried, entering her lover’s humble abode. “My Luigi, we have no fortune but our love.”

“Then I am richer than kings!” he exclaimed.

“My father and mother have cast me off,” she said sadly.

“I will love you in their place,” he promised.

“Then let us be happy—we WILL be happy!” she declared, voice full of mixed joy and dread.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The day after Ginevra was expelled from her father's house, she sought refuge with Madame Servin until her marriage to Luigi. Madame Servin received her coldly, resentful of the trouble Ginevra had caused her husband. Proud yet stunned by this selfishness, Ginevra rented a room near Luigi, who devoted himself to her, painting a bright future that lifted her spirits despite her father's harshness.

One morning Ginevra received trunks of essentials from her mother, accompanied by a letter urging her to reconsider the marriage. The letter, full of love and sorrow, deeply moved Ginevra. Just as she felt compelled to return home, Luigi arrived and her resolve to abandon him faded. She chose to bury her grief for the sake of their love.

On the wedding day Ginevra was alone; Luigi brought witnesses to sign the marriage certificate. They were simple men in ordinary dress, their presence contrasting with the usual joy of a wedding. Ginevra's beauty silenced them, creating a somber atmosphere. As they walked to the mayor's office, they passed other wedding parties full of celebration, which only highlighted the difference in their situation.

Inside, the festive air around them made their own love feel overshadowed. The mayor's clerk noted the absence of Ginevra's parents, and when the mayor inquired about their protest, the clerk confirmed it. The bureaucratic formality felt oppressive, a reminder of her family's disapproval, and the ceremony was reduced to a legal act without celebration.

After signing the registers, Ginevra and Luigi left the office with mixed relief and sadness. Luigi expressed gratitude, and Ginevra felt a fragile happiness despite everything. They hurried to the church, where an old priest married them in a modest chapel amid grander weddings. The priest's remarks about their parents' absence stung, but a sergeant's blessing comforted Ginevra.

Once married they moved into a modest apartment, where Luigi embraced Ginevra, calling it their true wedding. They explored the space, which had been thoughtfully arranged with artistic supplies for her. Delightful details made them revel in their shared home, feeling like they had found paradise despite its limitations.

In the early days of marriage they indulged in their love, often losing themselves in each other's presence. Reality soon set in and they recognized the need to work. Ginevra began copying paintings and gained clients, while Luigi, after initial struggle, used his handwriting skills to offer copying services. His efforts paid off; he eventually secured enough work to hire assistants, providing a stable income for both.

This period marked the happiest time of their lives, filled with love and labor. They enjoyed evenings together, with Ginevra always smiling and never complaining. Each worked for the other, finding joy in shared effort, though Ginevra sometimes felt remorse for the sorrow she had caused her family. To mark an anniversary, she painted a self-portrait for Luigi that captured their love. Another year passed in contentment; no significant events disrupted their happiness.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At the beginning of 1819, picture-dealers urged Ginevra to produce original works instead of copies as competition grew. Madame Porta regretted not taking up genre painting sooner, which might have elevated her reputation. Ginevra tried portraiture but faced stiff competition from more established artists. Despite modest savings, she and Luigi grew anxious about their future.

Through the winter Luigi labored relentlessly as the market for writing shrank. Unable to afford help, he worked longer hours for the same pay. Ginevra painted several commendable pieces, but dealers avoided lesser-known artists, and low prices failed to sell her work.

Love and hope in their household were overshadowed by looming poverty. They concealed their worries—Ginevra lavished affection while Luigi hid his fears—clinging to one another and to the belief that love would sustain them.

One night Ginevra woke to find Luigi gone and discovered him working late to avoid burdening her. Moved, she began coloring lithographs to help, working through the night while Luigi toiled in his garret. Both pretended to sleep to spare the other their anxieties.

On another exhausted night, Luigi opened his window and saw Ginevra’s lamp across the courtyard, realizing she was also at work. When he confronted her, she replied that she could not be idle while he suffered. They shared a tender moment watching the dawn together and felt briefly hopeful.

Their struggles deepened when Ginevra gave birth to a son, Bartolomeo. The joy of parenthood briefly masked their financial burdens, but soon they were selling possessions and jewels. Poverty eroded their comfort and pride, escalating their desperation.

Months later, frail and worn, Ginevra struggled to care for a sickly child. Luigi, feeling helpless, sought aid but found none; he sold paintings and remaining furniture for a meager sum that only prolonged their hardship. Ginevra showed remarkable strength, managing the household and caring for the boy while often starving herself to provide for Luigi.

One evening she offered Luigi her last piece of bread and urged him to eat. Realizing her sacrifice, he was consumed by despair and left to find help. In desperation he decided to sell himself as a military substitute, hoping the money would save his family.

After securing an advance, he hurried home, only to find Ginevra asleep with their child. When he roused her, she smiled, then saw Bartolomeo cold and lifeless. Luigi, horrified, sought aid, but it was too late. In delirium Ginevra spoke of her love and sorrow; she died in his arms, leaving him devastated.

As Luigi mourned, Ginevra’s parents sat at home reflecting on their daughter, whom they had not seen in years. Elisa Piombo spoke of hoping to rekindle Bartolomeo’s love for Ginevra. Just as he seemed to soften, a man arrived and revealed Ginevra and Luigi’s tragic fate. The old couple was shattered; Bartolomeo’s heart was broken. The cycle of suffering had claimed another victim, leaving only memories and grief.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #####
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

Many tales, rich in incident or twist, can be told simply without losing charm. But some human experiences demand a delicate touch. Certain details—almost anatomical—require a nuanced rendering of thought. Portraits must carry a soul, and some truths are expressed only through secret harmonies shaped by time, circumstance, or an inner moral inclination.

Such revelations are needed for this simple story, aimed at the grave and reflective who find sustenance in tender emotions. If the writer, like a surgeon at a dying friend’s side, feels reverence for the subject, should not the reader share that feeling? Many recognize the vague sadness that surrounds us, a gentle suffering that can be oddly pleasing. If the reader has mourned a dear one, is alone, or the day is waning, they may continue; otherwise they should set this book aside. Those who have never buried a beloved relative will not grasp these pages, which may seem fragrant to some and colorless to others. The reader must have known the luxury of tears and the silent pangs of memory, evoking both regret for loss and smiles for past joys.

The writer assures that this narrative is a true story, worthy of the reader's emotional investment, if they possess any.

In contemporary France, language reflects as many idioms and idiosyncrasies as there are individuals. It amuses to hear differing interpretations of the same person from various Parisians. Ask a Practical man about Madame Firmiani: he measures her by means—a fine house in the rue du Bac, well-furnished salons, an income of one hundred thousand francs, and a husband once a receiver-general—concluding they are solid people with nothing to criticize.

A Lounger knows her socially, praises her respectable house, excellent tea, and exclusive gatherings. An Egotist dismisses her salons as tedious, believing a cultured mind should avoid trivial conversation. A Student calls her enchanting and wishes to introduce you. A Fop notes faded beauty but lingering charm and remnants of allure.

A Connoisseur admires her painting collection. A Spiteful woman warns other women against her, labeling her a dangerous siren who provokes jealousy. An Attache recalls her beauty from ten years earlier. A Distinguishing person praises her elite connections despite modest household circumstances.

A Duke claims not to know her, recognizing only those presented at court. A Ninny mistakenly insists she used to sing at the Opera, while two old ladies discuss her lineage and ties to the noble Cadignan family.

An Original assures you her salon is safe, since any scoundrels there are of high quality. An Observer describes her as a beautiful woman who rarely rises to greet guests, a mystery married to a mythical husband; he notes her charm, conversational skill, many admirers, but insists none are favored.

A Contradictor alleges she was once Murat’s mistress, while an Envious person dismisses her wealth as coquetry that ruined a young man’s prospects—an oft-seen species of envy whose stubbornness remains perplexing.

Each portrait is partial yet revealing: the Practical man measures by property and rank; the Lounger by hospitality and exclusivity; the Egotist by his disdain for common society; the Student by romantic admiration; the Fop by faded beauty; the Connoisseur by art; the Spiteful by rivalrous warning; the Attache by memory; the Distinguishing by connections; the Duke by court recognition; the Ninny by mistake; the old ladies by lineage; the Original by quality of companions; the Observer by elusive charm; the Contradictor and the Envious by rumor and resentment.

In sum, Madame Firmiani is a complex figure seen through many lenses, each shaped by the observer’s habits and biases. The narrative invites readers to explore these interpretations while reflecting on deeper emotional truths—reverence, loss, memory, and the quiet harmonies—that connect them all.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In January 1824 opinions about Madame Firmiani varied so widely that no clear picture emerged: she might be a widow or a wife, wise or foolish, virtuous or otherwise, rich or poor, beautiful or plain. This multiplicity showed how society can distort a reputation, like lithographic blocks producing different images from slight variations.

Monsieur de Bourbonne, an elderly provincial magnate from Touraine, became intrigued that winter. Practical and observant from managing an estate and dealing with peasants, he went to Paris to learn more, but gossip only deepened his confusion. A man of honor, he cherished his nephew Octave de Camps and had high hopes for him; he was annoyed when Octave sold an estate without consulting him. A relative then claimed Octave had squandered his fortune on Madame Firmiani and been reduced to teaching mathematics—a report that alarmed Monsieur de Bourbonne.

Refusing to assume the worst, he went to Paris determined to investigate. Using connections in the faubourg Saint-Germain, he gathered information and resolved to meet Madame Firmiani under the name de Rouxellay to learn the truth of her relation to Octave. Choosing a moment when Octave would be occupied, he soon confirmed his nephew’s financial troubles.

Monsieur de Rouxellay was no typical provincial uncle; a former member of the King’s guard, he knew society’s manners and politics. Entering Madame Firmiani’s salon with the Marquise de Listomere, he expressed pity for Octave, wondering how a woman of such apparent luxury could be involved with someone in such straits. Yet upon seeing Madame Firmiani his anger softened. In the salon’s atmosphere her beauty and elegance captivated him and made him rethink his initial judgments.

Madame Firmiani, thought to be twenty-five but closer to twenty-eight, had married the invisible Firmiani at sixteen. At this age she combined beauty, wealth, musical talent, intelligence, and wit. Esteemed in high society, she was admired by rivals and peers alike. But her popularity made her a target for malicious gossip: many envied her happiness and propriety, and unfounded rumors persisted, revealing the dangers of societal suspicion.

This brief portrait hints at a complex nature that could inspire admiration and intrigue. Her beauty, grace, and natural charm made her a figure of fascination, capable of stirring deep emotions and embodying elements from the noble to the tragic—reflecting the multifaceted nature of womanhood and the human experience.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The former guardsman lingered after the other guests had left; the clock struck two. “Madame,” he began as she rose, “I am the uncle of Monsieur Octave de Camps.”

Madame Firmiani sat down again, visibly affected. The old planter could not tell whether her pallor was shame or pleasure; women hide their joys, and the more delicate they are, the more they conceal them. Monsieur de Bourbonne misread her agitation.

“Well, monsieur?” she asked, giving him a look that left men baffled.

“Well, madame,” he replied, “someone in my province told me my nephew has ruined himself for you, living in a garret while you enjoy wealth. Forgive my rustic frankness; I thought you should know.”

“Stop, monsieur,” she interrupted, raising her hand. “I know of all that. You are too polite to continue if I ask you to drop the subject, and too gallant not to admit you have no right to question me. It would be absurd to defend myself. Believe me when I say I despise money in principle, though I married a wealthy man. Your nephew’s wealth or poverty is irrelevant; I welcome him as a friend. All my friends respect one another; I cannot include those I do not esteem. My guardian angel has kept me from gossip or dishonesty.”

Her voice rose slightly, then she finished with the composed teasing of Celimene before retreating to her rooms.

“Ah! the mischief!” Monsieur de Bourbonne thought. “What a woman! She is either sly or an angel.” He left in his hired coach.

The next morning, around eight, Monsieur de Bourbonne climbed to Octave’s rooms. The young professor was astonished. The uncle, who had stayed up all night, sat down and scolded him.

“You rascal! Since when is it fashionable to mock uncles with twenty-six thousand francs a year? What fault do you find with me? Have I not been a good uncle? I’m making you a fine estate in Touraine, though I won’t give it until the last moment. And what do you do? Sell your property and live like a lackey!”

“Uncle—”

“I’m not talking about uncles; I’m talking about nephews. Have you been gambling? Lost money at the Bourse? Say, ‘Uncle, I’m a wretch,’ and I’ll forgive you. But if you lie, I’ll go back to my youthful extravagance. I saw Madame Firmiani yesterday—she is charming. Have you ruined yourself for her?”

“Yes, uncle.”

“In my day women were cleverer at ruining men, but this one—she’s from the old school.”

“Uncle,” Octave said, “you are mistaken. Madame Firmiani deserves your esteem.”

“Youth, youth! Always the same!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Go on, tell me the same old story. But remember, I’ve had my share of gallantry.”

“My dear uncle, here is a letter that will explain nearly everything,” Octave said, taking an elegant portfolio. “When you’ve read it, I’ll tell you the rest.”

“I don’t have my spectacles; read it aloud.”

Octave began: “‘My beloved—’”

“Then you are still intimate with her?” his uncle interrupted.

“Of course.”

“You haven’t parted from her?”

“Parted! We are married.”

“Heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Then why do you live in a garret?”

“Let me continue.”

Octave read on, emotion making him falter: “‘My beloved Husband, you ask why I am sad. I cannot deceive you; this may be a misfortune, for a wife should be gay and caressing. I cannot deceive you, even if my happiness depended on it. I long to love you forever; a woman’s glory is the man she loves. Esteem, consideration, honor—must they not belong to him who receives our all? Well, my angel has fallen. The tale you told me has tarnished my past joys. I feel humiliated by you—whom I thought the most honorable of men. I must have deep confidence in your heart to make this avowal: how can you, knowing your father unjustly deprived others of their property, keep it? You told me of this in the room where we loved; you are a gentleman, yet you think yourself noble and remain silent. I try to excuse you for youth and thoughtlessness, but your laughs wounded me. Reflect on that ruined family always in distress; poor young girls who might curse you nightly; an old father saying each night, “We might not be starving if that man’s father had been honest.”’”
