“Good heavens!” cried Monsieur de Bourbonne. “Surely you didn’t tell that woman about your father’s affair with the Bourgneufs? Women know more about wasting a fortune than making one.”

“They know about integrity. But let me read on: ‘Octave, no power on earth can change the principles of honor. Look into your conscience and ask by what name you call the action of keeping your property.’”

The nephew looked at his uncle, who lowered his head.

“‘I will not tell you all the thoughts that assail me; they reduce to one: I cannot respect the man who knowingly keeps ill-gotten gains, whatever the amount. Five francs stolen at play or five times a hundred thousand won by a legal trick are equally dishonorable. I feel degraded by the very love which has hitherto been my joy. There rises in my soul a voice my tenderness cannot stifle. I have wept to feel that I have more conscience than love. Were you to commit a crime, I would hide you from justice, but my devotion could go no farther. Love for a woman means boundless confidence and reverence for the being to whom she belongs. I have always conceived love as a fire that purifies noble feelings. I have one thing to say: come to me poor, and my love shall be redoubled. If not, renounce it. Should I see you no more, I shall know what it means. But I do not urge restitution as a sacrifice to love. Consult your conscience. A man who loves sincerely respects the sanctity of her trust too deeply to dishonor himself. I blame myself for preaching; scold me if you will—but not much. Between us, the power is yours—you alone should perceive your faults.’”

“Well, uncle?” Octave asked, tears in his eyes.

“There’s more; finish it.”

“Oh, the rest is for a lover’s eyes only,” Octave smiled.

“Yes, right, my boy,” the old man said gently. “I have had many affairs, but I too have loved. But I don’t understand why you teach mathematics.”

“My dear uncle, I am your nephew; isn’t that as good as saying I dipped into my father’s capital? After reading this letter, a revolution occurred within me. I paid my entire debt of remorse in one day. I cannot describe my state. As I drove in the Bois, a voice said, ‘That horse is not yours’; when I ate, it said, ‘You have stolen this food.’ I was ashamed. The fresher my honesty, the deeper it was. I rushed to Madame Firmiani. That day I experienced pleasures of the heart far beyond millions. Together, we calculated what was due to the Bourgneufs, and I condemned myself to pay three percent interest. But all I had did not cover the full amount. We were lovers enough for her to offer her savings, and I accepted.”

“What! Besides other virtues, does that adorable woman save money?” his uncle exclaimed.

“Don’t laugh at her, uncle; her position forced prudence. Her husband went to Greece in 1820 and died three years later. It has been impossible to obtain legal proof of his death or the will he made leaving everything to her; those papers were lost or stolen during the troubles in Greece. Uncertain whether she might lose her fortune, she lived carefully. I wished to acquire property of my own to provide for her if she lost hers.”

“But why didn’t you tell me? I love you enough to pay your debts. I’ll play the generous uncle now and take revenge!”

“Ah! uncle, I want to get rich by my industry. If you want to help, give me an allowance of two or three thousand francs a year until I find a venture needing capital. Right now, I’m so happy all I desire is the means to live. I give lessons so I do not live at anyone’s expense. If you only knew the joy of making that restitution! I found the Bourgneufs miserable. The old father was a lottery agent; the daughters kept his books and cared for the house; the mother was always ill. The daughters are charming but have learned that beauty without money is undervalued. My visit was a drama: to be Providence itself, to fulfill that futile wish, ‘If heaven sent us twenty thousand francs a year’—to bring wealth to a family sitting by one lamp over a poor fire! Words cannot describe it. My extreme justice seemed unjust to them. If there is a Paradise, my father is happy there now. As for me, I am loved as no man has been. Madame Firmiani gives me more than happiness; she inspires a delicacy of feeling I lacked. I call her my dear conscience. I find honesty profitable; I shall get rich by myself. I have an industrial scheme; if it succeeds, I’ll earn millions.”

“Ah! my boy, you have your mother’s soul,” said the old man, eyes filling with memories of his sister.

At that moment a carriage stopped.

“There she is!” he exclaimed. “I know her horses by the way they pull up.”

Moments later Madame Firmiani entered.

“Ah!” she said, annoyed to see Monsieur de Bourbonne. “But our uncle is not in the way,” she added quickly, smiling. “I came to humbly entreat my husband to accept my fortune. The Austrian Embassy has sent me a document proving Monsieur Firmiani’s death and the will, which his valet kept safe for me. Octave, you can accept it all; you are richer than I, for you have treasures here,” she added, laying her hand on his heart, “to which none but God can add.” Unable to contain her joy, she laid her head on her husband’s breast.

“My dear niece,” said the old man, “in my day we made love; in yours, you love. You women are all that is best in humanity; you are not even guilty of your faults, for they come through us.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The Rue du Tourniquet-Saint-Jean, once a dark, winding lane near the Hôtel de Ville that ended at the Rue Martroi, was so narrow it scarcely exceeded six feet and was closed by a turnstile until 1823. It filled with gutter water, remained perpetually muddy, received sunlight only briefly in summer, and retained a constant dampness in the air.

Residents lit their lamps at five and kept them through winter. A passerby moving toward the Marais felt as if he were passing through cellars, like many Paris streets. The corner house at the junction with the Rue de la Tixeranderie betrayed its age: iron rings still scarred the walls where chains had once fastened passersby for safety.

Built to combat dampness, the house raised its cellars above ground, its entrance reached by three steps. The door had a closed arch crowned by a keystone carved with a female head; three high windows, barred in iron, let light into a small dwelling. Through them curious passersby could glimpse two beds draped in green serge and an old woman tending a brazier while preparing a stew of porters’ wives. The kitchen-dining room was sparsely furnished yet kept clean and thrifty.

The old woman, pale and wrinkled, seemed as fixed as the walls. She sat by the hearth with an air that mixed mischief and resignation, while her daughter worked at an embroidery frame. The mother’s fingers moved slowly behind spectacles, occasionally glancing out the window; the daughter bent over her work with a downcast gaze.

Their modest home reflected the lives of working-class women in Paris. Despite the surroundings, the girl—Caroline—retained youthful charm: rosy cheeks and bright eyes that hinted at a spirit not yet dimmed. The mother speculated about passersby, dreaming of a better future for her daughter; Caroline remained absorbed in her embroidery and the daily grind.

One August day in 1815 the mother noticed a new face among the regular passersby: a tall, thin man in black, whose solemn demeanor intrigued her. She guessed he was a clerk from the Prefect’s office and brightened at the thought. Caroline, roused from indifference by her mother’s remarks, looked up but the stranger had already gone.

The old woman eagerly awaited his return, certain he would pass again. When he did, Caroline saw him and felt an unfamiliar sympathy, noticing traces of suffering on his face. This was the first time a passerby had pierced her indifference; for the first time she felt compassion beyond her own struggles. As she resumed her embroidery she found herself looking forward to seeing him again, intrigued by the possibility that this stranger might alter the course of her life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For over two months the “Black Gentleman” behaved erratically, not always passing the Rue du Tourniquet at the same hour. Madame Crochard noticed his distracted air—lost in thought and scarcely seeing her or her daughter Caroline. One mid-September morning Caroline’s presence caught him; he paused to watch her at the window. That pause began a series of furtive glances, suggesting a growing understanding.

In the weeks that followed he passed twice daily, and their brief encounters became a routine they both anticipated. Their quiet exchanges showed mutual concern: Caroline sensed his troubles, and he knew when she was anxious about work. Yet both remained cautious, fearing that a closer bond might bring complications or heartbreak.

Madame Crochard, annoyed by her daughter’s indecision, grew displeased with the Gentleman, who had once drawn her benign attention. As winter approached and hardships wore on Caroline’s spirit, the Gentleman overheard a conversation revealing severe financial strain. Touched, he secretly left money for them; Caroline found it the next day, and a moment of silent gratitude passed between them.

In early May Caroline suggested a trip to Montmorency, and the Gentleman—more somber than usual—unexpectedly accompanied them. On the carriage ride Madame Crochard pretended to sleep, giving Caroline and the Gentleman space for a lighthearted conversation that revealed their growing fondness. The countryside’s beauty and the warmth of their exchange cheered him, and he began to see Caroline differently.

Their bond deepened as they traded personal stories: Caroline spoke of family sacrifices and her mother’s efforts; the Gentleman, later called Roger, was drawn to her honesty and resilience. The day in Montmorency brimmed with laughter and joy, a sharp contrast to earlier somber moments.

As evening approached Caroline voiced the happiness she felt, prompting Roger to consider the possibility of joy beyond that day. Their shared understanding hinted at a deeper attachment. Preparing to leave, they playfully held hands—a discreet, significant gesture Madame Crochard, though pretending not to notice, was aware of. The moment marked an important step in their relationship, full of unspoken promises and hope for a brighter future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The adventures of Roger and Caroline continue in modern Paris, where new apartments suit honeymooners. In a pristine building on Rue Taitbout, Caroline has lived for over a month in a charming second-floor apartment, carefully furnished. The rooms wear fresh paint and elegant English-influenced furnishings.

Caroline's bedroom has gray hangings trimmed with green silk, comfortable seating, and a writing table for love letters. A bronze clock showing Love crowning Psyche and a Gothic-patterned carpet complete the cozy atmosphere. As she readies for Roger's visit, her hairdresser flatters her about his admiration.

On a chilly September day in 1816, Caroline dresses in a green grenadine gown edged with chinchilla. She steps onto the balcony, heart racing, watching for Roger. After fifteen minutes her impatience grows, but her spirits lift when she finally sees him approaching.

Roger arrives and they embrace passionately, reveling in their reunion after three days apart. He tells her he must attend a meeting with the Attorney-General, disappointing Caroline, who protests the idea of enjoying the theatre without him and voices her deep affection.

Despite this, Caroline soon focuses on the joy of being with him. They share tender moments; she expresses her wish to take the name Caroline de Bellefeuille, sits on his lap, and says she'll use her earnings to help the poor, emphasizing gratitude for their life together.

Her excitement grows as she plays a sonata, but Roger, mindful of obligations, prepares to leave. They exchange a playful farewell; she watches him drive away, savoring the parting and longing for his return.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Five years after Mademoiselle Caroline de Bellefeuille settled on Rue Taitbout, she sits calmly with her four-year-old son Charles, who plays with a rocking horse. Now twenty-four, Caroline’s beauty reflects contentment. She has embraced motherhood, dedicating herself to nurturing her children and cultivating their appreciation for goodness and beauty. She enjoys simple pleasures and shows little interest in luxury or social status.

Rocking her infant daughter Eugenie, Caroline reflects on the past, especially happy visits to Bellefeuille with Roger. Their love has deepened in the simplicity of life together, free from social complications, though Caroline is puzzled by Roger’s secrecy about his own happiness.

On May 6, 1822, the anniversary of an important day for them, Caroline prepares dinner for Roger. He brings a document detailing Eugenie’s marriage portion, prompting a tense discussion about unequal provisions for their children. Roger argues that Charles, as a boy, must learn self-sufficiency, while Eugenie, as a girl, should receive a fortune. Their evening remains warm and lively, ending with a lantern show for Charles. As night deepens, Roger must leave; Caroline feels the familiar ache of separation. He promises three days off, but duty soon intervenes.

Shortly after this anniversary, Caroline hurries to her mother, Madame Crochard, who is gravely ill. The widow’s acquaintances, unaware Caroline is her daughter, have summoned a priest for the last rites. Caroline arrives amid confusion and deceit: the women hope to benefit from Madame Crochard’s wealth. Despite their motives, Caroline’s devotion is clear as she tries to care for her mother.

In her final moments, Madame Crochard expresses regret over their estrangement and reveals that the priest compelled her to confess about Caroline’s benefactor. This disclosure hints at a complex web of relationships and secrets that will unfold.

To clarify those connections, we return to events before Madame Crochard’s death. In November 1805, a young barrister, Monsieur de Granville, finds himself in a precarious position after leaving a gathering at the High Chancellor’s residence. He’s offered a career boost thanks to his uncle’s influence. Then he receives a letter from his father urging him to return home: a marriage to Mademoiselle Angelique Bontems could secure his future. The letter stresses the financial advantages despite the Bontems family’s social stigma. Granville, torn between ambition and feeling, recalls childhood affection for Angelique, whom he has not seen in years.

The next day he travels to Bayeux, where his father meets him and brings him to the Bontems’ home, dominated by the widow’s strict religious practices. Granville senses dread at the prospect of marriage, yet his father’s insistence weighs heavily. During a church service he glimpses Angelique; her beauty captivates him anew. Their brief exchange rekindles his feelings and, impatient at the service’s end, he approaches her—setting the stage for a reunion that could alter both their lives.

The intertwining stories of Caroline and Granville reveal complexities of love, duty, and societal expectation as they navigate desire, familial obligation, and personal ambition.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For about two weeks after the Vicomte de Granville’s engagement to Mademoiselle Bontems, he visited her dreary drawing-room regularly. He watched Angelique prepare her trousseau; she never raised religious topics herself, and when Granville playfully handled her rosary or mocked practices she quietly returned it to its bag with a serene smile. She firmly believed in the Church, insisting a mother must have a religion and questioning how one could judge between disbelievers and God.

Granville, captivated by her conviction, sometimes felt tempted to adopt her beliefs, though he mistook his desire for her as love. Angelique, balancing feeling and duty, left him uncertain about his own sentiments. He was grateful to Madame Bontems for instilling religion in Angelique, which he thought would help her face life’s challenges.

At their marriage-contract signing Madame Bontems required Granville to promise respect for Angelique’s religious practices and freedom of conscience. Angelique’s innocent gaze convinced him; their spiritual guide, the Abbe Fontanon, approved. Soon after they married and moved to Paris, where Granville became public prosecutor.

Angelique persuaded him to take a large ground-floor apartment in the Marais, near a church and chapel, arguing it would benefit future children. Granville, who preferred a more fashionable district, acquiesced. He concentrated on his study and left decoration to Angelique, who delighted in furnishing their home. But Granville was dismayed by the apartment’s cold, solemn atmosphere, reflecting Angelique’s austere upbringing.

Despite efforts to understand her choices, he grew increasingly frustrated by the lack of warmth and charm at home, attributing the sober decor to her Bayeux upbringing. He tried to comfort Angelique, believing happiness would come from her love and gentleness rather than material comforts.

From April through winter their life seemed harmonious: Granville kept busy with work, and he remained unaware of strict religious observances Angelique enforced—Lenten meals and other austerities she disguised as carelessness. He accompanied her to church on Sundays but did not grasp the depth of her fervor until a disruptive event.

In November 1808 the Canon of Bayeux, who had earlier guided Angelique, arrived in Paris alarmed by what he saw as her moral decline and sought to reclaim her from a lax life. Angelique adopted stricter practices, which led to misunderstandings about social obligations. Granville grew frustrated by her frequent excuses to avoid social events, especially balls.

After a particularly tense evening he confronted Angelique about her demeanor and attire, urging her to embrace her social role. Arguments escalated; Angelique cited prohibitions from her director against attending balls. Granville insisted her priest overstepped his bounds, and their dispute turned theological, culminating in Angelique writing to the Pope for guidance.

The Pope’s reply sided with Granville, asserting a wife should accompany her husband even if it risked compromising her virtue. Rather than resolve matters, this deepened their divide; Angelique and her director dismissed the Pope’s advice. Granville, frustrated by her strict observances, began asserting his own preferences—ordering meat daily despite her objections.

As the household atmosphere grew oppressive, Granville withdrew from social life, feeling weighed down by Angelique’s bigotry. The home became stifling and gloomy, affecting everyone, including the servants. He recognized their house had turned into a place devoid of joy, marked by rigid piety.

His discontent deepened as Angelique’s beauty was overshadowed by austerity. He felt trapped in a marriage where duty replaced affection; her devotion left little room for love. The children, raised under Angelique’s strict rules, suffered from the oppressive climate, prompting Granville to send his sons to school early and resolve to marry off his daughters as soon as possible.

Granville’s isolation grew as he struggled with his marriage. He sought solace in work, attempting to write a book on law, while Angelique tried to convert him back to her views, adding to his frustration. Their relationship deteriorated, leaving him increasingly alienated from his wife and their home, which had become a place of coldness and rigidity rather than warmth and companionship.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Between 1806 and 1825 the de Granville household experienced little outward change, but its inner life was tense and stony. Madame de Granville, after losing her husband's affection, kept up her devotions and paid for Masses to discover and amend her faults in hopes of restoring their relationship. Granville, now a prominent judge, withdrew to the entresol of their home to avoid her; each morning a housekeeper passed messages between them while he routinely declined breakfast, citing pressing court business.

At thirty-five she looked older, waiting for his return and ready with narrow-minded remarks. When Granville refused the presidency of a provincial court, the Countess’s friends and her confessor suspected an illicit affair, a thought that horrified her because she could not imagine her husband capable of such sin. During Lent of 1822 her health suffered from stress and low fever, a condition aggravated by his indifference. Although she tried to be more affectionate, her bigotry often undermined her efforts.

In late May, after some improvement, she consulted the Abbe Fontanon, who delivered a crushing disclosure: Granville had lived in concubinage for seven years, fathered two children, and squandered much of their fortune. Incredulous and torn between faith and betrayal, she wavered; the Abbe urged forgiveness and patience for Granville’s possible enlightenment, but she was consumed by turmoil. Resolute, she left home to confront him.

At the same time, Roger and Mademoiselle de Bellefeuille were in a fraught exchange: she professed hatred for his wife, whom she blamed for their unhappiness. Roger offered reassurances and hopes for a future together, but their conversation was cut short when Madame de Granville suddenly fainted. On recovering she directly confronted Granville, demanding to know whether he loved and had been faithful to her. He countered by accusing her of crushing his happiness with piety and failing in her duties as a loving wife.

Their confrontation revealed deep, irreconcilable differences. Granville argued that genuine love requires sacrifice and understanding and that he had sought happiness elsewhere because she had neglected their marriage for religious devotion. Madame de Granville clung to moral righteousness; jealousy and anger flared as she realized the extent of their estrangement. Granville admitted loving another and refused to revive a feeling that had faded after years of neglect.

The exchange ended in a bitter recognition of a failed marriage: Granville maintained that love cannot be commanded or reclaimed at will, and Madame de Granville was left to face the consequences of their choices and an uncertain future.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Early in December 1833, a white-haired, worn man, aged by grief rather than years, walked the Rue Gaillon at midnight. He paused before a modest two-story house; a dim light flickered through attic windows, some patched with paper. As he watched, a young man emerged. Recognizing him, the young man approached hesitantly. “What, is it you, Monsieur le Président? Alone at this hour, so far from the Rue Saint-Lazare. Allow me to give you my arm. The pavement is so greasy this morning that if we do not hold each other up, we shall find it hard to escape a tumble.”

The Comte de Granville replied, “But, my dear sir, I am no more than fifty-five, unfortunately for me. A physician of your celebrity must know that at that age a man is still hale and strong.”

“Then you are in waiting on a lady, I suppose,” said Horace Bianchon. “You are not in the habit of going about Paris on foot. When a man keeps such fine horses—”

“Still, when I am not visiting in the evening, I commonly return from the Courts or the club on foot,” Granville answered.

“And with large sums of money about you, perhaps!” the doctor exclaimed. “It is a positive invitation to the assassin’s knife.”

“I am not afraid of that,” Granville said with melancholy indifference.

“Then do not stand about,” Bianchon urged, leading the Count toward the boulevard. “A little more and I shall believe you are bent on robbing me of your last illness, dying by some other hand than mine.”

“You caught me playing the spy,” Granville admitted. “For some time past I have observed at a window on the third floor of your house the shadow of a person who seems to work with heroic constancy.”

Granville paused, as if in pain. “I take as great an interest in that garret as a citizen of Paris must feel in the finishing of the Palais Royal.”

“Well,” Bianchon said eagerly, “I can tell you—”

“Tell me nothing,” Granville interrupted. “I would not give a centime to know whether the shadow that moves across that shabby blind is that of a man or a woman, nor whether the inhabitant of that attic is happy or miserable. Though I was surprised to see no one at work there this evening, I stopped to look solely for the pleasure of indulging in conjectures as numerous and as idiotic as those of idlers who see a building left half-finished. For nine years I have ceased to wonder that old men amuse themselves with growing flowers and planting trees; the events of life have taught them disbelief in all human affection. I grew old within a few days. I will no longer attach myself to any creature but to unreasoning animals, plants, or superficial things. I think more of Taglioni’s grace than of all human feeling. I abhor life and the world in which I live alone. Nothing, nothing can move or interest me.”

“But you have children?” Bianchon asked.

“My children!” Granville repeated bitterly. “Yes—my eldest daughter is the Comtesse de Vandenesse. The other will, through her sister’s connections, make a good match. My sons have succeeded; the Viscount was public prosecutor at Limoges and is now President of the Court at Orléans; the younger is public prosecutor in Paris. My children have their own cares, their own anxieties. If one of those hearts had been devoted to me, if one had tried to fill the void I have here,” he said, striking his breast, “that one would have failed in life, sacrificing it to me. And why should he? To bring sunshine into my few remaining years—and would he have succeeded? Might I not have accepted such generosity as a debt? But, doctor,” he added with deep irony, “it is not for nothing that we teach them arithmetic and how to count. At this moment perhaps they are waiting for my money.”

“O Monsieur le Comte, how could such an idea enter your head—you who are kind, friendly, and humane! If I were not myself a living proof of the benevolence you exercise so liberally—”

“To please myself,” Granville replied. “I pay for a sensation, as I would pay a pile of gold to recover the most childish illusion that would make my heart glow. I help my fellow-creatures for my own sake, just as I gamble; I look for gratitude from none. I should see you die without blinking; and I beg of you to feel the same with regard to me. The events of life have swept over my heart like the lavas of Vesuvius over Herculaneum. The town is there—dead.”

“Those who have brought a soul as warm and living as yours to such a pitch of indifference are indeed guilty!” Bianchon exclaimed.

“Say no more,” Granville said, shuddering with aversion.

“You have a malady which you ought to allow me to treat,” Bianchon insisted.

“What, do you know of a cure for death?” Granville cried irritably.

“I undertake, Monsieur le Comte, to revive the heart you believe to be frozen.”

“Are you a match for Talma, then?” Granville asked satirically.

“No, Monsieur le Comte. But Nature is as far above Talma as Talma is superior to me. Listen: the garret you are interested in is inhabited by a woman of about thirty, and her love is carried to fanaticism. The object of her adoration is a young man of pleasing appearance but endowed with every conceivable vice. This fellow is a gambler, and it is hard to say which he is more addicted to—wine or women; he has committed acts deserving punishment by law. To him, this unhappy woman sacrificed a life of ease, a man who worshiped her, and the father of her children.”

“What is wrong, Monsieur le Comte?” Granville asked.

“Nothing. Go on,” he replied.

“She has allowed him to squander a perfect fortune; she would give him the world if she had it; she works night and day; and many times she has seen the wretch she adores rob her even of the money saved for her children’s clothes and food. Only three days ago she sold her hair, the finest I ever saw; he came in, and she could not hide the gold piece quickly enough. For a smile, for a kiss, she gave up the price of a fortnight’s life and peace. Is it not dreadful, and yet sublime? But work is wearing her cheeks hollow. Her children’s crying has broken her heart; she is ill, and at this moment on her wretched bed. This evening they had nothing to eat; the children have not strength to cry; they were silent when I went up.”

Bianchon stood still. Granville, despite himself, put his hand into his waistcoat pocket.

“I can guess, my young friend, how it is that she is yet alive if you attend her,” Granville said.

“O poor soul!” Bianchon cried. “Who could refuse to help her? I only wish I were richer, for I hope to cure her of her passion.”

“But how can you expect me to pity a form of misery of which the joys to me would seem cheaply purchased with my whole fortune!” Granville exclaimed, withdrawing his hand empty of the notes Bianchon had assumed he was feeling for. “That woman feels; she is alive! Would not Louis XV. have given his kingdom to rise from the grave and have three days of youth and life? And is not that the history of thousands of dead men, thousands of sick men, thousands of old men?”

“Poor Caroline!” Bianchon lamented.

At the name Granville shuddered and grasped the doctor’s arm tightly.

“Her name is Caroline Crochard?” he asked, his voice breaking.

“Then you know her?” Bianchon replied, astonished.

“And the wretch’s name is Solvet. Ay, you have kept your word!” Granville exclaimed. “You have roused my heart to the most terrible pain it can suffer until it is dust. That emotion, too, is a gift from hell, and I always know how to pay those debts.”

They reached the corner of the Rue de la Chaussée-d’Antin. A night scavenger, with a shriveled face, stood by the curb.

“Do you ever pick up a thousand-franc note?” Granville asked.

“Now and then, master,” the scavenger answered.

“And you restore them?”

“It depends on the reward offered.”

“You’re the man for me.” Granville handed him a thousand-franc note. “Take this, but remember, I give it to you on condition of your spending it at the wineshop, getting drunk, fighting, beating your wife, blacking your friends’ eyes. That will give work to the watch, the surgeon, the druggist—perhaps to the police, the public prosecutor, the judge, and the prison warders. Do not try to do anything else, or the devil will be revenged on you sooner or later.”

A draughtsman would need the pencil of Charlet and Callot, the brush of Teniers and Rembrandt to capture the night scene accurately.

“Now I have squared accounts with hell and had some pleasure for my money,” Granville said, pointing out the scavenger’s grotesque face to Bianchon, who stood stupefied. “As for Caroline Crochard—she may die of hunger and thirst, hearing the heartrending cries of her starving children, convinced of the baseness of the man she loves. I will not give a sou to rescue her; and because you have helped her, I will see you no more—”

Granville left Bianchon standing like a statue and walked briskly to the Rue Saint-Lazare, where a carriage waited at his door.

“Monsieur, your son, the attorney-general, came about an hour ago,” the servant said. “He is waiting for you in your bedroom.”

Granville dismissed the servant and entered the room. “What motive can be strong enough to require you to infringe the order I have given my children never to come to me unless I send for them?” he asked his son.

“Father,” the younger man replied tremulously, “I hope you will forgive me when you have heard me.”

“Your reply is proper,” Granville said. “Sit down,” he gestured to a chair. “But whether I walk up and down or take a seat, speak without heeding me.”

“Father,” the son continued, “this afternoon, at four o’clock, a very young man who was arrested in the house of a friend of mine, whom he had robbed, appealed to you. He says he is your son.”

“His name?” Granville asked hoarsely.

“Charles Crochard.”

“That will do,” Granville said, waving his hand imperiously.

He paced in silence; his son remained quiet.

“My son,” he began in a mild, fatherly voice, causing the young lawyer to start, “Charles Crochard spoke the truth. I am glad you came to me tonight, my good Eugène.” He handed him a bundle of banknotes. “Here is a considerable sum; use it as you see fit in this matter. I trust you implicitly and approve whatever arrangements you may make, now or later. Eugène, my dear son, kiss me. We part perhaps for the last time. I shall tomorrow crave my dismissal from the King, and I am going to Italy.

“Though a father owes no account of his life to his children, he is bound to bequeath to them the experience Fate sells him dearly—is it not a part of their inheritance? When you marry,” he continued, involuntarily shuddering, “do not undertake it lightly; that act is the most important of all which society requires of us. Remember to study the character of the woman who is to be your partner; consult me too, I will judge of her myself. A lack of union between husband and wife, from whatever cause, leads to terrible misfortune; sooner or later we are always punished for contravening the social law. But I will write to you on this subject from Florence. A father who presides over a supreme court of justice must not have to blush in the presence of his son. Good-bye.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In the midst of the story — the middle of the story: events, turning point.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

The incident in this sketch occurred in late November 1809, at the height of Napoleon’s empire. The echoes of the Battle of Wagram still resonated in Austria as peace was negotiated between France and the Coalition. Kings and princes orbited Napoleon, who reveled in power and showcased the splendor of Parisian entertainments around his marriage to an Austrian archduchess. The opulence of the French aristocracy—diamonds and gold on women and uniforms—contrasted sharply with the Republic’s earlier austerity. The atmosphere was intoxicating as military leaders indulged in wealth amassed through the sacrifices of countless soldiers.

Many women adopted a carefree attitude reminiscent of the Louis XV era. Whether influenced by the fallen monarchy or the Imperial family, both sexes embraced a hedonistic lifestyle that seemed to foretell impending doom. The allure of military men became a frenzy aligned with the Emperor’s interests: frequent calls to arms made every peace treaty temporary, and women’s affections shifted rapidly, with some moving from mistress to widow within weeks.

The appeal of soldiers lay in several things: the prospect of early widowhood, the lure of historical legacy, or the romantic notion of courage. Women were drawn to the idea that their passions would remain secret, buried on the battlefield. This fascination with soldiers—seen as embodiments of honor and adventure—created a culture where bravery was prized. The period also featured a mania for glittering possessions: diamonds and fireworks were in vogue, and men adorned themselves with jewels, following extravagant exemplars like Murat.

Comte de Gondreville, once Citizen Malin, had risen to prominence and delayed a grand entertainment to outshine rival courtiers and flatter Napoleon. His drawing-rooms filled with ambassadors, influentials, and a few princes, all awaiting the Emperor’s arrival. A private dispute between Napoleon and Josephine, hinting at divorce, kept him away from the festivities, though the assembled guests remained unaware of the tension.

The most beautiful women in Paris flocked to Gondreville’s event, embodying luxury and elegance. The financial elite, proud of their fortunes, sought to rival the military’s splendor, using grand balls to present daughters to Napoleon’s inner circle—hoping to exchange wealth for favor. Women confident in their beauty tested their allure, masking true intentions behind smiles; amid the gaiety many were more wary of friends than enemies, since the pursuit of wealth and power underpinned social life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
These remarks are necessary to explain the incidents surrounding the little imbroglio that is the subject of this study, reflecting the tone prevalent in Paris drawing-rooms at the time.

“Look toward the pedestal supporting that candelabrum—do you see the young lady with her hair styled a la Chinoise? She’s in the corner to the left, with bluebells in her chestnut curls. She’s so pale you might think she’s ill, delicate-looking, and very small. Now she’s turning her head; her almond-shaped blue eyes seem made for tears. She’s leaning forward to see Madame de Vaudremont below the crowd.”

“I see her now. You only had to mention her lovely complexion; I would have recognized her. She has the whitest skin here, but she’s either a prude or a coquette, as her bodice barely hints at her beauty. What shoulders! What lily-whiteness!”

“Who is she?” asked the first speaker.

“I don’t know.”

“An aristocrat! Do you want to keep them all to yourself, Montcornet?”

“You of all people to tease me!” replied Montcornet with a smile. “Do you think you can insult a poor general like me just because you’re a rival of Soulanges? Or is it because I’ve only just arrived in this Promised Land? Come, Monsieur le Maitre des Requetes, let us glean in the field you can only hold precariously. We have a right to live! If you knew the German women, you might help me with the Parisian you love best.”

“Well, General, since you’ve noticed that lady, have you seen her dance?”

“Where have you been? There’s a triple rank of the most undaunted coquettes of Paris between her and the swarm of dancing men. It’s a miracle anyone could spot her in that corner, buried in gloom despite the candles. Between her and us is a sparkle of diamonds and glances, so many floating plumes and fluttering lace that it’s impossible for any dancer to detect her among those stars. How could you think she’s the wife of some sous-préfet from Lippe or Dyle?”

“Oh, he will be!” exclaimed the Master of Appeals.

“I doubt it,” replied the Colonel of Cuirassiers, laughing. “She seems as raw in intrigue as you are in diplomacy. I bet you don’t know how she got there.”

The lawyer looked at the Colonel with a mix of contempt and curiosity.

“Well,” Montcornet continued, “she probably arrived punctually at nine, the first of the company, and embarrassed the Comtesse de Gondreville, who can’t think straight. Repulsed by the mistress of the house and driven into the corner by jealous ladies, she became a victim of their coalition against her. Each of those sweet faces would have conspired to keep the men from taking her out. That’s how those tender-looking women would have formed a coalition against the stranger without saying a word beyond, ‘Do you know that little woman in blue?’ If you care to run the gauntlet of flattering glances and inviting questions, try to get through the rampart defending that Queen of Dyle, Lippe, or Charente. You’ll see how even the dullest woman will invent a wile to keep the plaintive stranger in the shadows. Doesn’t she look like an elegy?”

“Do you think so, Montcornet? Then she must be married?”

“Why not a widow?”

“She would be less passive,” said the lawyer, laughing.

“She might be the widow of a gambler,” replied the handsome Colonel.

“Of course; since the peace, there are many widows of that sort!” said Martial. “But my dear Montcornet, we are simpletons. That face is too innocent; there’s too much youth and freshness for her to be married. What splendid flesh-tints! Nothing has sunk in the modeling of her nose. Her lips and chin are as fresh as a white rosebud, though her expression is veiled by clouds of sadness. Who could make that young creature weep?”

“Women cry for little,” said the Colonel.

“I don’t know,” replied Martial; “but she’s not crying because she’s left without a partner; her grief is not new. It’s clear she beautified herself for this evening with intention. I would wager she’s in love already.”

“Bah! She’s perhaps the daughter of some German princeling; no one talks to her,” said Montcornet.

“Dear! How unhappy a poor child may be! Can there be anything more graceful and refined than our little stranger? Yet not one of those furies around her, who think they can feel, will say a word to her. If she would only speak, we’d see if she has fine teeth.”

“Bless me, you boil over like milk at the least increase of temperature!” cried the Colonel, a bit nettled at finding a rival in his friend.

“What!” exclaimed the lawyer, ignoring the Colonel’s question. “Can nobody here tell us the name of this exotic flower?”

“Some lady companion!” said Montcornet.

“A companion! Wearing sapphires fit for a queen and a dress of Malines lace? Tell that to the marines, General. You wouldn’t shine in diplomacy if you jump from a German princess to a lady companion.”

Montcornet stopped a man by taking his arm—a fat little man with iron-gray hair and clever eyes, mingling unceremoniously with various groups.

“Gondreville, my friend,” said Montcornet, “who is that charming little woman sitting under that huge candelabrum?”

“The candelabrum? Ravrio’s work; Isabey made the design.”

“Oh, I recognized your lavishness and taste; but the lady?”

“Ah! I don’t know. Some friend of my wife’s, no doubt.”

“Or your mistress, you old rascal.”

“No, on my honor. The Comtesse de Gondreville is the only one capable of inviting people no one knows.”

Despite this acerbic comment, the fat little man’s lips retained a smile from the Colonel’s suggestion. Montcornet returned to the lawyer, who had rejoined a neighboring group, intent on asking, but in vain, for information about the fair unknown. He grasped Martial’s arm and said in his ear:

“Mind what you’re about. Madame de Vaudremont has been watching you for some time with ominous attentiveness; she can guess by the movement of your lips what you say to me. Our eyes have already told her too much; she’s thinking even more than we are about the little blue lady.”

“That’s too old a trick in warfare, my dear Montcornet! However, what do I care? Like the Emperor, when I make a conquest, I keep it.”

“Martial, your fatuity cries out for a lesson. You, a civilian, and so lucky as to be the husband-designate of Madame de Vaudremont, a widow of two-and-twenty with four thousand napoleons a year—who slips such a diamond on your finger,” he added, taking the lawyer’s left hand, which the young man complacently allowed; “and you affect the Lovelace, as if you were a colonel obliged to maintain the military reputation! Fie! Just think of all you may lose.”

“At any rate, I shall not lose my liberty,” replied Martial with a forced laugh.

He cast a passionate glance at Madame de Vaudremont, who responded only with a smile of unease, having seen the Colonel examining the lawyer’s ring.

“Listen to me, Martial. If you flutter around my young stranger, I shall set to work to win Madame de Vaudremont.”

“You have my full permission, my dear Cuirassier, but you will not gain this much,” and the young Maitre des Requetes put his polished thumb-nail under an upper tooth with a mocking click.

“Remember that I am unmarried,” said the Colonel; “that my sword is my whole fortune; and that such a challenge is setting Tantalus down to a banquet he cannot devour.”

“Prrr.”

This defiant roll of consonants was the only reply to the Colonel’s declaration, as Martial looked him over before turning away.

The fashion of the time required men to wear white kerseymere breeches and silk stockings at a ball. This costume showcased Montcornet’s fine shape. At thirty-five, he attracted attention with his stalwart height, emphasized by the handsome uniform of the Cuirassiers of the Imperial Guard. A black moustache highlighted his soldierly countenance, with a broad forehead, aquiline nose, and bright red lips. Montcornet’s manner, marked by a certain superiority from his command experience, might please a woman sensible enough not to aim at making a slave of her husband. The Colonel smiled as he looked at the lawyer, one of his favorite college friends, whose small figure made it necessary for Montcornet to look down slightly as he answered his raillery with a friendly glance.

Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon was a young Provencal favored by Napoleon; his fate might lead to a splendid embassy. He had won the Emperor with his Italian suppleness, a talent for intrigue, and a knowledge of manners, which are good substitutes for the higher qualities of a sterling man. Though young and eager, his face had acquired the rigid brilliancy of tinned iron, a characteristic of diplomatists that allows them to conceal emotions. The heart of a diplomat may be an insoluble problem, as the most illustrious ambassadors of the time were distinguished by enduring hatreds and romantic attachments.

Martial, however, was one of those men capable of reckoning on the future amid their enjoyment; he had learned to judge the world and hid his ambition under the guise of a lady-killer, cloaking his talent under the commonplace of mediocrity as he observed the rapid advancement of those who gave the master little umbrage.

The two friends parted with a cordial handshake. The introductory tune, signaling the ladies to form squares for a fresh quadrille, cleared the men from the space they had filled while talking in the large drawing-room of Gondreville’s mansion. Their whispered conversation had taken place during the usual interval between dances. The chandeliers and flambeaux on the chimney-shelf illuminated their faces, failing to conceal the faint expressions of their feelings from the keen-sighted countess or the artless stranger. This espionage of thoughts is perhaps one of the pleasures idle people find in society, while many disappointed individuals are bored without daring to admit it.

To fully appreciate the interest of this conversation, it’s necessary to relate an incident that would serve as an invisible bond, drawing together the actors in this little drama, currently scattered through the rooms.

At about eleven o’clock, just as the dancers were returning to their seats, the company observed the entrance of the handsomest woman in Paris, the queen of fashion, the only person missing from the brilliant assembly. She made it a rule never to appear until a party reached a pitch of excitement that doesn’t allow women to preserve the freshness of their faces or dresses. This brief hour is the springtime of a ball. An hour later, when pleasure falls flat and fatigue sets in, everything is spoiled. Madame de Vaudremont never committed the blunder of remaining at a party to be seen with drooping flowers, hair out of curl, tumbled frills, and a face courting sleep—not always without success. She took care not to let her beauty be seen drowsy, as her rivals did; she cleverly maintained her reputation for smartness by always leaving a ballroom in brilliant order, as she had entered it. Women whispered with envy that she planned and wore as many different dresses as parties she attended in one evening.

On this occasion, Madame de Vaudremont was not destined to leave when she wished. Pausing on the threshold, she shot swift, observant glances at the women present, hastily scrutinizing their dresses to assure herself that her own eclipsed them all.

The illustrious beauty presented herself to the admiration of the crowd at the same moment as one of the bravest colonels of the Guards’ Artillery and the Emperor’s favorite, the Comte de Soulanges. The transient association of these two had an air of mystery. Upon hearing the names announced, a few women sitting by the wall rose, and men hurried in from side-rooms, pressing forward to the principal doorway. One of the jesters in the assembly remarked that “women have as much curiosity about seeing a man faithful to his passion as men do in studying a woman difficult to enthrall.”

Though the Comte de Soulanges, a young man of about thirty-two, possessed a nervous temperament that gives rise to fine qualities, his slender build and pale complexion were not initially attractive. His black eyes revealed great vivacity, but he was taciturn in company, and nothing in his appearance hinted at the oratory that would later distinguish him in the legislative assembly under the Restoration.

The Comtesse de Vaudremont, a tall woman, somewhat plump, with dazzlingly white skin and a small head carried well, inspired love through her gracious manner. She was one of those beings who keep all the promise of their beauty.

The pair, for a few minutes, became the center of general observation, but did not long give curiosity a chance to exercise itself. The Colonel and the Countess seemed to understand that accident had placed them in an awkward position. Martial, as they approached, hastened to join a group of men by the fireplace, watching Madame de Vaudremont with the jealous anxiety of a first flame of passion from behind a barrier of heads. A secret voice warned him that the success he prided himself on might be precarious. However, the coldly polite smile with which the Countess thanked Monsieur de Soulanges and her little bow of dismissal as she sat down by Madame de Gondreville relaxed the rigidity of his face, which jealousy had made tense. Seeing Soulanges still standing near the sofa where Madame de Vaudremont sat, apparently unaware of the glance that conveyed to him that they were both playing a ridiculous part, the volcanic Provencal knitted his brows and smoothed his chestnut curls to maintain his composure, watching the faces of the Countess and M. de Soulanges while still chatting with his neighbors. He then took the hand of Colonel Montcornet, who had just renewed their acquaintance, but he listened without hearing him; his mind was elsewhere.

Soulanges gazed calmly at the women, sitting four ranks deep around the immense ballroom, admiring the display of diamonds, rubies, gold, and shining hair, which almost outshone the blaze of waxlights, the cut glass of the chandeliers, and the gilding. His rival’s indifference unsettled the lawyer. Unable to control his impatience, Martial approached Madame de Vaudremont with a bow. Upon seeing the Provencal, Soulanges cast him a covert glance and impertinently turned away his head. A solemn silence reigned in the room, where curiosity peaked. Eager faces wore strange mixed expressions; everyone anticipated an outbreak that men of breeding carefully avoid. Suddenly, the Count’s pale face turned as red as the scarlet facings of his coat, and he fixed his gaze on the floor to hide his agitation. Spotting the unknown lady humbly seated by the pedestal of the candelabrum, he moved away with a melancholy air, passing in front of the lawyer, and took refuge in one of the cardrooms. Martial and the company thought Soulanges had publicly surrendered his position out of fear of the ridicule that follows a discarded lover. The lawyer proudly raised his head and looked at the strange lady; then, as he took his seat near Madame de Vaudremont, he listened so inattentively that he missed her words spoken behind her fan:

“Martial, please don’t wear that ring you took from me. I have my reasons and will explain them when we leave. You must give me your arm to go to the Princess de Wagram’s.”

“Why did you come in with the Colonel?” asked the Baron.

“I met him in the hall,” she replied. “But leave me now; everybody is looking at us.”

Martial returned to the Colonel of Cuirassiers. It was then that the little blue lady became the object of curiosity that agitated the Colonel, Soulanges, Martial, and Madame de Vaudremont.

When the friends parted after their challenge, the Baron flew to Madame de Vaudremont and led her to a place in the most brilliant quadrille. Favored by the intoxication dancing produces in a woman and the turmoil of a ball, where men appear in all the trickery of dress, which enhances their attractions as much as it does women’s, Martial thought he might yield to the charm that drew his gaze to the fair stranger. Though he managed to hide his first glances towards the lady in blue from the Countess’s watchful eyes, he was soon caught in the act; and while he excused himself once for his absent-mindedness, he could not justify the unseemly silence that followed the most insinuating question a woman can ask:

“Do you like me very much this evening?”

The more dreamy he became, the more the Countess pressed and teased him.

While Martial danced, the Colonel moved from group to group, seeking information about the unknown lady. After exhausting the good humor of even the most indifferent, he resolved to take advantage of a moment when the Comtesse de Gondreville seemed at liberty to ask her the name of the mysterious lady. He noticed a little space cleared between the pedestal of the candelabrum and the two sofas, which ended in that corner. The dance had left several chairs vacant, forming rows of fortifications held by mothers or middle-aged women; the Colonel seized the opportunity to make his way through this palisade of shawls and wraps. He began by making himself agreeable to the dowagers, and from one to another, through compliments, he finally reached the empty space next to the stranger. At the risk of catching on to the gryphons and chimeras of the huge candelabrum, he stood there, braving the glare and dripping wax, to Martial’s annoyance.

The Colonel, too tactful to speak suddenly to the little blue lady on his right, began by addressing a plain woman seated on the left:

“This is a splendid ball, madame! What luxury! What life! Every woman here is pretty! You’re not dancing—because you don’t care for it, no doubt.”

This vapid conversation aimed to induce his right-hand neighbor to speak; however, she, silent and absent-minded, paid no attention. The officer had prepared several phrases leading to: “And you, madame?”—a question from which he hoped for great things. But he was surprised to see tears in the strange lady’s eyes, which seemed wholly absorbed in gazing at Madame de Vaudremont.

“You are married, no doubt, madame?” he asked hesitantly.

“Yes, monsieur,” replied the lady.

“And your husband is here, of course?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

“And why, madame, do you remain in this spot? Is it to attract attention?”

The mournful lady smiled sadly.

“Allow me the honor, madame, of being your partner in the next quadrille, and I will ensure not to bring you back here. When so many are ready to ascend the throne, and Royalty is the mania of the day, I cannot imagine you will refuse the title of Queen of the Ball that your beauty may claim.”

“I do not intend to dance, monsieur.”

The curt tone of her replies was so discouraging that the Colonel found himself compelled to withdraw. Martial, guessing what the officer’s last request had been and the refusal he had met with, began to smile, stroking his chin and making the diamond on his finger sparkle.

“What are you laughing at?” asked the Comtesse de Vaudremont.

“At the poor Colonel’s failure, who just put his foot in it----”

“I begged you to take your ring off,” said the Countess, interrupting him.

“I did not hear you.”

“If you can hear nothing this evening, at least you see everything, Monsieur le Baron,” said Madame de Vaudremont, vexed.

“That young man is displaying a very fine diamond,” the stranger remarked to the Colonel.

“Splendid,” he replied. “The man is the Baron Martial de la Roche-Hugon, one of my most intimate friends.”

“I thank you for telling me his name,” she said; “he seems an agreeable man.”

“Yes, but he is rather fickle.”

“He seems to be on the best terms with the Comtesse de Vaudremont?” asked the lady, looking inquiringly at the Colonel.

“On the very best.”

The unknown turned pale.

“Hallo!” thought the soldier, “she is in love with that lucky devil Martial.”

“I fancied Madame de Vaudremont had long been devoted to M. de Soulanges,” said the lady, recovering from the suppressed grief that had clouded her beauty.

“For a week past, the Countess has been faithless,” replied the Colonel. “But you must have seen poor Soulanges when he came in; he’s still trying to disbelieve in his disaster.”

“Yes, I saw him,” said the lady. Then she added, “Thank you very much, monsieur,” in a tone that signified dismissal.

At that moment, the quadrille was coming to an end. Montcornet had only time to withdraw, telling himself, “She is married.”

“Well, valiant Cuirassier,” exclaimed the Baron, drawing the Colonel aside into a window-bay to breathe the fresh air from the garden, “how are you getting on?”

“She is a married woman, my dear fellow.”

“What does that matter?”

“Oh, deuce take it! I’m a decent sort of man,” replied the Colonel. “I have no intention of courting a woman I cannot marry. Besides, Martial, she expressly told me she did not intend to dance.”

“Colonel, I will bet a hundred napoleons to your gray horse that she will dance with me this evening.”

“Done!” said the Colonel, putting his hand in the coxcomb’s. “Meanwhile, I’m going to look for Soulanges; he might know the lady, as she seems interested in him.”

“You’ve lost, my good fellow,” cried Martial, laughing. “My eyes have met hers, and I know what they mean. My dear friend, you owe me no grudge for dancing with her after she has refused you?”

“No, no. Those who laugh last, laugh longest. But I’m an honest gambler and a generous enemy, Martial, and I warn you, she is fond of diamonds.”

With these words, the friends parted; General Montcornet made his way to the cardroom, where he saw the Comte de Soulanges sitting at a bouillotte table. Though there was no friendship between the two soldiers beyond the superficial comradeship from the perils of war and service, the Colonel of Cuirassiers was alarmed to see the Colonel of Artillery, known to be prudent, playing a game that could lead to ruin. The heaps of gold and notes piled on the fateful cards showed the frenzy of play. A circle of silent men stood around the players at the table. Occasionally, a few words were spoken—pass, play, I stop, a thousand Louis, taken—but the five motionless men seemed to converse only with their eyes. As the Colonel, alarmed by Soulanges’ pallor, approached him, the Count was winning. Field-Marshal the Duc d’Isemberg, Keller, and a famous banker rose from the table, completely cleaned out of considerable sums. Soulanges looked gloomier than ever as he swept up a quantity of gold and notes; he didn’t even count it; his lips curled with bitter scorn, defying fortune rather than being grateful for her favors.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Courage,” said the Colonel. “Courage, Soulanges!” He thought to help by pulling him away from the gaming table. “Come with me. I have good news, but on one condition.”

“What is that?” asked Soulanges.

“That you will answer a question.”

The Comte de Soulanges rose abruptly, gathering his winnings into his handkerchief, his expression so fierce no one objected to his departure; the players were relieved to see his morose face go. “Those soldiers are always as thick as thieves at a fair!” remarked a diplomat who took Soulanges’ place. A tired face turned to the newcomer and said, “When we say military men, we do not mean civil, Monsieur le Ministre.”

“My dear fellow,” Montcornet said, leading Soulanges aside, “the Emperor praised you this morning, and your promotion to field-marshal is certain.”

“The Master does not love the Artillery,” Soulanges muttered.

“No, but he adores the nobility, and you are an aristocrat. He said men who married in Paris during the campaign are not in disgrace. Well?”

Soulanges looked confused.

“And now I hope you will tell me if you know a charming woman sitting under a huge candelabrum----”

At this, Soulanges’ face brightened; he seized the Colonel’s hand. “My dear General,” he said, voice changed, “if anyone else had asked me that, I would have cracked his skull with this gold. Leave me, I beg you. I feel more like blowing out my brains tonight than— I hate everything I see. I am going. This gaiety, this music, these stupid faces, all laughing, are killing me!”

“My poor friend!” Montcornet replied gently, squeezing his hand. “You are too vehement. What would you say if I told you Martial is thinking so little of Madame de Vaudremont that he is quite smitten with that little lady?”

“If he speaks to her,” Soulanges shouted, trembling with rage, “I will thrash him flat, even if he’s in the Emperor’s lap!”

He sank into an easy chair; Montcornet left, realizing Soulanges was too furious for consolation.

Back in the ballroom Montcornet first noticed Madame de Vaudremont, agitated, and took a seat beside her.

“I wager something has vexed you?” he said.

“A mere trifle, General. I want to go to a ball at the Grand Duchess of Berg’s, but must first stop at the Princesse de Wagram’s. Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon amuses himself by flirting with the dowagers.”

“That’s not the whole secret of your disturbance. I bet a hundred louis you will stay here the whole evening.”

“Impertinent man!”

“Then I’ve hit the truth?”

“Well, tell me, what am I thinking?” she asked, tapping his fingers with her fan. “I might reward you if you guess right.”

“I won’t accept the challenge; I have too much advantage.”

“You are presumptuous.”

“You are afraid of seeing Martial at the feet—”

“Of whom?” she exclaimed, feigning surprise.

“Of that candelabrum,” he replied, glancing at the fair stranger and studying the Countess.

“You have guessed it,” she said, hiding behind her fan. “Old Madame de Lansac, as malicious as an old monkey, told me Monsieur de la Roche-Hugon is flirting with that stranger, who sits there like a skeleton at a feast. I would rather see a death’s head than that face—so cruelly beautiful and pale. She is my evil genius. Madame de Lansac, who only comes to a ball to watch everything while pretending to sleep, has made me miserable. Martial shall pay dearly for this trick. Urge him, since he is your friend, not to make me unhappy.”

“I just left a man who promises to blow his brains out if he speaks to that lady. And he is a man, madame, to keep his word. But I know Martial; such threats encourage him. Besides, we have wagered—” The Colonel lowered his voice.

“Can it be true?” asked the Countess.

“On my word of honor.”

“Thank you, my dear Colonel,” Madame de Vaudremont replied, her glance inviting. “Will you dance with me?”

“Yes; but the next quadrille. During this one I want to see what comes of this little intrigue and learn who the little blue lady is; she looks intelligent.”

Understanding Madame de Vaudremont wished to be left alone, the Colonel retired, pleased with his start.

At entertainments like this, women such as Madame de Lansac gather like old sailors watching younger mariners in a tempest. The Duchess, alert to the unfolding drama, could read the Countess’s heart despite the fan and smile. The faintest sign— a quiver, a curl of lips—spoke to her. From her large armchair, the coquette of a past generation admired the younger coquette’s attempt to hide annoyance and grief. Madame de Vaudremont felt as much sorrow as she feigned cheerfulness; she had believed Martial a man of talent who could adorn her life with influence, but now realized her mistake. Her sudden passion for Martial, though recent, wounded her as sharply as the loss of a limb.

The old Duchess, seeing a chance to speak with the Countess, dismissed her Ambassador; a lover’s quarrel overshadowed every other interest. Madame de Lansac shot a sardonic glance at the young woman, who feared her fate was in the dowager’s hands. There are looks between women that can feel like torches at a climax; none who did not know the Duchess appreciated the terror her expression inspired.

Madame de Lansac was tall; people said, “That must have been a handsome woman!” Her powdered cheeks disguised wrinkles, but her eyes were dim. She wore many diamonds, dressed tastefully; her sharp nose and ironical smile promised epigrams. Her exquisite politeness softened a mischievous nature, making it impossible to call her cruel.

Her eyes lit, and a triumphant glance with a smile that said “I promised you as much!” crossed the room, bringing hope to the pale young woman by the candelabrum. The alliance between Madame de Lansac and the stranger did not escape the Comtesse de Vaudremont’s practiced eye; she sensed a mystery and was determined to uncover it.

The Baron de la Roche-Hugon, having questioned all the dowagers without learning the blue lady’s name, turned in despair to Comtesse de Gondreville, who only said, “A lady the ‘ancient’ Duchesse de Lansac introduced to me.”

By chance the lawyer caught the Duchess’s knowing glance toward the stranger. Despite being on bad terms with her, he decided to speak. The Duchess, seeing the jaunty Baron approach, smiled with sardonic irony and looked at Madame de Vaudremont, making Montcornet laugh.

“If the old witch pretends to be friendly,” thought the Baron, “she will play me some spiteful trick.” “Madame,” he said, “I am told you have undertaken the charge of a very precious treasure.”

“Do you take me for a dragon?” said the old lady. “But of whom are you speaking?” she added sweetly, reviving Martial’s hopes.

“Of that little lady, unknown to all, whom the jealousy of these coquettes has imprisoned in that corner. You, no doubt, know her family?”

“Yes,” said the Duchess. “But what concern have you with a provincial heiress, married some time ago, a woman of good birth, who goes nowhere.”

“Why does she not dance? She is such a pretty creature. May we conclude a treaty of peace? If you tell me all I want to know, I promise to urge a petition for the restitution of the woods of Navarreins by the Commissioners of Crown Lands to the Emperor.”

The younger branch of the house of Navarreins bears the arms of Lansac, and the Duchess’s liaison with Louis XV had won her husband the ducal title. The Baron’s suggestion to petition for an estate of the elder branch was sheer trickery.

“Monsieur,” the old woman said with deceptive gravity, “bring the Comtesse de Vaudremont to me. I promise to reveal the mystery of the interesting unknown. You see, every man in the room shares your curiosity. All eyes are turned toward the corner where my protégée has modestly placed herself; she is reaping the homage the women have denied her. Happy the man she chooses for partner!” She fixed her eyes on Madame de Vaudremont with a look that plainly meant, “We are talking of you.” “I imagine you would rather learn the stranger’s name from your own lips than from mine.”

Defiance in the Duchess’s attitude made Madame de Vaudremont rise, approach her, and take the chair Martial offered. Without acknowledging him, she said, “I can guess, madame, you are talking of me; but I admit my lack of perspicacity; I do not know whether that is for good or evil.”

Madame de Lansac pressed the young woman’s pretty hand in her dry, wrinkled fingers and replied, low and compassionate, “Poor child!”

The women looked at each other. Madame de Vaudremont understood Martial was in the way and dismissed him with an imperious “Leave us.”

The Baron, displeased to see the Countess under the dangerous sibyl’s spell, gave one of those looks a man can give—potent over a blinded heart but ridiculous to a woman beginning to criticize her suitor.

“Do you think you can play the Emperor?” said Madame de Vaudremont, turning to give an ironical sidelong glance at the lawyer.

Martial, a man of the world with wit and acumen, would not risk breaking with a woman favored at Court, whom the Emperor wished to see married. He counted on provoking the Countess’s jealousy as the surest means of discovering her coolness’s cause and withdrew willingly as a new quadrille began.

Making room for dancing, the Baron leaned against a marble console, folded his arms, and watched the two women talk. He followed their glances at the stranger. Comparing the Countess with this new beauty, attractive because of mystery, the Baron fell prey to the usual self-interest of an adventurous lover—hesitating between a fortune within reach and indulging his caprice. The light exaggerated his anxious face against the white silk hangings, making him resemble an evil genius. Observers might have said, “There is another poor wretch who seems to be enjoying himself!”

Meanwhile the Colonel, leaning against the doorway between ballroom and cardroom, laughed under his moustache. He enjoyed watching the dance turmoil, reading the Countess’s and Martial’s agitation, and wondering about Soulanges’ gloomy looks and the fair unknown’s alternating hope and dread. Montcornet stood like the king of the feast, receiving inviting smiles from many women. As a Colonel of the Imperial Guard, he was one of the best matches in the army.

It was nearly midnight. Conversation, gambling, dancing, flirtations, rivalries, and scheming had reached fervor. “A fine ball!” exclaimed a young man.

“My sweet little angel,” Madame de Lansac said to the Countess, “you are now at an age when I made many mistakes. Seeing you endure a thousand deaths, I thought to give some charitable advice. To go wrong at two-and-twenty spoils your future; it is like tearing the gown you must wear. Later, we learn to go about in it without crumpling it. Go on, sweetheart, making clever enemies and friends without sense of conduct, and you will see what a pleasant life you will lead!”

“Oh, madame, it is very hard for a woman to be happy, do you not think?” the Countess eagerly asked.

“My child, at your age you must choose between pleasure and happiness. You want to marry Martial, who is neither wise enough to be a good husband nor passionate enough to remain a lover. He is in debt, my dear; he will squander your fortune; that would be nothing if he could make you happy. Do you not see how aged he is? He must have been ill; he is making the most of what is left him. In three years he will be a wreck. He will be ambitious; perhaps he may succeed. I do not think so. What is he? A man of intrigue, who may have business acumen and be able to gossip agreeably, but he is too presumptuous for real merit; he will not go far. Besides—look at him. Is it not written on his brow that, at this very moment, what he sees in you is not a young and pretty woman, but the two million francs you possess? He does not love you, my dear; he is calculating you as if you were an investment. If you are bent on marrying, find an older man with an assured position who is halfway in his career. A widow’s marriage should not be a trivial love affair. Is a mouse to be caught a second time in the same trap? A new alliance should be a good speculation on your part; in marrying again you ought at least to hope to be addressed as Madame la Marechale!”

The Duchess’s counsel was sharp, worldly, intended to save the young woman from a reckless marriage. She painted Martial as a man of charm and intrigue but lacking the constancy, health, and genuine affection fit for a lasting union; his attraction, she urged, was less passion than calculation. The Countess listened, torn between young passion and prudent counsel, aware of the watching eyes and the stakes of reputation and fortune in the salons where marriages were both personal and political bargains.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As she spoke, both women naturally turned to Colonel Montcornet’s handsome face.

“If you prefer to flirt rather than marry again,” the Duchess said bluntly, “you, better than any woman, know how to stir up trouble and then calm it. But please, don’t disturb families or ruin wives’ happiness. I’ve played that dangerous game. For a moment of vanity, a virtuous woman can be destroyed—there are truly virtuous women, dear—and we can become mortally hated. I learned too late that, as the Duc d’Albe said, one salmon is worth a thousand frogs! Genuine affection brings far more happiness than fleeting passion. I came to preach to you; you are the reason for my visit to this lower-class house. Have I not just seen actors here? We used to receive them in our boudoir, but never in the drawing-room! Why are you looking at me in amazement? If you want to play with men, toy only with those whose lives are unsettled and who have no duties. The others won’t forgive us for the errors that make them happy. Take this advice from my long experience. Look at Soulanges, for instance, whom you’ve made lovesick for the past fifteen months. Do you realize what you’ve done? You’ve affected his entire life. He’s been married two years and is adored by a charming wife whom he loves but neglects; she lives in tears. Soulanges has felt remorse far worse than any pleasure you’ve given him. And you, you little minx, have abandoned him. Come see the result of your actions.”

The old lady took Madame de Vaudremont’s hand, and they rose.

“There,” said Madame de Lansac, pointing to a pale, trembling figure under the candlelight, “that is my grandniece, the Comtesse de Soulanges. Today she finally yielded to my persuasion and left the sorrowful room where her child brings her little consolation. You think her charming? Imagine how she must have looked when happiness and love illuminated that now-blighted face.”

The Countess looked away, lost in sad thought.

The Duchess led her to the card-room door, then, scanning the room, said, “And there is Soulanges!”

The Countess shuddered at the sight of Soulanges, pale and rigid in an easy chair. His indifference and the tension in his brow revealed his suffering. The players passed him by, ignoring him as if he were dead. The image of the tearful wife and the morose husband, separated amidst festivity, struck the Countess with a sense of foreboding about her own future. Though her heart was not entirely hardened, she pressed the Duchess’s hand, thanking her with a childlike smile.

“My dear child,” the old lady whispered, “remember that we can repel a man’s attentions just as easily as we can attract them.”

“She is yours if you’re not a fool,” Madame de Lansac whispered to Colonel Montcornet while the Countess remained absorbed in her compassion for Soulanges, still loving him enough to wish to restore his happiness.

“Oh! I will talk to him!” she declared to Madame de Lansac.

“Do nothing of the sort!” the old lady exclaimed as she returned to her chair. “Choose a good husband and shut your door to my nephew. A wife cannot accept her husband’s heart as a gift from another woman; she is far happier believing she has won it back. By bringing my niece here, I believe I’ve given her a chance to regain her husband’s affection. All I need from you is to play the Colonel.” She pointed to Montcornet, and the Countess smiled.

“Well, madame, do you finally know the name of the unknown?” Martial asked the Countess when they were alone.

“Yes,” replied Madame de Vaudremont, her expression playful.

Her smile and bright eyes were like a will-o’-the-wisp leading travelers astray. Martial, believing she still loved him, adopted a charming demeanor. “Will you be annoyed if I seem to care about that name?”

“Will you be annoyed if a remnant of affection prevents me from telling you? And if I forbid you to approach that young lady? It could be dangerous.”

“To lose your favor would be worse than losing my life.”

“Martial,” the Countess said sternly, “she is Madame de Soulanges. Her husband would kill you if he knew.”

“Ha! So the Colonel can leave the man in peace who has taken his love, yet would fight for his wife! What a reversal of principles! I beg you to let me dance with her. You’ll see how little love that icy heart could feel for you; if the Colonel allows me to dance with his wife after letting me—”

“But she loves her husband.”

“An even greater obstacle I shall enjoy overcoming.”

“But she is married.”

“A whimsical objection!”

“Ah!” the Countess said with a bitter smile, “you punish us for both our faults and our repentance!”

“Do not be angry!” Martial exclaimed eagerly. “Forgive me, I beseech you. I will think no more of Madame de Soulanges.”

“You deserve that I should send you to her.”

“I’m off then,” he laughed, “and I’ll return more devoted to you than ever. You’ll see that the prettiest woman cannot capture the heart that is yours.”

“Meaning you want to win Colonel Montcornet’s horse?”

“Ah! Traitor!” he said, playfully threatening his friend. The Colonel smiled and joined them; the Baron gave him a seat near the Countess, saying with a sardonic tone, “Here, madame, is a man who boasted he could win your favor in one evening.”

He left, thinking he had cleverly piqued the Countess’ pride and done Montcornet a disservice, unaware of the irony in Madame de Vaudremont’s words, as both were unconsciously moving toward each other.

At that moment, the lawyer approached Madame de Soulanges, who sat pale and timid, seemingly alive only in her eyes. Her husband entered the ballroom, his eyes flashing with anger. The Duchess, ever watchful, rushed to her nephew, asking for his arm to find her carriage, feigning boredom to prevent a scene. Before leaving, she shot a knowing glance at her niece, signaling her to take revenge.

Madame de Vaudremont noticed the exchange and felt a sudden realization; she feared being the target of the Duchess’s cunning.

“That duplicitous Duchess,” she thought, “may be preaching morality while plotting against me.”

This thought stirred her pride more than her curiosity to unravel the intrigue. In her distraction, she lost control of herself. The Colonel, misinterpreting her embarrassment, became more ardent. The old diplomats, entertained by the unfolding drama, had never witnessed so many simultaneous intrigues.
