######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

“Ah! madame,” replied the doctor, “I have some appalling stories in my collection. But each has its proper hour in a conversation—you know Chamfort’s jest to the Duc de Fronsac: ‘Between your sally and the present moment lie ten bottles of champagne.’”

“But it is two in the morning, and the story of Rosina has prepared us,” said the mistress of the house.

“Tell us, Monsieur Bianchon!” cried everyone.

The obliging doctor bowed, and silence reigned.

“At about a hundred paces from Vendôme, on the banks of the Loir,” he began, “stands an old brown house, crowned with very high roofs, so isolated that there is nothing near it, not even a fetid tannery or a squalid tavern. In front is a garden down to the river, where box shrubs once clipped to edge the walks now straggle at will. A few willows rooted in the stream have grown up quickly like an enclosing fence, half hiding the house. Wild plants have clothed the bank; the fruit trees, neglected for ten years, no longer bear, and their suckers form a thicket. The espaliers resemble a copse. The paths, once graveled, are overgrown—there is no trace of a path.

“From the hilltop where the ruins of the old castle of the Dukes of Vendôme cling, one can see this spot must once have been the joy of a country gentleman devoted to horticulture, especially to choice fruit. An arbor, or rather its wreck, with a table still standing, remains. The aspect of this garden evokes the peaceful life of the provinces, like reading a worthy tradesman’s epitaph. To complete the mournful impression, one wall bears a sundial graced with the motto, ‘Ultimam cogita.’

“The roof is dreadfully dilapidated; the shutters are always closed; the balconies are hung with swallows’ nests; the doors forever shut. Straggling grasses outline flagstones; the ironwork is rusty. Moon and sun, winter, summer, and snow have eaten into the wood, warped the boards, and peeled off the paint. The dreary silence is broken only by birds and cats, polecats, rats, and mice, free to scamper around. An invisible hand has written over it all: ‘Mystery.’

“If you look at this house from the street, you will see a large gate with a round-arched top, full of holes made by children. I later learned that this door had been blocked for ten years. Through these breaches you see the courtyard side in perfect harmony with the garden side: the same ruin. Weeds outline the paving-stones; the walls are scored by enormous cracks; the blackened coping is laced with pellitory. The stone steps are disjointed; the bell-cord is rotten; the gutter-spouts broken. What fire from heaven could have fallen there? By what decree has salt been sown on this dwelling? Has God been mocked here? Or was France betrayed? These are the questions we ask ourselves. Reptiles crawl over it, but give no reply. This empty, deserted house is a vast enigma.

“It was formerly a little domain, held in fief, known as La Grande Bretèche. During my stay at Vendôme, where Despleins had left me in charge of a rich patient, the sight of this strange dwelling became one of my keenest pleasures. Was it not better than a ruin? A ruin keeps memories; this house, still standing though slowly destroyed by an avenging hand, contained a secret, an unrevealed thought. More than once in the evening I climbed the wild hedge surrounding the enclosure, braving scratches to enter this ownerless garden, lingering for hours in its disorder. I would not have asked a single question of any gossiping native for the price of the story to which this strange scene was due. There I wove romances and indulged in melancholy that enchanted me. Had I known the reason for the neglect, I would have lost the unwritten poetry that intoxicated me. To me, this refuge represented phases of human life shadowed by misfortune; sometimes the peace of a graveyard without the dead, who speak in the language of epitaphs; one day I saw it as the home of lepers; another, the house of the Atridae; but above all, I found there provincial life, contemplative and hour-glass-like. I often wept there; I never laughed.

“More than once, involuntary terrors seized me as I heard overhead the dull hum of some hurrying wood-pigeon. The earth is dank; you must watch for lizards, vipers, and frogs wandering about with wild freedom; above all, you must have no fear of cold, for in a few moments you feel an icy cloak settle on your shoulders, like the Commendatore’s hand on Don Giovanni’s neck.

“One evening I felt a shudder; the wind had turned an old rusty weathercock, and the creaking sounded like a cry from the house, just as I was finishing a gloomy drama to account for this monumental embodiment of woe. I returned to my inn, lost in gloomy thoughts. After supper, the hostess came into my room with an air of mystery, saying, ‘Monsieur, here is Monsieur Regnault.’

“‘Who is Monsieur Regnault?’

“‘What, sir, do you not know Monsieur Regnault?—Well, that’s odd,’ she said, leaving the room.

“Suddenly a tall, slim man dressed in black appeared, hat in hand, entering like a ram. He had a receding forehead, a small pointed head, and a colorless face like dirty water. You would have taken him for an usher. His coat was old, worn at the seams, but he had a diamond in his shirt frill and gold rings in his ears.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘whom have I the honor of addressing?’ He took a chair, placed himself in front of my fire, put his hat on my table, and answered while rubbing his hands: ‘Dear me, it is very cold.—Monsieur, I am Monsieur Regnault.’

“I encouraged myself with the thought: Seek!

“‘I am,’ he continued, ‘notary at Vendôme.’

“‘I am delighted to hear it, monsieur,’ I exclaimed. ‘But I am not in a position to make a will for reasons best known to myself.’

“‘One moment!’ he said, holding up his hand as if to gain silence. ‘Allow me, monsieur! I am informed that you sometimes walk in the garden of la Grande Bretèche.’

“‘Yes, monsieur.’

“‘One moment! That constitutes a misdemeanor. Monsieur, as executor under the will of the late Comtesse de Merret, I come in her name to beg you to discontinue the practice. One moment! I am not a Turk and do not wish to make a crime of it. Besides, you are free to be ignorant of the circumstances which compel me to leave the finest mansion in Vendôme to fall into ruin. Nevertheless, you must be a man of education, and you should know that the laws forbid, under heavy penalties, any trespass on enclosed property. A hedge is the same as a wall. But the state in which the place is left may excuse your curiosity. For my part, I should be quite content to make you free to come and go in the house; but being bound to respect the will of the testatrix, I have the honor, monsieur, to beg that you will go into the garden no more. I myself, monsieur, since the will was read, have never set foot in the house, which, as I had the honor of informing you, is part of the estate of the late Madame de Merret. We have done nothing there but verify the number of doors and windows to assess the taxes I have to pay annually out of the funds left for that purpose by the late Madame de Merret. Ah! my dear sir, her will made a great commotion in the town.’

“The good man paused to blow his nose. I respected his volubility, understanding that the administration of Madame de Merret’s estate had been the most important event of his life, his reputation, his glory, his Restoration. As I was forced to bid farewell to my beautiful reveries and romances, I was to reject learning the truth on official authority.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said, ‘would it be indiscreet if I were to ask you the reasons for such eccentricity?’

“At these words, an expression of pleasure spread across the lawyer’s countenance. He pulled up his collar, took out his snuffbox, opened it, and offered me a pinch; on my refusing, he took a large one. He was happy! A man without a hobby does not know all the good to be had in life. At that moment I understood Sterne’s charming passion and had a perfect idea of the delight with which my uncle Toby, encouraged by Trim, bestrode his hobby-horse.

“‘Monsieur,’ said Monsieur Regnault, ‘I was head-clerk in Monsieur Roguin’s office in Paris. A first-rate house, which you may have heard of? No! An unfortunate bankruptcy made it famous. Not having enough money to purchase a practice in Paris at the inflated prices of 1816, I came here and bought my predecessor’s business. I had relations in Vendôme; among others, a wealthy aunt who allowed me to marry her daughter. Three months after being licensed by the Keeper of the Seals, one evening, as I was going to bed—it was before my marriage—I was sent for by Madame la Comtesse de Merret to her Château of Merret. Her maid, a good girl who is now a servant in this inn, was waiting at my door with the Countess’ own carriage. Ah! one moment! I ought to tell you that Monsieur le Comte de Merret had gone to Paris to die two months before I came here. He came to a miserable end, flinging himself into every kind of dissipation. You understand?

“‘On the day when he left, Madame la Comtesse had quitted la Grande Bretèche, having dismantled it. Some people even say she burnt all the furniture, the hangings—in short, everything used in furnishing the premises now let by the said M.—In short, that she burnt everything in the meadow at Merret. Have you been to Merret, monsieur?—No,’ he answered himself, ‘Ah, it is a very fine place.’

“‘For about three months previously,’ he continued with a jerk of his head, ‘the Count and Countess had lived in an eccentric way; they admitted no visitors; Madame lived on the ground floor, and Monsieur on the first floor. When the Countess was left alone she was never seen except at church. Subsequently at the château she refused to see friends who went to call on her. She was already very much altered when she left la Grande Bretèche to go to Merret. That dear lady—I say dear, for it was she who gave me this diamond, but indeed I saw her but once—was very ill; she had, no doubt, given up all hope, for she died without choosing to send for a doctor; indeed, many fancied she was not quite right in her head. Well, sir, my curiosity was strangely excited by hearing that Madame de Merret had need of my services. Nor was I the only person who took an interest in the affair. That very night, though it was already late, all the town knew that I was going to Merret.

“‘The waiting-woman replied vaguely to my questions on the way; nevertheless, she told me that her mistress had received the Sacrament in the course of the day at the hands of the Curé of Merret and seemed unlikely to live through the night. It was about eleven when I reached the château. I went up the great staircase. After crossing some large, lofty, dark rooms, diabolically cold and damp, I reached the state bedroom where the Countess lay. From the rumors current concerning this lady (monsieur, I should never end if I were to repeat all the tales that were told about her), I had imagined her a coquette. Imagine, then, that I had great difficulty in seeing her in the great bed where she was lying. To light this enormous room, with old-fashioned heavy cornices, and so thick with dust that merely to see it was enough to make you sneeze, she had only an old Argand lamp. Ah! but you have not been to Merret. Well, the bed is one of those old world beds, with a high tester hung with flowered chintz. A small table stood by the bed, on which I saw an ‘Imitation of Christ,’ which, by the way, I bought for my wife, as well as the lamp. There were also a deep armchair for her confidential maid and two small chairs. There was no fire. That was all the furniture, not enough to fill ten lines in an inventory.

“‘My dear sir, if you had seen, as I then saw, that vast room, papered and hung with brown, you would have felt yourself transported into a scene of a romance. It was icy, nay more, funereal,’ and he lifted his hand with a theatrical gesture and paused.

“‘By dint of seeking, as I approached the bed, at last I saw Madame de Merret, under the glimmer of the lamp, which fell on the pillows. Her face was as yellow as wax, and as narrow as two folded hands. The Countess had a lace cap showing her abundant hair, but as white as linen thread. She was sitting up in bed, and seemed to keep upright with great difficulty. Her large black eyes, dimmed by fever no doubt, and half-dead already, hardly moved under the bony arch of her eyebrows.—There,’ he added, pointing to his own brow. ‘Her forehead was clammy; her fleshless hands were like bones covered with soft skin; the veins and muscles were perfectly visible. She must have been very handsome; but at this moment I was startled into an indescribable emotion at the sight. Never, said those who wrapped her in her shroud, had any living creature been so emaciated and lived. In short, it was awful to behold! Sickness so consumed that woman, that she was no more than a phantom. Her lips, pale violet, seemed not to move when she spoke to me.

“‘Though my profession has familiarized me with such spectacles, by calling me not infrequently to the bedside of the dying, I confess that families in tears and the agonies I have seen were as nothing in comparison with this lonely and silent woman in her vast château. I heard not the least sound; I did not perceive the movement which the sufferer’s breathing ought to have given to the sheets that covered her, and I stood motionless, absorbed in looking at her in a sort of stupor. In fancy, I am there still. At last her large eyes moved; she tried to raise her right hand, but it fell back on the bed, and she uttered these words, which came like a breath, for her voice was no longer a voice: “I have waited for you with the greatest impatience.” A bright flush rose to her cheeks. It was a great effort to her to speak.

“‘“Madame,” I began.’ She signed to me to be silent. At that moment, the old housekeeper rose and said in my ear, “Do not speak; Madame la Comtesse is not in a state to bear the slightest noise, and what you say might agitate her.”

“‘I sat down. A few instants later, Madame de Merret collected all her remaining strength to move her right hand and slipped it, not without infinite difficulty, under the bolster; she then paused a moment. With a last effort, she withdrew her hand; and when she brought out a sealed paper, drops of perspiration rolled from her brow. “I place my will in your hands—Oh! God! Oh!” and that was all. She clutched a crucifix that lay on the bed, lifted it hastily to her lips, and died.

“‘The expression of her eyes still makes me shudder as I think of it. She must have suffered much! There was joy in her last glance, and it remained stamped on her dead eyes.

“‘I brought away the will, and when it was opened, I found that Madame de Merret had appointed me her executor. She left the whole of her property to the hospital at Vendôme except for a few legacies. But these were her instructions regarding la Grande Bretèche: She ordered me to leave the place, for fifty years from the day of her death, in the state it was at the time of her death, forbidding anyone, whoever he might be, to enter the apartments, prohibiting any repairs whatever, and even settling a salary to pay watchmen if it were necessary to secure the absolute fulfillment of her intentions. At the expiration of that term, if the will of the testatrix has been duly carried out, the house is to become the property of my heirs, for, as you know, a notary cannot take a bequest. Otherwise, la Grande Bretèche reverts to the heirs-at-law, but on condition of fulfilling certain conditions set forth in a codicil to the will, which is not to be opened until the expiration of the said term of fifty years. The will has not been disputed, so——’ And without finishing his sentence, the lanky notary looked at me with an air of triumph; I made him quite happy by offering him my congratulations.

“‘Monsieur,’ I said in conclusion, ‘you have so vividly impressed me that I fancy I see the dying woman whiter than her sheets; her glittering eyes frighten me; I shall dream of her tonight.—But you must have formed some idea as to the instructions contained in that extraordinary will.’

“‘Monsieur,’ said he, with comical reticence, ‘I never allow myself to criticize the conduct of a person who honors me with the gift of a diamond.’

“However, I soon loosened the tongue of the discreet notary of Vendôme, who communicated to me, not without long digressions, the opinions of the deep politicians of both sexes whose judgments are law in Vendôme. But these opinions were so contradictory and diffuse that I was near falling asleep despite my interest in this authentic history. The notary’s ponderous voice and monotonous accent, accustomed no doubt to listen to himself and to make himself listened to by his clients or fellow-townsmen, were too much for my curiosity. Happily, he soon went away.

“‘Ah, ha, monsieur,’ said he on the stairs, ‘a good many persons would be glad to live five-and-forty years longer; but—one moment!’ and he laid the first finger of his right hand to his nostril with a cunning look, as much as to say, ‘Mark my words!—To last as long as that—as long as that,’ said he, ‘you must not be past sixty now.’

“I closed my door, having been roused from my apathy by this last speech, which the notary thought very funny; then I sat down in my armchair, with my feet on the fire-dogs. I had lost myself in a romance à la Radcliffe, constructed on the juridical base given me by Monsieur Regnault, when the door, opened by a woman’s cautious hand, turned on the hinges. I saw my landlady come in, a buxom, florid dame, always good-humored, who had missed her calling in life. She was a Fleming, who ought to have seen the light in a picture by Teniers.

“‘Well, monsieur,’ said she, ‘Monsieur Regnault has no doubt been giving you his history of la Grande Bretèche?’

“‘Yes, Madame Lepas.’

“‘And what did he tell you?’

“I repeated in a few words the creepy and sinister story of Madame de Merret. At each sentence my hostess put her head forward, looking at me with an innkeeper’s keen scrutiny, a happy compromise between the instinct of a police constable, the astuteness of a spy, and the cunning of a dealer.

“‘My good Madame Lepas,’ said I as I ended, ‘you seem to know more about it. Heh? If not, why have you come up to me?’

“‘On my word, as an honest woman——’

“‘Do not swear; your eyes are big with a secret. You knew Monsieur de Merret; what sort of man was he?’

“‘Monsieur de Merret—well, you see he was a man you never could see the top of, he was so tall! A very good gentleman from Picardy, who had, as we say, his head close to his cap. He paid for everything down, so as never to have difficulties with anyone. He was hot-tempered, you see! All our ladies liked him very much.’

“‘Because he was hot-tempered?’ I asked.

“‘Well, maybe,’ she said; ‘and you may suppose, sir, that a man had to have something to show for a figurehead before he could marry Madame de Merret, who, without any reflection on others, was the handsomest and richest heiress in our parts. She had about twenty thousand francs a year. All the town was at the wedding; the bride was pretty and sweet-looking, quite a gem of a woman. Oh, they were a handsome couple in their day!’

“‘And were they happy together?’

“‘Hm, hm! so-so—so far as can be guessed, for, as you may suppose, we of the common sort were not hail-fellow-well-met with them.—Madame de Merret was a kind woman and very pleasant, who had no doubt sometimes to put up with her husband’s tantrums. But though he was rather haughty, we were fond of him. After all, it was his place to behave so. When a man is a born nobleman, you see——’

“‘Still, there must have been some catastrophe for Monsieur and Madame de Merret to part so violently?’

“‘I did not say there was any catastrophe, sir. I know nothing about it.’

“‘Indeed. Well, now, I am sure you know everything.’

“‘Well, sir, I will tell you the whole story.—When I saw Monsieur Regnault go up to see you, it struck me that he would speak to you about Madame de Merret as having to do with la Grande Bretèche. That put it into my head to ask your advice, sir, seeming to me that you are a man of good judgment and incapable of playing a poor woman like me false—for I never did anyone a wrong, and yet I am tormented by my conscience. Up to now, I have never dared to say a word to the people of these parts; they are all chatter-mags, with tongues like knives. And never till now, sir, have I had any traveler here who stayed so long in the inn as you have, and to whom I could tell the history of the fifteen thousand francs——’

“‘My dear Madame Lepas, if there is anything in your story of a nature to compromise me,’ I said, interrupting her flow of words, ‘I would not hear it for all the world.’

“‘You need have no fears,’ said she; ‘you will see.’

“Her eagerness made me suspect that I was not the only person to whom my worthy landlady had communicated the secret of which I was to be the sole possessor, but I listened.

“‘Monsieur,’ said she, ‘when the Emperor sent the Spaniards here, prisoners of war and others, I was required to lodge at the charge of the Government a young Spaniard sent to Vendôme on parole. Notwithstanding his parole, he had to show himself every day to the sub-prefect. He was a Spanish grandee—neither more nor less. He had a name in os and dia, something like Bagos de Férédia. I wrote his name down in my books, and you may see it if you like. Ah! he was a handsome young fellow for a Spaniard, who are all ugly they say. He was not more than five feet two or three in height, but so well made; and he had little hands that he kept so beautifully! Ah! you should have seen them. He had as many brushes for his hands as a woman has for her toilet. He had thick, black hair, a flame in his eye, a somewhat coppery complexion, but which I admired all the same. He wore the finest linen I have ever seen, though I have had princesses to lodge here, and, among others, General Bertrand, the Duc and Duchesse d’Abrantes, Monsieur Descazes, and the King of Spain. He did not eat much, but he had such polite and amiable ways that it was impossible to owe him a grudge for that. Oh! I was very fond of him, though he did not say four words to me in a day, and it was impossible to have the least bit of talk with him; if he was spoken to, he did not answer; it is a way, a mania they all have, it would seem.

“‘He read his breviary like a priest and went to mass and all the services quite regularly. And where did he post himself?—we found this out later.—Within two yards of Madame de Merret’s chapel. As he took that place the very first time he entered the church, no one imagined that there was any purpose in it. Besides, he never raised his nose above his book, poor young man! And then, monsieur, of an evening he went for a walk on the hill among the ruins of the old castle. It was his only amusement, poor man; it reminded him of his native land. They say that Spain is all hills!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“One evening, shortly after he arrived, he stayed out late. I was uneasy when he didn’t return until midnight, but we were used to his whims; he took the key and we didn’t wait for him. He lived in a house we owned on Rue des Casernes. One of our stable-boys mentioned he thought he saw the Spanish Grandee swimming in the river. When he returned, I warned him about the weeds, and he seemed annoyed at being seen.

“Eventually, one morning, we found he hadn’t returned. Searching his belongings, I discovered a note in his drawer, along with fifty doubloons worth about five thousand francs and a sealed box containing diamonds worth ten thousand francs. The note stated that if he didn’t return, he left us this money and the diamonds to fund masses in gratitude for his escape.

“At that time, my husband went searching for him. Interestingly, he returned with the Spaniard’s clothes, found under a stone on a breakwater near la Grande Bretèche. He went early, so no one saw him. After reading the letter, he burned the clothes and, following Count Férédia’s wishes, we announced he had escaped.

“The sub-prefect sent the constabulary after him, but he was never caught. Lepas believed the Spaniard drowned himself. I, however, think he was involved in the matter concerning Madame de Merret, especially since Rosalie told me that the crucifix her mistress cherished and was buried with was made of ebony and silver; early in his stay, Monsieur Férédia had a similar crucifix, which I never saw again. Now, monsieur, do you think I should feel remorse about the Spaniard’s fifteen thousand francs? Aren’t they truly mine?

“Certainly. But have you ever questioned Rosalie?” I asked.

“Oh, I have, sir. But what can be done? That girl is like a wall. She knows something, but it’s impossible to make her talk.”

After a brief chat, my hostess left me with vague, sinister thoughts, romantic curiosity, and a religious dread, like entering a dark church at night and seeing a faint light under a high vault. La Grande Bretèche, with its overgrown grass, shuttered windows, rusty ironwork, and deserted rooms, came vividly to mind. I felt compelled to explore the mysterious dwelling to uncover the heart of this solemn story, this drama that had claimed three lives.

Rosalie became the most intriguing person in Vendôme to me. Observing her, I sensed an inner thought beneath her blooming health. There was an element of sorrow or hope in her soul; her demeanor suggested a secret, like devout souls who pray excessively or a girl haunted by a child’s last cry. Yet she was simple and clumsy, her vacant smile devoid of criminality; one would deem her innocent seeing the large checked kerchief over her sturdy figure and lilac-and-white-striped gown. No, I resolved, I will not leave Vendôme without knowing the full story of la Grande Bretèche. To achieve this, I would make advances toward Rosalie if necessary.

“Rosalie!” I called one evening.

“Your servant, sir?”

“You’re not married?” She flinched slightly.

“Oh! There’s no shortage of men if I ever fancy being miserable!” she laughed, quickly regaining composure; every woman, from the highest lady to the inn-servant, has a natural presence of mind.

“Yes; you’re attractive enough to never lack suitors! But tell me, Rosalie, why did you become an inn-servant after leaving Madame de Merret? Didn’t she leave you a small annuity?”

“Oh yes, sir. But my position here is the best in all Vendôme.”

Her answer seemed evasive. Rosalie appeared central to this affair; she seemed tied to its core. It wasn’t ordinary courtship material; this girl contained the final chapter of a romance, and from that moment, all my attention was on Rosalie. Studying her, I noticed many admirable qualities: she was clean, neat, and undeniably attractive, soon embodying every charm desire can bestow on a woman, regardless of station. A fortnight after the notary’s visit, one early morning, I said to Rosalie:

“Come, tell me all you know about Madame de Merret.”

“Oh!” she replied, “I will tell you, but keep it a secret.”

“Of course, my child; I will guard your secrets with a thief’s honor, the most loyal known.”

“If it’s all the same to you,” she said, “I’d prefer it be with your own.”

She adjusted her head-kerchief and settled in to tell the tale; a particular attitude of confidence is necessary for storytelling. The best tales are told at a certain hour—just as we are all here at table. No one tells a story well standing or fasting.

If I were to reproduce Rosalie’s diffuse eloquence, a whole volume would scarcely suffice. Now, as the event she recounted stands between the notary’s gossip and Madame Lepas’s account, I will summarize it concisely.

The room at la Grande Bretèche where Madame de Merret slept was on the ground floor; a small cupboard in the wall, about four feet deep, served as her wardrobe. Three months before the evening in question, Madame de Merret had been seriously ill, so much so that her husband had taken to sleeping on the first floor. By chance, he returned home two hours later than usual from the club, where he had been reading the papers and discussing politics. His wife assumed he was in bed and asleep. However, the evening’s animated discussions and his losses at billiards had delayed him.

For some time, Monsieur de Merret had relied on Rosalie to tell him if his wife was in bed; her affirmative responses led him to his room, trusting her completely. But that evening, he decided to check on Madame de Merret to share his misfortunes and perhaps seek comfort. He had noticed her looking particularly lovely at dinner and thought her convalescence had enhanced her beauty, a realization that often comes too late for husbands.

Upon entering, he thought he heard the closet door shut, but when he entered, Madame de Merret stood alone by the fireplace. His instincts were piqued by a nagging doubt, and he noticed an anxious expression in her eyes.

“You’re very late,” she said, her voice slightly husky.

Monsieur de Merret remained silent as Rosalie entered, which startled him. He began pacing the room, arms crossed.

“Have you received bad news, or are you unwell?” his wife asked timidly while Rosalie helped her undress. He didn’t respond.

“You can go, Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said; “I can manage my curl-papers myself.” She sensed disaster in her husband’s demeanor and wanted to be alone with him. Once Rosalie left, or was thought to have left, for she lingered in the passage, Monsieur de Merret confronted his wife, stating coldly, “Madame, there’s someone in your cupboard!” She calmly replied, “No, monsieur.”

This “No” pierced Monsieur de Merret’s heart; he didn’t believe it, yet his wife appeared more saintly than ever. He moved to open the closet door, but Madame de Merret took his hand, stopping him, and said with strange emotion, “Remember, if you find no one there, everything must end between us.”

The extraordinary dignity of her stance filled him with deep respect and inspired a resolve that could become legendary on a grander stage.

“No, Josephine,” he said, “I won’t open it. Regardless of the outcome, we would be forever parted. I know your soul’s purity; you wouldn’t commit a deadly sin to save your life.” At these words, Madame de Merret looked at him with a haggard expression. “Here is your crucifix,” he continued. “Swear to me before God that there’s no one in that closet; I will believe you—I will never open that door.”

Madame de Merret took the crucifix and swore, “I swear it.”

“Louder,” he insisted; “repeat: ‘I swear before God that there is nobody in that closet.’” She repeated the words without flinching.

“That will do,” said Monsieur de Merret coldly. After a moment, he remarked, “You have a fine piece of work there, which I’ve never seen before,” examining the ebony and silver crucifix.

“I found it at Duvivier’s; last year, when that troop of Spanish prisoners passed through Vendôme, he bought it from a Spanish monk.”

“Indeed,” said Monsieur de Merret, hanging the crucifix back on its nail, and he rang for Rosalie.

He waited for her to arrive. “Go to bed, all of you,” he instructed, beckoning Jean, his coachman and confidential servant, to come close. He whispered, “When they’re all asleep—mind, asleep—you understand?—come down and tell me.”

Monsieur de Merret kept his gaze on his wife while giving orders, returning to her at the fireside to discuss the billiards game and the club’s discussions. When Rosalie returned, she found them conversing amiably.

Not long before, Monsieur de Merret had had new ceilings installed in all the reception rooms on the ground floor. Plaster was scarce in Vendôme, and he had ordered a considerable quantity, knowing he could sell any excess. This circumstance inspired his plan.

“Gorenflot is here, sir,” Rosalie whispered.

“Tell him to come in,” he replied aloud.

Madame de Merret paled at the sight of the mason.

“Gorenflot,” said her husband, “go fetch some bricks from the coach-house; bring enough to wall up the cupboard door; you can use the leftover plaster as cement.” Then, pulling Rosalie and the mason close, he whispered, “Listen, Gorenflot, you’ll sleep here tonight; tomorrow morning, I’ll provide you a passport to take you abroad to a place I’ll specify. I’ll give you six thousand francs for your journey. You must live in that town for ten years; if you don’t like it, you may settle elsewhere, but it must be in the same country. Go through Paris and wait there until I join you. I’ll give you an agreement for six thousand francs more, to be paid upon your return, provided you fulfill the conditions of the bargain. In return, you must keep silent about what you do tonight. Rosalie, I’ll secure ten thousand francs for you, which won’t be paid until your wedding day, contingent on your marrying Gorenflot; but to marry, you must remain silent. If not, no wedding gift!”

“Rosalie,” Madame de Merret said, “come and brush my hair.”

Her husband quietly paced the room, keeping an eye on the door, the mason, and his wife, without displaying any suspicion. Gorenflot made some noise. Madame de Merret seized a moment when he was unloading bricks to say to Rosalie: “My dear child, I’ll give you a thousand francs a year if you tell Gorenflot to leave a crack at the bottom.” Then she added casually, “You’d better help him.”

Monsieur and Madame de Merret remained silent while Gorenflot walled up the door. This silence was intentional on the husband’s part; he didn’t want to give his wife the chance to say anything ambiguous. On Madame de Merret’s side, it was either pride or prudence. When the wall was half built, the cunning mason took advantage of his master’s back being turned to break one of the two panes in the top of the door with his pick. Madame de Merret understood that Rosalie had spoken to Gorenflot. They all three then saw the face of a dark, gloomy-looking man with black hair and fiery eyes.

Before her husband turned around, the poor woman nodded to the stranger, signaling “Hope.”

At four o’clock, as dawn broke in September, the work was completed. The mason was placed in charge of Jean, and Monsieur de Merret slept in his wife’s room.

The next morning, he casually mentioned, “Oh, by the way, I must go to the Maire for the passport.” He donned his hat, took a few steps toward the door, paused, and grabbed the crucifix. His wife trembled with joy.

“He will go to Duvivier’s,” she thought.

As soon as he left, Madame de Merret rang for Rosalie and cried in a terrible voice, “The pick! Bring the pick! We must work. I saw how Gorenflot did it yesterday; we have time to make a gap and build it up again.”

In an instant, Rosalie brought her mistress a cleaver; with an intensity beyond words, she began demolishing the wall. She had removed a few bricks when, turning to deliver a stronger blow, she saw Monsieur de Merret behind her. She fainted.

“Lay madame on her bed,” he said coldly.

Foreseeing what would happen in his absence, he had set this trap for his wife; he had merely written to the Maire and summoned Duvivier. The jeweler arrived just as the disorder in the room was being repaired.

“Duvivier,” Monsieur de Merret asked, “didn’t you buy any crucifixes from the Spaniards who passed through town?”

“No, monsieur.”

“Very well; thank you,” he said, casting a fierce glare at his wife. “Jean,” he added, turning to his valet, “you can serve my meals here in Madame de Merret’s room. She is ill, and I won’t leave her until she recovers.”

The cruel man remained in his wife’s room for twenty days. During the early days, when there was some noise in the closet, and Josephine wanted to intercede for the dying man, he said, without allowing her to speak, “You swore on the Cross that there was no one there.”

After this story, all the ladies rose from the table, breaking the spell Bianchon had cast over them. Some among them had shivered at the last words.

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############

##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### CONTINUES

############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

One of the few drawing-rooms where the Archbishop of Besançon appeared during the Restoration was that of the Baronne de Watteville, closely connected to him by her devoutness.

Monsieur de Watteville, descended from the notorious Watteville, was mild and unassuming by contrast. After a quiet life in La Franche-Comté, he married the heiress Mademoiselle de Rupt, bringing substantial income. Their match, arranged in 1802, was formalized in 1815 after the second Restoration. Following her grandparents’ death, Madame de Watteville grew increasingly pious, and her devotion influenced Besançon’s upper circles.

Thin and unremarkable, Monsieur de Watteville occupied himself as a turner and collector of local geological specimens, especially fossils. Some perceived a noble soul in him: recognizing he would not dominate his wife, he threw himself into work and hobbies.

The Rupt residence was grand, reflecting both families’ standing, with elegant furnishings and ceremonious meals; Monsieur often acted as his own butler. Madame’s wealth was considerable, while her husband’s estate was modest. Their close ties with the Archbishop drew several notable abbés to their home.

At a ceremonial dinner in September 1834, guests welcomed Monsieur l’Abbé de Grancey, who announced a favorable verdict in a lawsuit over family property. The Vicar-General said the victory was unexpected in the political climate and credited a young lawyer, Monsieur Savaron.

Curious, Madame de Watteville asked about Savaron’s background. The Vicar-General said he lived nearby but his precise origins were unknown, which heightened interest. Amedee de Soulas, present, would later prove significant to the unfolding story.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In all ages France and England have exchanged cultural influences, especially in language and fashion, often bypassing customs. The label "English" in Paris is "French" in London, reflecting mutual influence. For example, the English national anthem, "God Save the King," was composed by Lulli for a French play, and hoop skirts, once ridiculed, became dominant in Europe. By 1815 English long waists were mocked in Paris, but French styles soon evolved.

England contributed terms to French usage, such as "dandy" and "lion." The latter, popularized by Alfred de Musset’s poetry, inspired a vogue for stylish young men in Paris that spread to provincial towns. In 1834 Besançon had its own "lion" in Amedee-Sylvain de Soulas, a young man of Spanish descent who felt out of place in the dull military town. He lived modestly in a sparsely furnished apartment but maintained a lavish appearance, employing a young servant, Babylas, as his "tiger." Amedee dressed Babylas fashionably and spent extravagantly on his wardrobe while surviving on limited income.

Though seen as a spendthrift, Amedee was skilled at budgeting, often dining out to save on household costs. His expensive grooming and clothes were a façade to impress the local aristocracy and win the favor of Mademoiselle Rosalie de Watteville, a wealthy heiress. His travels and knowledge of Parisian culture earned him a reputation as well-informed despite financial strain.

Besançon was conservative and resistant to change; the local aristocracy—called "the Colony"—maintained strict hierarchies. Amedee’s distinctive fashion, like trouser-straps, set him apart and reinforced his status as a "lion." The town’s aristocrats were eager to appear modern to visiting Parisians, and Amedee provided that cosmopolitan touch.

Amedee’s ambitions included marrying Rosalie, who had been raised in a strict religious household with little exposure to the wider world. At eighteen she was modest and unassuming, educated under her mother’s watchful eye, trained in domestic skills rather than social graces, and sheltered from contemporary influences.

Despite a passive demeanor, Rosalie possessed a strong spirit inherited from her family. Her mother, Madame de Watteville, was fiercely protective and controlling, convinced her daughter’s honor was at stake. This dynamic produced tension as Amedee courted Rosalie: the Baroness’s strictness caused Rosalie to suppress feelings, creating a complex relationship marked by maternal jealousy and the daughter’s uncertainty.

As Amedee pursued Rosalie he navigated Besançon’s social intricacies, aiming for a favorable marriage while preserving his reputation. The town’s elite found him charming, and talk of an engagement circulated among the nobility. Yet Rosalie’s upbringing and her mother’s influence complicated his efforts, since she remained largely unaware of the romantic expectations around her.

Amedee de Soulas’s life in Besançon thus exemplifies the interplay of social ambition, cultural exchange, and provincial constraints as he strives to win Rosalie de Watteville amid rigid social structures.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Madame,” said Monsieur de Soulas to the Baroness as he waited for his soup to cool, “a gentleman from Paris arrived at the Hotel National and took the first floor of Mademoiselle Galard’s house on Rue du Perron. He registered at the Mairie and had his card entered on the list of barristers under the name ALBERT SAVARON.”

“The Savaron name is famous,” Mademoiselle de Watteville interrupted, “one of Belgium’s oldest and richest families.”

“He’s French and not of that family,” Amedee de Soulas replied. “If he claims the Savaron arms he must add a bar-sinister. The only Brabant left is a wealthy heiress, Mademoiselle de Savarus.”

“The bar-sinister marks a bastard, but a bastard of a Comte de Savarus is still noble,” Rosalie chimed in.

“Enough, mademoiselle!” the Baroness said.

“You taught her heraldry,” Monsieur de Watteville remarked. “She knows it well.”

“Go on, Monsieur de Soulas.”

“In a small town like Besançon, where everything’s catalogued, Albert Savaron was welcomed by the lawyers. They wondered who sent him and what he aimed for, especially since he sent his card to judges instead of calling personally. Three days later they forgot about him. He hired Jerome, the late Monsieur Galard’s servant, who could cook a little. No one really noticed him.”

“Does he go to mass?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“He attends early service at Saint-Pierre on Sundays. He rises between one and two, works until eight, breakfasts, continues working, walks in his garden in long circles, dines, and goes to bed between six and seven.”

“How do you know all this?” she pressed.

“I live on Rue Neuve, opposite his house. My servant speaks with Jerome.”

“And you gossip with Babylas?”

“What else to do while riding?”

“How did you come to hire a stranger for your defense?” the Baroness asked the Vicar-General.

“The President of the Court assigned him to defend a half-witted peasant accused of forgery. Savaron proved the man innocent and exposed the real culprits, who were arrested. His defense impressed Court and jury. A merchant then trusted him with a hard case, which he won. Monsieur Berryer advised us to employ Savaron given our situation; I met him and felt confident— I was right.”

“Is he so remarkable?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked.

“Certainly,” the Vicar-General said.

“Tell us more,” urged Madame de Watteville.

“The first time I met him,” the Abbe de Grancey recalled, “he received me in an outer room panelled like oak and lined with law books. The furniture was plain: a writing table, six armchairs, gray curtains, green carpet. I did not expect my advocate to be young. He matched his rooms: a black dressing gown with red accents. He had a magnificent head—black hair streaked with gray, thick curls, a white throat, a broad forehead furrowed by thought, an olive complexion, a square nose, and fiery eyes. He can be passionate yet calm; his voice is sweet but can thunder.”

“Monsieur Albert Savaron is of average height, neither stout nor thin, with hands like a prelate,” the Abbe added.

“The second time I saw him he received me in his shabby bedroom; the third, I caught him having a frugal breakfast in his library. There’s much behind that terrible yet gentle face; he seems to carry a heavy burden.”

“Why would such an eloquent man leave Paris for Besançon?” Madame de Chavoncourt asked. “Doesn’t he know how little chance a stranger has here?”

“After seeing his head and hearing him answer a prominent Parisian lawyer,” the Abbe replied, “I believe he could make a great mark.”

“Why discuss him? You’ve won your case and paid him,” Madame de Watteville said, watching her daughter listen with rapt attention.

The conversation moved on, but the Vicar-General’s portrait of Savaron transfixed Rosalie. For the first time she encountered someone exceptional, a contrast to the mundane Count who had courted her. Albert Savaron became an enigma she wanted to unravel.

“Albert Savaron de Savarus,” she mused, longing to see him. She replayed the Abbe’s words—his forehead, his hands—and found nothing like him at the table.

“Rosalie, why aren’t you eating?” her mother asked.

“I’m not hungry, mamma,” she answered, lost in thoughts of the prelate’s hands. She recalled a lighted window she had once glimpsed from her bed and realized it belonged to him. “I must see him!” she decided.

“Monsieur de Grancey, is the Chapter’s lawsuit settled?” Rosalie asked during a lull.

“What does that matter to you?” her mother said.

“It might go to the Court of Appeal,” the Abbe answered.

“I never thought Rosalie would think about a lawsuit at dinner,” Madame de Watteville laughed.

“Nor I,” Rosalie said dreamily, prompting more amusement. The rest of the meal passed without further mention of Savaron, but the idea of him lingered with Rosalie. She planned to wake between two and three to glimpse his windows that night. When she did, she thrilled to see candlelight moving behind the trees and imagined his furniture and rooms.

“While the world sleeps, he is awake—like God!” she thought.

The education of girls is delicate; mothers shape the nation's future, and schools or religious systems must decide how much girls should know. Enlightening them too much risks premature seriousness; suppressing thought can cause sudden outbreaks, as in Molière’s Agnes. Rosalie’s crisis was set off by the Abbe’s portrayal of Savaron.

Next morning, while dressing, Rosalie saw Albert walking in the garden behind the Hotel de Rupt. “What if he lived elsewhere? At least I can see him here. What is he thinking?” she wondered. Determined to enter his house, hear his voice, and see his eyes, she resolved to act.

She stitched through the day with a focused air that concealed deep thought. She decided to go to confession and arranged to confess after Mass at half-past seven, hoping to encounter Albert.

She visited her father in his workshop and suggested he make large turnings and build a grotto in the garden to showcase his work. Pleased, he promised not to tell her mother, sharing her desire to surprise her. Rosalie pictured an observatory from which to watch Albert’s private study. Young girls often contrive much for the sake of a man, even if he is unaware.

Sunday arrived and Rosalie dressed more carefully than usual. Her maid Mariette noticed her fidgeting.

“It’s the first time I’ve seen you so nervous,” Mariette said.

“Perhaps you’re more particular on certain days,” Rosalie replied, blushing.

She crossed the courtyard with a pounding heart and went to church early, praying briefly, then checking the confessional to see if Abbe Giroud was in place. She positioned herself to see Albert enter. No man seemed unattractive to her now; Albert Savaron, strikingly handsome, made a deep impression. His solemn air suggested a heavy burden; his deep gaze hinted at intense thought. She understood the Vicar-General’s words fully: those eagle-like eyes revealed hidden ardor. Boldly, she intercepted his path to exchange glances, and warmth flooded her.

After Albert sat, Rosalie found a spot to watch him during her confession. When Mariette announced Monsieur Giroud, her time in the confessional felt swift. By the time she emerged, Mass had ended and Albert had left.

“The Vicar-General was right,” she thought. “He is unhappy. Why has this eagle come to Besançon? I must know everything! But how?”

Resolved, she redoubled her attention to embroidery to hide her distraction from her mother.

From that Sunday, after their glance, Rosalie threw herself into promoting the Belvedere her father planned. “Mamma,” she said, “my father has an idea for a Belvedere in the garden. Do you approve?”

“I approve of everything he does,” Madame de Watteville replied dryly. “Why should I object to something that amuses him?”

“Because from there we’ll see into Monsieur de Soulas’ rooms and he might see us,” Rosalie warned.

“Do you presume to guide your parents, Rosalie?” her mother rebuked.

“I’ll say no more, mamma. Besides, my father mentions a cool room in the grotto for coffee.”

Madame de Watteville approved the design and chose a spot unseen from Monsieur de Soulas’ windows but with a view into Albert Savaron’s rooms. A builder was hired to construct a grotto with paths of periwinkle, clematis, ivy, honeysuckle, and Virginia creeper. The interior would be rustic: a mirror, ottoman, and a table of inlaid bark. Monsieur de Soulas suggested an asphalt floor; Rosalie proposed a rustic chandelier. Rumors of the Wattevilles’ project circulated.

“They’re wealthy and can afford whims—” people said.

“A thousand crowns!” Madame de Chavoncourt exclaimed.

“Yes, a thousand crowns,” young Monsieur de Soulas confirmed. “A man from Paris will decorate the interior; it will be pretty. Monsieur de Watteville is carving the chandelier himself.”

“Berquet is making a cellar under it,” an Abbe added.

“No,” young de Soulas corrected, “it’s on a concrete foundation to avoid damp.”

“You know least of what happens in that house,” Madame de Chavoncourt snapped, glancing at her daughter Rosalie, who had waited a year to marry.

Mademoiselle de Watteville felt proud of the Belvedere’s success, pleased at outshining others. No one suspected that the ostensible plan to enjoy family leisure was actually Rosalie’s scheme to get a closer look at Albert Savaron’s study.

Meanwhile, Albert’s brilliant defense of the Cathedral Chapter was soon eclipsed by jealousy. He remained secluded and avoided society, which helped him slip by as a stranger in Besançon. Still, he argued three complex cases at the Commercial Tribunal and won four prominent merchants as clients who appreciated his skill.

On the day the Watteville family inaugurated the Belvedere, Savaron was launching a new venture. Quietly, he had gathered merchants to found a fortnightly paper called the Eastern Review, funded by forty shares of five hundred francs each from his first ten clients, who believed Besançon needed promotion as a trade center.

To compete with Strasbourg, Besançon must become a center of enlightenment and commerce. Savaron’s aim was to challenge the literary dominance of Strasbourg and Dijon and illuminate eastern France. Merchants, feeling their own interests at stake, found his ideas attractive.

Savaron kept his name from the limelight. He let his chief client, Monsieur Boucher, manage finances while he kept editorial control and a share of profits. The Review appealed across regions; at a low subscription price it quickly gained five hundred subscribers.

To avoid offending local pride, Savaron enlisted the Boucher family’s eager son, Alfred, to handle the literary side. Albert guided the content subtly while allowing Alfred to think he was gaining recognition. The commercial backers happily paid only a fraction of their shares. With two hundred more subscribers the publication would pay dividends; the editor would remain unpaid.

By the third issue the Review had earned recognition among French publications. Albert read the other reviews at home. The third number included a tale attributed to him, which stirred discussion in Madame de Chavoncourt’s drawing room even as higher circles in Besançon dismissed it for its liberal bent.

“Papa,” Rosalie said, “there’s a Review published in Besançon; you should subscribe and keep it in your room, for mamma wouldn’t let me read it, but you could lend it to me.”

Monsieur de Watteville, anxious to please Rosalie, who had shown him much affection, subscribed to the Eastern Review and lent her the four issues already out. That night Rosalie devoured the tale—the first she had ever read. Given her limited experience, its impact was profound. She expected to find in it the sentiments and perhaps the life of Albert; from the first pages she believed it reflected his experiences, convinced that the tale was a confession drawn from his life.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In 1823 two young men left Lucerne for a holiday in Switzerland to tour the Lake of the Four Cantons. The journey offered striking mountain, river, and meadow views. Passing the hamlet of Gersau, one friend, Rodolphe, was captivated by a newly built house and a woman at the window and decided to stay; his friend Leopold continued the trip.

Rodolphe and Leopold had studied law together: Leopold was bound for a notary’s post in Paris, while Rodolphe, the illegitimate son of a nobleman, had a more unsettled background. His mother had sacrificed everything to secure his education, hoping to improve his prospects. Passionate and sensitive, Rodolphe was often led by desire, a contrast to Leopold’s calm demeanor.

In Gersau Rodolphe lodged with a couple who took him in. He learned the woman at the window was Fanny Lovelace, who had moved there with her elderly father for his health. They were modestly off, and Fanny supported her father by translating and writing. Rodolphe was struck by her beauty and musical talent.

The next day he sought permission to visit the Bergmanns’ gardens; the Bergmanns rented land to the Lovelaces and were initially hesitant but allowed him to explore. There he noticed a deaf-mute girl, Gina, whose presence hinted at a distinctive past. Eager to meet Fanny, Rodolphe encountered resistance from the protective Bergmanns.

That evening he heard Fanny singing and was enchanted. He later met Fanny and Gina in person, but their conversation was cut short when an unknown assailant attacked and wounded him. Fanny and Gina aided him and he was taken to safety.

While recovering, Rodolphe received devastating news: his mother had died. The loss plunged him into deep depression, but he found solace in Fanny’s company. They bonded over shared interests, and Rodolphe confessed his feelings, only to learn Fanny was married to an older man, Emilio. Despite this, Fanny’s affection for Rodolphe grew and they shared intimate moments.

Fanny disclosed that her husband was not as old as he appeared and that she had married him under duress. Rodolphe was torn between his love for her and the reality of her marriage. Fanny encouraged him to pursue his ambitions, believing in his potential.

Their relationship deepened, yet Rodolphe struggled with jealousy and insecurity, especially when news arrived of Tito, a young man from Fanny’s past. Tito’s appearance provoked rivalry in Rodolphe, who feared losing her affection.

Despite these challenges, Rodolphe and Fanny’s connection remained strong. They shared dreams of freedom and a future together, but the complexities of their circumstances—Rodolphe’s ambition and Fanny’s ties to her husband—created persistent tension. As they navigated their feelings, Rodolphe wrestled with the demands of love, ambition, and the sacrifices required for happiness; the beauty of their surroundings mirrored the intensity of their emotions, even as reality threatened their dreams.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
For twenty days Rodolphe stayed at the Bergmanns’, watching Francesca. He saw in her the youthful imprudence and unbroken spirit of a woman who sometimes resisted love and sometimes yielded to it. The old man treated her like a father; her gratitude stirred a native nobility that intrigued Rodolphe and made her an enigma.

Those days mingled secret joys and melancholy, their quarrels as delightful as their reconciliations. Rodolphe became increasingly captivated by Francesca’s steady tenderness, growing jealous over trifles.

One evening, when Francesca said she wanted to leave Gersau because it lacked luxury, Rodolphe asked whether she cared for luxury. She answered passionately, equating her fondness for luxury with a love of art and beauty and insisting she had never complained through hardships. Playfully she argued that it was natural for ordinary people to aspire to greatness, revealing her charming character.

She then insisted Rodolphe not come with them to Geneva, citing town gossip and her wish to protect her husband’s reputation. She affirmed her love for Rodolphe but demanded it remain concealed. She kissed him and left him astonished.

The next day the Bergmanns’ lodgers departed early, and restless, Rodolphe went to Vevay and then to Geneva by the end of October, taking a longer route. To avoid the town’s discomforts he rented a room in Eaux-Vives. His first inquiry was after Italian refugees, especially a bookseller named Lamporani, but the landlord said the only new arrivals were Prince and Princess Colonna and Prince and Princess Gandolphini.

Rodolphe’s first walk took him to the Villa Diodati, whose beauty struck him. Approaching, he saw Francesca in an elegant carriage with an old lady and felt dizzy with surprise. Learning they were at the Colonna residence, a veil seemed to lift: the implications of their past interactions became clear.

He feared he had been a mere whim for Francesca, struggling with the reality of her noble standing. He sent his card to Prince Gandolphini and was received by a false Lamporani, who invited him to a concert that evening. Though curious, Rodolphe refrained from requesting an audience with the Princess and returned to Eaux-Vives with mingled anticipation and anxiety.

As evening approached his passion deepened and he felt compelled to elevate his social position to match hers. When he finally saw her at the concert she was dazzling; their eyes met with immediate recognition. Francesca’s beauty and grace captivated him and he felt a profound connection, convinced she had remained faithful to their past despite her rank.

After the concert Rodolphe was introduced to the Colonnas and to Francesca, who playfully styled herself “Princess Rodolphini.” The jest revived memories of their time in Gersau; he was overwhelmed by the beauty of her voice and the intimacy of their proximity.

In the ensuing days Francesca perceived Rodolphe’s deep love, which for her felt natural. She said their affection should be faithful and enduring, whatever the distance. Rodolphe, however, was driven by ambition; he wanted to achieve greatness so Francesca could be proud of him.

Francesca was a remarkable blend of gaiety and tenderness. Once destined for a cloister, family misfortunes had compelled her to marry Prince Gandolphini to preserve her family’s standing. Yet despite her noble birth she had acquired a taste for independence and liberal ideas, inclining her toward revolutionary sentiments.

Rodolphe spent the winter in Geneva where time flew in the company of the intelligent and beautiful Francesca. But with spring came inner turmoil: Francesca’s calmness contrasted with his emotional unrest; he wished she would show more feeling, believing Italian women to be more expressive.

One day on the terrace he voiced a bitter complaint about the happiness of others, singling out a woman of noble lineage; his words wounded Francesca, and she withdrew. Rodolphe followed and found her in tears. In a moment of passion he kissed her, but she repelled him and asserted her dignity.

Francesca then ordered him to leave, declaring they would not meet again until Naples. Her command was firm; Rodolphe, recognizing the strength of her will, complied.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On returning to Paris, Rodolphe discovered a portrait of Princess Gandolphini by Schinner, an artist who had refused many women. Doubting the Prince could persuade him, Francesca charmed Schinner into painting an original for Rodolphe and a duplicate for Emilio; she announced it in a letter that began a correspondence between Rodolphe and Francesca—their sole indulgence.

Ambitious and driven by love, Rodolphe invested everything in a venture that deceit ruined after three years of struggle. The Villele ministry fell just as he faced ruin. Seeking solace, he turned to politics, but went to Naples to recuperate at the Villa Gandolphini, where the Prince and Princess had been restored to their positions. The three-month respite renewed his courage and hope.

He then tried to rebuild his fortune; his talents were noticed and high post promised for his dedication. But the July 1830 storm dashed his ambitions again.

Only Francesca and God witnessed the brave, daring efforts of this man of fine qualities but unlucky in fortune. Sustained by love, he returned to new challenges, guided by a faithful presence and a devoted heart.

Lovers! Pray for him!
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
As Mademoiselle de Watteville finished her narrative, Rosalie’s cheeks burned and tears fell. The novel, in the popular mode of the time, was her first encounter with a love story told through a man’s experience, and it stirred deep emotion. Her agitation grew into jealousy of Francesca Colonna, whom she believed to be Albert’s beloved.

Consumed by curiosity and a fierce desire to win Albert despite insecurities about her looks and lack of musical talent, Rosalie feared he would never love her. That night her imagination hatched schemes typical of a young woman raised under strict rules: sneaking into Albert’s garden, writing to him, or contriving reasons to bring him to her father’s estate.

Watching Albert’s window, she noticed something that might reveal his secrets: Jerome, Albert’s servant, being hoisted over the wall by Mariette, the maid. Rosalie observed Mariette’s plainness but also her steadiness and means, which explained Jerome’s interest. The sight taught Rosalie a dangerous lesson: her mother’s rigid upbringing could be undermined by a servant’s choices.

The next morning Rosalie accompanied Mariette to Mass and seized the chance to question her about Jerome. Confronting Mariette about their secret meetings, she surprised herself by offering support in return for access to the letters Albert sent through Jerome. Relieved, Mariette agreed.

Rosalie’s obsession deepened as she read a letter from Albert to his friend Leopold that revealed his noble lineage and past struggles. She became enthralled by his ambitions and the complexity of his character: a man striving for success while devoted to the woman he loved—Francesca.

As she continued through Albert’s correspondence, Rosalie grew more aware of her inadequacy and the moral implications of spying. She wrestled with guilt over invading his privacy but rationalized it as proof of love. Her passion slid into dangerous infatuation, prompting penances and self-imposed suffering meant to demonstrate her devotion.

One evening Mariette brought another letter intended for Francesca, plunging Rosalie into jealousy and doubt. She hesitated but yielded to the urge to read it. The letter confirmed Albert’s enduring love for Francesca, outlining his ambitions and hopes for their future together.

Rosalie’s heart ached as she absorbed its contents and grasped the depth of his feeling for another. Though she had once resolved to respect their relationship, she found herself torn between admiration for their fidelity and her own longing. In a clear moment she chose to step back, recognizing the nobility of their bond and acknowledging her shortcomings.

Preparing to return the letter with Mariette, Rosalie reflected on her feelings and the nature of love. She felt sorrow and resignation, recognizing that true love often demands sacrifice. In her heart she longed for the devotion Albert and Francesca shared, while coming to terms with the reality of her unrequited affection.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Rosalie spent the winter of 1834–35 in turmoil; by April, at nineteen, she fantasized about triumphing over the Duchesse d’Argaiolo. Alone, that struggle fueled a dark passion and drove her to devise plans. Many young women like Rosalie exist; her story reads as a cautionary tale.

That winter Albert de Savarus made significant advances in Besançon, eagerly awaiting the Chamber’s dissolution. He’d secured the backing of a wealthy contractor influential in the moderate party. The Romans had once invested heavily in town water supplies, and Besançon drew water from Arcier, a distant hill. Restoring an aqueduct to drink the Romans’ water seemed absurd in a town already served by the Doubs, but convincing citizens would generate substantial expenses benefiting the contractor.

Savarus argued the river water was unfit and promoted Arcier water. Articles in the local Review echoed this, and townspeople across political lines agreed on the desirability of drinking like the Romans. The issue became a focal point, driven by hidden interests: opponents were dismissed as old-fashioned while Savarus’s projects dominated discussion. After eighteen months he had stirred the stagnant town and gained influence without popularity.

Throughout the winter he won several lawsuits for local priests, and his ambition consumed him. His apparent indifference to personal profit masked a form of moral usury: he anticipated a far greater reward.

In October 1834 he helped a struggling merchant with funds from Léopold Hannequin, which also gave him a qualification for election. The Abbé de Grancey, who perceived Savarus’s ambitions, remarked on his remarkable nature and suggested he’d taken a wrong turn in life.

Meanwhile Rosalie resolved to introduce Savarus to the society of the Hôtel de Rupt, at first content to observe him from afar. The Wattevilles’ estate, les Rouxey, yielded ten thousand francs a year, but its management was neglected by the Baron, who left it to an old servant named Modinier. The Baron’s indifference sprang from his wife’s substantial income; they often retreated to les Rouxey, a picturesque spot enhanced by the famous Watteville.

Watteville had built a dam and lake, improving the area’s beauty, but his heirs maintained a questionable claim over the village of Riceys, which had long enjoyed rights to the land. The Baron’s death left a legacy of usurpation, and his descendants continued to assert control over the village.

Rosalie’s maneuvers were subtle. In May 1835 she left Besançon with her father for les Rouxey, where the lake’s beauty captivated her and she associated it with love. On a boat trip her father confided about legal troubles with Riceys over land rights; Rosalie urged him to hire Savarus as their lawyer, stressing the importance of securing the Dent de Vilard for the estate.

She argued a lawsuit would enliven their lives and insisted on bringing Savarus to les Rouxey. Determined to secure her future there, she envisioned creating beautiful gardens.

Four days later the Abbé de Grancey visited Savarus to persuade him to represent the Wattevilles. Savarus hesitated, fearing such an alignment would compromise his neutrality. The Abbé urged him to consider the political advantages of supporting the Wattevilles, especially given potential votes.

Rosalie, meanwhile, sought to keep Savarus in Besançon and prevent his election. She wrote to the préfet to reveal Savarus’s ambitions, but the préfet disregarded her. Rosalie understood the political landscape and tried to manipulate it to her advantage.

As the election approached, a meeting of influential men in Besançon decided to nominate Savarus as a candidate, much to his surprise. He had been unaware of the political machinations. The Abbé, watching closely, warned Savarus of potential betrayals and advised him to be transparent about his past.

Savarus delivered a passionate speech outlining his vision and ambitions, captivating his audience. Yet he faced challenges as the préfet worked to undermine his support and his voter base began to decline. Despite his efforts, the political scene shifted against him and he struggled to maintain his position.

On the day of the first voting the results were disappointing for Savarus; he felt the weight of his ambitions and the pressure of the election. He returned home in despair, reflecting on his love for Francesca and the uncertainty of his future.

Just as hope faded, the Abbé de Grancey arrived with encouraging news. The priest had been working behind the scenes to secure support for Savarus, urging him to visit the Wattevilles and promising their case would be resolved in time for the election.

The Abbé revealed that winning the Rouxey lawsuit could secure crucial votes and encouraged Savarus to consider a marriage with Sidonie de Chavoncourt to solidify his political standing. Initially resistant, Savarus realized the potential benefits of such a union.

The Abbé’s guidance proved invaluable. Savarus prepared to navigate the complexities of both love and politics, understanding that his future depended on choices he would soon have to make.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
By nine o’clock the following evening Madame la Baronne de Watteville’s rooms were filled with Besançon’s aristocracy, assembled because of the unusual decision to go to the polls for the daughter of the Rupts. The former Master of Appeals, a secretary to a loyal minister of the Elder Branch, was to be presented. Madame de Chavoncourt came with her younger daughter Sidonie, elegantly dressed; her elder sister, confident in her relationship, had made no effort—an omission remarked on in small towns. The Abbe de Grancey moved through the groups, listening and offering incisive comments that steered the conversation.

“If the Elder Branch returns,” he asked an elderly statesman, “what politicians would they find?” The statesman said Berryer, isolated on his bench, was uncertain and might disrupt the Government if supported. They discussed the Duc de Fitz-James in Toulouse and how voting for Monsieur Savarus could draw Republicans to them instead of the Moderates.

By nine-thirty Albert had still not arrived, and Madame de Watteville found his lateness disrespectful. Madame de Chavoncourt advised her not to let trifles affect serious business, suggesting Albert might be delayed by a consultation. Rosalie, watching her mother and Madame de Chavoncourt, observed her leniency toward Albert, hinting at a possible match between Sidonie and him.

At ten Albert’s absence provoked growing irritation; some gentlemen began playing cards. The Abbe de Grancey, perplexed, went to a window where Rosalie stood and exclaimed, “He must be dead!” The Vicar-General, Monsieur de Watteville and Rosalie went into the garden to check Albert’s rooms, which were dark and silent.

“Jerome!” Rosalie called to the servant. When he confirmed Albert had left in a post-chaise, the Abbe cried, “He is ruined! Or he is happy!” Rosalie’s face showed joy, which the Vicar-General noticed but pretended not to, wondering at her involvement.

Back in the drawing-room, Monsieur de Watteville announced Albert’s unexplained departure. By half-past eleven only fifteen guests remained: Madame de Chavoncourt, the Abbe de Godenars, the two Chavoncourt girls, Monsieur de Vauchelles, the Abbe de Grancey, Rosalie, Amedee de Soulas, a retired magistrate who had supported Albert’s election, and a few others. The Abbe de Grancey stationed himself near the Baroness to observe Rosalie, whose usually pale face now showed agitation.

“What could have happened to Monsieur de Savarus?” Madame de Chavoncourt wondered. At that moment a servant brought a letter for the Abbe de Grancey on a silver tray. The Baroness urged him to read it. As he did he saw Rosalie turn pale and recognized the handwriting. He folded the letter and slipped it into his pocket, exchanging a glance with Rosalie that hinted at her feeling.

“She is in love with Albert Savarus!” he concluded. As he prepared to leave, Rosalie intercepted him. “Monsieur de Grancey, it was from Albert!” she cried. When he asked how she knew the writing, she answered passionately, “I love him! What is the matter?” He told her that Albert was withdrawing from the election. Rosalie, stunned, begged him to keep the news secret, realizing that if the election ended so too would the prospect of her sister’s marriage to Albert.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In the morning, on her way to Mass, Mademoiselle de Watteville learned from Mariette about Albert’s sudden disappearance. An elderly gentleman from Paris had arrived at the Hotel National and called on Monsieur Savaron. The lawyer turned pale at the gentleman’s card; they met for over an hour. Afterwards the gentleman left with a large package, and Savaron was distraught. He went to the Prefecture, bought an old carriage, ordered post-horses, left his keys, and told his servant to seek other employment.

The next day Savaron’s flight caused a stir in Besançon; the Prefect quickly granted him a passport. The affair fed local prejudice against outsiders, and Albert was soon forgotten by most—except Girardet, the Vicar-General, and Rosalie, who alone knew the truth about the Duc d’Argaiolo’s death.

By April 1836 there was still no news of Albert. The Baroness de Watteville urged Rosalie to marry, noting how much she had changed. The Baron agreed to give Rosalie the family estate, les Rouxey, but the Baroness fretted over finances. She and the Baron arranged a match with Monsieur de Soulas, which Rosalie firmly rejected; she insisted she was content as she was.

Conflict between Rosalie and her mother escalated, and a summer at les Rouxey made Rosalie’s refusal public. After Mariette and Jerome married, the Baron improved the estate, provoking the Baroness’s resentment. As tensions rose, the Vicar-General tried to mediate.

One day Rosalie confessed to the Vicar-General that she had interfered in Albert’s relationship with Francesca, the Duchess d’Argaiolo: she had intercepted letters and caused their separation. The Vicar-General was horrified, stressing the moral gravity of her deception and urging her to seek forgiveness and to recognize the consequences she had set in motion.

Rosalie’s guilt deepened when a Paris clerk arrived to sell Albert’s effects, revealing that he had renounced the world and entered a monastery. The Vicar-General wrote to the monastery and received a letter from Albert expressing complete forgiveness for Rosalie and his wish to remain in solitude.

When the Vicar-General shared this, he urged reconciliation with her mother and acceptance of Monsieur de Soulas. Rosalie remained defiant, unwilling to sacrifice her mother’s happiness. The Vicar-General’s death left a void; soon after, Madame de Watteville married Monsieur de Soulas, believing Rosalie’s support.

Now legally of age, Rosalie encouraged the marriage and thus secured her independence. With a substantial fortune she entered Parisian society intent on confronting the Duchess d’Argaiolo. At a ball she publicly revealed her role in Albert’s downfall, leaving the Duchess shaken.

Rosalie returned to Besançon, having exposed the truth, yet the emotional toll of her actions weighed heavily. The tale of love, betrayal, and ambition unfolded, leaving a lasting impact on all involved.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Mademoiselle de Watteville lived alone on her estate of les Rouxey, riding, hunting, and improving the land, and was well known in the Eastern provinces. Madame de Soulas, who has two children, appears younger; her husband, Monsieur de Soulas, has grown old. He remarked to young Chavoncourt, “My fortune has cost me dear. To know a bigot, it is unfortunately necessary to marry her!”

Mademoiselle de Watteville was known for eccentric vagaries. Each year she visited the walls of the Grande Chartreuse, perhaps following a grand-uncle who once broke through monastery walls to seek freedom. In 1841 she left Besançon, ostensibly for marriage, though the true reason was unclear. She returned after a Loire steamboat boiler explosion that so grievously injured her she lost her right arm and left leg and was left with disfiguring facial scars. Her health declined; in constant pain, she now leads a life of religious observance at the Chartreuse of les Rouxey.

PARIS, May 1842.
