Mignon and Dumay set off to Havre; the colonel planned a lesson in character for his daughter.

On arrival at the Chalet the household greeted the count’s return with joy. The colonel, tender with his wife, watched Modeste with delight; Modeste, pale but composed, lavished kisses on her father. Dumay’s silence and the colonel’s resolve unnerved her. The colonel told Modeste to rise early next day for a walk by the shore—there would be talk about her poems. On the walk he told her he knew all and more than she did. He reproached her for writing to a stranger without consulting family and for treating marriage as a poem rather than a matter of prudence. Modeste defended herself: she had been drawn by genius, not by a stranger. The colonel argued family oversight existed to spare girls from the dangers of romantic illusion. He insisted Modeste must choose between La Brière—the plain, honorable secretary—and Canalis—the famed poet she admired. To his surprise, Modeste swore to obey him in the matter of marriage. He warned her that the man she loved might be a deceiver; she insisted she loved an honest, candid soul. The colonel insisted the family must guide her and set a plan: she must see both candidates in the country among ordinary pursuits and choose freely. Modeste promised to obey.

Disenchanted, Modeste fell from poetic heights into the cold plain of reality. Her imagination—the source of her idealizing love—found in daily life only humiliation; she became numb and morose. Once exultant, she now tasted savage pleasure in her suffering and withdrew into morbid melancholy. Yet the shock clarified her mind: her head cleared, malice and discernment grew. She developed a resolve to humiliate La Brière—revenge on the false Canalis—though her mother counseled courtesy.

A third suitor soon arrived. Latournelle declared that the Duc d’Herouville, a titled but impecunious grand equerry and peer of France, had offered himself after seeing Modeste at the Vilquins. Rumor already exaggerated the Mignon fortune, and the duke’s family pressed the match. The duke, thin, pale, and timid but with courtly prestige, was as much a caricature of faded nobility as many such provincial peers; yet titles still had weight. The house gossip noted that Modeste, now thought an heiress, had revived attention and competition.

Gobenheim and others speculated about Colonel Mignon’s wealth. The colonel, refusing to part with his chosen servants, installed them and began to live in a style that stirred local rumor. In time the arrival of Canalis and La Brière at a villa at Sanvic was announced; they would come to the Chalet the following evening. Canalis, anxious to make a strong impression, engaged Germain, his valet, to arrange a grand household, and in Havre Germain’s air and insistence on treating La Brière as a secretary suggested the social gap that would work against Ernest. Canalis sedulously cultivated effects—carriage painted with arms, servants, and airs; he planned to appear indifferent and disdainful to pique Modeste. La Brière, sincere and despondent, saw himself as prose against Canalis’s poetry. Nevertheless, both men planned to spend an evening at the Chalet.

Canalis’s entrance at the Chalet was studied: he adopted pompous postures, declamatory diction, and the theatrical manner he had perfected under the Duchesse de Chaulieu’s flattery. His affectations, harmless in Paris, were conspicuous in provincial Havre. He lavished public homage on war and nation and on the local veterans, flattering Dumay and praising Napoleon’s soldiers with lofty rhetoric to win the provincials’ respect. He charmed the women and Modeste with talk of poetry and politics, arguing poets could become statesmen. Modeste, determined to worship him, sat as at a theater; La Brière, modest, felt eclipsed but listened to her. Canalis complimented La Brière exaggeratedly, calling him “Pylades” and praising his talents; poor Ernest, flattered, took it as a balm.

The visit ended with an invitation to dinner. Modeste and the mothers debated whether Canalis was rich, and whether modish fame or real fortune mattered. The colonel suggested Modeste might appear in the splendor of an heiress if she ordered her Paris wardrobe. Many of the household, including Gobenheim, quipped about marriages and disguises. They expected more visitors soon.

At night on the seashore, La Brière brooded on his hopeless love. Butscha, the hunchbacked clerk and unofficial guardian of Modeste’s reputation, revealed himself and spoke with surprising eloquence about devotion and study of women. He claimed to be one of three pledged to serve Modeste and to guard her happiness. He knew the local intrigues, the duchess’s vindictiveness, and the valet Germain’s ties; Butscha promised to watch Canalis and to see whether the great poet could love truly. La Brière, heartened by Butscha’s fidelity, treated him as a comrade. Butscha declared he studied love as a science and would soon reveal Canalis’s true character, and he intended to ensure Modeste’s happiness. La Brière resolved to make a friend of the dwarf.

The next morning the two Parisian guests rode and explored the country; both felt the strain between devotion and ambition. Canalis smugly assumed an advantage in society; Ernest resolved on stoic endurance, to love Modeste quietly and honorably even if she chose another. Germain bought flamboyant horses and the ducal and Canalis households prepared to stake their reputations.

Desplein, the famous surgeon, arrived, examining Madame Mignon’s cataract; he promised the mother she might regain sight. His brisk competence, studious manner, and a moment of gallantry toward Modeste impressed her—an encounter that revealed to her the difference between true genius and the pose of Canalis. She served his simple breakfast; he promised to help her mother see again, and his gravity and skill charmed her. The household hoped meanwhile for the forthcoming theatre of suitors, as Modeste prepared to see three men—the poet, the honest secretary, and the titled duke—determine in practice whether she would choose fame, substance, or rank.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
This visit of the great surgeon was the event of the day and left a luminous trace in Modeste’s soul. The young enthusiast admired the man whose life belonged to others and in whom long study of pain had destroyed manifestations of egoism. That evening, with Gobenheim, the Latournelles, Butscha, Canalis, Ernest, and the Duc d’Herouville in the salon, they all congratulated the Mignon family on the hopes Desplein encouraged. The conversation, with Modeste again predominant, turned on the man whose genius, sadly appreciable chiefly by the faculty and men of science, drew praise and a characteristic remark from Gobenheim—“He makes a mint of money.”

“They say he is very grasping,” added Canalis.

Modeste’s praise of Desplein annoyed the poet. Vanity acts like a woman; both feel defrauded when love or praise is given to another. Canalis challenged the judgment of genius.

“Do you think, monsieur,” said Modeste, smiling, “that we should judge genius by ordinary standards?”

“Perhaps we ought first to define the man of genius,” replied Canalis. “One condition is invention—form, system, force. Napoleon invented war-making; Scott, Linnaeus, Cuvier renewed arts and sciences. But Desplein merely applies known laws skillfully; he is an executive genius, like Moscheles or Paganini—performers, not creators. To one belongs the immortal crown, to the other innumerable five-franc pieces.”

Ernest de La Brière quietly opposed this exclusiveness. “Genius must be estimated by utility,” he said. “Parmentier popularized the potato, Jacquard the loom: they change a state. In that sense Desplein is a man of genius; he follows a generation of sufferers whose pains he relieves.”

Modeste opposed Ernest’s view. “If that be so, then the man who could discover a way to mow wheat without injuring the straw, by a machine that could do the work of ten men, would be a man of genius.”

“Yes, my daughter,” said Madame Mignon; “and the poor would bless him—he that is blessed by the poor is blessed of God.”

“That is putting utility above art,” Modeste objected.

“Without utility what would become of art?” said Charles Mignon. “What would support it? You must be lodged and paid.”

“Oh! my dear papa, such opinions are flat and antediluvian!” Modeste retorted, insolent. Her impertinence astonished Madame Mignon, Madame Latournelle, and Dumay. Butscha looked to Monsieur Mignon for indignation.

“A little more, young lady, and you will be wanting in respect for your father,” said the colonel, smiling.

“I am your only child,” she answered saucily.

“Child, indeed,” remarked the notary, significantly.

“Monsieur,” Modeste turned upon him, “my father is delighted to have me for his governess; he gave me life and I give him knowledge; he will soon owe me something.”

“There seems occasion for it,” said Madame Mignon.

Canalis rose, standing before the fireplace in one of his grandest attitudes, and weighed in. “God has given food and clothing to man, but not art. We need the life of the soul as much as that of the body—hence, two utilities. True, an epic does not feed like a charity kitchen. But industrial perfections will not breathe life into a people nor tell futurity of a civilization. Works of genius are the summum of civilization; they presuppose utility. Nations cherish survival beyond death; the survival of a people is the work of men of genius. France, though excelled by England in commerce, leads the world in art and taste. We shall reign longer by books than by swords. In La Brière’s system, all that is glorious and lovely would be suppressed. All useful things are ugly; a kitchen is indispensable, but you live in the salon and adorn it. There is nothing beautiful but what seems useless.”

“Rubbish! yes, that may be, but my rubbish is dear to me,” laughed the Duc d’Herouville.

Butscha attacked Canalis. “Does art exist at all? Isn’t it a splendid lie? I don’t want a landscape painting when I can see one done by God. Happiness and virtue exist above art and genius.”

“Bravo, Butscha!” cried Madame Latournelle.

Canalis’s long rhetoric lost the country-folk. Modeste’s indifference toward La Brière and disrespect for her father depressed the young man. The duke took up Butscha’s argument, declaring Saint Theresa’s ecstasies superior to Lord Byron. Modeste countered, “Hers was purely personal poetry, whereas the genius of Byron and Molière benefits the world.”

Charles Mignon teased Modeste. The exchange widened the gulf between Modeste and Ernest. La Brière’s quiet voice contrasted with the baron’s peremptory tone. Canalis asserted that every work of art implies a social utility: books employ paper-makers, printers, booksellers; a symphony or an opera needs hands and machinery. Works of genius have an expensive basis and are useful to labor. He spoke with fine metaphor and inward complacency, then returned unknowingly to La Brière’s position.

“He will make an admirable constitutional minister,” said the little duke slyly.

“Oh!” replied Canalis with a grand gesture, “what is the use of these discussions? All things are true and false according to surroundings.”

“He is a poet,” whispered Gobenheim.

Canalis, ten leagues above his audience, took the coolness as provincial ignorance, content that Modeste understood him. He spoke of the Duchesse de Chaulieu with rapture, declaring his gratitude and affection. Modeste, affected, said, “Who can think of a poet without a muse!”

“He would be without a heart,” replied Canalis.

Dumay reminded Canalis of having denied feeling to which Canalis ironically replied with a boast of loving as well as La Brière and being able to give his love literary form. His triumph led him to declaim; Modeste, swept into his labyrinth of fancy, thought, “He loves me!” Butscha noted Canalis’s declamations and inattention and later commented cruelly: Canalis’s habit of not listening often offended and wounded others. Not to listen is disrespect; Canalis often forgot to answer a speech, passing abruptly to what preoccupied him. That impertinence breeds hatred.

Modeste begged Canalis to read some poems. He took the volume and cooed an imitation of Moore’s “Loves of the Angels” titled “Vitalis,” greeted by yawns. Gobenheim’s remark about whist raised a laugh; Canalis flung the volume impatiently on a table. The incident showed the peril of a drawing-room hero stepping out of his sphere.

The whist game began with Canalis, the duke, Gobenheim, and Latournelle as partners. Modeste sat near the poet, much to Ernest’s disappointment. La Brière watched Modeste and the progress of Canalis’s fascination. He lacked the gift of seduction; there is always a comic element in the actor-poet’s art of feigning sentiment to win. Ernest imitated the duke, becoming gloomy and watchful, prey to concentrated jealousy; he could not obtain a glance from Modeste. He left the room with Butscha.

“It is all over!” he said. “She is caught by him; I am more disagreeable to her, and she is right. Canalis is charming; there’s intellect in his silence, passion in his eyes, poetry in his rhodomontades.”

“Is he an honest man?” asked Butscha.

“Oh, yes,” La Brière replied. “He is loyal and chivalrous, capable of getting rid, under Modeste’s influence, of those affectations Madame de Chaulieu taught him.”

“You are a fine fellow,” said the hunchback; “but will he love her?”

“I don’t know,” answered La Brière.

He asked whether Modeste had said anything about him. Butscha repeated Modeste’s speech about disguises. La Brière was beside himself, flung onto a bench, and wept. He described how he had tried to undeceive Modeste before she took off the mask and how her recent letter had been flattering to the individual he wished. Butscha urged him to show himself as he was and not let his rival practice his exercises. La Brière lamented his inability to speak when she was present, the strange physical sensations her looks caused.

At this moment Modeste, followed by Canalis who had lost the rubber, came out with her father and Madame Dumay to breathe the starry night. La Brière left. The next day Modeste, favoring Canalis, walked alone with him across the gravel. The d’Herouville ladies interrupted, turning conversation to court appointments and the distinction of rank: “To have a duke for a son,” said one, “is a fortune.” Canalis, displeased, asked why the duke had had little success despite title; the ladies shot him venomous glances. Ten days later the tide at the Chalet turned against Canalis. The poet’s prodigality of wit tired those preferring the solid; forced to behave like an ordinary man, his faults were exposed. La Brière, by contrast, was sober, discreet, and gaining ground: modesty, simplicity, and utility won favor.

Canalis, impulsive and ambitious, displayed impatience, depression, and capricious moods noted by Madame Latournelle and others. The attention shifted to La Brière. For two days the poet worked to fascinate Modeste alone, weaving passionate language. The d’Herouville ladies slandered him. At dinner the duke proposed arranging a hunt in a crown forest; glances among the d’Herouville group said, “The heiress is ours!” The poet, sensing danger, resolved to get a pledge then and there. Modeste, half-frightened, hinted at the calumnies to him and begged him to be silent; he promised.

“These stabs of the tongue are fair in the great world,” he said. “They shock you; I laugh at them; I am pleased. These ladies must feel the duke’s interests are in peril.”

Canalis entered his defence with warmth and passion and declared himself plainly with vows and protestations. Modeste listened.

“Monsieur,” she said, “freedom granted me by my parents has allowed me to listen to you; but you must address them.”

“But,” cried Canalis, “tell me that if I obtain their consent, you will ask nothing better than to obey them.”

“I know beforehand,” she replied, “my father has fancies that may wound your family’s pride. He wishes his name borne by grandsons.”

“Ah, dear Modeste, what sacrifices would I not make to commit my life to an angel like you.”

“You will permit me not to decide in a moment the fate of my whole life,” she said, turning to rejoin the d’Herouville ladies.

Butscha waylaid Modeste at the portico. “Mademoiselle, I do hope you don’t call him Melchior.”

“I’m very near it, my Black Dwarf,” she said sweetly.

“Good God!” exclaimed Butscha.

“You?” she said, “would your La Brière accept me without a fortune?”

“Ask your father,” replied Butscha. “I am ready to give not only my life but my honor for you. Has that sublime Canalis made the disinterested offer you flung as a reproach at poor Ernest?”

“Yes.”

“Do you believe it?”

“That question undervalues the strength of my self-love.”

“You are laughing, mademoiselle; then there’s no danger: I hope you are only making a fool of him.”

“Pray what would you think of me, Monsieur Butscha, if I allowed myself to make fun of those who do me the honor to wish to marry me? Even if a girl affects to despise the most despicable attentions, she is flattered by them.”

“Then I flatter you?” he said, illuminated.

“You give me the most precious friendship—a feeling as disinterested as a mother’s for a child. Compare yourself to no one; even my father is obliged to be devoted to me.” She paused. “I cannot say I love you, in the sense men give the word, but what I give you is eternal.”

“Then,” said Butscha, bowing, “suffer me to watch over you as a dragon guards a treasure. The poet covered you with lace-work and tinsel of his promises. If, as soon as he finds your small fortune, he changes, will you still marry him? shall you still esteem him?”

“He would be another Francisque Althor,” she said bitterly.

“Make him play that pretty little comedy, and—”

“That I will! he shall play it through and through within three days—on Wednesday. Until then amuse yourself with the little tunes of the lyre so the discords may come out more distinctly.”

Modeste returned to the salon where La Brière, sitting by the window, rose respectfully as she approached. He followed her with a furtive look; she sat by Canalis, who would have looked at her more boldly. La Brière and Butscha paced the terrace.

“It is nine o’clock,” cried Ernest. “I shall start for Paris; I can get there by tomorrow morning. From you she will accept anything; let me give her a riding-whip in your name.”

“Ah, you are happy,” said Butscha ruefully; “you have money!”

He rode to Paris, secured a place on a mail-coach back, and bought a gold fox-head whip-handle with a ruby—seven thousand francs—engraved with La Bastie’s arms and placed in a morocco case stamped with interlaced M.’s. He returned Wednesday morning to breakfast with Canalis. Butscha met him and instructed Germain the valet to put the box on Modeste’s dressing-table.

Canalis and Germain spoke apart. Germain reported gossip from the quays and fishermen indicating the brig connected with Monsieur Mignon had been sold to another’s account, and the cargo was worth only three or four hundred thousand francs at most. Canalis served champagne and took the gossip lightly. Butscha, hidden prompter, requested the secretary to say nothing about his trip and not to interfere. He had exploited an unfavorable feeling in Havre about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune by urging fishermen to speak ill; they obliged. Butscha was a lively slanderer, and over wine he poured forth scandalous gossip. He declared Modeste might have two or three hundred thousand in ready money and that Dumay’s property might add to her fortune—perhaps six hundred thousand francs when the old soldiers died.

This coarse honesty sobered Canalis. To Butscha such a sum seemed colossal. La Brière, bound by a secrecy oath to the colonel, had not meant to disclose the earlier rumor to Canalis. Pressed, Ernest revealed that Colonel Mignon had told him in Paris he did not possess the colossal fortune spoken of; he intended to give two hundred thousand francs to his daughter in ready money, entailing his estates for descendants to retain the title. Canalis, who had expected to marry with a fortune to equal his own, was shaken.

“Will you promise me on your word of honor to forget what I tell you and to behave to me as though this confidence had never been made?” Ernest asked.

“I’ll swear that,” Canalis answered.

Butscha dozed; Canalis brooded: “Fool! then take her,” he said, “and break your oath.” But he feared losing both love and office. Yet Butscha urged directness: go speak frankly to Colonel Mignon.

Canalis rose to dress for the excursion. Butscha, pursued by servants’ laughter, took a kiosk and wallowed in triumph at having fooled a great man.

Modeste appeared in a bottle-green habit, showing the gifted whip. “Did it come from you, Monsieur le duc?” she said, holding the handle toward him. “There was a card saying ‘Guess if you can.’ Françoise and Dumay credit Butscha; my father sent his with a turquoise top.”

“I would give ten years of my old age,” said the duke, “to offer you that jewel.”

“Ah, here comes the audacious giver!” cried Modeste as Canalis rode up. “La Brière rode at full gallop from Havre?”

“Does your secretary take such liberties?” she asked, turning pale, and threw the whip to Françoise with scorn. “Give me your whip, papa.”

“Poor Ernest, half-dead with fatigue!” said Canalis, overtaking her. “You are pitiless, mademoiselle. The poor fellow said, ‘I have but this one chance to remain in her memory.’”

“And should you think well of a woman who could take presents from half the parish?” she returned.

She received no answer; Canalis’s inattention attributed to horse’s feet. His admiration seemed too intense to be natural. At a cross-road overlooking the Seine, Canalis let Modeste and the duke pass, then joined the colonel.

“Monsieur le comte,” he said, “you are an open-hearted soldier. I am twenty-nine, without landed estates, full of ambition. Mademoiselle Modeste pleases me. The question of money is of the utmost importance. I have about thirty thousand francs a year; if my wife brought me as much more, I should be about equal. Shall you give Mademoiselle a million?”

“We have not reached that point,” said the colonel cautiously.

“Then suppose we go no further. Give me your word of honor to say nothing, even to Mademoiselle Modeste, because my circumstances may change and I may ask for her without dowry.”

“I promise,” said the colonel. “People exaggerate fortunes. I await accounts of my agents; the sale of my merchandise and ship is not yet concluded. I did say to La Brière in Paris I would guarantee two hundred thousand francs in ready money. I wish to entail my estates and enable grandchildren to inherit my arms and title.”

Canalis barely listened. They reached a table-land with a view of the Seine valley. “Butscha was right; God is the greatest landscape painter,” he said.

Modeste remarked on fictitious wants and vanities; Canalis called it a bucolic written on tablets of gold. The colonel invited the duke and Canalis to dine in riding-dress. That evening Modeste gave La Brière’s whip back: “Please ask Monsieur de La Brière to keep it until you know whether we are rich enough to buy it back.” The colonel kissed her forehead approvingly.

Canalis, alarmed at the indecency of his earlier conduct and its consequences, tried to excuse himself to Modeste, pleading jealousy and the delicacy of a poet’s sensibility. “I attach more value to words and looks than ordinary men,” he said, “and demand infantile security.” Modeste countered, calling his demand excessive and resenting his mention of the duchess. “I admire you, my dear poet,” she said lightly, but rebuked his presumption. Their intercourse cooled; Canalis left early, and the field opened to the duke. Butscha, walking with Madame Latournelle, whispered in Modeste’s ear, “Was I right?”

“Alas, yes,” she answered. “Anger got the better of me.”

“Well, so much the better. When you are both so angry you can’t speak, I will make him desperately in love until you are deceived yourself.”

“Come, Butscha; he is a great poet; he is a gentleman; he is a man of intellect.”

“Your father’s millions are more to him than all that,” answered Butscha.

“Eight millions!” cried Modeste.

“My master, who sold his practice, is going to Provence to attend to buying lands your father’s agent suggested. The purchase is four millions; your father has agreed. You are to have a dot of two millions and another million for a Paris establishment. Count up.”

“If it hadn’t been for that comedian Canalis you would have kept his whip thinking it came from me,” said Butscha, advocating La Brière.

Modesty’s reaction sealed matters. Canalis, sleepless, composed a letter to the Duchesse de Chaulieu explaining his absence and his interference on La Brière’s behalf. He described Mademoiselle Modeste as “a rather pale, insignificant, thread-papery little thing” who liked literature and had coquetted with the duchess’s Melchior. He asked Eleonore to verify facts through banker Mongenod about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune and to help La Brière by transmission of a father-in-law’s title. His confidential reminiscences were too intimate.

Meanwhile Butscha, under the name Jean Jacmin, had received and replied to a letter from his fictitious cousin Philoxene, and the duchess, wounded by Melchior’s silence, dictated and read replies and then summoned Mongenod to inquire about Monsieur Mignon’s fortune. Finding evidence that Modeste had wealth, Eleonore—who had felt betrayed and angry—quickly changed to generosity after reading Canalis’s letter: if he had not been faithless, she would give him the girl and her millions—provided Mademoiselle Mignon was as ugly as Canalis had written.

The duke arrived. The duchess, eager, wrote to Diane de Maufrigneuse and to Mademoiselle d’Herouville to arrange an invitation and hinted to secure the hunt. Eleonore sent Canalis a sharp answer:

My dear poet,—Mademoiselle de La Bastie is very beautiful; Mongenod has proved she has millions. I thought of marrying you to her; I am displeased at your want of confidence. You are a child. The duke thinks you less than a gentleman, reflecting on your mother’s honor. Now I intend to see things for myself and will manage to have you invited to Rosembray.

Canalis flung the letter at Ernest across breakfast. “That’s the two thousandth love-letter I have had from that woman,” he cried. “Marry, try your luck! The worst marriage is better than this halter.”

Caught between two lies, Canalis was at a loss. “Play by rule, and you lose!” he muttered, then resolved that if he must retire, his ten years of fidelity to Eleonore would ensure some good match. The hunt would unite passions and prove a truce among adversaries. Canalis wished to appear courteous and simply charming; he talked finances with Gobenheim and war with the colonel, biasing them in favor of La Brière. The d’Herouville ladies continued their intrigues to secure Modeste’s dot. Invitations from the Duc de Verneuil and the Master of the Hunt extended to the Basties to stay at Rosembray for a grand hunt.

La Brière, full of dark presentiments, craved Modeste’s presence. The duke arrived to arrange Modeste’s departure; the colonel invited the suitors to breakfast the morning of the start. Canalis planned to reconquer Modeste and throw over the duchess, La Brière had resolved to bid eternal farewell, and each suitor prepared a last plea. The colonel pressed Modeste for decision.

“Our position with the d’Herouville family will be intolerable at Rosembray,” he said. “Do you mean to be a duchess?”

“No, father,” she answered.

“Then do you love Canalis?”

“No, papa, a thousand times no!” she exclaimed; the colonel rejoiced.

“Ah, I chose in Paris the man of fixed principles and sound moralities,” he said slyly.

“I was not even thinking of him, papa. Please leave me liberty to refuse the duke myself; after a glimpse of court life at Rosembray I will tell you my secret.”

“Monsieur de La Brière,” cried the colonel, “I hope you are going to this hunt?”

“No, colonel, I have come to take leave; I return to Paris—”

“You have no curiosity,” said Modeste.

“If that is all, you must stay to please me; I wish it,” said the colonel, moving away to meet Canalis and leaving daughter and suitor together.

“Mademoiselle,” said La Brière, raising his eyes, “let me carry away with me your forgiveness. My life can never be happy; it will be full of remorse—”

“Before we part forever,” said Modeste interrupting, “I wish to ask you one thing; though you once disguised yourself, I think you cannot be so base as to deceive me now. Will you be frank?”

La Brière related the scene in the poet’s study and the challenge that followed. “You have said enough,” said Modeste, restraining emotion. “If you have not my heart, monsieur, you have at least my esteem.” These words gave him a violent shock; staggering, he leaned against a tree. Modeste left him but returned, taking his hand. “What is the matter?” she asked.

“Forgive me—I thought you despised me.”

“But,” she answered, distant, “I did not say that I loved you.”

She left him. Yet the esteem she’d given filled him with joy. Modeste saw in La Brière’s sensitivity the possibility of a love that might fulfill the ideal every woman hopes for.

Canalis approached Modeste later. “In spite of the little value you attach to my sentiments, my honor is concerned in effacing a stain under which I have suffered,” he said, and let her read lines from the duchess’s letter that proved Mongenod’s inquiry and intention to marry Modeste to La Brière—he tore that passage from the letter and gave the fragment to Modeste. “I cannot let you read the rest,” he added; “but a girl who could accuse me of ignoble motives is capable of suspecting collusion. Ah, Modeste,” he added with tears, “your poet has as much poetry in his heart as in his mind. Suspend judgment until you have seen the duchess.”

“Oh, dear!” she said; “it seems they are all angels—and not marriageable; the duke is the only one that belongs to humanity.”

Butscha appeared with a parcel. He had been busy with Spanish bits and had shown a piece to the colonel, who thanked him generously. The dwarf departed like a man who’d heard of a rich uncle.

“My dear father,” Modeste said to the colonel in the salon, “I would like that beautiful whip—suppose you ask Monsieur de La Brière to exchange it for your picture by Van Ostade.” She looked furtively at Ernest as the colonel made the proposition; she thought, “He will be at the hunt.”

Curiously, Modeste’s three lovers each went to Rosembray with hearts full of hope. Rosembray—bought by the Duc de Verneuil—was a chateau of the grand order, approached by an avenue of elms, presenting a courtyard with orange trees and a façade with fluted pilasters and a pediment carved by Coysevox. Its park extended to immense ponds, gardens in the French style, and thirty acres of woodland contiguous with other forests, royal and state—a noble landscape fitting the drama to come.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Modeste’s arrival at Rosembray caused a sensation in the avenue. A carriage in France’s livery drew up, accompanied by the grand equerry, the colonel, Canalis, and La Brière on horseback, an outrider in full dress, six servants including Negroes and a mulatto, and the colonel’s britzka holding two waiting-women and luggage. The four-horse carriage had postilions arrayed with an elegance enforced by the grand equerry, who often took more care with such matters than the king. Dazzled by this magnificence and by the little Versailles before her, Modeste feared seeming awkward or parvenue as she entered; she even regretted having wished for a hunt.

As the carriage stopped, Modeste saw an old man in a blond wig frizzed into little curls, his calm, plump, smooth face wearing a fatherly smile and a monastic cheerfulness almost noble in its half-veiled glance—the Duc de Verneuil, master of Rosembray. The duchess, a woman of strict piety and the only daughter of a deceased rich chief-justice, spare and erect and mother of four, resembled Madame Latournelle if imagination lent the notary’s wife an abbatial grace.

“Ah, good morning, dear Hortense!” Mademoiselle d’Herouville cried, kissing the duchess with the sympathy that united their proud natures; “let me present to you and to the dear duke our little angel, Mademoiselle de La Bastie.”

“We have heard so much of you, mademoiselle,” said the duchess, “that we were eager to receive you.”

“And regret the time lost,” added the Duc de Verneuil with courteous admiration.

“Monsieur le Comte de La Bastie,” said the grand equerry, presenting the colonel to the duke and duchess with the air of respect due.

“I am glad to welcome you, Monsieur le comte!” said Monsieur de Verneuil. “You possess more than one treasure,” he added, looking at Modeste.

The duchess took Modeste under her arm and led her into an immense salon where a dozen women were grouped about the fireplace. The men remained with the duke on the terrace except Canalis, who respectfully made his way to the superb Éléonore. The Duchesse de Chaulieu, at an embroidery frame, was showing Mademoiselle de Verneuil how to shade a flower.

Madame de Chaulieu bestowed on Modeste a cold, haughty, contemptuous stare so sharp it seemed to prick her; for an instant Modeste saw nothing but that woman, and she saw through her. To understand such cruelty among passionate women, one must see women with each other. Modeste’s involuntary admiration would have disarmed almost anyone but Éléonore, whom she might have thought thirty-six but for other surprises.

The poet had miscalculated a great lady’s anger: the face radiant, the rest menacing. No king could match the exquisite politeness of a mistress’s cold anger when she guards it with steel. Canalis tried to cling to that steel, but his fingers slipped; his words slid off a heart armored by civility. The duchess’s gracious face and bearing hid the steel of her wrath. Modeste’s appearance—sublime beauty dressed as well as Diane de Maugrigneuse herself—had set off the train of reflection Éléonore had been composing.

All the women had gone to the windows to see the new wonder alight from the royal carriage, attended by her three suitors.

“Do not let us seem so curious,” Madame de Chaulieu had said, cut to the heart by Diane’s exclamation—“She is divine! where in the world does she come from?”—and with that the bevy flew back to their seats, resuming composure though Éléonore’s heart was full of vipers clamoring for a meal.

Mademoiselle d’Herouville murmured to the Duchesse de Verneuil, “Éléonore receives her Melchior very ungraciously.”

“The Duchesse de Maugrigneuse thinks there is a coolness between them,” said Laure de Verneuil simply—one of those words socially acceptable yet chilly, like a north wind.

“Why so?” asked Modeste of the pretty young girl who had lately left the Sacré-Cœur.

“The great poet,” said the pious duchess, signaling her daughter to be silent, “left Madame de Chaulieu without a letter for more than two weeks after he went to Havre, having said he went there for his health—”

Modeste made a hasty movement that caught the attention of Laure, Hélène, and Mademoiselle d’Herouville.

“—and during that time,” continued the devout duchess, “she was trying to secure him the command of the Legion of Honor and a post at Baden.”

“Oh, that was shameful in Canalis; he owes everything to her,” exclaimed Mademoiselle d’Herouville.

“Why did not Madame de Chaulieu come to Havre?” asked Modeste innocently.

“My dear,” said the Duchesse de Verneuil, “she would let herself be cut in little pieces without saying a word. Look at her—she is regal; her head would smile, like Mary Stuart’s, after it was cut off; in fact, she has some of that blood in her veins.”

“Did she not write to him?” asked Modeste.

“Diane tells me,” answered the duchess, prompted by a nudge from Mademoiselle d’Herouville, “that in answer to Canalis’s first letter she made a cutting reply a few days ago.”

This explanation made Modeste blush with shame for the man before her; she longed, not to crush him, but to revenge herself by one of those malicious acts sharper than a dagger’s thrust. She looked haughtily at the Duchesse de Chaulieu—

“Monsieur Melchior!” she said.

All the women sniffed the air and looked alternately at the duchess, who was speaking in an undertone to Canalis over the embroidery frame, and then at the young girl so ill brought up as to disturb a lovers’ meeting—a thing not permissible in society. Diane de Maugrigneuse nodded as if to say, “The child is in the right.” The women smiled at each other; they were enraged with a woman fifty-six and still handsome enough to take the dues of youth. Melchior looked at Modeste with feverish impatience and made the gesture of a master to a valet, while the duchess lowered her head with the movement of a lioness disturbed at a meal; her eyes fixed on the poet emitted red flames in the direction of his conscience, stabbing like epigrams, each word a triple insult.

“What, mademoiselle?” demanded the poet when Modeste repeated his name.

Forced to rise, he remained standing half-way between the embroidery frame near a window and the fireplace where Modeste sat with the Duchesse de Verneuil on a sofa. Bitter reflections assailed him: to obey Modeste would be to end forever his position with his protectress; to refuse would be to confess his slavery, lose the gains of his base maneuvering, and flout the plainest laws of decency and civility. The folly of obedience intensified the duchess’s demand. Modeste’s beauty and money thus pitted against Éléonore’s rights and influence made his hesitation as terrible as a matador’s peril in the arena. A man seldom feels such palpitations except before the green table where fortune or ruin is decided.

“Mademoiselle d’Herouville hurried me from the carriage, and I left behind me,” said Modeste to Canalis, “my handkerchief—”

Canalis shrugged significantly.

“And,” continued Modeste, taking no notice of his gesture, “I had tied into one corner of it the key of a desk which contains the fragment of an important letter; have the kindness, Monsieur Melchior, to get it for me.”

Between an angel and a tiger equally enraged, Canalis, who had turned livid, no longer hesitated—the tiger seemed least dangerous of the two—and he was about to do as he was told and commit himself irretrievably when La Brière appeared at the salon door, seeming to his anguished mind like the archangel Gabriel tumbling from heaven.

“Ernest, here, Mademoiselle de La Bastie wants you,” said the poet, hastily returning to his chair by the embroidery frame.

Ernest rushed to Modeste without bowing to any one; he saw only her, took his commission with undisguised joy, and darted from the room with the secret approbation of every woman present.

“If you speak to her, if you ever look at her, all is over between us,” the duchess had whispered to the poet, not satisfied with the dubious termination Ernest’s arrival had given the scene; “and remember, if I am not present, I leave eyes behind that will watch you.”

So saying, the duchess rose and walked toward the group around Diane de Maugrigneuse, stepping daintily as a deer. Though of medium height and a little too stout—like many over fifty who retain beauty—she carried herself with the exquisite delicacy born of a vigorous nervous system controlling and vitalizing the flesh. None but women of long descent know, as Éléonore did, how to be majestic despite a buxom tendency. One might pity Philoxène while admiring the graceful lines and the minute care bestowed upon a morning dress worn with the elegance of a queen and the ease of a young girl. Her abundant hair, still undyed, was simply wound in plaits; she bared her snowy throat and shoulders and displayed the celebrated hand and arm with pardonable pride. Modeste, together with all other antagonists of the duchess, recognized in her a woman they were forced to say, “She eclipses us.” Éléonore was one of the rare grandes dames: to attempt to analyze what made her august—the carriage of the head, the curve of the throat, the harmony of movement, the nobility of bearing and the arts that had become second nature—would be to attempt the sublime; people feel it rather than explain it.

Madame de Chaulieu bowed in salutation to Hélène and her aunt; then, in a pure, even tone without a trace of emotion, “Is it not time to dress, duchess?” she made her exit accompanied by her daughter-in-law and Mademoiselle d’Herouville. As she left, she spoke in an undertone to the old maid, who pressed her arm, saying, “You are charming,”—which meant, “I am all gratitude for the service you have just done us.” After that, Mademoiselle d’Herouville returned to the salon to play her part of spy, and her first glance apprised Canalis that the duchess had made him no empty threat. The apprentice in diplomacy became aware that his science was insufficient for such a struggle, and his wit served him to take a more honest position, if not a worthier one.

When Ernest returned bringing Modeste’s handkerchief, the poet seized his arm and took him out on the terrace.

“My dear friend,” he said, “I am not only the most unfortunate man in the world, but I am also the most ridiculous; and I come to you to get me out of the hornet’s nest into which I have run myself. Modeste is a demon; she sees my difficulty and she laughs at it; she has just spoken to me of a fragment of a letter of Madame de Chaulieu, which I had the folly to give her; if she shows it I can never make my peace with Éléonore. Therefore, will you at once ask Modeste to send me back that paper, and tell her, from me, that I make no pretensions to her hand. Say I count upon her delicacy, upon her propriety as a young girl, to behave to me as if we had never known each other. I beg her not to speak to me; I implore her to treat me harshly—though I hardly dare ask her to feign a jealous anger, which would help my interests amazingly. Go, I will wait here for an answer.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On re-entering the salon, Ernest de La Briere found the Vicomte de Serizy, a young officer from the Havre guard who had come from Rosny to announce that Madame must be present at the opening of the Chambers. The importance of this constitutional solemnity—when Charles X delivered his speech surrounded by the royal family, Madame la Dauphine and Madame in their gallery—made the choice of emissary a mark of attention to Diane, adored by the charming Vicomte, son of a minister and heir to an immense fortune. The Duchesse de Maufrigneuse tolerated his attentions partly to show up Madame de Serizy’s age; whispered chronicles hinted she had once deprived Lucien de Rubempre of his heart.

“You will do us the pleasure, I hope, to remain at Rosembray,” the severe duchess said to the young officer.

While scandal was discussed, the devout lady shut her eyes to the duke’s carefully chosen guests; these excellent women tolerated much under the pretense of reclaiming lost sheep. “We reckoned without our constitutional government,” said the grand equerry; “and Rosembray, Madame la duchesse, will lose a great honor.”

“We shall be more at our ease,” said a tall thin old man of about seventy-five, dressed in blue cloth and wearing his hunting-cap by permission of the ladies. Resembling the Duc de Bourbon, he was the Prince de Cadignan, Master of the Hunt, one of the last great French lords. Just then La Briere tried to slip behind the sofa to speak with Modeste, and a man of thirty-eight, short, fat, and common in appearance, entered the room.

“My son, the Prince de Loudon,” said the Duchesse de Verneuil to Modeste, who could not restrain her amazement at seeing a historic name attached to such a commonplace figure.

“Gaspard,” called the duchess; he came at once, and she motioned to Modeste, “Mademoiselle de La Bastie, my friend.”

The heir presumptive, whose marriage with Desplein’s only daughter had lately been arranged, bowed without appearing struck, as his father had been, by Modeste’s beauty. She compared today’s youth with the old age of a past epoch; the old Prince de Cadignan had shown a reverence that made her feel he rendered homage to womanhood as much as to royalty. The Duc de Rhetore, eldest son of the Duchesse de Chaulieu, bowing rather cavalierly, illustrated the contrast between fathers and sons—young men no longer felt themselves great beings and dispensed with the duties of greatness; the fathers retained the politeness of vanished grandeur, like mountain-tops still gilded by the sun when all is twilight in the valley.

Ernest at last slipped a word into Modeste’s ear, and she rose immediately.

“My dear,” thought the duchesse, assuming Modeste was going to dress, and pulled a bell-rope, “they shall show you your apartment.”

Ernest accompanied Modeste to the foot of the grand staircase and presented the plea of the luckless poet, trying to touch her feelings by describing Melchior’s agony.

“You see, he loves—he is a captive who thought he could break his chain.”

“Love in such a rapid seeker after fortune!” retorted Modeste.

“Mademoiselle, you are at the entrance of life; you do not know its defiles. The inconsistencies of a man who falls under the dominion of a woman much older than himself should be forgiven; he is really not accountable. Think how many sacrifices Canalis has made to her. He has sown too much seed of that kind to resign the harvest; the duchess represents to him ten years of devotion and happiness. You made him forget all that, and unfortunately, he has more vanity than pride; he did not reflect on what he was losing until he met Madame Chaulieu here today. If you really understood him, you would help him. He is a child, always mismanaging his life. You call him a seeker after fortune, but he seeks very badly; like all poets, he is a victim of sensations—childish, easily dazzled by anything that shines, and pursuing its glitter. He used to love horses and pictures, and he craved fame—well, he sold his pictures to buy armor and old Renaissance and Louis XV furniture; just now he is seeking political power. Admit that his hobbies are noble things.”

“You have said enough,” replied Modeste; she went on to her father with a motion of the head and took his arm. “Come with me, and I will give you that scrap of paper; you shall carry it to the great man and assure him of my condescension to his wishes, but on one condition—you must thank him in my name for the pleasure I have taken in seeing one of the finest German plays performed in my honor. I have learned that Goethe’s masterpiece is neither Faust nor Egmont—” and, seeing Ernest’s puzzled look, she added, “It is Torquato Tasso! Tell Monsieur de Canalis to re-read it.” She smiled. “Repeat to your friend word for word what I say; it is not an epigram, it is the justification of his conduct—with this difference, that he will, I trust, become more and more reasonable, thanks to the folly of his Eleonore.”

The duchess’s head-woman conducted Modeste and her father to their apartment, where Françoise Cochet had already put everything in order; the colonel was astonished when he heard there were thirty other apartments in the château decorated with the same taste.

“This is what I call a proper country-house,” said Modeste.

“The Comte de La Bastie must build you one like it,” replied her father.

“Here, monsieur,” said Modeste, giving the bit of paper to Ernest; “carry it to our friend and put him out of his misery.”

The phrase our friend struck the young man’s heart. He looked at Modeste to see if there was any real community of interests in her admission; she, understanding his look, added, “Come, go at once, your friend is waiting.”

La Briere colored excessively, and left the room in a state of doubt and anxiety less endurable than despair. For the true lover the path that approaches happiness is like the narrow way Catholic poetry calls the entrance to Paradise—a dark passage echoing with the last cries of earthly anguish.

An hour later the illustrious company was assembled in the salon; some played whist, others conversed; the women had their embroideries, and all awaited the announcement of dinner. The Prince de Cadignan drew Monsieur Mignon out upon China and his campaigns under the Empire, making him talk about the Portendueres, the L’Estorades, and the Maucombes, Provencal families; he blamed him for not seeking service and assured him nothing would be easier than to restore him to his rank as colonel of the Guard.

“A man of your birth and your fortune ought not to belong to the present Opposition,” said the prince, smiling.

This society of distinguished persons not only pleased Modeste but enabled her, during her stay, to acquire a perfection of manners she might otherwise have lacked. Seeing these women was an education; Modeste had the instinct to appropriate the distinctive qualities of Madame de Maufrigneuse and Madame de Chaulieu. Whereas a bourgeois would have ridiculed their ways or made them absurd by clumsy imitation, a well-born, well-educated young woman like Modeste fell naturally into connection with these people and saw at once the differences separating the aristocratic world from the bourgeois world, the provinces from the Faubourg Saint-Germain. She caught almost imperceptible shadings and perceived the grace of the grande dame without doubting she could acquire it. She noticed also that her father and La Briere appeared infinitely better in this Olympus than Canalis. The great poet, abdicating his real power—the mind—became nothing more than a courtier seeking a ministry, intriguing for an order, flattering the galaxy. Ernest de La Briere, without ambitions, could be himself; while Melchior became, to use a vulgar expression, a mere toady, courting the Prince de Loudon, the Duc de Rhetore, the Vicomte de Serizy, or the Duc de Maufrigneuse, like a man not free to assert himself, as Colonel Mignon did, proud of his campaigns and of Napoleon’s confidence. Modeste noted the strained efforts of the man of real talent seeking witticisms or compliments to please these grand personages; in her eyes the peacock had plucked out his tail-feathers.

Toward the middle of the evening the young girl sat down with the grand equerry in a corner of the salon. She led him there purposely to end a suit she could no longer encourage if she wished to retain her self-respect.

“Monsieur le duc, if you really knew me,” she began, “you would understand how deeply I am touched by your attentions. It is because of the profound respect I feel for your character, and the friendship which a soul like yours inspires in mine, that I cannot endure to wound your self-love. Before your arrival in Havre I loved sincerely, deeply, and forever one who is worthy of being loved, and my affection for whom is still a secret; but I wish you to know—and in saying this I am more sincere than most young girls—that had I not already formed this voluntary attachment, you would have been my choice, for I recognize your noble and beautiful qualities. A few words your aunt and sister have said to me as to your intentions lead me to make this frank avowal. If you think it desirable, a letter from my mother shall recall me, on pretense of her illness, tomorrow morning before the hunt begins. Without your consent I do not choose to be present at a fête I owe to your kindness, and where, if my secret should escape me, you might feel hurt and defrauded. You will ask why I have come here at all. I could not withstand the invitation. Be generous enough not to reproach me for what was almost a necessary curiosity. But this is not the chief, not the most delicate thing I have to say you. You have firm friends in my father and myself—more so than perhaps you realize; and as my fortune was the first cause that brought you to me, I wish to say—but without intending to use it as a sedative to calm the grief gallantry requires you to testify—that my father has thought over the affair of the marshes, his friend Dumay thinks your project feasible, and they have already taken steps to form a company. Gobenheim, Dumay, and my father have subscribed fifteen hundred thousand francs, and undertake to get the rest from capitalists who will feel it in their interest to take up the matter. If I have not the honor of becoming the Duchesse d’Herouville, I have almost the certainty of enabling you to choose her, free from all trammels in your choice, and in a higher sphere than mine. Oh! let me finish,” she cried, at a gesture from the duke.

“Judging by my nephew’s emotion,” whispered Mademoiselle d’Herouville to her niece, “it is easy to see you have a sister.”

“Monsieur le duc, all this was settled in my mind the day of our first ride, when I heard you deplore your situation. This is what I have wished to say to you. That day determined my future life. Though you did not make the conquest of a woman, you have at least gained faithful friends at Ingouville—if you will deign to accord us that title.”

Her little discourse, carefully thought over, was said with so much charm of soul that tears came to the grand equerry’s eyes; he seized her hand and kissed it.

“Stay during the hunt,” he said; “my want of merit has accustomed me to these refusals; but while accepting your friendship and that of the colonel, you must let me satisfy myself by the judgment of competent scientific men, that the draining of those marshes will be no risk to the company you speak of, before I agree to the generous offer of your friends. You are a noble girl, and though my heart aches to think I can only be your friend, I will glory in that title, and prove it to you at all times and in all seasons.”

“In that case, Monsieur le duc, let us keep our secret. My choice will not be known, at least I think not, until after my mother’s complete recovery. I should like our first blessing to come from her eyes.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Ladies,” said the Prince de Cadignan, as the guests prepared to separate for the night, “I know that several of you propose to follow the hounds with us tomorrow, and it becomes my duty to tell you that if you will be Dianas you must rise, like Diana, with the dawn. The meet is for half-past eight o’clock. I have seen many women display greater courage than men, but for a few seconds only; and you will need a strong dose of resolution to keep you on horseback the whole day, except for a halt for breakfast, which we shall take, like true hunters and huntresses, on the nail. Are you still determined to show yourselves trained horse-women?”

“Prince, it is necessary for me to do so,” said Modeste, adroitly.

“I answer for myself,” said the Duchesse de Chaulieu.

“And I for my daughter Diane; she is worthy of her name,” added the prince. “So, then, you all persist in your intentions? However, I shall arrange, for the sake of Madame and Mademoiselle de Verneuil and others who stay at home, to drive the stag to the further end of the pond.”

“Make yourself quite easy, mesdames,” said the Prince de Loudon, when the Royal Huntsman had left the room; “that breakfast ‘on the nail’ will take place under a comfortable tent.”

The next day, at dawn, the weather promised a fine hunt: the sky veiled with light gray vapor showed patches of blue the northwest wind would likely clear. As the party left the chateau, the Master of the Hunt, the Duc de Rhetore, and the Prince de Loudon rode in front, noting the chateau’s white mass against autumn foliage.

“The ladies are fortunate in their weather,” remarked the Duc de Rhetore.

“Oh, in spite of all their boasting,” replied the Prince de Cadignan, “I think they will let us hunt without them!”

“So they might, if each had not a squire,” said the duke.

Their attention was drawn by a loud altercation as they reached the open at the forest of Rosembray, the meet. The cause was plain: the Prince de Loudon, faddish for English ways, had brought his own British hunting establishment and placed it under the Master of the Hunt. One of his men, a small fair Englishman—insolent, phlegmatic, neatly dressed—had posted himself on one side. John Barry wore a scarlet frock with buttons bearing the De Verneuil arms, white breeches, top-boots, a striped waistcoat, and carried a small hunting-whip and brass horn. He was first whipper-in to fifteen horses and sixty English hounds, which had cost the Duc de Verneuil much.

John found himself forestalled by three other whippers-in in charge of two royal packs brought in carts. They were the three best huntsmen of the Prince de Cadignan, a marked contrast to the Englishman in character and costume. Each wore a broad three-cornered hat above a swarthy, wrinkled face; lean, sinewy, vigorous—men for whom sport was a passion. They bore immense horns of Dampierre, wound with green worsted, but chiefly controlled their hounds by eye and voice. Those noble animals, marked white, black, or liver, had distinct looks; some young ones yelped impatiently, the old scarred hounds lay quiet with ears to the earth.

As the Englishman came up, the royal dogs and huntsmen looked at each other as though to say, “If we cannot hunt by ourselves his Majesty’s service is insulted.”

What began as jests grew fiercer between Monsieur Jacquin La Roulie, the old French whipper-in, and John Barry. The two princes guessed the subject from afar, and the Master of the Hunt, setting spurs to his horse, brought it to an end with authority.

“Who drew the wood?”

“I, monseigneur,” said the Englishman.

“Very good,” said the Prince de Cadignan, proceeding to take Barry’s report.

Dogs and men became silent and respectful before the Royal Huntsman, as all recognized his dignity. The prince laid out the day’s work; a hunt is like a battle, and the Master of Charles X.’s hounds was their Napoleon. Thanks to the admirable system he had introduced into French venery, he could concentrate on the science and strategy of it. He assigned driving the stag to water to the Prince de Loudon’s establishment, expecting the royal hounds to send the stag into the Crown forest on the horizon before the chateau. He soothed his old huntsmen’s pride by giving them the most arduous task, while employing the Englishman at his specialty, letting the two systems face each other and show their strengths.

“Does monseigneur wish us to wait any longer?” said La Roulie.

“I know what you mean, old friend,” said the prince. “It is late, but—”

“Here come the ladies,” said the second whipper-in.

At that moment sixteen riders approached, four ladies at the front with green veils. Modeste, accompanied by her father the grand equerry and La Briere, rode in advance beside the Duchesse de Maufrigneuse, escorted by the Vicomte de Serizy. Behind them rode the Duchesse de Chaulieu, flanked by Canalis, on whom she smiled without rancor. When they reached the open where the huntsmen in red coats and brass bugles, surrounded by hounds, made a tableau, the Duchesse de Chaulieu, despite her embonpoint, rode up to Modeste, deeming it more dignified than avoiding the young woman she'd ignored the evening before.

When the Master of the Hunt finished his compliments on their punctuality, Eleonore observed the magnificent whip glittering in Modeste’s little hand and graciously asked to see it.

“I have never seen anything more beautiful,” she said, showing it to Diane de Maufrigneuse. “It suits its possessor,” she added, returning it.

“You must admit, Madame la duchesse,” answered Mademoiselle de La Bastie, with a tender malicious glance at La Briere, “that it is a rather strange gift from a future husband.”

“I should take it,” said Madame de Maufrigneuse, “as a declaration of my rights, in remembrance of Louis XIV.”

La Briere’s eyes filled; for a moment he dropped his reins; a second glance from Modeste bade him not betray his happiness. The hunt began.

The Duc d’Herouville said in a low voice to his fortunate rival, “Monsieur, I hope you will make your wife happy; if I can be of use, command me—I should be glad to contribute to the happiness of so charming a pair.”

This great day, on which such vast interests of heart and fortune were decided, caused but one anxiety to the Master of the Hunt—whether the stag would cross the pond and be killed on the lawn before the house; huntsmen of his calibre are chess-players who can foresee checkmate. The happy old man attained his wish; the run was magnificent, and the ladies were freed from his attendance for the hunt two days later—which, however, proved rainy.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The Duc de Verneuil’s guests stayed five days at Rosembray. On the last day the Gazette de France announced Baron de Canalis’s appointment as commander of the Legion of Honor and minister at Carlsruhe.

Early in December, after Desplein’s operation, Madame de La Bastie regained her sight; seeing Ernest de La Briere, she pressed Modeste’s hand and whispered, “I should have chosen him myself.”

By late February Latournelle had signed the Provence deeds, and the La Bastie family obtained the king’s signature to the marriage contract and the ordinance transmitting their title and arms to La Briere, who then took the name La Briere‑La Bastie. Letters‑patent entailed the La Bastie estate at the end of April. La Briere’s witnesses at his marriage were Canalis and the minister he had served five years as secretary; the bride’s were the Duc d’Herouville and Desplein, whom the Mignons remembered with gratitude.

Later in this history we shall again meet Monsieur and Madame de La Briere‑La Bastie; readers will see how sweet and easy married life with an educated, intelligent woman can be. Modeste, avoiding pedantry, is the pride and happiness of her husband, family, and all who surround her.

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

BEATRIX

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

NOTE

Balzac, who examined character and life's small circumstances, was seldom accused of portraying real people in his many portraits. In “The Great Man of the Provinces in Paris” some likenesses were guessed: Jules Janin in Etienne Lousteau, Armand Carrel in Michel Chrestien, perhaps Berryer in Daniel d’Arthez. In this volume, “Beatrix,” however, he used traits of known persons, acknowledged at publication. Mademoiselle des Touches (Camille Maupin) reflects George Sand in character; the description sometimes applied to Mademoiselle Georges is recognizable from Couture’s drawing. Beatrix, Conti, and Claude Vignon sketch the Comtesse d’Agoult, Liszt, and the critic Gustave Planche.

The opening scene, showing manners and customs of an old Breton family and the transition of the vieille roche into present ideas, is among Balzac’s notable pictures in the “Comedy of Human Life.”

K.P.W.

BEATRIX

I. A BRETON TOWN AND MANSION

France contains towns, especially in Brittany, that remain outside the currents of the nineteenth century. Poor roads and irregular communication with Paris make new civilization something to watch rather than embrace. These places keep old manners: a moral archaeologist observing people rather than ruins would find images of Louis XV’s time in many Provençal villages, Louis XIV’s in Poitou, and older types in Brittany. Most such towns once belonged to small feudal states—counties or duchies absorbed by the crown or divided when male lines failed. Disinherited from active life, these heads became arms; arms without nourishment wither.

In the last thirty years these relics have begun to fade. Modern industry, serving masses, destroys creations of old art, once intimate to the consumer; today we have products, not works. Public buildings remain retrospective; monuments of modern industry are quarries, saltpetre mines, cotton factories. Soon even these old cities will be transformed and preserved only in prints.

One town preserving the feudal type most accurately is Guerande. The name evokes memories for artists who have visited its slopes—this splendid jewel of feudality commands tides and dunes, forming a triangle with Croisic and Batz. Few places, except Vitre and Avignon, keep a medieval aspect so intact.

Guerande is still circled with stout walls; moats are full, battlements entire, loopholes free of vegetation; ivy has never mantled the towers. Three gates with portcullis rings admit entrance by an iron-clamped drawbridge that, though never raised now, could be. The mayoralty was blamed in 1820 for planting poplars along the moat to shade the promenade, excusing it by noting the esplanade had become a mall a century earlier.

The houses keep their old frontages; none have added stories or been plastered. Some rest on wooden columns forming arcades beneath which pedestrians circulate; floor planks bend but do not break. Merchant houses are low, fronts veneered with slate. Decaying wood appears in carved window-casings and pillars, with grotesque faces and fantastic beasts—relics that resist change and present dusky tones painters prize.

The streets are as they were four hundred years ago, save that population no longer swarms. Social movement is so dead a traveler may walk alone through deserted streets where mullioned windows are boarded up to avoid the window-tax. A postern flanked by a masonry wall opens onto luxuriant vegetation, one of the richest in France. A painter might sit under the arch where the city’s life is silent and the landscape seen through casemate loop-holes like belvedere sashes.

Every step recalls habits and usages of long-past times; medieval ideas and superstitions remain. A gendarme’s silver-laced hat is an anachronism; modern objects are rare. Contemporary clothing is scarce and adapted to immutable customs and physiognomies. The public square is filled with Breton costumes that artists draw: the white linen of paludiers (salt‑gatherers) contrasts with peasants’ blues and browns and the sacredly preserved jewelry of women. These classes and sailors remain as distinct as castes, clearly marked; the revolutionary ideal found the masses too rugged to plane. Even after 1830, Guerande remained apart—essentially Breton, fervently Catholic, silent and self-contained—where modern ideas advanced little.

Geography explains this. The town overlooks salt‑marshes producing Guerande salt, credited by Bretons with making excellent butter and sardines. It is connected by two roads only: one from Savenay to Saint‑Nazaire; another from Vannes linking with Morbihan. Land communication beyond these is rare; from Saint‑Nazaire the sea links it with Nantes. Between this village and Guerande are eighteen miles the mail-coach does not serve; hardly three coach passengers a year travel it.

These obstacles deter travelers. Government moves slowly; inhabitants endure the separation. Guerande, at the continent’s extreme end, leads nowhere, and no one comes. Glad to be ignored, she cares for herself. The immense product of her salt‑marshes, which pays a tax of not less than a million to the Treasury, is chiefly managed at Croisic, a peninsula communicating with Guerande over quicksands that erase tracks at night, and by boats crossing the sea arm that makes Croisic’s port.

This town is the Herculaneum of feudality—intact rather than buried. If you reach Guerande from Croisic after crossing dreary salt‑marshes, you will feel a strong sensation at sight of that vast fortification. From Saint‑Nazaire its picturesque position and naive grace please no less. The surrounding country is ravishing: hedges full of flowers, honeysuckles, roses, box, and enchanting plants; it resembles an English garden. This rich nature, untrodden with violets or lilies in a forest glade, is framed by an African-like desert banked by the ocean—a barren where on sunny days paludiers, clothed in white and scattered among melancholy swamps, recall Arabs in burrows.

Thus Guerande bears no resemblance to any other French place. The town produces upon the mind the effect of a sleeping‑draught; it is silent as Venice. There is no public conveyance beyond the springless wagon of a carrier who takes travelers, merchandise, and occasionally letters from Saint‑Nazaire to Guerande and back. In 1829 Bernus, the carrier, was the factotum of this community. He went and came when he pleased; all the country knew him; he ran errands for all. The arrival of a carriage—perhaps a lady or an invalid going to Croisic for sea‑bathing—is a huge event. Peasants come on horseback, many with commodities for barter, induced to purchase jewels distinctive of their caste, and white linen or cloth for their clothing.

For ten miles round, Guerande remains GUERANDE—the town where the famous treaty was signed in 1365, the coast’s key, boasting a splendor lost in time. The jewels, linen, cloth, ribbon, and hats are made elsewhere, but buyers deem them from Guerande. Artists and some bourgeois feel, as at Venice, a desire to end their days amid its peace, walking the beautiful mall that surrounds the town toward the sea. Sometimes this image rises in memory’s temple; she reappears crowned with towers, clasped by her girdle; her flower‑strewn robe floats, and the golden mantle of her dunes enfolds her; the fragrant breath of her paths exhales at every step.

Near the church stands a mansion to the town what the town is to the region—an image of the past, the symbol of a great thing destroyed. This mansion belongs to the noblest local family, the du Guaisnics, who in du Guesclin’s days were as ancient and proud as Trojans to Romans. The Guaisnics (du Glaicquin), from whom du Guesclin comes, are old as Brittany’s granite. They are Bretons, Celts—once perhaps Druids—whatever their origin, today this pure race, equal to the Rohans without having become princes before Hugues Capet’s ancestors, possesses two thousand francs a year, its mansion in Guerande, and the little castle of Guaisnic. All lands belonging to the barony of Guaisnic, once the first in Brittany, are pledged to farmers and bring in sixty thousand francs a year despite ignorant culture. The du Guaisnics remain nominal owners although they receive none of the revenues, unable for two hundred years to pay off advances. They are in the position of the crown towards engagistes before 1789. Before the Revolution the fiefs under Guaisnic were worth fifty thousand francs a year; a vote of the National Assembly later suppressed seigneurs’ dues on inheritance.

In such a situation this family—of little account in France and laughed at in Paris—was in Guerande the whole of Brittany. In Guerande the Baron du Guaisnic is one of the great barons of France, a man above whom there is but one—the King. Today the name du Guaisnic, full of Breton significance (roots explained in “The Chouans”), has been altered as other names have been; the tax‑gatherer now writes du Guenic.

At the end of a silent, damp lane stands an arch of a gate wide enough for a horseman—proof that when built carriages did not exist. The granite arch frames an oak gate studded with enormous nails in geometric figures. The semicircular arch is carved with the Guaisnics’ arms as clean‑cut as though the sculptor had just laid down his chisel. This escutcheon delights lovers of heraldry by a simplicity proving the family’s pride and antiquity: Gules, a hand proper gonfaloned ermine, with a sword argent in pale, and the motto, FAC. A baronial coronet surmounts this simple blazon. Even a confirmed republican might be moved by the fidelity, nobleness, and grandeur in that dark lane. The du Guaisnics did well yesterday, and are ready to do well tomorrow. “To DO” is chivalry’s motto; the depth of carving matches the depth of the motto in the family’s soul.

When the gate opens a somewhat vast courtyard appears, with stables on the right and kitchen and offices on the left. The house is freestone from cellar to garret. The facade on the court-yard has a portico with a double range of steps, its wall covered with carved vestiges now effaced, but an antiquary can still make out the Hand bearing the sword. Granite steps are disjointed, grasses and moss forcing their way through fissures, yet solidity remains. The oak door opens to a noble hall leading to a portico that opens on the garden.

This hall is marvelously preserved. Panelled wainscot three feet high is chestnut. A Spanish leather with figures in relief, gilding now peeled or reddened, covers the walls. The ceiling is wooden boards artistically joined and painted; faint gold, a few red flowers and green foliage remain. Perhaps cleaning might reveal paintings like those found on Tristan’s house at Tours, proving the planks date or were restored in Louis XI’s reign. The chimney-piece is enormous carved stone; within are giant wrought‑iron andirons, precious workmanship. Furniture is wholly oak, each article bearing the family arms. Three English guns fit for chase or war, three sabres, two game‑bags and fishermen’s utensils hang from nails on the wall.

On one side is a dining-room connecting with the kitchen by a door cut through a corner tower. This tower corresponds on the facade toward the court-yard with another at the opposite corner, where a spiral staircase leads to the two upper stories.

The dining-room hangs with fourteenth‑century tapestries; the orthography of banderols beneath each figure proves their age, though their naive language resists transcription here. These tapestries, preserved where light scarcely penetrates, are framed in bands of oak black as ebony. The ceiling has projecting rafters enriched with varied foliage; spaces between are painted blue where garlands of golden flowers twine. Two old buffers face each other; on shelves rubbed by Mariotte the cook, one sees four old goblets, an ancient embossed soup‑tureen, two silver salt‑cellars; many pewter plates and pitchers of gray and blue pottery with arabesque designs and the du Guaisnics’ arms, covered by hinged pewter lids. The chimney-piece is modernized—an anachronism proving the family has lived in this room recently. It is carved stone in Louis XV style, ornamented with a mirror let in the back with gilt beaded moulding. On the mantel‑shelf, covered with red velvet, stands a tall tortoise‑shell clock inlaid with brass, flanked by silver candelabra of singular design. A large square table with solid legs fills the center; chairs are turned wood covered with tapestry. A round table near a garden window holds an odd lamp: a common glass globe fastened to a candlestick by a glass tube; a wick passes through a brass reed drawing nut‑oil coiled within like a tape‑worm in a surgeon’s phial. Windows into the garden and court‑yard are mullioned with hexagonal panes, draped by curtains with heavy valances of ancient crimson silk once called brocatelle.

Each of the two upper stories has but two rooms: the head of the family’s bedroom and the children’s. Guests lodged in chambers beneath the roof; servants above kitchens and stables. The pointed roof, protected with lead at angles, has noble pointed windows on each side, one looking down on the court‑yard, the other on the garden. These windows rise almost to the roof and have slender casings whose carvings have crumbled under salty vapors. Above each arch with stone crossbars grinds the vane of a noble.

Note a precious detail, full of naïveté: the spiral‑stair tower is placed at the corner of a great gable wall with no window. The staircase descends to a little arched door opening on a gravelly yard between house and stables. This tower is repeated on the garden side by another of five sides ending in a cupola containing a bell‑turret, instead of being roofed like the sister‑tower with a pepper‑pot. These towers connect on the first floor by a stone gallery supported by brackets each ending in a grotesque human head. The gallery has an exquisite balustrade. From the gable hangs a stone dais like those crowning saints’ statues at church portals. One can imagine a woman walking on this balcony and gazing over Guerande at sunshine gilding the sands and shimmering on Ocean. Admire the gable wall flanked by varied towers. The opposite gable of the Guaisnic mansion adjoins the next house. Harmony, carefully sought by architects of those days, is maintained on the court‑yard façade by the tower communicating between dining‑room and kitchen; it stops at the first upper story and its summit is a small open dome beneath which stands a now blackened statue of Saint Calyste.

The garden is magnificent for so old a place. It covers half an acre, its walls espaliered, divided into vegetable squares bordered with cordons of fruit‑trees that the man‑of‑all‑work, Gasselin, tends between grooming horses. At the farther end is a grotto with a seat; in the middle, a sun‑dial; the paths are gravelled. The garden façade has no towers corresponding to those on the court‑yard; but a slender spiral column rises from ground to roof that once bore the family banner—an iron socket at its summit where a few weak plants straggle. This detail, with vestiges of sculpture, proves to a practiced eye that a Venetian architect built the mansion. The graceful staff is a signature revealing Venice, chivalry, and thirteenth‑century delicacy. If any doubt remained, the ornamentation’s trefoils with four leaves instead of three would dispel it—Venetian art, depraved by commerce with the East, gives four leaves to clover while Christian art remained faithful to the Trinity.

If this ancient dwelling attracts your imagination, you may ask why such miracles are not renewed. Because today mansions are sold, pulled down, and their grounds turned into streets. No one is sure the next generation will possess the paternal dwelling; homes are no more than inns; whereas in former times when a dwelling was built, men worked, or thought they worked, for a family in perpetuity. Hence the grandeur of these houses. Faith in self, as well as faith in God, performed prodigies.

As for the arrangement of the upper rooms, they may be imagined after this ground‑floor description and an account of the family’s manners, customs, and physiognomy. For the last fifty years the du Guaisnics have received friends in the two rooms described, in which, as in the court‑yard and external accessories, the spirit, grace, and candor of old noble Brittany still survive. Without the town’s topography and the house’s minute depiction, the surprising figures of the family might be less understood. Therefore the frames have preceded the portraits. Everyone knows things influence beings. There are public buildings whose effect is visible upon persons living nearby. It would be difficult to be irreligious in the shadow of a cathedral like Bourges. When the soul is everywhere reminded of its destiny by surrounding images, it is less easy to fail of it.
