Produced by John Bickers and Dagny

PIERRE GRASSOU

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

Dedication

To The Lieutenant-Colonel of Artillery, Periollas, As a Testimony of the
Affectionate Esteem of the Author,

De Balzac

PIERRE GRASSOU

Whenever you have seriously looked at the exhibition of sculpture and
painting since the revolution of 1830, have you not felt uneasiness and
sadness at the sight of those long overcrowded galleries? Since 1830,
the true Salon no longer exists. The Louvre has been taken again—this
time by a populace of artists who have maintained themselves there.

In former days, when the Salon showed only the choicest works, it
conferred the highest honor on those exhibited. Among two hundred
selected paintings, the public could still choose: a crown was awarded
to the masterpiece by unseen hands. Eager discussions arose about some
picture. Abuse showered on Delacroix, on Ingres, contributed as much to
their fame as praise. Today, neither the crowd nor the criticism grows
impassioned about the products of that bazaar. Forced to make the
selection for itself, which the examining jury once made, the public's
attention soon tires and the exhibition closes. Before 1817 the pictures
admitted never went beyond the first two columns of the long gallery of
the old masters; but in that year, to the astonishment of the public,
they filled the whole space. Historical, high-art, genre paintings, easel
pictures, landscapes, flowers, animals, and water-colors—these eight
specialties could scarcely offer more than twenty pictures a year worthy
of attention; the public cannot give its attention to more. The more
artists increase, the more careful and exacting the jury of admission
should be.

The true character of the Salon was lost as soon as it spread along the
galleries. The Salon should have remained within fixed limits of
inflexible proportions, where each specialty could show its masterpieces.
A decade's experience has shown the excellence of the former institution.
Now, instead of a tournament we have a mob; instead of a noble
exhibition, a tumultuous bazaar; instead of a choice selection, a
chaotic mass. What is the result? A great artist is swamped. Decamps'
"Turkish Cafe," "Children at a Fountain," "Joseph," and "The Torture"
would have redounded far more to his credit if the four pictures had
been exhibited in the great Salon with the hundred good pictures of
that year, than his twenty among three thousand others, jumbled in six
galleries.

By some strange contradiction, ever since the doors opened to everyone
there has been talk of unknown and unrecognized genius. When, twelve
years earlier, Ingres' "Courtesan," Sigalon's, Gericault's "Medusa,"
Delacroix's "Massacre of Scio," Eugene Deveria's "Baptism of Henri IV."
were admitted by celebrated artists accused of jealousy, the world saw
that young vigorous palettes existed and no such complaint was made.
Now, when the veriest dauber can send his work, all the talk is of
genius neglected! Where judgment no longer exists, there is nothing
judged. But whatever artists may do now, they will return in time to
examination and selection, which present their works to the crowd for
whom they work. Without selection by the Academy there will be no
Salon, and without the Salon art may perish.

Ever since the catalogue has grown into a book, many names have appeared
in it that remain in obscurity, in spite of ten or a dozen pictures
attached to them. Among these names perhaps the most unknown to fame is
an artist named Pierre Grassou, from Fougeres, called simply "Fougeres"
among his brother-artists, who now holds a place, as they say, "in the
sun," and who suggested the rather bitter reflections by which this
sketch of his life is introduced—reflections applicable to many other
members of the tribe of artists.

In 1832, Fougeres lived in the rue de Navarin, on the fourth floor of
one of those tall narrow houses which resemble the obelisk of Luxor,
with an alley, a dark stairway with dangerous turnings, three windows
only on each floor, and, within, a courtyard, or, to speak more
correctly, a square pit. Above the three or four rooms occupied by
Grassou of Fougeres was his studio, looking toward Montmartre. The
studio was painted brick-color for a background; the floor was tinted
brown and well frothed; each chair had a bit of carpet bound round the
edges; the simple sofa was as clean as that in the bedroom of some
worthy bourgeoise. All these things denoted the tidy ways of a small
mind and the thrift of a poor man. A bureau held studio implements, a
table for breakfast, a sideboard, a secretary; in short, all the
articles necessary to a painter, neatly arranged and very clean. The
stove participated in this Dutch cleanliness, made more visible because
the little changing northern light flooded the vast apartment. Fougeres,
being merely a genre painter, did not need the immense machinery and
outfit that ruin historical painters; he never recognized sufficient
faculty to attempt high-art, and therefore clung to easel painting.

At the beginning of December of that year, a season when the bourgeois
of Paris periodically conceive the burlesque idea of perpetuating their
forms and figures already too bulky in themselves, Pierre Grassou, who
had risen early, prepared his palette, lighted his stove, ate a roll
steeped in milk, and waited till the frost on his windows had melted
enough to let the full light in. The weather was fine and dry. At that
moment the artist, who ate his bread with that patient, resigned air
that tells so much, heard and recognized the step of a man who had upon
his life the influence such men have on nearly all artists—the step of
Elie Magus, a picture-dealer, a usurer in canvas. The next moment Elie
Magus entered and found the painter beginning his work in the tidy
studio.

"How are you, old rascal?" said the painter.

Fougeres had the cross of the Legion of Honor, and Elie Magus bought his
pictures at two and three hundred francs apiece, so he gave himself the
airs of a fine artist.

"Business is very bad," replied Elie. "You artists have such pretensions!
You talk of two hundred francs when you haven't put six sous' worth of
color on a canvas. However, you are a good fellow; I'll say that. You
are steady; and I've come to put a good bit of business in your way."

"Timeo Danaos et dona ferentes," said Fougeres. "Do you know Latin?"

"No."

"Well, it means the Greeks never proposed a good bit of business to the
Trojans without getting their fair share. In the olden time they used
to say, 'Take my horse.' Now we say, 'Take my bear.' Well, what do you
want, Ulysses-Lagingeole-Elie Magus?"

These words show the mildness and wit with which Fougeres employed what
painters call studio fun.

"Well, I don't deny that you are to paint me two pictures for nothing."

"Oh! oh!"

"I'll leave you to do it, or not; I don't ask it. But you're an honest
man."

"Come, out with it!"

"Well, I'm prepared to bring you a father, mother, and only daughter."

"All for me?"

"Yes—they want their portraits taken. These bourgeois—they are crazy
about art—have never dared to enter a studio. The girl has a dot of a
hundred thousand francs. You can paint all three—perhaps they'll turn
out family portraits."

And with that the old Dutch log of wood who passed for a man and who
was called Elie Magus interrupted himself to laugh an uncanny laugh
which frightened the painter. He fancied he heard Mephistopheles talking
marriage.

"Portraits bring five hundred francs apiece," went on Elie; "so you can
very well afford to paint me three pictures."

"True for you!" cried Fougeres, gleefully.

"And if you marry the girl, you won't forget me."

"Marry! I?" cried Pierre Grassou—"I, who have a habit of sleeping
alone; who get up at cock-crow, and all my life arranged—"

"One hundred thousand francs," said Magus, "and a quiet girl, full of
golden tones, as you call 'em, like a Titian."

"What class of people are they?"

"Retired merchants; just now in love with art; have a country-house at
Ville d'Avray, and ten or twelve thousand francs a year."

"What business did they do?"

"Bottles."

"Now don't say that word; it makes me think of corks and sets my teeth
on edge."

"Am I to bring them?"

"Three portraits—I could put them in the Salon; I might go in for
portrait-painting. Well, yes!"

Old Elie descended the staircase to fetch the Vervelle family. To know
to what extent this proposition would affect the painter, and what
effect would be produced upon him by the Sieur and Dame Vervelle,
adorned by their only daughter, it is necessary to cast an eye on the
anterior life of Pierre Grassou of Fougeres.

When a pupil, Fougeres had studied drawing with Servin, thought a great
draughtsman in academic circles. After that he went to Schinner's to
learn the secrets of the powerful magnificent color that distinguished
that master. Master and scholars were discreet; at any rate Pierre
discovered none of their secrets. From there he went to Sommervieux'
atelier to acquire that portion of the art called composition, but
composition was shy and distant to him. Then he tried to snatch from
Decamps and Granet the mystery of their interior effects. The two
masters were not robbed. Finally Fougeres ended his education with
Duval-Lecamus. During these studies Fougeres' habits and ways of life
were tranquil and moral to a degree that furnished matter of jesting to
the various ateliers where he sojourned; but everywhere he disarmed his
comrades by his modesty and by the patience and gentleness of a
lamblike nature. The masters, however, had no sympathy for the good
lad; masters prefer bright fellows, eccentric spirits, droll or fiery,
or else gloomy and deeply reflective, which argue future talent.
Everything about Pierre Grassou smacked of mediocrity. His nickname
"Fougeres" (that of the painter in the play of "The Eglantine") was the
source of much teasing; but, by force of circumstances, he accepted the
name of the town where he had first seen light.

Grassou of Fougeres resembled his name. Plump and of medium height, he
had a dull complexion, brown eyes, black hair, a turned-up nose, rather
wide mouth, and long ears. His gentle, passive, resigned air gave a
certain relief to these leading features of a physiognomy full of
health, but wanting in action. This young man, born to be a virtuous
bourgeois, having left his native place and come to Paris to be a clerk
with a color-merchant (formerly of Mayenne and a distant connection of
the Orgemonts) made himself a painter simply by the obstinacy that
constitutes the Breton character. What he suffered, the manner in which
he lived during those years of study, God only knows. He suffered as
much as great men suffer when hounded by poverty and hunted like wild
beasts by the pack of commonplace minds and troops of vanities athirst
for vengeance.

As soon as he thought himself able to fly on his own wings, Fougeres
took a studio in the upper rue des Martyrs, where he began to delve his
way. He made his first appearance in 1819. The first picture he
presented to the jury at the Louvre represented a village wedding
laboriously copied from Greuze. It was rejected. When Fougeres heard of
the decision, he did not fall into one of those fits of epileptic
self-love to which strong natures give themselves up, sometimes ending
in challenges to the director or even threats of assassination.
Fougeres quietly fetched his canvas, wrapped it in a handkerchief, and
brought it home, vowing he would still make himself a great painter.
He placed his picture on the easel, and went to one of his former
masters, a man of immense talent—Schinner—whose triumph at that year's
Salon was complete. Fougeres asked him to criticize the rejected work.
The great painter left everything and went at once. When Fougeres had
placed the work before him, Schinner, after a glance, pressed Fougeres'
hand.

"You are a fine fellow," he said; "you've a heart of gold, and I must
not deceive you. Listen; you are fulfilling the promises you made in
the studios. When you find such things at the tip of your brush, my
good Fougeres, you had better leave colors with Brullon, and not take
the canvas of others. Go home early, put on your cotton night-cap, and
be in bed by nine o'clock. The next morning go to some government
office, ask for a place, and give up art."

"My dear friend," said Fougeres, "my picture is already condemned; it is
not a verdict I want of you, but the cause of that verdict."

"Well—you paint gray and sombre; you see nature as a crape veil; your
drawing is heavy, pasty; your composition is a medley of Greuze, who
only redeemed his defects by qualities you lack."

While detailing these faults Schinner saw on Fougeres' face so deep an
expression of sadness that he carried him off to dinner and tried to
console him. The next morning at seven Fougeres was at his easel working
over the rejected picture; he warmed the colors; he made the corrections
suggested by Schinner; he touched up his figures. Then, disgusted with
such patching, he carried the picture to Elie Magus. Elie Magus, a sort
of Dutch-Flemish-Belgian, had three reasons for being what he became—
rich and avaricious. Coming last from Bordeaux, he was just starting in
Paris, selling old pictures and living on the boulevard Bonne-Nouvelle.
Fougeres, who relied on his palette to go to the baker's, bravely ate
bread and nuts, or bread and milk, or bread and cherries, or bread and
cheese, or bread and cherries, according to the seasons. Elie Magus, to whom Pierre offered his
first picture, eyed it for some time and then gave him fifteen francs.

"With fifteen francs a year coming in, and a thousand francs for
expenses," said Fougeres, smiling, "a man will go fast and far."

Elie Magus made a gesture; he bit his thumbs, thinking he might have
had that picture for five francs.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Several days Pierre walked down from the rue des Martyrs and stationed himself at the corner of the boulevard opposite Elie's shop, where his eye rested upon his picture, which obtained no notice from passers. After a week the picture disappeared; Fougeres walked slowly to the dealer's shop. The Jew was at his door.

"Well, I see you have sold my picture."

"No, here it is," said Magus; "I've framed it, to show it to some one who fancies he knows about painting."

Fougeres had not the heart to return to the boulevard. He set about another picture, and spent two months upon it,--eating mouse's meals and working like a galley-slave.

One evening he went to the boulevard, his feet leading him to the dealer's shop. His picture was not to be seen.

"I've sold your picture," said Elie Magus, seeing him.

"For how much?"

"I got back what I gave and a small interest. Make me some Flemish interiors, a lesson of anatomy, landscapes, and such like, and I'll buy them of you," said Elie.

Fougeres would fain have taken old Magus in his arms; he regarded him as a father. He went home joyful; the great painter Schinner was mistaken after all! In that immense city of Paris there were some hearts that beat in unison with Pierre's; his talent was understood and appreciated. The poor fellow of twenty-seven had the innocence of a lad of sixteen. Another man, one of those distrustful, surly artists, would have noticed the diabolical look on Elie's face and seen the twitching of the hairs of his beard, the irony of his moustache, and the movement of his shoulders which betrayed the satisfaction of Walter Scott's Jew in swindling a Christian.

Fougeres marched along the boulevard in a state of joy which gave his honest face an expression of pride. He was like a schoolboy protecting a woman. He met Joseph Bridau, one of his comrades, and one of those eccentric geniuses destined to fame and sorrow. Joseph Bridau, who had, to use his own expression, a few sous in his pocket, took Fougeres to the Opera. But Fougeres didn't see the ballet, didn't hear the music; he was imagining pictures, he was painting. He left Joseph in the middle of the evening, and ran home to make sketches by lamp-light. He invented thirty pictures, all reminiscence, and felt himself a man of genius. The next day he bought colors, and canvases of various dimensions; he piled up bread and cheese on his table, filled a water-pot, and laid in a provision of wood for his stove; then, to use a studio expression, he dug at his pictures. He hired several models and Magus lent him stuffs.

After two months' seclusion the Breton had finished four pictures. Again he asked counsel of Schinner, this time adding Bridau to the invitation. The two painters saw in three of these paintings a servile imitation of Dutch interiors by Metzu, in the fourth a copy of Rembrandt's "Lesson of Anatomy."

"Still imitating!" said Schinner. "Ah! Fougeres can't manage to be original."

"You ought to do something else than painting," said Bridau.

Fougeres lowered his head like a sheep when it rains. Then he asked and obtained certain useful advice, and retouched his pictures before taking them to Elie Magus. Elie paid him twenty-five francs apiece. At that price of course Fougeres earned nothing; neither did he lose, thanks to his sober living. He made a few excursions to the boulevard to see what became of his pictures, and there he underwent a singular hallucination. His neat, clean paintings, hard as tin and shiny as porcelain, were covered with a sort of mist; they looked like old daubs. Magus was out, and Pierre could obtain no information on this phenomenon. He fancied something was wrong with his eyes.

The painter went back to his studio and made more pictures. After seven years of continued toil Fougeres managed to compose and execute quite passable work. He did as well as any artist of the second class. Elie bought and sold all the paintings of the poor Breton, who earned laboriously about two thousand francs a year while he spent but twelve hundred.

At the Exhibition of 1829, Leon de Lora, Schinner, and Bridau, who all three occupied a great position and were, in fact, at the head of the art movement, were filled with pity for the perseverance and the poverty of their old friend; and they caused to be admitted into the grand salon of the Exhibition, a picture by Fougeres. This picture, powerful in interest but derived from Vigneron as to sentiment and from Dubufe's first manner as to execution, represented a young man in prison, whose hair was being cut around the nape of the neck. On one side was a priest, on the other two women, one old, one young, in tears. A sheriff's clerk was reading aloud a document. On a wretched table was a meal, untouched. The light came in through the bars of a window near the ceiling. It was a picture fit to make the bourgeois shudder, and the bourgeois shuddered. Fougeres had simply been inspired by the masterpiece of Gerard Douw; he had turned the group of the "Dropsical Woman" toward the window, instead of presenting it full front. The condemned man was substituted for the dying woman--same pallor, same glance, same appeal to God. Instead of the Dutch doctor, he had painted the cold, official figure of the sheriff's clerk attired in black; but he had added an old woman to the young one of Gerard Douw. The cruelly simple and good-humored face of the executioner completed and dominated the group. This plagiarism, very cleverly disguised, was not discovered. The catalogue contained the following:--

     510. Grassou de Fougeres (Pierre), rue de Navarin, 2.
     Death-toilet of a Chouan, condemned to execution in 1809.

Though wholly second-rate, the picture had immense success, for it recalled the affair of the "chauffeurs," of Mortagne. A crowd collected every day before the now fashionable canvas; even Charles X. paused to look at it. "Madame," being told of the patient life of the poor Breton, became enthusiastic over him. The Duc d'Orleans asked the price of the picture. The clergy told Madame la Dauphine that the subject was suggestive of good thoughts; and there was, in truth, a most satisfying religious tone about it. Monseigneur the Dauphin admired the dust on the stone-floor,--a huge blunder, by the way, for Fougeres had painted greenish tones suggestive of mildew along the base of the walls. "Madame" finally bought the picture for a thousand francs, and the Dauphin ordered another like it. Charles X. gave the cross of the Legion of honor to this son of a peasant who had fought for the royal cause in 1799. (Joseph Bridau, the great painter, was not yet decorated.) The minister of the Interior ordered two church pictures of Fougeres.

This Salon of 1829 was to Pierre Grassou his whole fortune, fame, future, and life. Be original, invent, and you die by inches; copy, imitate, and you'll live. After this discovery of a gold mine, Grassou de Fougeres obtained his benefit of the fatal principle to which society owes the wretched mediocrities to whom are intrusted in these days the election of leaders in all social classes; who proceed, naturally, to elect themselves and who wage a bitter war against all true talent. The principle of election applied indiscriminately is false, and France will some day abandon it.

Nevertheless the modesty, simplicity, and genuine surprise of the good and gentle Fougeres silenced all envy and recriminations. Besides, he had on his side all of his clan who had succeeded, and all who expected to succeed. Some persons, touched by the persistent energy of a man whom nothing had discouraged, talked of Domenichino and said:--

"Perseverance in the arts should be rewarded. Grassou hasn't stolen his successes; he has delved for ten years, the poor dear man!"

That exclamation of "poor dear man!" counted for half in the support and the congratulations which the painter received. Pity sets up mediocrities as envy pulls down great talents, and in equal numbers. The newspapers, it is true, did not spare criticism, but the chevalier Fougeres digested them as he had digested the counsel of his friends, with angelic patience.

Possessing, by this time, fifteen thousand francs, laboriously earned, he furnished an apartment and studio in the rue de Navarin, and painted the picture ordered by Monseigneur the Dauphin, also the two church pictures, and delivered them at the time agreed on, with a punctuality that was very discomforting to the exchequer of the ministry, accustomed to a different course of action. But--admire the good fortune of men who are methodical--if Grassou, belated with his work, had been caught by the revolution of July he would not have got his money.

By the time he was thirty-seven Fougeres had manufactured for Elie Magus some two hundred pictures, all of them utterly unknown, by the help of which he had attained to that satisfying manner, that point of execution before which the true artist shrugs his shoulders and the bourgeoisie worships. Fougeres was dear to friends for rectitude of ideas, for steadiness of sentiment, absolute kindliness, and great loyalty; though they had no esteem for his palette, they loved the man who held it.

"What a misfortune it is that Fougeres has the vice of painting!" said his comrades.

But for all this, Grassou gave excellent counsel, like those feuilletonists incapable of writing a book who know very well where a book is wanting. There was this difference, however, between literary critics and Fougeres; he was eminently sensitive to beauties; he felt them, he acknowledged them, and his advice was instinct with a spirit of justice that made the justness of his remarks acceptable. After the revolution of July, Fougeres sent about ten pictures a year to the Salon, of which the jury admitted four or five. He lived with the most rigid economy, his household being managed solely by an old charwoman. For all amusement he visited his friends, he went to see works of art, he allowed himself a few little trips about France, and he planned to go to Switzerland in search of inspiration. This detestable artist was an excellent citizen; he mounted guard duly, went to reviews, and paid his rent and provision-bills with bourgeois punctuality.

Having lived all his life in toil and poverty, he had never had the time to love. Poor and a bachelor, until now he did not desire to complicate his simple life. Incapable of devising any means of increasing his little fortune, he carried, every three months, to his notary, Cardot, his quarterly earnings and economies. When the notary had received about three thousand francs he invested them in some first mortgage, the interest of which he drew himself and added to the quarterly payments made to him by Fougeres. The painter was awaiting the fortunate moment when his property thus laid by would give him the imposing income of two thousand francs, to allow himself the otium cum dignitate of the artist and paint pictures; but oh! what pictures! true pictures! each a finished picture! chouette, Koxnoff, chocnosoff! His future, his dreams of happiness, the superlative of his hopes--do you know what it was? To enter the Institute and obtain the grade of officer of the Legion of honor; to sit down beside Schinner and Leon de Lora, to reach the Academy before Bridau, to wear a rosette in his buttonhole! What a dream! It is only commonplace men who think of everything.

Hearing the sound of several steps on the staircase, Fougeres rubbed up his hair, buttoned his jacket of bottle-green velveteen, and was not a little amazed to see, entering his doorway, a simpleton face vulgarly called in studio slang a "melon." This fruit surmounted a pumpkin, clothed in blue cloth adorned with a bunch of tintinnabulating baubles. The melon puffed like a walrus; the pumpkin advanced on turnips, improperly called legs. A true painter would have turned the little bottle-vendor off at once, assuring him that he didn't paint vegetables. This painter looked at his client without a smile, for Monsieur Vervelle wore a three-thousand-franc diamond in the bosom of his shirt.

Fougeres glanced at Magus and said: "There's fat in it!" using a slang term then much in vogue in the studios.

Hearing those words Monsieur Vervelle frowned. The worthy bourgeois drew after him another complication of vegetables in the persons of his wife and daughter. The wife had a fine veneer of mahogany on her face, and in figure she resembled a cocoa-nut, surmounted by a head and tied in around the waist. She pivoted on her legs, which were tap-rooted, and her gown was yellow with black stripes. She proudly exhibited unutterable mittens on a puffy pair of hands; the plumes of a first-class funeral floated on an over-flowing bonnet; laces adorned her shoulders, as round behind as they were before; consequently, the spherical form of the cocoa-nut was perfect. Her feet, of a kind that painters call abatis, rose above the varnished leather of the shoes in a swelling that was some inches high. How the feet were ever got into the shoes, no one knows.

Following these vegetable parents was a young asparagus, who presented a tiny head with smoothly banded hair of the yellow-carroty tone that a Roman adores, long, stringy arms, a fairly white skin with reddish spots upon it, large innocent eyes, and white lashes, scarcely any brows, a leghorn bonnet bound with white satin and adorned with two honest bows of the same satin, hands virtuously red, and the feet of her mother. The faces of these three beings wore, as they looked round the studio, an air of happiness which bespoke in them a respectable enthusiasm for Art.

"So it is you, monsieur, who are going to take our likenesses?" said the father, assuming a jaunty air.

"Yes, monsieur," replied Grassou.

"Vervelle, he has the cross!" whispered the wife to the husband while the painter's back was turned.

"Should I be likely to have our portraits painted by an artist who wasn't decorated?" returned the former bottle-dealer.

Elie Magus here bowed to the Vervelle family and went away. Grassou accompanied him to the landing.

"There's no one but you who would fish up such whales."

"One hundred thousand francs of 'dot'!"

"Yes, but what a family!"

"Three hundred hundred thousand francs of expectations, a house in the rue Boucherat, and a country-house at Ville d'Avray!"

"Bottles and corks! bottles and corks!" said the painter; "they set my teeth on edge."

"Safe from want for the rest of your days," said Elie Magus as he departed.

That idea entered the head of Pierre Grassou as the daylight had burst into his garret that morning.

While he posed the father of the young person, he thought the bottle-dealer had a good countenance, and he admired the face full of violent tones. The mother and daughter hovered about the easel, marvelling at all his preparations; they evidently thought him a demigod. This visible admiration pleased Fougeres. The golden calf threw upon the family its fantastic reflections.

"You must earn lots of money; but of course you don't spend it as you get it," said the mother.

"No, madame," replied the painter; "I don't spend it; I have not the means to amuse myself. My notary invests my money; he knows what I have; as soon as I have taken him the money I never think of it again."

"I've always been told," cried old Vervelle, "that artists were baskets with holes in them."

"Who is your notary--if it is not indiscreet to ask?" said Madame Vervelle.

"A good fellow, all round," replied Grassou. "His name is Cardot."

"Well, well! if that isn't a joke!" exclaimed Vervelle. "Cardot is our notary too."

"Take care! don't move," said the painter.

"Do pray hold still, Antenor," said the wife. "If you move about you'll make monsieur miss; you should just see him working, and then you'd understand."

"Oh! why didn't you have me taught the arts?" said Mademoiselle Vervelle to her parents.

"Virginie," said her mother, "a young person ought not to learn certain things. When you are married--well, till then, keep quiet."

During this first sitting the Vervelle family became almost intimate with the worthy artist. They were to come again two days later. As they went away the father told Virginie to walk in front; but in spite of this separation, she overheard the following words, which naturally awakened her curiosity.

"Decorated--thirty-seven years old--an artist who gets orders--puts his money with our notary. We'll consult Cardot. Hein! Madame de Fougeres! not a bad name--doesn't look like a bad man either! One might prefer a merchant; but before a merchant retires from business one can never know what one's daughter may come to; whereas an economical artist--and then you know we love Art--Well, we'll see!"

While the Vervelle family discussed Pierre Grassou, Pierre Grassou discussed in his own mind the Vervelle family. He found it impossible to stay peacefully in his studio, so he took a walk on the boulevard, and looked at all the red-haired women who passed him. He made a series of the oddest reasonings to himself: gold was the handsomest of metals; a tawny yellow represented gold; the Romans were fond of red-haired women, and he turned Roman, etc. After two years of marriage what man would ever care about the color of his wife's hair? Beauty fades,--but ugliness remains! Money is one-half of all happiness. That night when he went to bed the painter had come to think Virginie Vervelle charming.

When the three Vervelles arrived on the day of the second sitting the artist received them with smiles. The rascal had shaved and put on clean linen; he had also arranged his hair in a pleasing manner, and chosen a very becoming pair of trousers and red leather slippers with pointed toes. The family replied with smiles as flattering as those of the artist. Virginie became the color of her hair, lowered her eyes, and turned aside her head to look at the sketches. Pierre Grassou thought these little affectations charming, Virginie had such grace; happily she didn't look like her father or her mother; but whom did she look like?

During this sitting there were little skirmishes between the family and the painter, who had the audacity to call pere Vervelle witty. This flattery brought the family on the double-quick to the heart of the artist; he gave a drawing to the daughter, and a sketch to the mother.

"What! for nothing?" they said.

Pierre Grassou could not help smiling.

"You shouldn't give away your pictures in that way; they are money," said old Vervelle.

At the third sitting pere Vervelle mentioned a fine gallery of pictures which he had in his country-house at Ville d'Avray--Rubens, Gerard Douw, Mieris, Terburg, Rembrandt, Titian, Paul Potter, etc.

"Monsieur Vervelle has been very extravagant," said Madame Vervelle, ostentatiously. "He has over one hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures."

"I love Art," said the former bottle-dealer.

When Madame Vervelle's portrait was begun that of her husband was nearly finished, and the enthusiasm of the family knew no bounds. The notary had spoken in the highest praise of the painter. Pierre Grassou was, he said, one of the most honest fellows on earth; he had laid by thirty-six thousand francs; his days of poverty were over; he now saved about ten thousand francs a year and capitalized the interest; in short, he was incapable of making a woman unhappy. This last remark had enormous weight in the scales. Vervelle's friends now heard of nothing but the celebrated painter Fougeres.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The day Fougeres began Mademoiselle Virginie's portrait he was already virtually son‑in‑law to the Vervelle family. The three Vervelles flourished in his studio, treating it as a residence; they felt an attraction to the clean, neat, artistic abode. Toward the end of the sitting the stairway shook; Joseph Bridau burst in like a whirlwind, hair flying, his haggard face casting quick glances about. He paced the studio, returned to Grassou, pulled his coat over his stomach and fumbled at a missing button.

"Wood is dear," he said to Grassou.

"Ah!"

"The British are after me," he added—creditors. "Gracious! do you paint such things as that?"

"Hold your tongue!"

"Ah! to be sure, yes."

The Vervelles flushed at the apparition.

"Brings in, hey?" continued Joseph. "Any shot in your locker?"

"How much do you want?"

"Five hundred. I've got a bull‑dog dealer after me; if he gets his teeth in he won't let go while there's a bit of me left. What a crew!"

"I'll write you a line for my notary."

"Have you got a notary?"

"Yes."

"That explains why you still paint cheeks like a perfumer's sign."

Grassou colored; Virginie sat.

"Take Nature as you find her," resumed the great painter, lecturing. "Mademoiselle is red‑haired. Is that a sin? All things are magnificent in painting. Put some vermilion on your palette, warm those cheeks; touch the little brown spots; butter it in. Do you pretend to know more than Nature?"

"Look here," said Fougeres, "take my place while I go and write that note."

Vervelle rolled to the table and whispered to Grassou: "Won't that country lout spoil it?"

"If he would only paint the portrait of your Virginie it would be worth a thousand times more than mine," replied Fougeres vehemently.

Hearing this, the bourgeois withdrew to his wife, stupefied by the intruder and uneasy about his daughter's portrait.

"Here, follow these indications," said Bridau, returning the palette and taking the note. "I won't thank you. I can go back to d'Arthez' chateau, where I'm doing a dining‑room and Leon de Lora the door‑tops—masterpieces! Come and see us."

He left without taking leave, having had enough of looking at Virginie.

"Who is that man?" asked Madame Vervelle.

"A great artist," Grassou answered.

"Are you sure," said Virginie, "that he has done no harm to my portrait? He frightened me."

"He has only done it good," replied Grassou.

"Well, if he is a great artist, I prefer a great artist like you," said Madame Vervelle.

The ways of genius had ruffled these orderly bourgeois.

Saint Martin's summer was beginning. Timid as a neophyte before a man of genius, Vervelle invited Fougeres to his country‑house the following Sunday. "You artists want emotions, great scenes, witty talk; but you'll find good wines, and my picture collection will compensate an artist like you for dining with mere merchants," he said.

This flattery stroked poor Pierre Grassou's innocent self‑love; he was unaccustomed to such compliments. The honest artist—an atrocious mediocrity with a heart of gold, loyal soul, poor draughtsman, worthy fellow—decorated by royalty with the Legion of Honor, prepared to go to Ville‑d'Avray to enjoy the last fine days of the year. He went modestly by public conveyance and admired the bottle‑dealer's villa, a five‑acre park atop Ville‑d'Avray, commanding a noble view. He imagined marrying Virginie and owning that villa.

The Vervelles received him with enthusiastic bourgeois absurdity that overwhelmed him. It was a day of triumph. The prospective son‑in‑law was marched about on raked nankeen paths; the trees looked brushed and the lawns freshly mown. The country air carried enticing cooking smells. Everything about the mansion proclaimed: "We have a great artist among us."

Little Vervelle rolled like an apple through his park, the daughter meandered, and the mother followed. For seven hours they attended Pierre Grassou. After a magnificent dinner, Monsieur and Madame Vervelle staged their grand theatrical effect—the opening of the picture gallery, lit by carefully arranged lamps. Three neighbors (retired merchants), an old uncle (expected to inherit), an elderly Demoiselle Vervelle, and other guests joined Grassou, eager to hear his opinion of Pere Vervelle's famous collection, whose value he enjoyed inflating. The bottle‑merchant fancied himself rivaling King Louis‑Philippe and the galleries of Versailles.

The pictures, magnificently framed, bore labels in black letters on gold:

               Rubens
     Dance of fauns and nymphs

                         Rembrandt
     Interior of a dissecting room. The physician van Tromp
             instructing his pupils.

There were one hundred and fifty pictures, varnished and dusted; some were veiled by green baize curtains not drawn in the presence of young ladies.

Pierre stood with arms pendent and mouth agape as he recognized half his own pictures among them. He was Rubens, Rembrandt, Mieris, Metzu, Paul Potter, Gerard Douw—twenty great masters in one.

"What is the matter? You've turned pale!" someone cried.

"Daughter, a glass of water, quick!" Madame Vervelle exclaimed. The painter took Pere Vervelle by the button and led him aside, ostensibly to look at a Murillo—Spanish pictures being in vogue.

"You bought your pictures from Elie Magus?"

"Yes, all originals."

"Between ourselves, tell me what he made you pay for those I shall point out to you."

They walked the gallery together; the guests watched the gravity with which the artist and host examined each picture.

"Three thousand francs," whispered Vervelle as they reached the last, "but I tell everybody forty thousand."

"Forty thousand for a Titian!" said the artist aloud. "Why, it is nothing at all!"

"Didn't I tell you," said Vervelle, "that I had three hundred thousand francs' worth of pictures?"

"I painted those pictures," said Pierre Grassou in Vervelle's ear, "and I sold them one by one to Elie Magus for less than ten thousand francs the whole lot."

"Prove it to me," said the bottle‑dealer, "and I double my daughter's dot, for if it is so, you are Rubens, Rembrandt, Titian, Gerard Douw!"

"And Magus is a famous picture‑dealer!" said the painter, now understanding why his pictures had been aged in Elie's shop and why Magus had specified certain subjects.

Far from losing esteem, Monsieur de Fougeres (as the family called him) gained so much that, when the portraits were finished, he presented them gratuitously to his father‑in‑law, mother‑in‑law, and wife.

At the present day Pierre Grassou, who never misses exhibiting at the Salon, is considered in bourgeois circles a fine portrait‑painter. He earns some twenty thousand francs a year and spoils a thousand francs' worth of canvas. His wife brought six thousand francs' dowry, and he lives with his father‑in‑law. The Vervelles and the Grassous, who agree delightfully, keep a carriage, and are content. Pierre never leaves the bourgeois world, where he is esteemed; no family portrait between the barrier du Trone and the rue du Temple escapes him; none costs less than five hundred francs. The great reason bourgeois families employ him is: "Say what you will of him, he lays by twenty thousand francs a year with his notary."

Because Grassou took a creditable part in the May 12th riots he was made an officer of the Legion of Honor and is a major in the National Guard. The Museum of Versailles commissioned a battle‑piece from such an excellent citizen, and he walked about Paris telling old comrades, "The King has given me an order for the Museum of Versailles."

Madame de Fougeres adores her husband, who has presented her two children. Though a good father and husband, he cannot banish a fatal thought: artists laugh at his work, his name is a term of contempt in studios, and the feuilletons ignore his pictures. Yet he persists; he aims for the Academy, where he will doubtless enter. And — oh, the vengeance that fills him — he buys works by celebrated artists short of means and substitutes these true works for the daubs in the Ville‑d'Avray collection.

Many mediocrities are more aggressive and mischievous than Pierre Grassou, who is anonymously benevolent and obliging.

ADDENDUM

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

EUGENIE GRANDET

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

                              DEDICATION

  To Maria.

  May your name, that of one whose portrait is the noblest ornament
  of this work, lie on its opening pages like a branch of sacred
  box taken from an unknown tree, sanctified by religion, and kept ever
  fresh and green by pious hands to bless the house.

          De Balzac.

EUGENIE GRANDET

I

There are houses in some provincial towns whose aspect inspires a
melancholy like that of sombre cloisters, dreary moorlands, or ruins.
Within them silence and barrenness prevail; life seems stagnant until a
pale, motionless face peers from a window at an unaccustomed step.

Such sadness marked a dwelling in Saumur at the upper end of the steep
street leading to the château. This street—now little frequented, hot
in summer, cold in winter, and dark in places—is notable for the
resonance of its pebbled pavement, its narrow tortuous roadway, and the
quiet of houses overtopped by the ramparts. Many are three centuries
old, built of wood with carved ends blackened by time. Slate-covered
beams trace a bluish line along frail walls; rotten shingles bend beneath
the years. Blackened window-sills scarcely bear the clay pots of working
women. Doors studded with huge nails show vanished domestic hieroglyphics:
here a Protestant attested his belief, there a Leaguer cursed Henry IV.,
elsewhere a bourgeois carved his trade—the whole history of France
is written there.

Next to a tottering artisan’s house rose a country gentleman’s mansion,
whose stone arch still held battered armorial bearings. In this hilly
street the ground-floors are neither shops nor warehouses; lovers of the
Middle Ages will find the ouvrouir of our forefathers in all its naive
simplicity. These low rooms, without show-windows, are deep, dark, and
unadorned. Their doors open in two parts; the upper half is fastened
back, the lower swings continually, admitting air through the upper door
or a space between the ceiling and a low front wall, closed each evening
by heavy shutters and iron bars.

This wall serves as a counter. No delusive display: only samples—tubs
of cod and salt, bundles of sail-cloth, cordage, copper wire, iron hoops,
a few cloth pieces on shelves. Enter; a neat girl in a white kerchief
drops her knitting and calls her father or mother, who sells you what you
want, phlegmatically, civilly, or arrogantly. A cooper may sit twirling
his thumbs, seeming to own little beyond a few boat-ribs and laths, yet
his wood-yard below supplies the cooperage trade of Anjou. A hot season
makes him rich, a rainy one ruins him; puncheons worth eleven francs
may fall to six in a morning.

As in Touraine, atmospheric vicissitudes control commerce here. Wine-growers,
proprietors, wood-merchants, coopers, innkeepers, mariners—all watch the
sun. They dread frost, rain, wind, drought; a perpetual duel goes on
between the heavens and terrestrial interests. The barometer smooths,
saddens, or brightens their countenances. From end to end of this street,
formerly the Grand’Rue de Saumur, men call: “Here’s golden weather,” or
“It rains louis,” knowing what sun or rain will bring them.

On fine Saturdays, these traders, having provided for vineyards, fields,
and provisions, close shop and spend two days in the country. Purchases
and profits arranged, they have hours for pleasure, observation,
criticism, and spying. A housewife cannot buy a partridge without
neighbors asking if it was cooked right; a girl is seen by idling groups
if she peeps at a window. Consciences are held in the light; the dark,
silent houses hide no mysteries. Life is mostly in the open air; every
household sits at its threshold to breakfast, dine, and quarrel. No one
passes without being examined; formerly a stranger entering a provincial
town was bantered from door to door—the origin of many good stories and
the nickname copieux for Angers folk who excelled in such urban sarcasm.

The ancient mansions of Saumur’s old town cluster at the top of this street,
once occupied by neighborhood nobility. The melancholy dwelling where the
following events occur is one of these venerable relics. Follow the
picturesque thoroughfare and you will find the dark recess where the
door of Monsieur Grandet’s house is hidden. To explain that provincial
expression—the house of Monsieur Grandet—we must give the biography of
Monsieur Grandet himself.

Monsieur Grandet enjoyed a reputation in Saumur whose causes and
effects are best understood provincially. In 1789—still called by some
le Père Grandet—he was a master-cooper, able to read, write, and cipher.
When the Republic sold church property in the arrondissement, the forty-year-old
cooper had just married the daughter of a rich wood-merchant. With about
two thousand louis-d’or—his own fortune and his wife’s dowry—and
help from two hundred double louis given by his father-in-law to the
surly republican presiding over sales, Grandet bought for a song one of
the finest vineyards in the arrondissement, an old abbey, and several farms.
The inhabitants, so little revolutionary, thought Père Grandet bold and
patriotic; in truth his thought was open only to vineyards. Appointed to
the administration of Saumur, his pacific influence showed itself
politically and commercially: he protected the ci-devant nobles and
prevented, where possible, the sale of émigrés’ lands; he furnished the
Republican armies with thousands of puncheons of white wine and took
payment in splendid fields reserved for sale.

Under the Consulate Grandet became mayor, governed wisely, and reaped
better pickings. Under the Empire he was called Monsieur Grandet; Napoleon
superseded him in office but Grandet left without regret. He had built
roads to his properties; his house and lands, assessed advantageously,
paid moderate taxes; and his vineyards, through his care, became the
“head of the country,” a local term for the finest yielders. He might
well have asked for the Legion of Honor.

In 1806 Monsieur Grandet was fifty-seven, his wife thirty-six, and an only
daughter, the fruit of their love, was ten. That year he inherited three
fortunes—those of Madame de la Gaudinière, née de la Bertellière, of old
Monsieur de la Bertellière (her grandfather), and of Madame Gentillet,
her grandmother—a hoard amassed by avarice. Old Monsieur de la Bertellière
loved his gold more than life, and his savings were famous in the
arrondissement.

Monsieur Grandet thus obtained a modern sort of nobility. He became the
most imposing personage in the arrondissement. He farmed a hundred acres
of vineyard, which in good years yielded seven or eight hundred hogsheads
of wine; he owned thirteen farms, an old abbey whose windows he had
walled to save expense, one hundred and twenty-seven acres of meadow
with three thousand poplars planted in 1793, and the house in which he
lived. This was his visible estate; as to other property, only two men
could hazard a guess at its value: Monsieur Cruchot, a notary who handled
Grandet’s usurious investments, and Monsieur des Grassins, Saumur’s
richest banker, in whose profits Grandet had a secret share.

Though Cruchot and des Grassins were discreet, their public respect for
Grandet led observers to estimate his wealth by their obsequiousness.
No one in Saumur doubted Monsieur Grandet had a private treasure, a
hiding-place of louis he nightly gazed upon. Avaricious people saw proof
in his eyes, which seemed tinged by yellow metal. The glance of a man
accustomed to draw enormous interest acquires furtive, eager, mysterious
movements—an unspoken language of passion. Grandet inspired respect as
one who owed no man anything: skilful cooper and experienced wine-grower,
he could tell with precision whether to make a thousand puncheons or
five hundred; he never failed in speculation and always had casks when
casks were dear. He could store his vintage and sell at two hundred
francs when small proprietors took five louis. His famous 1811 vintage,
judiciously stored and slowly sold, brought him over two hundred and
forty thousand francs.

Financially, Grandet was part tiger, part boa—able to lie in wait, spring
on his prey, swallow masses of louis, and rest like a snake digesting:
impassible, methodical, and cold. No one passed without admiration mingled
with fear: had not many felt the rending of those polished steel claws?
For one man Cruchot procured money at eleven per cent; for another des
Grassins discounted bills at frightful deductions. Few days passed without
Grandet’s name in market talk. To some, his fortune was an object of
patriotic pride. Innkeepers and merchants would tell strangers: “We have
two or three millionaire establishments; but as for Monsieur Grandet, he
does not himself know how much he is worth.”

In 1816 Saumur’s best reckoners put his landed property near four
millions; having made on average a hundred thousand francs a year from
1793 to 1817, it was fair to presume he held cash nearly equal to his
estate. After a game of boston or talk on vines, people said: “Le Père
Grandet? Le Père Grandet must have at least five or six millions.”

“You are cleverer than I; I have never been able to find out the
amount,” Cruchot or des Grassins would answer when overheard.

If some Parisian mentioned Rothschild or Lafitte, Saumur folk asked if
he were as rich as Monsieur Grandet. If the Parisian affirmed, they
shook their heads incredulously. Such a fortune explained all his
actions. Early peculiarities that once provoked ridicule had long died
away. His slightest actions, like the blinking of his eyes, were law to
the countryside, where men had studied him like a naturalist studies
instinct.

“It will be a hard winter,” said one; “Père Grandet has put on his fur
gloves.”

“Père Grandet is buying quantities of staves; there will be plenty of
wine this year.”

Monsieur Grandet never bought bread or meat: his farmers supplied
capons, chickens, eggs, butter, and his tithe of wheat. He owned a mill;
the tenant took grain as part of rent and returned flour and bran.
La Grande Nanon, his only servant, baked bread every Saturday. Grandet
arranged with kitchen-gardeners, his tenants, to supply vegetables. He
sold most of his fruits in the market. His firewood came from hedgerows
and half-rotten sheds whose planks farmers carted to town, cut, and
stacked in his wood-house, receiving his thanks. His only known expenses
were consecrated bread, clothing for his wife and daughter, hire of
their chairs in church, La Grande Nanon’s wages, tinning saucepans,
lights, taxes, repairs, and industrial costs. He had six hundred acres
of woodland recently purchased, which he put under a neighbor’s care
for a promised indemnity. After acquiring this he ate game for the
first time.

Monsieur Grandet’s manners were simple. He spoke little, in short
sententious phrases in a soft voice. Early on he stuttered when required
to speak at length; this incoherence, attributed to defects of
education, was really assumed and will be explained later. Four brief
sentences sufficed him: “I don’t know; I cannot; I will not; I will see
about it.” He never said yes or no, and never committed himself to
writing. If people talked, he listened coldly, chin in his right hand,
right elbow resting on the left, forming opinions he never changed.
He reflected long before any business agreement; when his opponent
confidently revealed purposes, Grandet answered: “I can decide nothing
without consulting my wife.” His wife, reduced to helpless slavery, was
a useful screen in business. He went nowhere among friends; he neither
gave nor accepted dinners; he made no stir, economizing even in motion.
He never disturbed others’ things out of respect for property, yet at
home coarse language and habits of his nature surfaced where he
controlled himself less.

Physically, Grandet was five feet high, thick-set, square-built, with
calves twelve inches in circumference, knotted knees, and broad
shoulders; his face was round, tanned, pitted by small-pox; his chin
straight, lips uncurved, teeth white; his eyes had the calm, devouring
expression of a basilisk; his forehead showed transverse wrinkles and
significant protuberances; his yellow-gray hair was mockingly called
silver and gold by young jesters. His nose, thick at the end, bore a veined
wen the common people said was full of malice. His whole countenance
showed dangerous cunning, an integrity without warmth, and the egotism
of a man long used to concentrate every feeling on avarice’s enjoyments
and upon the only human being who mattered to him—his daughter and sole
heiress, Eugenie. Attitude, manners, bearing, all testified to the
self-belief habit of success gives.

Thus, though outwardly unctuous, Monsieur Grandet’s nature was iron.
His dress never varied since 1791: stout shoes tied with thongs, thick
woollen stockings, short breeches of coarse maroon cloth with silver
buckles, a velvet waistcoat of yellow and puce stripes, a large maroon
coat with wide flaps, a black cravat, and a quaker’s hat. His gloves,
thick as a gendarme’s, lasted twenty months; to preserve them he always
laid them on his hat in one particular spot. Saumur knew nothing further.

Only six persons had the right of entrance to Grandet’s house. The most
important of the first three was a nephew of Monsieur Cruchot. Since his
appointment as president of the Civil courts of Saumur this young man
had added Bonfons to Cruchot, signing himself C. de Bonfons. Any litigant
so ill-advised as to call him Monsieur Cruchot would soon feel his folly
in court. The magistrate protected those who called him Monsieur le
président, but favored with smiles those who addressed him as Monsieur
de Bonfons. Monsieur le président was thirty-three, owned Bonfons worth
seven thousand francs a year; he expected to inherit his uncle the
notary’s property and that of another uncle, Abbé Cruchot, a dignitary
of Saint-Martin de Tours. These three Cruchots, backed by cousins and
allies, formed a party like the Medici.

Madame des Grassins, mother of a son of twenty-three, came assiduously
to play cards with Madame Grandet, hoping to marry her Adolphe to
Mademoiselle Eugenie. Monsieur des Grassins promoted his wife’s schemes
by services constantly rendered to the old miser, arriving in time upon
the field of battle. The three des Grassins had adherents and cousins.
On the Cruchot side, the abbé—the family Talleyrand—backed by his brother
the notary, sharply contested every inch with his female adversary and
tried to obtain the heiress for his nephew the président.

This secret warfare for Eugenie’s hand kept Saumur agitated. Would
Mademoiselle Grandet marry Monsieur le président or Monsieur Adolphe des
Grassins? Some said Monsieur Grandet would give his daughter to neither.
The old cooper, eaten with ambition, was said to seek a peer of France,
to whom an income of three hundred thousand francs would make the
Grandet casks acceptable. Others replied the des Grassins were nobles
and rich; Adolphe was personable; unless the old man had a papal nephew
at his beck, such an alliance should satisfy a man who came from
nothing—whom Saumur remembered with an adze in his hand and the bonnet
rouge. Certain wise heads noted Cruchot de Bonfons had the right of
entry at all times whereas his rival was received only on Sundays; others
trusted Madame des Grassins’ intimacy with the women. “It is diamond
cut diamond,” said a Saumur wit.

The oldest, wiser inhabitants declared the Grandets knew better than to
let the property go out of the family, and that Mademoiselle Eugenie
Grandet of Saumur would marry the son of Monsieur Grandet of Paris, a
wealthy wholesale wine-merchant. To this the Cruchotines and Grassinists
replied: the two brothers had seen each other only twice in thirty years;
Monsieur Grandet of Paris, mayor and deputy and colonel of the National
Guard, disowned the Saumur Grandets and meant to ally with some ducal
family favored by Napoleon. In short, nothing was not said of the heiress,
talked of for fifty miles and even on conveyances from Angers to Blois.

At the beginning of 1811, the Cruchotines won an advantage. The estate
of Froidfond, notable for its park, mansion, farms, streams, ponds, and
forests, worth about three millions, was put up for sale by the young
Marquis de Froidfond, who had to liquidate. Maître Cruchot, the président,
and the abbé, aided by adherents, prevented sale in small lots. The
notary concluded a bargain for the whole property, payable in gold,
persuading the marquis that suits against purchasers of small lots would
delay collection; it was better to sell the whole to Monsieur Grandet,
solvent and able to pay in ready money. The marquisate of Froidfond was
accordingly conveyed down the gullet of Monsieur Grandet, who, to Saumur’s
astonishment, paid for it, under proper discount, with the usual formalities.

This affair echoed from Nantes to Orléans. Monsieur Grandet took advantage
of a cart returning by way of Froidfond to see his château. After casting
a master’s eye over the property, he returned to Saumur, satisfied he had
invested at five per cent, and seized by the stupendous thought of
extending the marquisate by concentrating his property there. Then, to
fill his coffers, now nearly empty, he resolved to thin out his woods
and sell the poplars in the meadows.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One now understands fully what “the house of Monsieur Grandet” meant—that cold, silent, pallid dwelling above the town, sheltered by the ruined ramparts. The two pillars and the arch of the porte-cochere were of tufa, a Loire stone so soft it scarcely lasts two centuries. Irregular holes bored or eaten by weather made the arch and side walls look like vermiculated stonework, like a jail gateway. Above the arch a long bas-relief in hard stone represented the four seasons; its faces were already crumbling and blackened. A projecting plinth above bore chance growths—yellow pellitory, bindweed, convolvuli, nettles, plantain, even a little cherry-tree.

The archway door was solid oak, brown, shrunken, split; frail though it seemed, it was held by iron bolts in symmetrical patterns. A small square grating with close bars, red with rust, filled the middle panel and served as the knocker’s aperture; a ring struck upon a grinning head of a huge nail. The jaquemart-like knocker resembled a huge exclamation point; an antiquary might have detected traces of the burlesque figure it once represented, now worn away. Through the grating—originally for recognizing friends in civil war—one could perceive, at the far end of the dark slimy vault, a few broken steps leading to a garden shut in by thick damp walls whose moisture nourished tufts of sickly herbage. These walls were the ruined ramparts under which stretched the gardens of neighboring houses.

The most important room on the ground floor was a large hall entered from beneath the porte-cochere vault. Few know the importance of a hall in the little towns of Anjou, Touraine, and Berry. The hall is simultaneously antechamber, salon, office, boudoir, and dining-room; it is the theatre of domestic life. There the barber came twice a year to cut Monsieur Grandet’s hair; there farmers, the cure, the under-prefect, and the miller’s boy came on business. This room, with two windows looking on the street, was entirely of wood. Gray panels with ancient mouldings covered the walls from top to bottom; the ceiling showed beams painted gray, the spaces between washed white and yellowed with age. An old brass clock inlaid with arabesques adorned the mantel of the ill-cut white stone chimney-piece, above which hung a greenish mirror whose bevel reflected a thread of light along a gothic damascened steel frame. Two copper-gilt candelabra decorated the chimney-piece corners; removing their side branches each main stem, fastened to a bluish marble pedestal tipped with copper, served as a candlestick for a single candle on ordinary occasions. Antique chairs were covered with tapestry of La Fontaine’s fables; faded colors and darning blurred the figures.

At the four corners of the hall were closets, or buffets, surmounted by dirty shelves. An old marquetry card-table, its top a chessboard, stood between the windows. Above it hung an oval barometer with a black border enlivened by gilt bands, on which flies had so disported that the gilding was problematical. Opposite the chimney-piece were two pastel portraits, supposed to represent Madame Grandet’s grandfather, old Monsieur de la Bertelliere, as a lieutenant in the French guard, and the deceased Madame Gentillet in the guise of a shepherdess. The windows were draped with red gros de Tours curtains held back by silken cords with ecclesiastical tassels. This luxury, little in keeping with Monsieur Grandet’s habits, had been included in the purchase of the house along with the steel pier-glass, the tapestries, and the rosewood buffets.

By the window nearest the door stood a straw chair on castors to lift Madame Grandet high enough to see passers-by. A stained cherry-wood work-table filled the embrasure, and Eugenie Grandet’s little armchair stood beside it. Here their lives had flowed peacefully for fifteen years in a round of work from April to November. On the first of November they took their winter station by the chimney. Grandet permitted a fire only from November 1 to March 31, regardless of early spring or wintry autumn chills. A foot-warmer filled with embers la Grande Nanon contrived to save mother and daughter from chilly mornings and evenings in April and October. Mother and daughter managed the family linen, spending their days in labor; if Eugenie wished to embroider a collar for her mother she had to take time from sleep and deceive her father to obtain light. For a long time the miser dispensed the tallow candle to his daughter and la Grande Nanon just as he did bread and other daily necessities.

La Grande Nanon was perhaps the only human being capable of willingly accepting her master’s despotism. The whole town envied Monsieur and Madame Grandet for possessing her. La Grande Nanon, so called for her height of five feet eight inches, had lived with Monsieur Grandet thirty-five years. Though she received only sixty francs a year, she was reputed one of Saumur’s richest serving-women; those sixty francs, accumulated over thirty-five years, had enabled her to invest four thousand francs in an annuity with Maitre Cruchot. This result of long, persistent economy seemed gigantic. Every servant in town, seeing the poor sexagenarian secure of bread for her old age, was jealous and never thought of the hard slavery through which it had been won.

At twenty-two she had been unable to find a situation because her face repelled most; unjustly, for it would have been admired on a grenadier’s shoulders. Forced to leave a farm where she kept cows because the dwelling burned down, she came to Saumur, full of robust courage that shrinks from no labor. Le Pere Grandet then thought of marriage and setting up his household. He espied the girl, rejected from door to door. A good judge of corporeal strength from his trade as a cooper, he guessed the work to be got from a female shaped like a Hercules—firm on her feet, strong in the hips, square in the back, with hands of a cartman and honesty as sound as her unblemished virtue. Neither the warts on her martial visage, nor the brick tints of her skin, nor her sinewy arms, nor her ragged garments dismayed the cooper, who was still of an age when the heart shudders. He fed, shod, and clothed her, paid wages, and set her to work without treating her too roughly. Seeing herself thus welcomed, la Grande Nanon wept secretly and attached herself sincerely to her master, who from that day ruled and worked her with feudal authority. Nanon did everything. She cooked, made lye, washed linen in the Loire and brought it home on her shoulders; she rose early and slept late; she prepared the vine-dressers’ food during harvest, watched the market-people, protected her master’s property like a faithful dog, and, in blind confidence, obeyed his absurd exactions without murmur.

In the famous year 1811, when the grapes were gathered with unheard-of difficulty, Grandet resolved to give Nanon his old watch—the first present in twenty years of service. Though he turned over his old shoes (which fitted her), it is impossible to consider that quarterly benefit a gift, for the shoes were always thoroughly worn out. Necessity had made the poor girl so niggardly that Grandet had grown to love her as we love a dog, and Nanon allowed him to fasten a spiked collar round her throat whose spikes no longer pricked. If Grandet cut bread with parsimony, she made no complaint; she gladly shared hygienic benefits from the household’s severe regime, in which no one was ever ill. Nanon was, in fact, one of the family; she laughed when Grandet laughed, felt gloomy or chilly, warmed herself, and toiled as he did. Never did the master need to find fault with the servant for pilfering grapes, plums, or nectarines. “Come, fall-to, Nanon!” he would say in years when branches bent under fruit and farmers handed it to the pigs.

To the poor peasant who had earned nothing but harsh treatment, Grandet’s ambiguous laugh was like a sunbeam. Nanon’s simple heart and narrow head could hold only one feeling and one idea. For thirty-five years she never ceased to see herself before Monsieur Grandet’s wood-yard, ragged and barefoot, and to hear him say, “What do you want, young one?” Her gratitude was ever new. Sometimes Grandet, reflecting that she had never heard a flattering word and might one day appear before God more chaste than the Virgin Mary, would say, looking at her, “Poor Nanon!” The exclamation was always followed by an undefinable look from the old servant. The words, uttered from time to time, formed a chain of friendship nothing ever parted, each exclamation adding a link. Such compassion, arising in the miser and accepted gratefully by the old spinster, had something inconceivably horrible about it. This cruel pity, recalling a thousand pleasures to the cooper, was to Nanon the sum total of happiness. Who does not likewise say, “Poor Nanon!” God will recognize his angels by the inflexions of their voices and by their secret sighs.

Many households in Saumur treated servants better but received less satisfaction in return. Thus people asked: “What have the Grandets ever done to make their Grande Nanon so attached? She would go through fire and water for them!” Her kitchen, whose barred windows looked into the court, was always clean, neat, cold—a true miser’s kitchen where nothing went to waste. When Nanon had washed dishes, locked up dinner remains, and put out her fire, she left the kitchen, separated by a passage from the living-room, and went to spin hemp beside her masters. One tallow candle sufficed the family for the evening. The servant slept at the end of the passage in a closet lit only by a fan-light. Her robust health enabled her to live in this hole; she could hear the slightest noise through the deep silence that reigned day and night. Like a watchdog, she slept with one ear open and took her rest alert.

A description of other parts of the dwelling will appear with the events of this history, though this sketch of the hall, where the household’s whole luxury appears, may enable the reader to surmise the nakedness of the upper floors.

In 1819, at the beginning of an evening in mid-November, la Grande Nanon lighted the fire for the first time. The autumn had been fine. This day was a fête well known to the Cruchotines and the Grassinists. The six antagonists, armed at all points, prepared to meet at the Grandets and surpass each other in testimonials of friendship. That morning all Saumur had seen Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet, accompanied by Nanon, on their way to Mass, and everyone remembered the day as Mademoiselle Eugenie’s birthday. Calculating when the family dinner would end, Maitre Cruchot, the Abbe Cruchot, and Monsieur C. de Bonfons hastened to arrive before the des Grassins and be first to pay compliments. All three brought enormous bouquets gathered in their greenhouses. The president’s flower stalks were ingeniously wound with a white satin ribbon fringed with gold. In the morning Monsieur Grandet, following his custom on Eugenie’s birth and fête days, went to her bedside and solemnly presented his paternal gift—which for thirteen years consisted regularly of a curious gold-piece. Madame Grandet gave her a winter or summer dress as needed. These two dresses and the gold pieces, supplemented by two more on New Year’s day and her father’s fête, gave Eugenie a little revenue of about a hundred crowns, which Grandet loved to see her amass. Was it not putting money from one strongbox to another, and training his heiress’s parsimony? He sometimes demanded an account of her treasure, formerly increased by the Bertelliere gifts, saying: “It is to be your marriage dozen.”

The “marriage dozen” is an old custom still in force in parts of central France. In Berry and Anjou, when a young girl marries, her family, or that of the husband, gives her a purse containing, according to means, twelve pieces, or twelve dozen, or twelve hundred pieces of gold. The poorest shepherd-girl never marries without her dozen, even if only coppers. They still tell in Issoudun of a “dozen” presented to a rich heiress containing one hundred and forty-four portugaises d’or. Pope Clement VII., uncle of Catherine de’ Medici, gave her when he married her to Henri II. a dozen antique gold medals.

During dinner, pleased to see Eugenie in a new gown, Grandet exclaimed, “As it is Eugenie’s birthday let us have a fire; it will be a good omen.”

“Mademoiselle will be married this year, that’s certain,” said la Grande Nanon, carrying away the remains of the goose—the pheasant of tradesmen.

“I don’t see anyone suitable for her in Saumur,” said Madame Grandet, glancing timidly at her husband, revealing the conjugal slavery under which she languished.

Grandet looked at his daughter and exclaimed gaily, “She is twenty-three years old to-day; we must soon begin to think of it.”

Eugenie and her mother silently exchanged a glance of intelligence.

Madame Grandet was a dry, thin woman, as yellow as a quince, awkward, slow—one born to be down-trodden. She had big bones, a big nose, a big forehead, big eyes, and bore a vague resemblance to mealy fruits without savor. Her teeth were black and few, her mouth wrinkled, her chin long and pointed. She was an excellent woman, a true la Bertelliere. L’abbe Cruchot occasionally told her she had not done ill, and she believed him. Angelic sweetness, resignation like that of an insect tortured by children, rare piety, a good heart, and unalterable equanimity made her universally pitied and respected. Her husband never gave her more than six francs at a time for personal expenses. Ridiculous as it seems, this woman, who by fortune and inheritances had brought Pere Grandet more than three hundred thousand francs, felt so profoundly humiliated by dependence and slavery—against which her gentleness prevented revolt—that she had never asked for a penny or remarked on the deeds Maitre Cruchot brought for her signature. This foolish secret pride, this nobility of soul perpetually misunderstood and wounded by Grandet, ruled her conduct.

Madame Grandet habitually wore a greenish levantine silk gown, making it last nearly a year; a large white cotton kerchief, a bonnet of plaited straw, and almost always a black-silk apron. As she seldom left the house she wore out few shoes and never asked anything for herself. Grandet, seized with occasional remorse when he remembered how long since he gave her the last six francs, always stipulated “the wife’s pin-money” when he sold his yearly vintage. The four or five louis presented by the Belgian or Dutchman who purchased wine were the chief visible signs of Madame Grandet’s annual revenues. But after receiving the five louis, her husband would often say, as though their purse were common, “Can you lend me a few sous?” and the poor woman, glad to help a man her confessor upheld as her lord and master, returned several crowns in winter out of the “pin-money.” When Grandet drew from his pocket the five-franc piece he allowed monthly for his daughter’s minor expenses—thread, needles, and toilet—he never failed to ask as he buttoned his breeches’ pocket, “And you, mother, do you want anything?”

“My friend,” Madame Grandet would answer, moved by maternal dignity, “we will see about that later.”

Wasted dignity! Grandet thought himself generous to his wife. Philosophers who meet the like of Nanon, Madame Grandet, and Eugenie have surely a right to say that irony lies at the bottom of Providence.

After the dinner at which Eugenie’s marriage was first mentioned, Nanon fetched a bottle of black-currant ratafia from Monsieur Grandet’s bed-chamber and nearly fell coming down the stairs.

“You great stupid!” said her master; “are you going to tumble about like other people, hey?”

“Monsieur, it was that step on your staircase which has given way.”

“She is right,” said Madame Grandet; “it ought to have been mended long ago. Yesterday Eugenie nearly twisted her ankle.”

“Here,” said Grandet, seeing Nanon pale, “as it is Eugenie’s birthday, and you came near falling, take a little glass of ratafia to set you right.”

“Faith! I’ve earned it,” said Nanon; “most people would have broken the bottle; but I’d sooner have broken my elbow holding it up.”

“Poor Nanon!” said Grandet, filling a glass.

“Did you hurt yourself?” asked Eugenie kindly.

“No, I didn’t fall; I threw myself back on my haunches.”

“Well! as it is Eugenie’s birthday,” said Grandet, “I’ll have the step mended. You people don’t know how to set your foot in the corner where the wood is still firm.”

Grandet took the candle, leaving his wife, daughter, and servant with only the hearthlight, and went into the bakehouse to fetch planks, nails, and tools.

“Can I help you?” cried Nanon, hearing him hammer.

“No, no! I’m an old hand at it,” he answered.

While Grandet mended the worm-eaten staircase and whistled the songs of his youth, the three Cruchots knocked.

“Is it you, Monsieur Cruchot?” asked Nanon, peeping through the grating.

“Yes,” answered the president.

Nanon opened the door, and the hearth’s light enabled the Cruchots to find their way into the room.

“Ha! you’ve come a-greeting,” said Nanon, smelling the flowers.

“Excuse me, messieurs,” cried Grandet, recognizing their voices; “I’ll be with you in a moment. I’m not proud; I am patching up a step on my staircase.”

“Go on, go on, Monsieur Grandet; a man’s house is his castle,” said the president sententiously.

Madame and Mademoiselle Grandet rose. The president, profiting by the darkness, said to Eugenie, “Will you permit me, mademoiselle, to wish you, on this your birth-day, a series of happy years and the continuance of health?” He offered a huge bouquet of rare flowers and, taking the heiress by the elbows, kissed each side of her neck with complacency that made her blush. The president, who looked like a rusty iron nail, felt his courtship progressing.

“Don’t stand on ceremony,” said Grandet, entering. “How well you do things on fête-days, Monsieur le président!”

“When it concerns mademoiselle,” said the abbe, armed with his own bouquet, “every day is a fête-day for my nephew.” The abbe kissed Eugenie’s hand. Maitre Cruchot boldly kissed her on both cheeks, remarking, “How we sprout up, to be sure! Every year is twelve months.”

As he replaced the candlestick beside the clock, Grandet, who never forgot his jokes and repeated them when he fancied them funny, said, “As this is Eugenie’s birthday let us illuminate.” He carefully removed the candelabra branches, put a socket on each pedestal, took from Nanon a new tallow candle with paper twisted around its end, set it in the hollow, made it firm, lit it, and sat down beside his wife, looking alternately at friends, his daughter, and the two candles. The Abbe Cruchot, a plump puffy man with a red wig plastered down and a face like an old female gambler, stretched his feet well shod in stout shoes with silver buckles and asked, “The des Grassins have not come?”

“Not yet,” said Grandet.

“But are they coming?” asked the old notary, twisting his face, which had as many holes as a colander, into a queer grimace.

“I think so,” answered Madame Grandet.

“Are your vintages all finished?” asked Monsieur de Bonfons.

“Yes, all of them,” said the old man, rising to walk up and down with chest swelling with pride as he repeated the words, “all of them.” Through the passage door he saw la Grande Nanon sitting by her fire with a candle, preparing to spin so as not to intrude among guests.

“Nanon,” he said, going into the passage, “put out that fire and that candle, and come and sit with us. Pardieu! the hall is big enough for all.”

“But monsieur, you are to have the great people.”

“Are not you as good as they? They are descended from Adam, and so are you.”

Grandet returned to the president and said, “Have you sold your vintage?”

“No, not I; I shall keep it. If the wine is good this year, it will be better two years hence. The proprietors, you know, have agreed to keep up the price; and this year the Belgians won’t get the better of us. Suppose they are sent off empty-handed for once, faith! they’ll come back.”

“Yes, but let us mind what we are about,” said Grandet in a tone that made the president tremble.

“Is he driving some bargain?” thought Cruchot.
