At that moment the knocker announced the des Grassins, interrupting a conversation begun between Madame Grandet and the abbe.

Madame des Grassins was one of those lively, plump little women with pink-and-white skins who, thanks to provincial calm and virtuous habits, keep their youth past forty. She was like the last rose of autumn—pleasant though the petals had a frostiness and slight perfume. She dressed well, took fashions from Paris, set the tone in Saumur, and gave parties. Her husband, formerly a quartermaster in the Imperial guard, wounded at Austerlitz and since retired, still retained the seeming frankness of an old soldier.

“Good evening, Grandet,” he said, holding out his hand and affecting a superiority with which he always crushed the Cruchots. “Mademoiselle,” he added to Eugenie after bowing to Madame Grandet, “you are always beautiful and good; I do not know what to wish you.” So saying, he offered a little box from his servant containing a Cape heather—a recently imported, very rare flower.

Madame des Grassins kissed Eugenie affectionately, pressed her hand, and said, “Adolphe wishes to make you my little offering.”

A tall, blond young man, pale and slight, with tolerable manners and seeming shy though he had spent eight or ten thousand francs over his allowance in Paris while studying law, came forward and kissed Eugenie on both cheeks, offering her a workbox with silver-gilt utensils—mere show trumpery, despite the monogram E.G. in well-engraved gothic letters. As she opened it, Eugenie felt one of those unexpected perfect delights that make a young girl blush and tremble with pleasure. She turned her eyes to her father as if asking permission, and Monsieur Grandet replied, “Take it, my daughter,” in a tone that would have made an actor illustrious.

The three Cruchots felt crushed as they saw the joyous look cast upon Adolphe des Grassins by the heiress, to whom such riches were unheard-of. Monsieur des Grassins offered Grandet snuff, took some himself, shook off grains that fell on the Legion of Honor ribbon in his buttonhole, then looked at the Cruchots with an air that seemed to say, “Parry that thrust if you can!” Madame des Grassins cast her eyes on the blue vases holding the Cruchot bouquets, regarding the enemy’s gifts with the pretended interest of a satirical woman. At this delicate juncture the Abbe Cruchot left the company seated in a circle round the fire and joined Grandet at the lower end of the hall. As they reached the far window embrasure the priest said in the miser’s ear, “Those people throw money out of the windows.”

“What does that matter if it gets into my cellar?” retorted the old wine-grower.

“If you want to give gilt scissors to your daughter, you have the means,” said the abbe.

“I give her something better than scissors,” answered Grandet.

“My nephew is a blockhead,” thought the abbe as he looked at the president, whose rumpled hair added to the ill grace of his brown countenance. “Couldn’t he have found some little trifle which cost money?”

“We will join you at cards, Madame Grandet,” said Madame des Grassins.

“We might have two tables, as we are all here.”

“As it is Eugenie’s birthday you had better play loto all together,” said Pere Grandet; “the two young ones can join,” and the old cooper, who never played, motioned to his daughter and Adolphe. “Come, Nanon, set the tables.”

“We will help you, Mademoiselle Nanon,” said Madame des Grassins, quite joyous at the joy she had given Eugenie.

“I have never in my life been so pleased,” the heiress said to her; “I have never seen anything so pretty.”

“Adolphe brought it from Paris, and he chose it,” Madame des Grassins whispered.

“Go on! go on! damned intriguing thing!” thought the president. “If you ever have a suit in court, you or your husband, it shall go hard with you.”

The notary, sitting in his corner, looked calmly at the abbe, thinking, “The des Grassins may do what they like; my property and my brother’s and my nephew’s amount in all to eleven hundred thousand francs. The des Grassins, at most, have not half that; besides, they have a daughter. They may give presents; heiress and presents will be ours one of these days.”

At half-past eight the two card-tables were set. Madame des Grassins succeeded in seating her son beside Eugenie. The actors in this scene, so full of interest though commonplace, were provided with bits of pasteboard striped in many colors and numbered, and counters of blue glass. They seemed to listen to the notary’s jokes, who never drew a number without a remark, while in fact all thought of Monsieur Grandet’s millions. The old cooper, with inward self-conceit, contemplated Madame des Grassins’ pink feathers and fresh toilette, the banker’s martial head, the faces of Adolphe, the president, the abbe, and the notary, saying to himself:—

“They are all after my money. Hey! neither the one nor the other shall have my daughter; but they are useful—useful as harpoons to fish with.”

This family gaiety in the old gray room dimly lighted by two tallow candles; this laughter, accompanied by the whirr of Nanon’s spinning-wheel, sincere only on Eugenie’s and her mother’s lips; this triviality mingled with important interests; this young girl, who, like certain birds made victims by the price put on them, was now lured by proofs of friendship of which she was the dupe—all contributed to make the scene a melancholy comedy. It is, moreover, a drama of all times and places, though here in simplest expression. The figure of Grandet, playing his own game with false friendship and reaping enormous profits, dominates the scene and throws light upon it. The modern god—the only god in whom faith is preserved—money, is here, in all its power, manifested in a single countenance. Tender sentiments hold a secondary place; only three pure simple hearts—Nanon’s, Eugenie’s, and her mother’s—were inspired by them. How much ignorance there was in their simplicity! Eugenie and her mother knew nothing of Grandet’s wealth; they estimated life’s things by the glimmer of their pale ideas, and neither valued nor despised money because they were used to do without it. Their feelings, bruised though they did not know it, ever-living, were the secret spring of their existence and made them curious exceptions among lives purely material. Frightful condition of the human race! There is no joy that does not come from some species of ignorance.

At the moment Madame Grandet had won a loto of sixteen sous—the largest ever pooled in that house—and while la Grande Nanon laughed watching Madame pocket her riches, the knocker resounded on the house-door so loudly the women jumped in their chairs.

“There is no man in Saumur who would knock like that,” said the notary.

“How can they bang in that way!” exclaimed Nanon; “do they want to break in the door?”

“Who the devil is it?” cried Grandet.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Nanon took a candle and went to open the door, followed by her master.

“Grandet! Grandet!” cried his wife, alarmed, running to the room’s door.

All the players exchanged looks.

“Shall we all go?” said Monsieur des Grassins; “that knock seems ill‑omened.”

A young man appeared, accompanied by a coach-office porter with two trunks and a carpet-bag. Monsieur Grandet turned sharply on his wife.

“Madame Grandet, go back to your loto; leave me to speak with monsieur.”

He shut the door; the players returned to their seats without resuming the game.

“Is he from Saumur?” asked Madame des Grassins.

“No, a traveller,” replied Monsieur des Grassins.

“From Paris, then,” said the notary, checking his watch; “it’s nine; the Grand Bureau diligence is punctual.”

“Is the gentleman young?” asked the Abbe Cruchot.

“Yes,” said Monsieur des Grassins; “and he has luggage enough for a small army.”

“Nanon has not returned,” said Eugenie.

“He must be a relation,” remarked the president.

“Let us continue our game,” Madame Grandet murmured. “Your husband’s tone shows he’s cross; he would not like us to talk of his affairs.”

“Mademoiselle,” Adolphe whispered to his neighbor, “it’s likely your cousin Grandet — a handsome young man; I saw him at Baron de Nucingen’s ball.” When his mother trod on his toes, he was scolded to be quiet.

Grandet returned without la Grande Nanon; the porter’s and Nanon’s steps were heard on the stairs. He was followed by the traveller whose arrival had stirred the room — an intrusion as incongruous as a peacock among hens.

“Sit by the fire,” said Grandet.

The stranger saluted with easy grace; the men inclined their heads, the women bowed. Madame Grandet, with conventional hospitality, inquired whether he had travelled far.

“Just like all women!” muttered the old wine‑grower, absorbed in a letter. “Let monsieur rest.”

“But father, perhaps monsieur would like something,” Eugenie offered.

“He has got a tongue,” replied the old man sternly.

Only the stranger seemed surprised by this brusqueness; the others were used to their master’s rule. The newcomer rose, turned his back to the fire, lifted his foot to warm his boot, and to Eugenie said, “Thank you, my cousin, but I dined at Tours. And,” turning to Grandet, “I need nothing; I am not even tired.”

“Monsieur has come from the capital?” asked Madame des Grassins.

Monsieur Charles — the son of the Grandet in Paris — answered as he took an eye‑glass on a chain and inspected the table and company. He scrutinized Madame des Grassins with a certain impertinence and remarked, “Yes, madame. You are playing at loto, aunt. Don’t let me interrupt you; it’s too amusing to leave.”

Madame des Grassins was certain now it was the cousin; she watched him and Eugenie with mingled curiosity and apprehension. Monsieur des Grassins put a counter on his wife’s card. Eugenie stole furtive glances at her cousin, feeling anxious though she could not define why.

Charles Grandet, a handsome twenty‑two‑year‑old, contrasted sharply with the provincial company. His air of Parisian fashion provoked sarcastic looks from the Grassins and Cruchots. At twenty‑two, a young man may be both childlike and coquettish; Charles affected the superiority of a man of the world, bringing with him a wardrobe of Parisian fripperies: a hunting outfit, fine gun, various waistcoats and collars, fine linen, a gold toilet set from his mother, and a small desk from a lady named Annette — essentially the tools of a dandy.

His father had told him to travel modestly, yet Charles had taken the coupé alone, relishing privacy. At Tours, a hairdresser re‑curled his chestnut locks; he changed linen, put on a black satin cravat and a round collar framing his fair face. A half‑buttoned travelling great‑coat revealed layers of waistcoats and a watch on a gold chain; his grey trousers had black silk seams. He twirled a cane with a gold knob and wore gray gloves. His foppery was carried off by the air of a young man confident in his pistols and his aim.

Picture the Cruchots: sallow, snuffling, dingy shirts, twisted flabby cravats and threadbare coats. Their faces and garments matched the room’s general negligence. The Grassins and Cruchots were united only by a horror of fashion.

When Charles used his eye‑glass to inspect the joists and fly‑specks, the loto‑players stared as if at a curiosity. Monsieur des Grassins and his son, acquainted with fashion, were nevertheless surprised; they looked with satirical amusement, as if saying, “That’s what they have in Paris!”

Grandet, however, remained absorbed in a long letter, holding the only candle on the card‑table and paying no heed to his guests. Eugenie, unfamiliar with such perfection of dress, thought she beheld a seraphic being. She longed to touch his gloves, envied his small hands and refined features; to her practical life mending her father’s clothes, Charles looked like an engraving. When he drew an embroidered handkerchief from his pocket, Eugenie watched to see if he would use it; his manners, his eye‑glass, his contemptuous glance at a coffer that had pleased her, all delighted her. She dreamed long dreams of her romantic cousin.

The loto ended slowly. La Grande Nanon entered: “Madame, I want the sheets for monsieur’s bed.” Madame Grandet followed. Madame des Grassins suggested they stop playing and keep their stakes. The party drifted toward the fire.

“Have you finished?” asked Grandet without looking up.

“Yes, yes!” replied Madame des Grassins, taking a seat near Charles.

Eugenie, moved by a young girl’s awakening sentiment, left to help her mother and Nanon prepare the guest’s room. It was more than duty: she desired to make the room elegant for her cousin. She arrived in time to find everything undone. She suggested passing a warming‑pan between the sheets, covering the old table with a cloth and changing it each morning, lighting a generous fire, and bringing a pile of wood into the corridor without telling her father. She fetched a lacquer tray from the late Monsieur de la Bertellière’s effects, a six‑sided crystal goblet, a little gilt spoon, and an antique flask engraved with cupids, placing them triumphantly on the chimney piece. In a quarter of an hour she conceived more ideas than she had ever had.

“Mamma,” she said, “my cousin will not bear the smell of a tallow candle; suppose we buy a wax one?” She darted to fetch the five francs she had just received for expenses. “Here, Nanon.”

“What will your father say?” Madame Grandet objected, seeing her daughter carrying a Sevres sugar‑bowl Grandet had brought from Froidfond. “And where will you get sugar? Are you mad?”

“Nanon can buy sugar as well as a candle,” Eugenie insisted.

“But your father?”

“Surely his nephew should not go without a glass of sugared water. Besides, he will not notice it.”

“Your father sees everything,” warned Madame Grandet.

Nanon hesitated; she knew her master. “Come, Nanon, go — because it is my birthday,” Eugenie added, laughing at the little jest she had never made before. Nanon obeyed.

While Eugenie and her mother fussed over the room, Madame des Grassins openly courted Charles. “You are brave to leave the pleasures of the capital at this season and take up in Saumur,” she purred. “If we do not frighten you, you will find amusements even here.” She cast the provincial flirt’s ogling glance. Charles, out of his element and having imagined a sumptuous stay, found a resemblance to Parisian faces and responded gracefully, drawing her into confidential conversation. Madame des Grassins gradually lowered her voice and indulged coquettish confidences, complaining of the tedium of life at Grandet’s: her husband valued both commerce and nobility, their salon mixed business with the aristocracy, and she could relieve his monotony. “If you stay only with Monsieur Grandet, what will become of you? Your uncle is a sordid miser; your aunt is pious but scatterbrained; and your cousin is a little fool, without education, who will spend her life darning towels.”

“She is very nice,” Charles thought as he replied to the compliments.

“It seems, wife, you are taking possession of monsieur,” laughed Monsieur des Grassins.

The notary and the president dropped a few pointed remarks; the abbe, arranging his snuff, slyly declared that no one could do the honors of Saumur for monsieur so well as Madame des Grassins. He had already guessed the direction of the conversation.

“Monsieur,” Adolphe said to Charles with forced ease, “do you remember me? I danced vis‑à‑vis with you at Baron de Nucingen’s ball.”

“Perfectly; I remember,” Charles answered, pleased at being noticed.

“To you, madame, was Monsieur des Grassins very young in Paris?” Charles asked.

The abbe remarked that in the provinces women of thirty and over could be as fresh as Madame des Grassins, who blushed with pleasure at the compliment. The abbe, with sly banter about past triumphs, prodded the ladies’ vanity and made Madame des Grassins feel flattered and half‑conquered. Charles, unbuttoning his great‑coat and adopting the absent pose of a poet, thought his prospects in Saumur promising.

Pere Grandet’s distracted calm did not escape the notary and president, who tried to guess the letter’s content from the miser’s slight motions beneath the candlelight. He read a fatal letter from Victor‑Ange‑Guillaume Grandet in Paris. The dying man declared he could not survive the shame of bankruptcy: his broker and notary had failed, carrying off his last resources and leaving him nearly four million in debt, with assets only some twenty‑five percent of that. The fall in wine prices from the other Grandet’s vintage ruined his cellars. In three days Paris would call him a knave. He could not endure such infamy. He bewailed the ruin he brought upon his son, whom he idolized, and begged his elder brother to be a father to Charles. He entreated that the boy be gently treated and given means to venture in trade or the Indies. He confessed he had nothing to leave and asked his brother to obtain from Charles a renunciation of any claim to his estate as inheritance. If the brother refused, remorse would be lasting; if he accepted, a voice would pray for him. He closed with a blessing and trust that his brother would accept guardianship.

Grandet folded the letter carefully and put it in his waistcoat. “So you are talking?” he asked, with a humble, timid air that concealed feeling and calculation. “Have you warmed yourself?”

“Thoroughly, my dear uncle,” Charles replied.

“Well, where are the women?” Grandet said, resuming composure. Eugenie and Madame Grandet returned.

“Is the room ready?” Grandet asked.

“Yes, father,” they answered.

“Well then, my nephew, if you are tired, Nanon shall show you your room. It isn’t a dandy’s room; but you will excuse a poor wine‑grower who never has a penny to spare. Taxes swallow up everything.”

“We do not wish to intrude, Grandet,” said the banker; “you may want to talk to your nephew, so we will bid you good night.”

The assembly rose and made their parting bows. The notary fetched his lantern and offered to accompany the des Grassins. Madame des Grassins had not foreseen the evening ending so soon; her servant had not arrived.

“Will you do me the honor to take my arm, madame?” said the abbe.

“Thank you, monsieur l’abbe, but I have my son,” she answered dryly.

“Ladies cannot compromise themselves with me,” said the abbe.

“Take Monsieur Cruchot’s arm,” her husband suggested. The abbe walked off with Madame des Grassins so swiftly they were soon ahead.

“That’s a good‑looking young man, madame,” murmured the abbe, pressing her arm. “We may as well say goodbye to the grapes; the vintage is done. Say adieu to Mademoiselle Grandet — unless this cousin is enamoured of a Parisian woman, your son Adolphe will find another rival.”

“Not at all, monsieur l’abbe. This young man will soon see that Eugenie is a little fool — a girl without freshness. Did you notice her? She was as yellow as a quince.”

“Perhaps you made the cousin notice it?”

“I did not take the trouble.”

“Always sit beside Eugenie, madame,” the abbe counseled; “you need not say anything; the young man will make his own comparisons.”

“Well, he has promised to dine with me day after tomorrow.”

“Ah! if you only would,” the abbe sighed.

“What do you want me to do?” said Madame des Grassins. “You talk as if I would compromise myself. I am not thirty‑nine with a stain on my reputation to risk it now, even for the Great Mogul.” She accused the abbe of proposing wickedness, though he protested innocence; their banter floated between flirtation and calculated caution.

“It is clear,” said the president loudly, “that Monsieur Grandet of Paris sent his son to Saumur with matrimonial intentions.”

“But then the cousin wouldn’t have come among us like a cannon‑ball,” answered the notary.

“That doesn’t prove anything,” Monsieur des Grassins said; “the old miser is always making mysteries.”

“Des Grassins, my friend, I have invited the young man to dinner. You must ask Monsieur and Madame de Larsonnière and the du Hautoys, and the demoiselle du Hautoy, of course. Gentlemen, I trust you will join us,” Madame des Grassins said, stopping to address the Cruchots.

“Here you are at home, madame,” said the notary.

After bowing to the des Grassins, the Cruchots returned home, turning over the night’s event in their provincial minds. The arrival of the handsome Parisian had altered social positions; a temporary alliance between Grassinists and Cruchotines seemed necessary against a common rival. They resolved to prevent Eugenie from loving her cousin and to make the cousin think ill of her by circulating subtle insinuations, faint praise, and artless denials — a campaign of slanders designed to mislead and separate them.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the four were left alone, Monsieur Grandet told his nephew they must go to bed and defer business until morning. He outlined the household routine—breakfast at eight, a light midday repast, dinner at five—and invited Charles to explore the town if he wished, excusing himself from accompanying him on account of his occupations. He allowed gossip about his wealth to pass but declared he had “not a penny” and worked like an apprentice. Nanon brought a wax candle—an Anjou candle, yellow and smoke-stained—which Grandet, incapable of imagining such extravagance under his roof, did not notice.

Grandet led Charles through the passage that divided the hall from the kitchen rather than out the archway. The house was drafty despite sand-bags, and Nanon bolted the outer door and released a fierce wolf-dog that obeyed no one but her. As Charles climbed the worm-eaten stairs, he began to doubt his father’s wisdom in sending him here; the place seemed mean and shabby. The landing had three Etruscan-red doors with iron bolts; the first, above the kitchen, was evidently walled up and served as Grandet’s office and strong-room. No one, not even Madame Grandet, could enter; Grandet kept the key and the room was his secret laboratory where, people said, he weighed louis, stored title-deeds, and plotted his profits as if by some magic. Opposite that walled door was Eugenie’s chamber; at the other end of the landing were Grandet’s and Madame Grandet’s rooms, the latter entered through a glass door. Grandet lodged Charles in the mansarde above his bedroom so he might hear him come and go.

When they reached Charles’s attic room, Grandet brusquely told Nanon to remove the warming-pan she’d put for the guest, mocking its redundancy. Charles surveyed the attic—yellow paper, a stone fireplace, varnished chairs, a scant rag-carpet—and, amused and dismayed, joked with Nanon, who marveled at his green silk dressing-gown brocaded with gold. She begged him to give it to the parish as an altar-cloth to save his soul; Charles laughed and promised she could have it when he left. Nanon fell asleep tricked out in dreams of embroidery; Eugenie dreamed of love.

For Eugenie, an innocent and devout provincial girl, a new sensibility stirred the next morning. She rose early, said her prayers, and, attending to her toilet with unusual care, felt a fresh, delightful emotion at the prospect of pleasing. She smoothed her chestnut hair, washed her hands until they reddened, put on her prettiest stockings and shoes, and laced her corset carefully—unaccustomed to self-adornment, she delighted in her new gown. The parish clock struck seven; she had risen too early and sat by her window to watch the yard and garden. The small, hedged court, the well with its pulleys, the vine-strangled walls, the precise woodpile, the lichen-blackened pavement, the half-buried steps, the trellis of rotten wood, stunted apple-trees, gravel walks edged with box, raspberries, and the drooping walnut near her father’s sanctum—all took on a novel charm in the autumn sun. Nature’s quiet harmonies matched the stirrings of her heart; she felt, with that vague, inexplicable joy that preludes love, that something luminous had begun within her.

Eugenie judged herself too plain for Charles—humble, modest—though the narrator insists she possessed a chastened, spiritual beauty: a large, noble head, gray eyes of calm light, soft features left delicate despite smallpox scars, a kind mouth, round throat and well-formed bust. She stood at life’s shore where young illusions flower. She opened her door and listened; hearing Nanon’s cough, she went down to ask for a little cream for her cousin’s breakfast. Nanon laughed at the thought and praised Charles’s finery; the servant and the young girl fell into the innocent conspiracy of wishing to give the guest comforts.

A quarrel broke out when Grandet, keys in hand, came to dole the day’s provisions. “We are five to-day,” Nanon reminded him; Grandet, counting and calculating in his parsimonious way, had strict views about loaves and sugar. He believed Parisian youths would not eat bread anyway. Nanon wanted flour, butter, and wood to make a galette for the young ones; Grandet permitted only a little flour and a few ounces of butter and reluctantly allowed the oven if it could cook all dinner in one fire. Sugar was a matter of contention—Grandet treated the commodity like a treasure—and when Nanon argued for a couple more pieces he was adamant: she might give the nephew two pieces and go without herself. Nanon, shrewd, abandoned sugar and pursued the galette.

Grandet, in his parsimony, admired the nephew’s boots and wondered what “more money” went into boots than into the man who wore them. He kept the garden and fruit locked but allowed some provisions for the day. After fastening the closets, he announced he would go to straighten his hedge along the high-road, taking Eugenie with him. They walked to his fields on the Loire, where thirty workmen were clearing the land and where Grandet bragged, with calculating pride, about profits made from cutting poplar-trees at the right time. To his notary friend Cruchot’s amazement, he computed the returns of his planting and removal—poplars on poor ground, trees yielding hay, and a profit “coming to sixty thousand francs.” The old man’s arithmetic was boastful, his habitual stutter slipping into sardonic clarity as he explained how to plant poplars where the government would nourish them.

While Eugenie watched the Loire scenery and listened half-attentive to the men’s talk, Cruchot remarked that all Saumur was talking about Grandet’s nephew from Paris and that he would soon draw up the marriage-contract. Grandet bluntly declared he would “rather fling his daughter into the Loire than give her to her cousin,” a brutal, startling denial that blasted Eugenie’s nascent hopes. The cruel refusal turned her immediate joy to sorrow; she walked back trembling, suddenly conscious of her father’s power as master of fate. At the street threshold, Grandet paused for Cruchot’s account of the Funds and the notary showed him a newspaper.

Cruchot read that Monsieur Grandet of Paris—one of the most respected merchants—had shot himself the previous day after failing in business, undone by the failures of his broker and notary. The report noted that his good name might have secured assistance; it was regrettable he gave way to despair. Grandet of Saumur listened calmly and remarked simply, “I knew it.” Cruchot, chilled, realized the likely meaning: the Paris Grandet had perhaps begged his Saumur cousin for help in vain. Grandet told him the son did not yet know.

Back home at breakfast, Nanon reported the young man sleeping soundly. Grandet told them his brother had blown his brains out. Eugenie, hearing the news, was seized with pity for the stranger who, in the house above, slept unknowing; she wept. Her father’s reaction was different: he muttered “Poor young man! he isn’t worth a sou!” and ordered that no one tell Charles until he returned at noon after straightening a hedge; he announced that the nephew was “going off like a shot to the Indies” and that Eugenie would never see him again. The words stung and terrified the young girl.

Madame Grandet, with the motherly intuition that had kept their life intimate, perceived Eugenie’s emotion. She comforted her child, who revealed—by her gaze rather than words—that she had fallen in love. The mother, cautious, said it was wrong to love so quickly. Eugenie, impaired by no such caution, moved to set the table for Charles’s breakfast, hoping to please him: to make his coffee very strong, to get cream by midday, and to provide a galette. Nanon, eager and practical, went to fetch eggs and to buy coffee if necessary though fearful of the master’s anger; the small household conspired tenderly yet timidly to give the guest comforts beyond Grandet’s rigid parsimony.

Eugenie, arranging a dish of grapes, pears and vine-leaves as if for a feast, tiptoed not to wake Charles and listened to his quiet breathing. The thought came to her that sorrow might be watching over him while he slept. Nanon returned with two fresh eggs, obtained from a farmer she pleaded with; Eugenie hugged her in gratitude. The household’s small rebellions and tender contrivances revealed both the close affection of mother and servant toward the guest and the oppressive stinginess and authoritarian control of the father.

Thus the episode closes with Eugenie’s first passionate stirrings in contrast to her father’s austere, calculating world—his locked rooms, secret accounts, obsessive thrift, and ruthless decisions. Grandet’s refusal of any closer relation between his daughter and the young Parisian cousin, combined with the news of the Paris suicide, sets a decisive tone: paternal domination, private wealth hoarded and watched over like an alchemist’s treasure, and a young woman’s delicate awakening to love and pity in a household governed by fear, secrecy, and miserly pride.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
After two hours of anxious preparations—during which Eugenie rose twenty times to check the coffee or to listen for her cousin dressing—she set a simple breakfast, modest but a break with household custom. Midday breakfast was always standing: a slice of bread, a little fruit or butter, and a glass of wine. As she surveyed the modest table by the fire—an arm-chair before Charles’s plate, two dishes of fruit, an egg-cup, a bottle of white wine, bread, and sugar in a saucer—she trembled at the thought of her father’s look if he should come in and watched the clock to see whether Charles could eat before Monsieur Grandet returned.

“Don’t be troubled, Eugenie; if your father comes in, I will take it all upon myself,” said Madame Grandet. Eugenie wept, moved. Charles, who had been pacing his room singing, came down at eleven, dressed with the dandy’s care of a Parisian, and entered with a smiling, courteous youthfulness that made Eugenie’s heart beat with mournful joy. He laughed off the destruction of his castles in Anjou, approached his aunt gaily, and, after a little banter, admitted he was glad to eat, though he never breakfasted before midday. Pulling out a pretty Breguet watch, he declared it only eleven.

At the sight of his desire for partridge Eugenie’s secret longing stirred; she would gladly have given her small hoard for one. He let himself drop into an easy-chair with courtly grace; Eugenie and her mother took plain chairs near the fire and the three fell into the easy courtesy his manners invited. He treated them with a tenderness that melted them; his glance lingered on Eugenie’s features in a way that suggested innocent feeling neither fully understood nor resisted.

“Ah! my dear cousin, if you were in full dress at the Opera,” he said, flattering her, “you would make men commit the mortal sin of envy, and women the sin of jealousy.” Eugenie, half understanding, blushed. He spoke lightly about ridicule and Parisian manners, swallowed his buttered sippet elegantly, and amused them with talk of pistols and good hearts in Paris. Eugenie admired his ring, touched his pink nails, and Nanon, entering with eggs, announced “your chickens—in the shell.” He welcomed fresh eggs; the partridge forgotten, he asked for butter. Nanon and Eugenie fetched it.

Charles watched the rustic coffee—an oblong brown earthenware pot with grounds bubbling—laughed, and offered to teach them to make coffee in a Chaptal coffee-pot. Nanon scorned the fuss: who would tend the cow while the coffee was fussed over? Eugenie volunteered to make it. At the mention of “child” from Madame Grandet, a sorrow that would soon strike the young man flashed across their faces. The three women fell silent, sympathy and fear mingling.

“Is anything the matter, my cousin?” Charles asked. Madame Grandet began to remind Eugenie of her father’s injunction that they not speak to monsieur—then corrected herself to call him Charles. Eugenie said the name with a delighted cry. Presentiments of trouble, however, were soon confirmed by the knock they all dreaded.

“There’s papa!” whispered Eugenie. She quickly removed the saucer of sugar, leaving a few pieces on the tablecloth; Nanon carried off the egg-cup; Madame Grandet sat up like a frightened hare. Charles, puzzled, asked what was wrong. Monsieur Grandet entered, surveyed the table and the young man, and saw everything at once.

“Ha! ha! so you have been making a feast for your nephew; very good, very good!” he barked. “When the cat’s away, the mice will play.” He took his horn-handled knife from his breeches, cut a slice of bread, spread a small bit of butter with methodical care, and ate standing. Charles sweetened his coffee; the miser spotted the bits of sugar, glanced at his wife, who turned pale, and, leaning near her, asked, “Where did you get all that sugar?”

“Nanon fetched it from Fessard’s; there was none,” she answered. The silent scene, charged with intensity, held every eye. Nanon, who had come from the kitchen, stood looking in. Charles, tasting his bitter coffee, searched for sugar, but Grandet had already put the saucer away. “What do you want?” the uncle demanded.

“The sugar,” said Charles.

“Put in more milk,” replied Grandet; “your coffee will taste sweeter.” Eugenie calmly placed the hidden saucer back on the table, looking at her father. The courage of that small act was proud; Charles never guessed the cruel agitation in his cousin’s heart.

“You are not eating your breakfast, wife,” said Grandet. The poor woman took a piece of bread and a pear. Eugenie, boldly seeking to spare Charles, offered him some grapes and preserves. Grandet gruffly warned that if no one stopped him they’d pillage Saumur for the nephew; when he had finished he had something to tell him “which can’t be sweetened.” The look the women cast at Charles made his misgivings clear.

“What is it you mean, uncle?” he asked. “Since the death of my poor mother,” his voice softened, “no other sorrow can touch me.”

Grandet scoffed at sentiment and implied the young man’s hands were soft, made to pick up silver pieces—an unflattering image that mocked gentility and hinted at the old man’s suspicion about money. He opened the door and, with a curt “Come!” led Charles into the garden. Madame Grandet, Eugenie, and Nanon followed in curiosity, compelled to see what must be said.

In the garden, where the gravel crunched under Grandet’s heavy step, the miser found no difficulty telling Charles of his father’s death; it was the pity for his penniless nephew and the need to soften the blow that taxed him. “Your father is ill—” began Grandet; Charles, confused, asked why he was there and whether he should order horses. Grandet’s blunt answer: horses and carriages were useless; his father was dead—he had “blown out his brains.” Then Grandet produced a newspaper article borrowed from Cruchot and pressed it under Charles’s eyes; the papers were talking. The young man burst into tears.

Grandet, observing the tears, thought, “That’s good; he’ll be all right if he weeps,” and then, not delicately, added that worse than the death was that Charles had been ruined: “He has ruined you, you haven’t a penny.” Charles’s grief turned to another horror: “What does that matter? My father! Where is my father?” he sobbed and fled to his chamber to weep alone, hiding his face in the sheets.

“The first burst must have its way,” Grandet remarked upon returning to the living-room, where Madame Grandet and Eugenie trembled. But then he showed his true concern: money. “That young man is good for nothing; his head is more taken up with the dead than with his money,” he said, contemptuous. Eugenie shuddered at such a coarse comment on the most sacred grief; from this point she began to judge her father.

Charles’s muffled sobs continued throughout the house until evening, ebbing into feebler groans. “Poor young man!” the aunt said. The remark put Grandet in mind of the sugar and the “extravagance.” He seized the opportunity to lecture: he would not have his money used to stuff the dandy with sugar. Eugenie began to explain that it was she who had arranged the breakfast; Grandet’s answer was quick and domineering. He interrupted her, reminding her that she was of age but that her father’s authority held. He dismissed Charles as “nothing at all to us; he hasn’t a farthing, his father has failed; and when this dandy has cried his fill, off he goes from here. I won’t have him revolutionize my household.”

“What is ‘failing,’ father?” Eugenie asked, naive.

“To fail,” he answered, “is to commit the most dishonorable action that can disgrace a man.” He described humiliation and theft in the same breath. Madame Grandet wondered whether their brother might be damned. Grandet was impatient of piety and scorned consolation. “To fail,” he repeated, “is to commit a theft which the law, unfortunately, takes under its protection. People entrusted their property to Guillaume Grandet trusting his honor; he has made away with it all and left them nothing but their eyes to weep with. A highway robber is better than a bankrupt: the one attacks you and you can defend yourself; the other—well, in short, Charles is dishonored.”

Upright, delicate, and simple-minded, Eugenie took her father’s atrocious explanation seriously; she could not yet distinguish involuntary failure from intentional fraud. She asked whether her father could not have prevented such a misfortune; Grandet explained that Guillaume did not consult him, and besides “he owes four millions.” She asked what a million was; Grandet offered a clumsy explanation in terms of twenty-sou pieces and napoleons: “it takes fifty thousand napoleons to make a million.” She was shocked such sums could exist. Grandet added that Charles was off to the West Indies by his father’s request to try to make a fortune; he would help pay as far as Nantes. Eugenie, grateful, threw herself into his arms, kissed him warmly, and for a rare moment the miser’s conscience pricked.

Eugenie, naive, asked how long it would take to amass a million. Grandet snorted at the impracticality: “Do you think there are francs on every bush?” At that moment a muffled cry from the garrets—more distressing—struck them all. “Nanon, go upstairs and see that he does not kill himself,” Grandet ordered, and then hurried off to see about Dutchmen leaving that day and to find Cruchot to talk over the affair. As soon as the door shut Eugenie and her mother breathed freer. That morning Eugenie had felt no constraint in her father’s presence; now her feelings and ideas began to change.

In the kitchen, Eugenie and her mother talked in low, practical tones about money. “Mamma, how many louis are there in a cask of wine?” she asked. Madame Grandet answered by speaking of the price at which the father sold wine; perhaps he was rich—but Cruchot had said he’d bought Froidfond two years ago and that might have pinched him. Unable to reconcile her father’s seeming wealth with his parsimony, Eugenie paused in her mental arithmetic.

Nanon returned: Charles had not wanted to be seen; he lay on his bed crying “like the Madeleine,” which Nanon deemed a blessing. Eugenie urged her mother to go and console him. They climbed to Charles’s room; the door stood open. The young man was inconsolable, uttering inarticulate cries. “How he loves his father!” Eugenie whispered, and in that phrase her feeling revealed a sudden passionate tenderness she did not yet understand. Madame Grandet, with a mother’s perception, warned, “Take care, you will love him!” Eugenie merely repeated his grief for her own silent reasons.

Charles saw them, turned over, and spoke of his father’s secret troubles; he had kissed him “quite coldly”—a memory that added to his anguish. He begged God to forgive his father. Madame Grandet said they would pray and resign themselves to God’s will. Eugenie, gently, tried to encourage him inwardly: “Take courage. Your loss is irreparable; therefore think only of saving your honor.” She sought to turn his thoughts to preserving what remained: his name, his character. The instinct was feminine and practical, mingled with sympathy.

“My honor?” Charles cried, seating himself and crossing his arms, then hid his face in his hands. “Ah! that is true. My uncle said my father had failed.” He burst anew into passionate lamentation, begging pardon for his father. His suffering, sincere and untheoretical, struck both women. It was a virgin grief that they understood; obeying his sign, they withdrew. They sat by the window, sewing for nearly an hour without speaking. Eugenie had noticed, in a furtive glance around his room, the pretties of his dressing-case—the gilded razors and scissors—luxuries that made the contrast with his grief more poignant and the young man more attractive. Never before had such an event stirred them so.

“Mamma,” Eugenie said at last, “we must wear mourning for my uncle.”

“Your father will decide that,” Madame Grandet replied. They relapsed into silence. Eugenie’s stitches slipped evenly under her fingers, betraying the whirl of thought. The first desire of her heart was to share her cousin’s mourning; she had become aware, with the sudden clarity of a young woman, that her compassion was turning into love.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
About four o’clock an abrupt knock at the door struck sharply on Madame Grandet’s heart.

“What can have happened to your father?” she asked their daughter.

Grandet entered in high spirits. After removing his gloves and rubbing his hard, tanned hands, his secret burst out.

“Wife,” he said without stammering, “I’ve trapped them all! Our wine is sold! The Dutch and the Belgians have gone. I loitered about the market-place in front of their inn, pretending to do nothing. That Belgian fellow—you know who I mean—came up to me. The owners of the good vineyards had held back their vintages to wait; well, I didn’t hinder them. The Belgian was in despair; in a minute the bargain was made. He takes my vintage at two hundred francs the puncheon, half down. He paid me in gold; the notes are drawn. Here are six louis for you. In three months wines will have fallen.”

Such calmly spoken, bitterly sarcastic words, if heard in Saumur’s market-place, would have provoked panic and ruined prices.

“Did you have a thousand puncheons this year, father?”

“Yes, little one.”

That pet name for his daughter was the surest expression of the old miser’s joy.

“Then that makes two hundred thousand pieces of twenty sous each?”

“Yes, Mademoiselle Grandet.”

“Then, father, you can easily help Charles.”

Cold, concentrated rage flashed across Grandet’s face; his nephew had been forgotten until Eugenie’s arithmetic inserted him into the sum. Fury was fiercer than any spectacle of Babylon’s doom.

“What’s this? Ever since that dandy set foot in my house everything goes wrong! You behave as if you had a right to buy sugar-plums and make feasts and weddings. I won’t have that sort of thing. I hope I know my duty at my time of life! I certainly sha’n’t take lessons from my daughter, or from anybody else. I shall do for my nephew what is proper, and you have no need to poke your nose into it. As for you, Eugenie,”—he faced her—“don’t speak of this again, or I’ll send you to the Abbaye des Noyers with Nanon, see if I don’t; and no later than to-morrow either, if you disobey me! Where is that fellow, has he come down yet?”

“No, my friend,” answered Madame Grandet.

“What is he doing then?”

“He is weeping for his father,” said Eugenie.

Grandet looked at his daughter without finding a word; after all, he was a father. He paced the room a few times, then hurried away to his secret den to think over an investment in the public Funds. The thinning of his two thousand acres of forest had yielded six hundred thousand francs; with sales of poplars and other gains he had nine hundred thousand francs, not counting the two hundred thousand from the sale just concluded. The twenty percent Cruchot promised in the Funds tempted him. He figured on the margin of the newspaper that gave his brother’s death account, all the while dimly hearing his nephew’s moans but not listening. Nanon knocked to summon him to dinner. On the last step he murmured to himself—

“I’ll do it; I shall get eight per cent interest. In two years I shall have fifteen hundred thousand francs, which I will then draw out in good gold,—Well, where’s my nephew?”

“He says he doesn’t want anything to eat,” Nanon answered.

“So much saved,” retorted her master.

“That’s so,” she agreed.

“Bah! he won’t cry long. Hunger drives the wolves out of the woods.”

Dinner was eaten in silence.

“My good friend,” Madame Grandet said when the cloth was removed, “we must put on mourning.”

“Upon my word, Madame Grandet! what will you invent next to spend money on? Mourning is in the heart, and not in the clothes.”

“But mourning for a brother is indispensable; and the Church commands us to—”

“Buy your mourning out of your six louis. Give me a hat-band; that’s enough for me.”

Eugenie raised her eyes to heaven without words. Her generous instincts, long repressed and now suddenly awakened, were galled. The evening passed in its usual monotony, yet it was certainly the most horrible: Eugenie sewed without lifting her head; Madame Grandet knitted; Grandet twirled his thumbs for hours, absorbed in calculations whose results would astonish Saumur the next day. No one visited; the town buzzed with Grandet’s sale, his brother’s failure, and the arrival of his nephew. The upper and middle-class wine-growers gossiped at Monsieur des Grassins, fulminating imprecations against the ex-mayor. Nanon spun; the whirr of her wheel was the only sound beneath the gray rafters.

“We don’t waste our tongues,” she said, showing her teeth like peeled almonds.

“Nothing should be wasted,” answered Grandet, rousing from reverie. He foresaw a sheet of gold—eight millions in three years—and wandered along it in fancy. “Let us go to bed. I will bid my nephew good-night for the rest of you, and see if he will take anything.”

Madame Grandet remained on the first landing to hear the coming conversation; Eugenie went up two stairs.

“Well, nephew, you are in trouble. Yes, weep, that’s natural. A father is a father; but we must bear our troubles patiently. I am a good uncle to you, remember that. Come, take courage! Will you have a little glass of wine?” (Wine costs nothing in Saumur.) “Why!” added Grandet, “you have got no light! That’s bad, very bad; you ought to see what you are about,” and he walked to the chimney-piece. “What’s this?” he cried. “A wax candle! How the devil did they filch a wax candle? The spendthrifts would tear down the ceilings of my house to boil the fellow’s eggs.”

Hearing this, mother and daughter slipped back into their rooms and burrowed in bed like frightened mice. Grandet came into his wife’s chamber.

“My friend, wait; I am saying my prayers,” the poor mother whispered, trembling.

“The devil take your good God!” growled Grandet.

Misers have little belief in a future life; the present is their all. This fact casts a harsh light on an epoch when money sways laws, politics, and morals. Institutions, books, and men conspire to undermine belief in a future beyond earthly pleasures. The grave, once a feared transition, loses terror when one believes only in this world; the future is brought into the present. To obtain by any means a terrestrial paradise of luxury—hardening the heart and macerating the body for fleeting possessions—is now widespread, inscribed even in laws that ask “What do you pay?” instead of “What do you think?” When this doctrine passes from bourgeoisie to populace, where will the country be?

“Madame Grandet, have you done?” asked the old man.

“My friend, I am praying for you.”

“Very good! Good-night; to-morrow morning we will have a talk.”

The poor woman slept like a schoolboy fearing his master’s anger. As she drew the sheet over her to stifle sound, Eugenie, in her nightgown and with bare feet, ran to her and kissed her brow.

“Oh! my good mother,” she said, “to-morrow I will tell him it was I.”

“No; he would send you to Noyers. Leave me to manage it; he cannot eat me.”

“Do you hear, mamma?”

“What?”

“He is weeping still.”

“Go to bed, my daughter; you will take cold in your feet: the floor is damp.”

Thus passed the solemn day that was to weigh upon the life of the rich and poor heiress, whose sleep was never again so calm or so pure. Certain actions seem improbable unless we trace the psychological impulses behind them. Eugenie’s sudden, deep passion should perhaps be analyzed: it became a malady affecting her existence. Many prefer to deny such effects rather than estimate the force of hidden ties in moral life. Eugenie’s past explains her naive lack of reflection and sudden emotions. The more tranquil her life had been, the more vivid her pity, the simpler the sentiments now developed.

Restless from the day’s events, she woke at intervals to listen for her cousin, thinking she heard his sighs. Sometimes she saw him dying or fainting from hunger. Toward morning she was certain she heard a cry. She dressed and ran, in the dawning light, to his chamber, whose door he had left open. The candle had burned to the socket. Charles, overcome, slept dressed in an armchair beside the bed, his head on the bedpost; he dreamed as men dream on an empty stomach. Eugenie might weep at her ease; she could admire the handsome face blotted with grief, the eyes swollen as with tears. Charles felt her presence; he opened his eyes and saw her pity.

“Pardon me, my cousin,” he said, evidently not knowing the hour or place.

“There are hearts who hear you, cousin, and we thought you might need something. You should go to bed; you tire yourself by sitting thus.”

“That is true.”

“Well, then, adieu!”

She escaped, ashamed and happy. Innocence alone can be so bold. Once enlightened, virtue calculates like vice. Eugenie, who had not trembled beside her cousin, could scarcely stand when she regained her chamber. Her ignorant life had suddenly ended; she reproached herself.

“What will he think of me? He will think that I love him!”

That was what she most wished him to think. An honest love announces itself. For this poor solitary girl, entering a young man’s chamber had the meaning of an espousal; some acts of love signify betrothal to certain souls. An hour later she dressed her mother as usual. They sat before the window waiting for Grandet with the cruel anxiety that freezes or warms the heart when a scene is feared. The goodman came down; he spoke to his wife absent-mindedly, kissed Eugenie, and sat to table without apparently remembering his threats.

“What has become of my nephew? The lad gives no trouble.”

“Monsieur, he is asleep,” answered Nanon.

“So much the better; he won’t want a wax candle,” said Grandet with a jeer.

This clemency and bitter gaiety struck Madame Grandet; she studied her husband. He took his hat and gloves, saying as he went out,—

“I am going to loiter about the market-place and find Cruchot.”

“Eugenie, your father certainly has something on his mind.”

Grandet, a poor sleeper, spent half his nights in preliminary calculations, giving accuracy to his schemes. All human power is a compound of time and patience. The miser’s life is constant exercise for self: self-love and self-interest, compact and intelligent, form his egotism. From this arises persistent curiosity and the habit of trading on others. Where is the man without desire? and what social desire can be satisfied without money?

Grandet indeed had something on his mind. Like all misers, he craved to play a commercial game, to win others’ money legally. To impose on others was a sign of power, proof he could despise those who let themselves be preyed upon. The miser fattens and slaughters the lamb, then despises it. His pasture is money and disdain. During the night his ideas had taken another course, explaining his sudden clemency. He had hatched a plot to trick the Parisians, to decoy and snare them, to make them sweat and hope—a plot to amuse himself, the old provincial cooper, sitting under his gloomy rafters. His nephew occupied his mind; he wished to save his dead brother’s honor without spending a penny on the son or himself. He would invest his own funds for three years; he needed only to manage his Saumur property. Finding nutriment for mischief in his brother’s failure, he resolved to crush the Parisians on Charles’s behalf and to play the good brother at the cheapest terms. Family honor counted so little that his intentions resembled a gambler’s interest in seeing a game well played without stake. The Cruchots were necessary; but he would not seek them—he resolved to make them come to him and to stage, that evening, a comedy whose plot he had conceived, which would make him the town’s admired man on the morrow without costing him a penny.

In his absence, Eugenie had the happiness of openly busying herself with her cousin, spending upon him fearlessly the treasures of her pity—woman’s sublime superiority, the only one she desired to have recognized and the only indulgence she pardons man for letting her assume. Three or four times she went to listen to his breathing to see if he slept; then when he rose she turned to his breakfast—the cream, eggs, fruits, the plates and glasses—each item a special care. At length she ran up the old staircase to hear the noise he made. Was he dressing? Did he still weep? She reached the door.

“My cousin!”

“Yes, cousin.”

“Will you breakfast downstairs, or in your room?”

“Where you like.”

“How do you feel?”

“Dear cousin, I am ashamed of being hungry.”

This conversation, held through the closed door, was like an episode in a poem to Eugenie.

“Well, then, we will bring your breakfast to your own room, so as not to annoy my father.”

She flew to the kitchen.

“Nanon, go and do his room!”

That staircase, often traversed and echoing to the slightest noise, lost its decaying aspect in Eugenie’s eyes. It grew luminous; it had a voice and spoke to her; it was young—young like the love it was now serving. Her kind, indulgent mother lent herself to the caprices of the child’s affection, and after the room was put in order both went to sit with the unhappy youth and keep him company. Does not Christian charity make consolation a duty? The two women drew little sophistries from religion to justify their conduct. Charles became the object of tender and loving care. His saddened heart felt the sweetness of the gentle friendship these two souls, crushed under restraint, displayed when for an instant they were unfettered in the regions of suffering.

Claiming relationship, Eugenie began to fold linen and arrange the toilet articles Charles had brought; she could admire at ease each luxurious bauble of silver and gold under the pretext of examination. Charles could not see without emotion the generous interest his aunt and cousin felt; he knew Paris society and, placed as he was, expected indifference. Eugenie thus appeared in unexpected splendor, and he admired the innocence of life and manners he had been inclined to ridicule. When Eugenie took from Nanon the bowl of coffee and cream and poured it with real feeling, giving him a kindly glance, the Parisian was filled with tears; he took her hand and kissed it.

“What troubles you?” she said.

“Oh! these are tears of gratitude,” he answered.

Eugenie abruptly turned to the chimney-piece to take the candlesticks.

“Here, Nanon, carry them away!” she said.

When she looked again she blushed, but her looks could at least deceive, not betray the excess of joy that flooded her heart; yet the eyes of both expressed the same sentiment—the future was theirs. This soft emotion was precious to Charles amid heavy grief because it was wholly unexpected. The knocker recalled the women to duty; happily they ran downstairs with speed to be seated at their work when Grandet entered; had he met them under the archway his suspicions might have been roused. After breakfast, which the goodman took standing, the keeper from Froidfond, whose promised indemnity had not yet been paid, appeared with a hare and partridges from the park, eels and two pike sent as tribute by the millers.

“Ha, ha! poor Cornoiller; here he comes, like fish in Lent. Is all that fit to eat?”

“Yes, my dear, generous master; it has been killed two days.”

“Come, Nanon, bestir yourself,” said Grandet; “take these things, they’ll do for dinner. I have invited the two Cruchots.”

Nanon opened her eyes, stupefied, and looked at everybody.

“Well!” she said, “and how am I to get the lard and the spices?”

“Wife,” said Grandet, “give Nanon six francs, and remind me to get some of the good wine out of the cellar.”

“Well, then, Monsieur Grandet,” said the keeper, prepared to settle the indemnity, “Monsieur Grandet—”

“Ta, ta, ta, ta!” replied Grandet; “I know what you want to say. You are a good fellow; we will see about it to-morrow, I’m too busy today. Wife, give him five francs,” he added to Madame Grandet as he decamped.

The poor woman was too happy to buy peace at eleven francs. She knew Grandet would let her alone for a fortnight after he had thus taken back, franc by franc, the money he had given her.

“Here, Cornoiller,” she said, slipping ten francs into his hand, “some day we will reward your services.”

Cornoiller could say nothing and went away.

“Madame,” said Nanon, who had donned her black coif and taken her basket, “I want only three francs. You keep the rest; it’ll go fast enough somehow.”

“Have a good dinner, Nanon; my cousin will come down,” said Eugenie.

“Something very extraordinary is going on, I am certain,” said Madame Grandet. “This is only the third time since our marriage that your father has given a dinner.”

About four o’clock, just as Eugenie and her mother had finished setting the table for six and the master had brought up a few bottles of the exquisite wine provincials cherish, Charles came down into the hall. He was pale; his gestures, expression, glance, and voice had a sadness full of grace. He truly suffered; the veil of pain gave him an air dear to women. Eugenie loved him the more for it. Sorrow had leveled the distance between them—Charles was no longer the rich, distinguished young man above her sphere, but a relation plunged into frightful misery. Misery begets equality. Women, like angels, claim suffering humanity. Charles and Eugenie spoke only with their eyes; the poor fallen dandy, orphaned and impoverished, sat apart, proud, calm, silent. From time to time the gentle caressing glance of the young girl shone upon him and drew him from sad thoughts into fields of hope, where she loved to hold him at her side.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At this moment Saumur buzzed more about the dinner Grandet gave the Cruchots than about the previous night's sale of his vintage, though that sale had outraged the wine-growers. If the politic old miser had entertained for a noble motive he might have been praised; believing himself above the townspeople, he cared little for their opinion.

The des Grassins soon learned of Guillaume Grandet’s failure and violent death and resolved to call that evening to commiserate and learn why he had invited the Cruchots. At five Monsieur C. de Bonfons and his uncle the notary arrived in their Sunday clothes. They dined with appetites; Grandet was grave, Charles silent, Eugenie mute, Madame Grandet reserved. When they rose, Charles said he must retire to undertake a long and painful correspondence.

As soon as the aunt and uncle were sure Charles could not overhear, Grandet dismissed his wife and daughter and, alone with the two Cruchots, put on the cunning that had earned him the nickname “the old dog.” He affected a stutter and slight deafness—tricks to manage conversation and dodge responsibility—to keep his intentions hidden.

He began, stammering, to raise the subject of bankruptcy. The president, flattered by Grandet’s awkwardly deferential address, took the lead and explained how a threatened merchant might avoid dishonor by liquidating rather than being declared insolvent. He described how creditors might buy up notes in the market at a reduction so that, if one held title to the debts and paid them off by agreement, the estate would be honorably released. Grandet, feigning ignorance, professed he knew nothing of such Paris tricks and that he lived in Saumur attending to vines and drains. He admitted he might be able to help his nephew, but must consider, for he had neither the time nor the knowledge to engage personally.

The president—smooth, sniffing snuff—went on about notes as merchandise, invoking Bentham’s reasoning, and suggested debts might be bought for twenty-five percent. The notary laughed; the president added practicalities: a friend could go to Paris, buy up the notes at a reduction, obtain extensions from creditors, and thus save the family’s honor. Grandet pretended distress at the complexity and expense, repeatedly claiming he was only a poor wine-grower; but his eye betrayed eagerness.

The notary, uneasy, warned his nephew to let Monsieur Grandet explain his intentions. Before Cruchot could resume, the des Grassins arrived with expressions of condolence. The notary took the opportunity to announce Grandet’s honorable intention to liquidate his brother’s affairs and that the president had offered to go to Paris to negotiate with the creditors. Des Grassins, who had been whispering on the way about the old man’s avarice and had almost accused him of fratricide, were astonished and delighted.

“Grandet is honorable to the backbone,” cried the banker, embracing him. The arrival allowed the des Grassins to present themselves as friends with business sense. The banker suggested that such a commercial matter required a consummate business man and offered to take charge, since he was going to Paris and could attend to it amid his own affairs.

Dropping some assumed hesitancy, Grandet confided privately to des Grassins that he trusted the banker more than the president and had other plans. He wanted to buy a few thousand francs in the Funds while the price was low; des Grassins knew about such things and could make the purchase by the end of the month. He signaled that none of this should be told to the Cruchots. The banker, pleased, promised to begin by the mail-post and to take final directions at five o’clock, just before dinner.

The parties lingered; des Grassins struck Grandet on the shoulder, murmuring it was fortunate to have such a relation. Grandet, rubbing his wen, declared his love for his brother and his intention to prove it—unless costs prevented him. Des Grassins had thus cut the Cruchots out of the commission; the president, disappointed at being sidelined, complained to his uncle that he had begun by being “de Bonfons” and ended as nothing but Cruchot. The notary advised modesty: let the des Grassins take this tack, and Eugenie would remain the president’s wife all the same.

In short order news of Grandet’s magnanimity spread through three houses and then the town. Everyone forgave his sale that had defied pledged good faith and lauded his apparent generosity; such sudden enthusiasm is a French tendency, quick to forget and quick to praise.

When Père Grandet shut the door he called Nanon and instructed her not to let the dog loose or go to bed; they had work to do. At eleven Cornoiller would bring the chariot from Froidfond; Nanon must tell him to come softly, for there must be no nocturnal racket and no neighborly curiosity. Grandet returned to his private room, moving cautiously so as not to wake his wife and daughter and, above all, not to rouse his nephew, whom he had already begun to condemn for his pale, despairing looks.

About the middle of the night Eugenie, anxious for Charles, fancied she heard a cry like that of a dying person. She wrapped herself in a pelisse and, thinking of the light under her cousin’s door, was about to go out when she saw Nanon’s heavy steps and heard horses. Peering into the corridor she met her father’s vague, unnoticing gaze and was terrified. Grandet and Nanon were linked by a stout stick; a thick rope over their shoulders carried a small keg like those Grandet made in the bakehouse—filled, as Nanon complained, with heavy copper sous. Grandet cautioned them to be careful of the candlestick; the scene was lit by a single candle on the staircase.

Cornoiller had come with horses; Grandet asked if he had pistols—there was nothing to fear—and told Nanon to say to his wife that he had gone into the country and would be back to dinner. The chariot drove off with all possible secrecy; Nanon bolted the great door, let the dog loose, and went to bed with a bruised shoulder, the neighbors unaware of either the departure or its purpose. Grandet’s precautions and reticence were habitual; no one had ever seen a penny in that house, though it was filled with gold.

In the morning, gossip at the port reported that exchange on gold had doubled because of military preparations at Nantes and that speculators had come to Angers to buy coin. The old wine-grower, having borrowed horses from his farmers, had seized the chance: he had gone to sell his gold and to bring back treasury notes, converting the sum he intended to put into the Funds and swelling it by advantage of the exchange.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“My father has gone,” thought Eugenie, hearing it from the head of the stairs. Silence fell; the carriage’s rumble died away. A cry came from her cousin’s room; a thin strip of light crossed the rotten stair.

“He suffers!” she said, rushing up. A moan brought her to the landing. The door stood ajar; she pushed it open. Charles slept with his head drooped over the old chair’s arm, the pen fallen from his hand nearly touching the floor. His strained posture alarmed her. She entered.

“He must be very tired,” she thought, and glanced at a dozen sealed letters on the table. She read the addresses—carriage-makers, a tailor—and guessed he had been settling affairs to leave France. Her eyes fell on two open letters. The words “My dear Annette” at the head of one stunned her. Her heart beat fast; her feet felt nailed.

“His dear Annette! He loves! He is loved! No hope! What does he say to her?” She saw the words everywhere. “I ought to go away—What if I do read it?”

She looked at Charles, then gently set his head against the chair back; he let her, like a sleeping child. She raised his drooping hand and kissed his chestnut hair. “Dear Annette!” a demon shrieked in her ear.

“I am doing wrong; but I must read it,” she decided, torn by honor and curiosity. Passion and curiosity triumphed. As she read, her heart swelled; the keen glow of first love made every feeling precious.

My dear Annette,—Nothing could have separated us but the great misfortune that has fallen on me. My father has killed himself; his fortune and mine are irretrievably lost. I am an orphan at an age when, by my education, I am still a child; yet I must lift myself as a man out of the abyss. If I wish to leave France an honest man—I am without a hundred francs to try my luck in the Indies or America. Yes, Anna, I must seek my fortune in those deadly climates. I cannot remain in Paris. Neither my nature nor my face are made to bear the affronts, neglect, disdain shown to a ruined man, the son of a bankrupt! Good God! think of owing two millions! I should be killed in a duel the first week; therefore I shall not return. Your love—the most tender and devoted love—cannot draw me back. I have no money to go to you, to give and receive a last kiss that might strengthen me.

“Poor Charles! I did well to read the letter. I have gold; I will give it to him,” thought Eugenie.

She wiped her eyes and read on.

I have never thought of the miseries of poverty. If I had the hundred louis for the journey, I have not a sou for an outfit. But no—I have not the hundred louis, not even one. I don’t know what will be left after I have paid my debts in Paris. If I have nothing, I shall go quietly to Nantes and ship as a common sailor; I will begin in the new world like other men who started with nothing and made fortunes. I have faced my future coolly. It seems more horrible to me because I was so petted by a mother who adored me, so indulged by a kindly father, so blessed to have known the love of an Anna. The flowers of life are all I have known. Such happiness could not last. Nevertheless I feel more courage than is supposed for a careless young man—above all, one used to caressing; oh, my father! Annette, he is dead!

I have thought of my position and yours. I have grown old in twenty-four hours. Dear Anna, if to keep me with you in Paris you were to sacrifice your luxury, your dress, your opera-box, we should still not have enough for my extravagant ways. Besides, I would never accept such sacrifices. No, we must part now and forever—

“He gives her up! Blessed Virgin! What happiness!” Eugenie quivered with joy. Charles moved; terror chilled her. He did not wake, and she resumed reading.

When shall I return? I do not know. The climate of the West Indies ages a European who works hard. In ten years your daughter will be eighteen; she may be your companion, your spy. Society will be cruel, daughters sometimes cruel; remember four years of happiness and, if you can, be faithful to my memory. I cannot demand faithfulness, for I must adapt to my new life and take a commonplace view of it. Therefore marriage becomes a necessity; I have found here in Saumur, in my uncle’s house, a cousin whose face, manners, mind, and heart would please you, and who, besides, seems to me—

“He must have been very weary to have ceased writing to her,” thought Eugenie as the letter broke off.

She excused him. How could an innocent girl suspect cold-heartedness in such a letter? Young, religiously brought up girls see love’s light everywhere; they reflect their warmth on their lover and attribute to him their highest thoughts. A woman’s errors often spring from belief in good and trust in truth. Eugenie’s simple heart heard “My dear Annette” like the sweetest words; the tears on the young man’s lashes seemed noble. How could she know that Charles, though he loved his father and mourned him, was moved more by paternal indulgence than by heroic resolve? Monsieur and Madame Guillaume Grandet had gratified every fancy of their son and lavished pleasures on him, keeping him from the hard reckonings other sons face.

Still, Charles was a child of Paris, schooled by society and by Annette to calculate; already, beneath youthful grace, he had learned the bitter prudence of social life, where in one evening more crimes of thought and speech are committed than justice punishes; where to “see clear” one must believe in nothing and weigh a friend’s purse daily—self-interest the mainspring of all things. After folly and adulation, the great lady—Annette—had made him think soberly; with a perfumed hand among his curls she spoke to him of his future, taught worldly prudence, effeminized and materialized him—a refined but real corruption.

Nearly all young girls yield to the promises such outward appearance offers: even had Eugenie been as prudent as many provincial girls, she would have found it hard to distrust a cousin whose manners and words matched youthful hopes. A fatal chance laid before her Charles’s last effusions; she heard the last stirrings of his conscience. She laid down the letter—so full of love—and smiled, watching him sleep; the fresh illusions of life were still on his face; she vowed to love him always. Then she glanced at the other letter, reading it for new proofs of noble qualities she already believed him to possess.

My dear Alphonse,—When you receive this letter I shall be without friends; but while I doubt the friendship of the world, I have never doubted yours. I beg you to settle my affairs and get as much as you can out of my possessions. You know my situation. I have nothing left, and I intend to go at once to the Indies. I have written to those I think I owe money, and enclose a list. My books, furniture, pictures, horses, etc., ought to pay my debts. I do not wish to keep anything, except perhaps a few baubles for my outfit. I will send a power of attorney so you can make these sales. Send me all my weapons. Keep Briton for yourself; nobody would pay his value, and I would rather give him to you—like a mourning-ring bequeathed by a dying man to his executor. Farry, Breilmann, & Co. built me a travelling-carriage not yet delivered; persuade them to keep it and not ask for payment. If they refuse, do what you can and avoid anything dishonorable. I owe the British Islander six louis lost at cards; don’t fail to pay him—

“Dear cousin!” whispered Eugenie, throwing down the letter and running softly back to her room, carrying a lighted candle. A thrill passed as she opened the drawer of an old oak cabinet topped with the faint royal salamander. She took out a large red velvet purse edged with gold fringe—a relic from her grandmother. She weighed it proudly and began counting the forgotten hoard: portugaises, genovines, Spanish quadruples, Dutch ducats of 1756, Indian rupees, and a napoleon of forty francs received the day before. Her father loved these coins for rarity, but Eugenie thought only of Charles. After a few miscalculations she made out about five thousand eight hundred francs in intrinsic value—nearly six thousand for collectors. She clapped her hands like a child. Father and daughter had each counted their fortune—he, to sell his gold; she, to fling hers into the sea of affection. She put the coins back, took the purse, and ran upstairs without hesitation. The hidden misery of her cousin made her forget hour and propriety; she was strong in conscience, devotion, and happiness.

At the threshold, holding the candle and purse, Charles woke and was speechless with surprise. Eugenie set the candle on the table.

“My cousin, I must beg pardon for a wrong I have done you; but God will pardon me—if you—will help me to wipe it out.”

“What is it?” asked Charles, rubbing his eyes.

“I have read those letters.”

Charles colored.

“How did it happen?” she continued; “how came I here? Truly, I do not know. I am tempted not to regret too much that I have read them; they have made me know your heart, your soul, and—”

“And what?” asked Charles.

“Your plans, your need of a sum—”

“My dear cousin—”

“Hush, hush! not so loud; we must not wake others. See,” she said, opening her purse, “here are the savings of a poor girl who wants nothing. Charles, accept them! This morning I did not value money; you have taught me. It is but a means. A cousin is almost a brother; you can surely borrow your sister’s purse.”

Eugenie, both woman and girl, never dreamed of refusal; but Charles remained silent.

“Oh! you will not refuse?” cried Eugenie, whose heart could be heard beating in the silence.

His hesitation mortified her, but the need of his position became clearer, and she knelt.

“I will never rise till you have taken that gold!” she said. “My cousin, I implore you, answer me! let me know if you respect me, if you are generous, if—”

Moved, Charles took her hands to keep her from kneeling; his tears fell on them. Eugenie sprang to the purse and poured its contents on the table.

“Ah! yes, yes, you consent?” she sobbed. “Fear nothing, my cousin, you will be rich. This gold will bring you happiness; some day you shall bring it back to me—are we not partners? I will obey all conditions. But you should not attach such value to the gift.”

Charles could finally speak.

“Yes, Eugenie; my soul would be small indeed if I did not accept. And yet—gift for gift, confidence for confidence.”

“What do you mean?” she said, frightened.

“Listen, dear cousin; I have here—” He pointed to a square box covered by a leather case on the drawers. “There,” he continued, “is something as precious to me as life itself. This box was a present from my mother. All day I have thought that if she could rise from her grave, she would herself sell the gold which her love for me lavished on this dressing-case; but were I to do so, it would seem a sacrilege.” Eugenie pressed his hand. “No,” he added after a pause with a tender look, “no, I will neither sell it nor risk its safety on my journey. Dear Eugenie, you shall be its guardian. Never did friend commit anything more sacred to another. Let me show it to you.”

He opened the case and revealed a richly wrought dressing-case with gold ornaments. “What you admire is nothing,” he said, pushing a spring that revealed a hidden drawer. He drew out two portraits, masterpieces by Madame Mirbel, richly set with pearls.

“Oh, how beautiful! Is it the lady to whom you wrote that—”

“No,” he smiled; “this is my mother, and here is my father, your aunt and uncle. Eugenie, I beg you on my knees, keep my treasure safely. If I die and your little fortune is lost, this gold and these pearls will repay you. To you alone could I leave these portraits; you are worthy to keep them. But destroy them at last, so they may pass into no other hands.” Eugenie was silent. “Ah, yes, say yes! You consent?” he added with winning grace.

Hearing his words as if he addressed himself, she turned to him with a look of love—the first look of womanhood, half coquette, half depth. He kissed her hand.

“Angel of purity! between us two money is nothing, never can be anything. Feeling and sentiment must be all henceforth.”

“You are like your mother—was her voice as soft as yours?”

“Oh! much softer—”

“Yes, for you,” she said, dropping her eyelids. “Come, Charles, go to bed; I wish it; you must be tired. Good-night.” She disengaged her hand; he lighted her way to her room. At the threshold—

“Ah!” he said, “why am I ruined?”

“What matter?—my father is rich; I think so,” she answered.

“Poor child!” said Charles, stepping in and leaning against the wall, “if that were so, he would never have let my father die; he would not let you live in this poor way; he would live otherwise himself.”

“But he owns Froidfond.”

“What is Froidfond worth?”
