If Charles had dropped from the Indian Isles he would have found the same people and the same interests. Madame des Grassins, to whom Eugenie was courteous, still tormented the Cruchots. Eugenie remained the central figure; and Charles, as before, would still have been sovereign. Yet there had been small progress. The flowers which the president formerly presented on her birthdays had become a daily institution. Every evening he brought the rich heiress a huge bouquet, which Madame Cornoiller placed conspicuously in a vase and secretly threw into a corner of the court-yard when the visitors had departed.

Early in spring Madame des Grassins tried to disturb the Cruchotines by speaking to Eugenie of the Marquis de Froidfond, whose ancient and ruined family might be restored if the heiress returned his estates through marriage. Madame des Grassins rang the changes on peerage and the marquise title until, mistaking Eugenie’s disdainful smile for acquiescence, she went about proclaiming that the marriage with “Monsieur Cruchot” was not as certain as people thought.

“Though Monsieur de Froidfond is fifty,” she said, “he does not look older than Monsieur Cruchot. He is a widower and has children, that’s true. But then he is a marquis; he will be peer of France; and in times like these where will you find a better match? I know for a fact that Pere Grandet, when he put all his money into Froidfond, intended to graft himself upon that stock; he often told me so. He was a deep one, that old man!”

“Ah! Nanon,” said Eugenie one night as she was going to bed, “how is it that in seven years he has never once written to me?”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
While these events unfolded in Saumur, Charles was making his fortune in the Indies. His commercial outfit sold well; he realized six thousand dollars. Crossing the line cleared many cobwebs from his brain; he perceived that the surest way to fortune in the tropics, as in Europe, was to buy and sell men. He went to the African coast and bought Negroes, combining this traffic with other profitable merchandise. He plunged into the business tirelessly, driven by the desire to return to Paris with the prestige of a large fortune and the hope of regaining a more brilliant position than the one he had lost.

Jostling with men, traveling through many lands, and observing conflicting customs modified his ideas and made him skeptical. He ceased to have fixed principles, since what one country called crime another praised as virtue. In the perpetual struggle of selfish interests his heart grew cold and contracted. The blood of the Grandets did its work: Charles became hard and eager for prey. He sold Chinamen, Negroes, bird-nests, children, artists; practiced usury on a large scale; his habit of defrauding custom-houses made him less scrupulous about others’ rights. He went to St. Thomas and bought, for a song, goods captured by pirates and took them to ports where he could sell them well. If Eugenie’s pure face had accompanied him on his first voyage, like an image of the Virgin on a mast, and if he attributed his first success to her prayers, later women—blacks, mulattoes, whites, Indian dancing-girls—orgies and adventures effaced recollection of his cousin, Saumur, the house, the bench, the stolen kiss. He remembered only the little garden behind the crumbling wall, where he had learned his fate; but he rejected his family. His uncle was an old dog who had filched his jewels; Eugenie had no place in his heart, though she remained in his accounts as a creditor for six thousand francs.

Such conduct explains Charles Grandet’s silence. In the Indies, at St. Thomas, on the African coast, at Lisbon, and in the United States, the adventurer used the name Shepherd so as not to compromise his family. As Charles Shepherd he could be indefatigable, bold, grasping, and greedy, like a man resolved to snatch his fortune quibuscumque viis and get the villainy over with to live honestly thereafter.

With such methods prosperity was rapid; and in 1827 Charles returned to Bordeaux on the brig Marie Caroline, belonging to a royalist house. He brought nineteen hundred thousand francs worth of gold-dust, from which he expected another seven or eight per cent at the Paris mint. On the brig he met Monsieur d’Aubrion, a gentleman-in-ordinary to Charles X., a worthy old man who had married a fashionable woman with a fortune from the West Indies. To meet Madame d’Aubrion’s extravagance he had gone to the Indies to sell property and was now returning with his family.

Monsieur and Madame d’Aubrion, of the old southern family d’Aubrion de Buch, were reduced to about twenty thousand francs income and had an ugly daughter whom the mother was resolved to marry off without a dot—the family fortune barely sufficing for her own expenses in Paris. The mother almost despaired of placing such a girl, but she made the best of her. Mademoiselle d’Aubrion was long, spare, with a disdainful mouth and a nose too long, thick at the end, sallow normally and very red after a meal—an unpleasant feature in a pale, dull face. Madame d’Aubrion made her daughter appear distinguished: hygienic treatment kept the nose manageable, she taught the art of dressing, charming manners, melancholy glances that interest a man, and the coquettish peep of the foot beneath a petticoat at the moment the nose reddened. By full sleeves, deceptive pads, puffed dresses, and tight corsets she had achieved strange feminine developments worth showing as an example to other mothers.

Charles became intimate with Madame d’Aubrion precisely because she sought his intimacy. Persons on the brig said the handsome Madame neglected no means to secure so rich a son-in-law. Landing at Bordeaux in June 1827, Monsieur, Madame, Mademoiselle d’Aubrion, and Charles lodged at the same hotel and set out together for Paris. The hotel d’Aubrion was hampered with mortgages; Charles was to free it. The mother told him she would gladly give the ground-floor to a son-in-law. Not sharing Monsieur d’Aubrion’s prejudices about nobility, she promised Charles that she would obtain a royal ordinance from Charles X. allowing him to take the d’Aubrion name and arms and, by buying the entailed estate for thirty-six thousand francs a year, to succeed to the titles of Captal de Buch and Marquis d’Aubrion. By uniting fortunes, living together, and profiting by sinecures, the two families might occupy the hotel d’Aubrion with an income exceeding a hundred thousand francs.

“And when a man has a hundred thousand francs a year, a name, a family, and a position at court—for I will get you appointed gentleman-of-the-bedchamber—you can do what you like,” she told Charles. “You can become master of the rolls, prefect, secretary to an embassy, ambassador if you like. Charles X. is fond of d’Aubrion; they have known each other from childhood.”

Intoxicated with ambition, Charles toyed with the hopes thus presented in confidences poured from heart to heart. Believing his father’s affairs settled by his uncle, he imagined himself anchored in the Faubourg Saint-Germain—reappearing as the Comte d’Aubrion, like the Dreux reappearing in Bréze. Dazzled by the Restoration’s prosperity, which had been tottering when he left France, fascinated by aristocratic splendor, his intoxication, begun on the brig, increased after he reached Paris, and he resolved to take the course his would-be mother-in-law indicated. His cousin counted for no more than a speck in this brilliant prospect; but he went to see Annette. A woman of the world, Annette advised him to marry and promised her support in his ambitious projects. In her heart she was delighted to fasten an ugly, uninteresting girl on Charles, whose West Indies life had made him very attractive: bronzed complexion, bold, decisive manners like a man used to sharp decisions, to rule, and to succeed. Charles breathed easier in Paris, conscious of his part to play.

Des Grassins, hearing of his return, his approaching marriage, and his fortune, came to see him and inquired about the three hundred thousand francs still required to settle his father’s debts. He found Grandet conferring with a goldsmith about jewels for Mademoiselle d’Aubrion’s corbeille. Charles had brought magnificent diamonds; the setting with plate and jewelry for the new establishment amounted to over two hundred thousand francs. When des Grassins, who had already called several times, presented himself, Charles treated him with the impertinence of a young man of fashion conscious of having killed four men in as many duels in the Indies. Coldly, and without fully grasping the plea, Charles replied:

“My father’s affairs are not mine. I am much obliged, monsieur, for the trouble you have been good enough to take—by which, however, I really cannot profit. I have not earned two millions by the sweat of my brow to fling them at the head of my father’s creditors.”

“But suppose that your father’s estate were within a few days to be declared bankrupt?”

“Monsieur, in a few days I shall be called the Comte d’Aubrion; you will understand, therefore, that what you threaten is of no consequence to me. Besides, you know as well as I do that when a man has an income of a hundred thousand francs his father has never failed.” So saying, he edged Monsieur des Grassins to the door.

At the beginning of August that year, Eugenie was sitting on the little wooden bench where her cousin had sworn to love her eternally, where she usually breakfasted when the weather was fine. The poor girl was happy for the moment in the fresh summer air, letting memory recall the great and little events of her love and the catastrophes that followed. The sun had just reached the angle of the ruined wall full of chinks that no one, by the mistress’s caprice, was allowed to touch, though Cornoiller often told his wife it would fall and crush somebody one day. At that moment the postman knocked and gave a letter to Madame Cornoiller, who ran into the garden, crying:

“Mademoiselle, a letter!” She gave it to her mistress, adding, “Is it the one you expected?”

The words rang as loudly in Eugenie’s heart as they echoed from wall to wall of the court and garden.

“Paris—from him—he has returned!”

Eugenie turned pale and held the letter for a moment. She trembled and could not break the seal. La Grande Nanon stood before her, hands on her hips, her joy showing in the lines of her face.

“Read it, mademoiselle!”

“Ah, Nanon, why did he return to Paris? He went from Saumur.”

“Read it, and you’ll find out.”

Eugenie opened the letter with trembling fingers. A cheque on the house of “Madame des Grassins and Coret, of Saumur,” fluttered out. Nanon picked it up.

My dear Cousin,—

“No longer ‘Eugenie,’” she thought, and her heart quailed.

You—

“He once said ‘thou.’” She folded her arms and dared not read further; great tears gathered.

“Is he dead?” asked Nanon.

“If he were, he could not write,” said Eugenie.

She read the whole letter, which ran as follows:

My dear Cousin,—You will, I am sure, hear with pleasure of the success of my enterprise. You brought me luck; I have come back rich, and I have followed my uncle’s advice, whose death, together with my aunt’s, I have just learned from Monsieur des Grassins. The death of parents is in the course of nature, and we must succeed them. I trust you are by this time consoled. Nothing can resist time, as I know. Yes, my dear cousin, the day of illusions is, unfortunately, gone for me. How could it be otherwise? Traveling through many lands, I have reflected on life. I was a child when I went away—I have come back a man. Today I think of many I did not dream of then. You are free, my dear cousin, and I am free still. Nothing apparently hinders the realization of our early hopes; but my nature is too loyal to hide from you the situation in which I find myself. I have not forgotten our relations; I have always remembered, throughout my long wanderings, the little wooden seat—

Eugenie rose as if sitting on live coals and went to the stone steps of the court.

—the little wooden seat where we vowed to love each other forever, the passage, the gray hall, my attic chamber, and the night when, by your delicate kindness, you made my future easier. Yes, these recollections sustained my courage; I said in my heart that you were thinking of me at the hour we had agreed upon. Have you always looked at the clouds at nine o’clock? Yes, I am sure of it. I cannot betray so true a friendship—no, I must not deceive you. An alliance has been proposed to me which satisfies all my ideas of matrimony. Love in marriage is a delusion. My experience warns me that in marrying we are bound to obey social laws and meet the world’s conventional demands. Between you and me there are differences which might affect your future more than mine. I will not speak here of your customs, inclinations, education, nor your habits, none of which fit Parisian life or the future I have marked out. My intention is to keep my household stately, to receive much company—in short, to live in the world; and I think I remember that you love a quiet and tranquil life. I will be frank and make you judge my situation; you have the right to understand and judge it.

I possess now an income of eighty thousand francs. This fortune enables me to marry into the Aubrion family, whose heiress, nineteen years old, brings me a title, a place as gentleman-of-the-bedchamber, and a brilliant position. I will admit, my dear cousin, that I do not love Mademoiselle d’Aubrion; but in marrying her I secure to my children a social rank whose advantages will become incalculable: monarchical principles are gaining favor. Thus my son, when he becomes Marquis d’Aubrion, having an entailed estate with a rental of forty thousand francs a year, can obtain any State position he chooses. We owe ourselves to our children.

You see, my cousin, with what good faith I lay my heart, hopes, and fortune before you. Possibly, after seven years’ separation, you have yourself forgotten our youthful loves; but I have never forgotten your kindness or my own words. I remember all, even lightly uttered words—words by which a less conscientious man, with a heart less upright, would scarcely feel bound. In telling you the marriage I propose is solely one of convenience, that I still remember our childish love, am I not putting myself entirely in your hands and making you the mistress of my fate? Am I not telling you that if I must renounce my social ambitions, I shall willingly content myself with the pure and simple happiness of which you have shown me so sweet an image?

“Tan, ta, ta—tan, ta, ti,” sang Charles Grandet to the air of Non più andrai, as he signed himself,—

Your devoted cousin, Charles.

“Thunder! that’s doing it handsomely!” he said, looking for the cheque; having found it, he added:

P.S.—I enclose a cheque on the des Grassins bank for eight thousand francs to your order, payable in gold, which includes the capital and interest of the sum you were kind enough to lend me. I am expecting a case from Bordeaux which contains a few things which you must allow me to offer you as a mark of my unceasing gratitude. You can send my dressing-case by the diligence to the hotel d’Aubrion, rue Hillerin-Bertin.

“By the diligence!” said Eugenie. “A thing for which I would have laid down my life!”

Terrible and utter disaster! The ship had gone down, leaving not a spar, not a plank, on a vast ocean of hope. Some women, abandoned, will try to tear a lover from a rival, commit crimes, or rush to the ends of the earth. Others bow their heads and suffer in silence; they go their way dying, resigned, weeping, forgiving, praying, and recollecting until the last breath. This was Eugenie’s love after she read that dreadful letter. She raised her eyes to heaven, thinking of her dying mother’s last words, who, with the prescience of death, had looked into the future: Eugenie, remembering that prophetic life, measured her destiny. Nothing was left for her; she could only unfold her wings, stretch upward, and live in prayer until deliverance.

“My mother was right,” she said, weeping. “Suffer—and die!”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Eugenie came slowly back from the garden to the house, avoiding the corridor. The memory of her cousin lingered in the gray old hall and on the chimney-piece, where stood the saucer and the Sèvres sugar-bowl she used each morning.

This day was to be solemn and eventful. Nanon announced the parish cure, connected to the Cruchots and allied with Monsieur de Bonfons’s interests. For some time the old abbé had urged him, from a religious point of view, to speak to Mademoiselle Grandet about the duty of marriage for a woman in her position. When she saw her pastor, Eugenie supposed he had come for the thousand francs she gave monthly to the poor, and she told Nanon to fetch them; the cure only smiled.

“To-day, mademoiselle,” he said, “I have come to speak to you about a poor girl in whom the whole town of Saumur takes an interest, who, through lack of charity to herself, neglects her Christian duties.”

“Monsieur le curé, you have come to me at a moment when I cannot think of my neighbor; I am filled with thoughts of myself. I am very unhappy; my only refuge is in the Church; her bosom is large enough to hold all human woe, her love so full that we may draw from its depths and never drain it dry.”

“Mademoiselle, in speaking of this young girl we shall speak of you. Listen! If you wish to insure your salvation you have only two paths—either leave the world or obey its laws. Obey either your earthly destiny or your heavenly destiny.”

“Ah! your voice speaks to me when I need a voice. Yes, God has sent you to me; I will bid farewell to the world and live for God alone, in silence and seclusion.”

“My daughter, you must think long before you take so violent a step. Marriage is life, the veil is death.”

“Yes, death,—a quick death!” she said, with dreadful eagerness.

“Death? but you have great obligations to society, mademoiselle. Are you not the mother of the poor, to whom you give clothes and wood in winter and work in summer? Your great fortune is a loan which you must return, and you have sacredly accepted it as such. To bury yourself in a convent would be selfishness; to remain an old maid is to fail in duty. In the first place, can you manage your vast property alone? May you not lose it? You will have lawsuits, you will find yourself surrounded by inextricable difficulties. Believe your pastor: a husband is useful; you are bound to preserve what God has bestowed upon you. I speak to you as a precious lamb of my flock. You love God too truly not to find your salvation in the midst of his world, of which you are a noble ornament and to which you owe your example.”

At that moment Madame des Grassins was announced. She came driven by vengeance and despair.

“Mademoiselle,” she said—“Ah! here is monsieur le curé; I am silent. I came to speak to you on business; but I see that you are conferring with—”

“Madame,” said the curé, “I leave the field to you.”

“Oh! monsieur le curé,” said Eugenie, “come back later; your support is very necessary to me just now.”

“Ah, yes, indeed, my poor child!” said Madame des Grassins.

“What do you mean?” asked Eugenie and the curé together.

“Don’t I know about your cousin’s return, and his marriage with Mademoiselle d’Aubrion? A woman doesn’t carry her wits in her pocket.”

Eugenie blushed and was silent for a moment. From this day forth she assumed the impassible countenance for which her father had been remarkable.

“Well, madame,” she presently said, ironically, “no doubt I carry my wits in my pocket, for I do not understand you. Speak, say what you mean, before monsieur le curé; you know he is my director.”

“Well, then, mademoiselle, here is what des Grassins writes me. Read it.”

Eugenie read the letter:

  My dear Wife,—Charles Grandet has returned from the Indies and has been in Paris about a month—

“A month!” thought Eugenie, her hand falling to her side. After a pause she resumed the letter,—

  I had to dance attendance before I was allowed to see the future Vicomte d’Aubrion. Though all Paris is talking of his marriage and the banns are published—

“He wrote to me after that!” thought Eugenie. She did not cry out, as a Parisian woman would have done, “The villain!” but though she said it not, contempt was none the less present in her mind.

  The marriage, however, will not come off. The Marquis d’Aubrion will never give his daughter to the son of a bankrupt. I went to tell Grandet of the steps his uncle and I took in his father’s business, and the clever manoeuvres by which we had managed to keep the creditor’s quiet until the present time. The insolent fellow had the face to say to me—to me, who for five years have devoted myself night and day to his interests and his honor!—that his father’s affairs were not his! A solicitor would have had the right to demand fees amounting to thirty or forty thousand francs, one per cent on the total of the debts. But patience! there are twelve hundred thousand francs legitimately owing to the creditors, and I shall at once declare his father a bankrupt.

  I went into this business on the word of that old crocodile Grandet, and I have made promises in the name of his family. If Monsieur le vicomte d’Aubrion does not care for his honor, I care for mine. I shall explain my position to the creditors. Still, I have too much respect for Mademoiselle Eugenie (to whom under happier circumstances we once hoped to be allied) to act in this matter before you have spoken to her about it—

There Eugenie paused, and coldly returned the letter without finishing it.

“I thank you,” she said to Madame des Grassins.

“Ah! you have the voice and manner of your deceased father,” Madame des Grassins replied.

“Madame, you have eight thousand francs to pay us,” said Nanon, producing Charles’s cheque.

“That’s true; have the kindness to come with me now, Madame Cornoiller.”

“Monsieur le curé,” said Eugenie with a noble composure, inspired by the thought she was about to express, “would it be a sin to remain a virgin after marriage?”

“That is a case of conscience whose solution is not within my knowledge. If you wish to know what the celebrated Sanchez says of it in his treatise ‘De Matrimonio,’ I shall be able to tell you to-morrow.”

The curé went away; Mademoiselle Grandet withdrew to her father’s secret room and spent the day there alone, not coming down to dinner despite Nanon’s entreaties. She appeared in the evening at the hour when the usual company began to arrive. Never was the old hall so full. The news of Charles’s return and his treachery had spread through the whole town. But however watchful the visitors might be, they were left unsatisfied. Eugenie, expecting scrutiny, allowed none of the cruel emotions that wrung her soul to appear. She met mournful looks and melancholy speeches with a smiling front, hiding misery behind courtesy. Towards nine the games ended and the players left, paying their losses and discussing the play. At the moment when the whole party rose to take leave, an unexpected event occurred, which resounded through Saumur and beyond.

“Stay, monsieur le président,” said Eugenie to Monsieur de Bonfons as she saw him take his cane.

There was not a person in that assembly who was unmoved by these words. The president turned pale and was forced to sit down.

“The president gets the millions,” said Mademoiselle de Gribeaucourt.

“It is plain enough; the president marries Mademoiselle Grandet,” cried Madame d’Orsonval.

“All the trumps in one hand,” said the abbé.

“A love game,” said the notary.

Each made his pun and looked at the heiress mounted on her millions. The drama begun nine years before had reached its conclusion. In provincial towns social conventionalities are rigidly enforced; an infraction like this constituted a solemn promise.

When they were left alone, Eugenie spoke with restrained feeling. “Monsieur le président,” she said, “I know what pleases you in me. Swear to leave me free during my whole life, to claim none of the rights which marriage will give you over me, and my hand is yours. Oh!” she added, seeing him about to kneel, “I have more to say. I must not deceive you. In my heart I cherish one inextinguishable feeling. Friendship is the only sentiment which I can give to a husband. I wish neither to affront him nor to violate the laws of my own heart. But you can possess my hand and my fortune only at the cost of doing me an inestimable service.”

“I am ready for all things,” said the president.

“Here are fifteen hundred thousand francs,” she said, drawing from her bosom a certificate of a hundred shares in the Bank of France. “Go to Paris—not to-morrow, but instantly. Find Monsieur des Grassins, learn the names of my uncle’s creditors, call them together, pay them in full all that was owing, with interest at five per cent from the day the debt was incurred to the present time. Be careful to obtain a full and legal receipt, in proper form, before a notary. You are a magistrate, and I can trust this matter in your hands. You are a man of honor; I will put faith in your word, and meet the dangers of life under shelter of your name. Let us have mutual indulgence. We have known each other so long that we are almost related; you would not wish to render me unhappy.”

The president fell at the feet of the rich heiress, his heart beating and wrung with joy.

“I will be your slave!” he said.

“When you obtain the receipts, monsieur,” she resumed, with a cold glance, “you will take them with all the other papers to my cousin Grandet, and you will give him this letter. On your return I will keep my word.”

The president understood that he owed her acquiescence to some bitterness of love, and he hurried to obey, lest time effect a reconciliation between the pair.

When Monsieur de Bonfons left her, Eugenie fell back in her chair and burst into tears. All was over.

The president took the mail-post and reached Paris the next evening. The morning after his arrival he went to see des Grassins, and together they summoned the creditors to meet at the notary’s office where the vouchers had been deposited. Not a single creditor failed to be present. Creditors though they were, justice must be done to them—they were all punctual. Monsieur de Bonfons, in the name of Mademoiselle Grandet, paid them the amount of their claims with interest. The payment of interest was remarkable in Parisian commerce of that day. When the receipts were legally registered, and des Grassins had received for his services the fifty thousand francs allowed him by Eugenie, the president went to the hôtel d’Aubrion and found Charles just entering his apartment after a serious encounter with his prospective father-in-law. The old marquis had told him plainly that he should not marry his daughter until all the creditors of Guillaume Grandet had been paid in full.

The president gave Charles the following letter:

  My Cousin,—Monsieur le président de Bonfons has undertaken to place in your hands the acquittance for all claims upon my uncle, also a receipt by which I acknowledge having received from you the sum total of those claims. I have heard of a possible failure, and I think that the son of a bankrupt may not be able to marry Mademoiselle d’Aubrion. Yes, my cousin, you judged rightly of my mind and of my manners. I have, it is true, no part in the world; I understand neither its calculations nor its customs; and I could not give you the pleasures that you seek in it. Be happy, according to the social conventions to which you have sacrificed our love. To make your happiness complete I can only offer you your father’s honor. Adieu! You will always have a faithful friend in your cousin

Eugenie.

The president smiled at the exclamation which the ambitious young man could not repress as he received the documents.

“We shall announce our marriages at the same time,” remarked Monsieur de Bonfons.

“Ah! you marry Eugenie? Well, I am delighted; she is a good girl. But,” added Charles, struck with a luminous idea, “she must be rich?”

“She had,” said the president, with a mischievous smile, “about nineteen millions four days ago; but she has only seventeen millions to-day.”

Charles looked thunderstruck.

“Seventeen mil—”

“Seventeen millions; yes, monsieur. We shall muster, Mademoiselle Grandet and I, an income of seven hundred and fifty thousand francs when we marry.”

“My dear cousin,” said Charles, recovering a little assurance, “we can push each other’s fortunes.”

“Agreed,” said the president. “Here is also a little case which I am charged to give into your own hands,” he added, placing on the table the leather dressing-case.

“Well, my dear friend,” said Madame d’Aubrion, entering the room without noticing the president, “don’t pay any attention to what poor Monsieur d’Aubrion has just said to you; the Duchesse de Chaulieu has turned his head. I repeat, nothing shall interfere with the marriage—”

“Very good, madame. The three millions which my father owed were paid yesterday.”

“In money?” she asked.

“Yes, in full, capital and interest; and I am about to do honor to his memory—”

“What folly!” exclaimed his mother-in-law. “Who is this?” she whispered in Grandet’s ear, perceiving the president.

“My man of business,” he answered in a low voice.

The marquise bowed superciliously to Monsieur de Bonfons.

“We are pushing each other’s fortunes already,” said the president, taking up his hat. “Good-by, cousin.”

“He is laughing at me, the old cockatoo! I’d like to put six inches of iron into him!” muttered Charles.

The president was out of hearing. Three days later Monsieur de Bonfons, on his return to Saumur, announced his marriage with Eugenie. Six months after the marriage he was appointed councillor in the Cour royale at Angers. Before leaving Saumur Madame de Bonfons had the gold of certain jewels, once so precious to her, melted up, and put, together with the eight thousand francs paid back by her cousin, into a golden pyx, which she gave to the parish church where she had so long prayed for him. She now spent her time between Angers and Saumur. Her husband, who had shown some public spirit on a certain occasion, became a judge in the superior courts, and finally, after a few years, president of them. He was anxiously awaiting a general election, in the hope of being returned to the Chamber of Deputies. He hankered after a peerage; and then—

“The king will be his cousin, won’t he?” said Nanon, la Grande Nanon, Madame Cornoiller, bourgeoise of Saumur, as she listened to her mistress, who was recounting the honors to which she was called.

Nevertheless, Monsieur de Bonfons (he had finally abolished his patronymic of Cruchot) did not realize his ambitions. He died eight days after his election as deputy of Saumur. God, who sees all and never strikes amiss, punished him, no doubt, for his sordid calculations and the legal cleverness with which, accurante Cruchot, he had drawn up his marriage contract, in which husband and wife gave to each other, “in case they should have no children, their entire property of every kind, landed or otherwise, without exception or reservation, dispensing even with the formality of an inventory; provided that said omission of said inventory shall not injure their heirs and assigns, it being understood that this deed of gift is, etc., etc.” This clause explains the profound respect which monsieur le président always testified for the wishes, and above all, for the solitude of Madame de Bonfons. Women cited him as the most considerate and delicate of men, pitied him, and even went so far as to find fault with the passion and grief of Eugenie, blaming her, as women know so well how to blame, with cruel but discreet insinuation.

“Madame de Bonfons must be very ill to leave her husband entirely alone. Poor woman! Is she likely to get well? What is it? Something gastric? A cancer?”—“She has grown perfectly yellow. She ought to consult some celebrated doctor in Paris.”—“How can she be happy without a child? They say she loves her husband; then why not give him an heir?—in his position, too!”—“Do you know, it is really dreadful! If it is the result of mere caprice, it is unpardonable. Poor president!”

Endowed with the delicate perception which a solitary soul acquires through constant meditation and the clear-sightedness with which a mind aloof from life fastens on all that falls within its sphere, Eugenie, taught by suffering and by later education to divine thought, knew well that the president desired her death that he might step into possession of their immense fortune, augmented by the property of his uncle the notary and his uncle the abbé, whom it had lately pleased God to call to himself. The poor solitary pitied the president. Providence avenged her for the calculations and the indifference of a husband who respected the hopeless passion on which she spent her life because it was his surest safeguard. To give life to a child would give death to his hopes—the hopes of selfishness, the joys of ambition, which the president cherished as he looked into the future.

God thus flung piles of gold upon this prisoner to whom gold was a matter of indifference, who longed for heaven, who lived, pious and good, in holy thoughts, succoring the unfortunate in secret, and never wearying of such deeds. Madame de Bonfons became a widow at thirty-six. She is still beautiful, but with the beauty of a woman nearly forty. Her face is white, placid and calm; her voice gentle and self-possessed; her manners simple. She has the noblest qualities of sorrow, the saintliness of one who has never soiled her soul by contact with the world; but she also retains the rigid bearing of an old maid and the petty habits inseparable from the narrow round of provincial life. In spite of her vast wealth, she lives as the poor Eugenie Grandet once lived. The fire is never lighted on her hearth until the day when her father allowed it to be lighted in the hall, and it is put out in conformity with the rules which governed her youthful years. She dresses as her mother dressed. The house in Saumur, without sun, without warmth, always in shadow, melancholy, is an image of her life. She carefully accumulates her income, and might seem parsimonious did she not disarm criticism by a noble employment of her wealth. Pious and charitable institutions, a hospital for old age, Christian schools for children, a public library richly endowed, bear testimony against the charge of avarice which some persons lay at her door. The churches of Saumur owe much of their embellishment to her. Madame de Bonfons (sometimes ironically spoken of as mademoiselle) inspires for the most part reverential respect: and yet that noble heart, beating only with tenderest emotions, has been, from first to last, subjected to the calculations of human selfishness; money has cast its frigid influence upon that hallowed life and taught distrust of feelings to a woman who is all feeling.

“I have none but you to love me,” she says to Nanon.

The hand of this woman stanches the secret wounds in many families. She goes on her way to heaven attended by a train of benefactions. The grandeur of her soul redeems the narrowness of her education and the petty habits of her early life.

Such is the history of Eugenie Grandet, who is in the world but not of it; who, created to be supremely a wife and mother, has neither husband nor children nor family. Lately there has been some question of her marrying again. The Saumur people talk of her and of the Marquis de Froidfond, whose family are beginning to beset the rich widow just as, in former days, the Cruchots laid siege to the rich heiress. Nanon and Cornoiller are, it is said, in the interests of the marquis. Nothing could be more false. Neither la Grande Nanon nor Cornoiller has sufficient mind to understand the corruptions of the world.

ADDENDUM

################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY #####
#############
##### MIDDLE OF THE STORY. ##### #####
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################

################################################
######### THIS IS THE END OF A STORY. #########
################################################

For souls to whom effusiveness comes easily there is a delightful hour between day and night; twilight softens light and shade and favors a dreamy mood. The prevailing silence is dear to artists, who grow contemplative, step back from pictures they can no longer work on, and pass judgment, rapt by meanings that flash on the inner eye of genius. He who has never stood pensive by a friend in such a poetic hour can hardly grasp its inexpressible soothingness. Favored by the clear-obscure, material skill used to produce illusion disappears. If the work is a picture, the figures seem to speak and walk; shade is shadow, light is day; flesh breathes, eyes move, and fabrics change sheen. Imagination heightens every realistic detail and sees only the work’s beauties. At that hour illusion reigns despotically; it wakens a mind’s night, peopled by dreams. It bears the soul aloft to a world of fancies where the artist forgets the real world—yesterday, tomorrow, the future—everything, miseries and goods alike.

At this magic hour a young, talented painter who saw in art nothing but Art itself was perched on a step-ladder working on a large, now nearly finished painting. Criticising and admiring himself, floating on the current of his thoughts, he lost himself in a meditative mood that elevated and soothed the soul. His reverie lasted long. Night fell. Whether he meant to come down or made some ill-judged movement believing himself on the floor—the cause escaped him—he fell, struck his head on a footstool, lost consciousness, and lay motionless for an unknown time.

A sweet voice roused him from his stunned state. When he opened his eyes a bright light made him close them again; through the mist he heard two women whispering and felt two timid hands supporting his head. He soon recovered; by the light of an old-fashioned Argand lamp he saw the most charming face he had seen, a head that for an artist realized the ideal beauty he had formed for himself. Her features recalled the refined delicacy of Prudhon’s school and the poetic sentiment of Girodet: fresh temples, regular eyebrows, pure outline, virginal innocence stamped on every feature. Her figure was slight and graceful. Her dress, simple and neat, revealed neither wealth nor penury.

As he came to, the painter expressed admiration and fumbling thanks. A handkerchief pressed to his forehead; amid the studio smell he recognized the strong odor of ether used to revive him. He saw an old woman, looking like a marquise of the old school, who held the lamp and advised the young girl.

“Monsieur,” said the younger woman in answer to a few questions the painter asked while still vague from the shock, “my mother and I heard the noise of your fall and fancied we heard a groan. The silence after the crash alarmed us, and we hurried up. Finding the key in the latch, we entered and found you lying motionless. My mother fetched what was needed to bathe your head and revive you. You have cut your forehead—there. Do you feel it?”

“Yes, I do now,” he replied.

“Oh, it will be nothing,” said the old mother. “Happily your head rested against this lay-figure.”

“I feel infinitely better,” replied the painter. “I need nothing further but a hackney cab to take me home. The porter’s wife will go for one.”

He tried to repeat his thanks, but the elder lady interrupted him at every sentence: “Tomorrow, monsieur, pray be careful to put on leeches, or to be bled, and drink a few cups of something healing. A fall may be dangerous.”

The young girl stole glances at the painter and the pictures. Her expression revealed perfect propriety; her curiosity seemed rather an absence of mind, and her eyes spoke the natural interest women feel in suffering. The two strangers seemed to forget the painter’s works in his mishap. When he reassured them they left, looking at him with anxious kindness free from insistence or familiarity, without indiscreet questions or invitations. Their noble and simple manners made a quiet impression on the painter; later, recalling details of the incident, he was greatly struck by them.

On the landing the old lady gently observed, “Adelaide, you left the door open.”

“That was to hear me fall,” said the painter with a grateful smile.

“You came down just now, mother,” replied the young girl with a blush.

“Would you like us to accompany you downstairs? The stairs are dark.”

“No, thank you, madame; I am much better.”

“Hold tightly by the rail.”

The two women remained to light him, listening to his steps.

To appreciate the interest this scene aroused, the painter had only a few days earlier established his studio in the attics of this house in the darker, muddier part of the Rue de Suresnes, almost opposite the Church of the Madeleine and near his rooms in the Rue des Champs-Elysees. Fame had recently made him one of the artists most dear to his country; he was beginning to feel free from want, and, as he put it, was enjoying his last privations. Instead of working in cheaper studios near the city gates he had spared himself the long walk and the loss of time now more valuable than before.

No man inspired more interest than Hippolyte Schinner, though he did not lightly reveal the secrets of his life. He was the idol of a necessitous mother who had raised him amid severe privations. Mademoiselle Schinner, daughter of an Alsatian farmer, had never married. Her gentle soul had been cruelly crushed long ago by a rich man who showed no delicacy in his love affairs. The day she lost her illusions was an age of reflection, religious thought, and resignation. She refused the betrayer’s alms, renounced the world, and made a glory of her shame. She devoted herself to maternal love, sought in it her joys instead of social pleasures, worked, and saved a treasure for her son. In after years one day amply repaid her for those sacrifices.

At the last exhibition her son received the Cross of the Legion of Honor. Newspapers hailed an unknown genius, artists acknowledged Schinner as a master, and dealers covered his canvases with gold. At twenty-five Hippolyte, to whom his mother had transmitted her woman’s soul, understood his standing. Eager to restore to his mother the pleasures society had denied her, he lived for her, hoping that fame and fortune would one day make her happy, rich, respected, and surrounded by people of mark. Schinner chose friends among the honorable and distinguished; fastidious in selection, he desired to raise still further a position his talent had placed high. His solitary work from boyhood had left him with beautiful beliefs that grace early days. His adolescent soul was not closed to the shy emotions that make a young man an apart being, full of poetry and virginal hopes, puerile to the worldly but deep for their singleness.

He had gentle, polite manners that spoke to the soul and fascinated even those who did not understand them. His voice, from the heart, stirred noble sentiments and testified to his modesty by ingenuous tones. Those who saw him felt an attraction of moral nature they could not analyze; they might call it galvanism or attempt to express it in formulas.

These details help explain why, in the absence of the porter whom he had sent to the end of the Rue de la Madeleine for a coach, Hippolyte did not question the porter’s wife closely about the two women who had tended him. He replied to her natural inquiries about his accident and the fourth-floor tenants but could not stop her chatter, led by lodge policy and gossip.

“Ah,” she said, “they were, no doubt, Mademoiselle Leseigneur and her mother, who have lived here four years. We do not know exactly what these ladies do; in the morning, till noon, an old woman who is half deaf comes in to help them; in the evening, two or three old gentlemen with ribbon loops, like you, monsieur, come to see them and often stay very late. One comes in a carriage with servants; they say he has sixty thousand francs a year. However, they are very quiet tenants, as you are, monsieur; and economical! they live on nothing, and pay as soon as a letter is brought. It is queer, monsieur, the mother’s name is not the same as the daughter’s. Ah, but when they go for a walk in the Tuileries, mademoiselle is very smart, and she never goes out without being followed by a lot of young men; but she shuts the door in their face, and she is quite right. The proprietor would never allow—”

The coach came; Hippolyte heard no more and went home. His mother dressed his wound and would not allow him to return to the studio the next day. After advice and treatments, Hippolyte remained at home three days. During this enforced idleness his fancy recalled, bit by bit, the scene of his fainting fit. The young girl’s profile returned clearly against the darkness of his inward vision; he saw the mother’s faded features again, felt Adelaide’s touch. He remembered some gesture whose grace memory enhanced; then an attitude, a tone of voice rising like objects from deep waters to the surface.

When he could resume work he went early to his studio; really he hurried to visit his neighbors. When passion wakes, inexplicable pleasures are felt; he mounted the fourth-floor stairs slowly, and his heart throbbed as he neared the humble brown door of Mademoiselle Leseigneur. He fancied similarity between their positions, imagined misfortunes of birth akin to his own. He lingered at the studio to think of them, dined there, then about seven o’clock went to call.

No painter of manners has sufficiently told the curious privacy of certain Parisian existences: fresh elegant toilettes and brilliant women whose homes betray doubtful comfort. If my picture is sometimes bold, blame the truth; the appearance of his neighbors’ rooms greatly influenced Hippolyte Schinner’s feelings and hopes.

The house belonged to one of those proprietors who abhor repairs and decoration, who regard being a Paris homeowner as business. Between miser and usurer, such men are allergic to moving a cupboard or opening an air-hole; when chimneys fall they deprive themselves of theatre nights “on account of repairs.” Hippolyte, who had seen a comical scene about decorative repairs, was not surprised by dark greasy paint, oily stains, and other disagreeable woodwork signs. These stigmata of poverty are not without a sort of poetry to an artist.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur opened the door. Recognizing the young artist she bowed and, with Parisian tact and pride, turned to shut a glass partition through which Hippolyte might have seen linen hung over patent ironing stoves, an old camp-bed, charcoal and irons, a filter, household crockery—utensils of a small household. Muslin curtains screened this capharnaum, lighted by windows facing a neighboring yard.

With an artist’s quick eye Hippolyte saw the uses, furniture, and general condition of this cut-in-half first room. The more honorable half, serving as ante-room and dining-room, was hung with old salmon-rose paper with a flock border patched with wafers. Prints of the battles of Alexander, in gilding-rubbed frames, were arranged on the walls. In the middle stood a massive, old-fashioned mahogany table worn at the edges. A small stove with a thin pipe stood before the chimney, whose hearth was blocked by a cupboard. Chairs showed remains of former splendor—carved mahogany, red morocco seats, gilt nails—scarred like a veteran.

This room was a museum of objects that show both luxury and penury: a splendidly finished telescope hung over a discolored glass on the chimney. Between chimney and partition was a wretched painted sideboard pretending to be mahogany. Slippery red quarries, shabby rugs, and all the furniture shone with hard cleanliness that accentuated oldness. An indescribable odor mixed exhalations from the lumber-room, dining-room vapors, and stair smells, though the window was partly open. Street air fluttered dusty curtains, carefully drawn to hide a window bay where former tenants left ornamental additions—a sort of domestic fresco.

Adelaide hastened to open the inner room door and announced the painter with obvious pleasure. Hippolyte, who had earlier seen similar poverty in his mother’s home, noted these signs with an impression vivid as earliest memories and entered with sympathy rather than scorn.

“Well, monsieur, I hope you no longer feel the effects of your fall,” said the old lady, rising from an antique armchair and offering a seat.

“No, madame. I have come to thank you for the kind care you gave me, and above all mademoiselle, who heard me fall.”

As he spoke—words stamped with the exquisite awkwardness of first love—Hippolyte looked at the young girl. Adelaide lit the Argand lamp, replacing a tallow candle in a large copper candlestick, then placed the lamp on the chimney shelf and sat a little behind the painter to look at him at her ease while appearing interested in the lamp. The flame sputtered in the damp chimney. Seeing the large mirror on the chimney-piece, Hippolyte fixed his eyes on it to admire Adelaide; her stratagem only embarrassed them both.

While talking with Madame Leseigneur—Hippolyte called her so—he examined the room unobtrusively. Egyptian figures on iron fire-dogs were scarcely visible under cinders; two brands struggled before a sham log. An old Aubusson carpet, faded and mended, left the tiled floor cold. Walls were hung with reddish paper imitating figured silk. Opposite the windows he saw a crack and the paper outline of double doors closing a recess where Madame Leseigneur likely slept, poorly disguised by a sofa. Above a mahogany chest of drawers hung a portrait of an officer of rank, so dim he could not judge it well; from what he saw it must have been painted in China. Window-curtains of red silk were as faded as the furniture in red-and-yellow worsted work. On the chest’s marble top was a costly malachite tray with a dozen magnificently painted coffee cups, perhaps Sevres. On the chimney shelf stood an Empire clock—a warrior driving four horses on a chariot whose wheel bore the hours. Tall candlesticks held smoke-yellowed tapers; porcelain vases crowned with dusty artificial flowers stood in the corners.

In the middle of the room a card-table with new packs suggested a painful dilemma: either these two women were perfectly honest or they lived by intrigue and gambling. Yet in looking at Adelaide, so pure-minded, Schinner could not but believe in her innocence and attribute the furniture’s incoherence to honorable causes.

“My dear,” said the old lady to the young one, “I am cold; make a little fire, and give me my shawl.”

Adelaide fetched a cashmere shawl—once costly, now faded and darned—and wrapped her mother in it. She then brought a bundle of small wood from the lumber-room and gallantly threw it on the fire to revive it.

It is hard to reproduce the ensuing conversation. Guided by the tact born of early hardship, Hippolyte did not remark on his neighbors’ poverty; any direct question would be indiscreet. The simplest pity can offend, so his thoughts and words diverged. The two ladies began by talking of painting; women easily guess the embarrassment of a first call and supply devices to end it. Questioning him about his art emboldened him to talk, and the chat’s light nothings led Hippolyte to reflections that revealed his habits and mind. Trouble had prematurely faded the old lady’s face; only prominent features remained, an outline suggesting shrewdness with grace in the eyes—an expression of the old Court that can hint either at artifice or refined delicacy.

A woman’s face hides mystery: the line between frankness and duplicity, intrigue and heart, is inscrutable. A penetrating eye might discern differences; such appreciation lies in intuition alone. The old lady’s face, like her room, made it hard to tell whether the squalor covered vice or highest virtue. But at Schinner’s age the heart’s first impulse is to believe in goodness. As he studied Adelaide’s noble, almost haughty brow and soulful eyes, he breathed the modest fragrance of virtue. He seized an opportunity to discuss portraits and thus examine the faded pastel whose chalk had fallen away.

“You are attached to that picture for the sake of the likeness, no doubt, mesdames, for the drawing is dreadful?” he said, looking at Adelaide.

“It was done at Calcutta, in great haste,” replied the mother, her voice agitated.

She gazed at the formless sketch with the absorption memories of happiness bring; in her face were traces of perennial regret. The painter interpreted her attitude as the mark of a life once happy. He then sat down beside her.

“Madame,” he said, “in a very short time the colors of that pastel will disappear. The portrait will only survive in your memory. Will you allow me to reproduce the likeness on canvas? It will be more permanently recorded. Grant me, as a neighborly favor, the pleasure of doing you this service. There are times when an artist is glad of a respite from greater undertakings by doing work of less lofty pretension; it will be a recreation for me.”

The old lady flushed at the offer, and Adelaide shot a glance of deep feeling. Hippolyte wanted some tie to his neighbors, to gain a right to mingle in their life. His offer appealed to the heart’s affections, gratified his pride as an artist, and could not wound the ladies. Madame Leseigneur accepted, without eagerness or reluctance, with the self-possession of a noble soul, aware of the character of bonds formed by such an obligation.

“I fancy,” said the painter, “that the uniform is that of a naval officer.”

“Yes,” she said, “that of a captain in command. Monsieur de Rouville—my husband—died at Batavia from a wound received in a fight with an English ship off the Asiatic coast. He commanded a frigate of fifty-six guns; the Revenge carried ninety-six. The struggle was unequal, but he defended his ship bravely, held out till nightfall and got away. When I returned to France Bonaparte was not yet in power, and I was refused a pension. When I applied again, quite lately, I was sternly informed that if the Baron de Rouville had emigrated I should not have lost him; that by this time he would have been a rear-admiral; finally, his Excellency quoted degrees of forfeiture. I took this step, urged by friends, only for Adelaide’s sake. I have always hated holding out my hand as a beggar in the name of a grief that deprives a woman of voice and strength. I do not like this money valuation for blood irreparably spilt—”

“Dear mother, this subject always does you harm.”

The Baronne Leseigneur bowed and was silent.

“Monsieur,” said the young girl, “I had supposed a painter’s work was generally fairly quiet?”

Schinner colored, remembering the noise he had made. Adelaide rose at the sound of a carriage stopping at the door. She went into her own room and returned with a pair of tall gilt candlesticks with partly burnt wax candles, which she quickly lit. Without waiting for the bell she opened the outer door where she set the lamp down. The sound of a kiss given and received touched Hippolyte’s heart. His impatience to see the man who treated Adelaide familiarly was not immediately gratified; the newcomers conversed in undertone with the young girl.

At last Mademoiselle de Rouville returned, followed by two men whose costumes and countenances need a long tale. The first, about sixty, wore one of those coats fashioned for Louis XVIII.: neither civil nor military, it might pass for either. Fleurs-de-lis embroidered the lapels and gilt buttons bore the same. Shoulder straps begged for useless epaulettes; a buttonhole blossomed with many colored ribbons. He probably always carried his three-cornered hat with a gold cord, for his powdered hair showed no traces of pressure. He might be taken for fifty and seemed robust. His frank, loyal emigre expression hinted also at libertine habits, the reckless gallantry of the Musketeers. His gestures and manner showed no intention of renouncing royalism, religion, or love affairs.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A fantastic figure stood behind this specimen of “Louis XIV.’s light infantry”—the Bonapartists’ nickname for these venerable Monarchy survivors. He ought to have been the picture’s principal subject, but served only as an accessory: a lean, dry man dressed like the first, his coat older, powder less white, fleurs-de-lis dimmer, shoulder-straps dog’s-eared; his intellect feebler, his life nearer its end. He realized Rivarol’s quip about Champcenetz—“He is the moonlight of me.” A paler double, he seemed the last impression of a lithograph.

The painter found this old Chevalier a mystery. He was silent; nobody spoke to him. Was he a friend, a poor relation, a companion who followed at the old gallant’s heels? Did he fill a place between dog, parrot, and friend? Had he saved his patron’s fortune, or only his life? At the Baronne de Rouville’s he provoked curiosity without satisfying it. Who after the Restoration could recall an attachment that, before the Revolution, had bound him to his friend’s wife, dead twenty years?

The leader, least dilapidated of these wrecks, gallantly kissed Madame de Rouville’s hand and sat by her. The Chevalier bowed and placed himself a couple of chairs away. Adelaide stood behind the old gentleman’s armchair, leaning her elbows on the back in an attitude like Guerin’s Dido’s sister.

Though the gentleman’s familiarity was fatherly, his freedom seemed to annoy the girl. “What, are you sulky with me?” he said, then shot at Schinner one of those diplomatic looks that ask, when they see a stranger, “Is he one of us?”

“This is our neighbor,” said the Baronne, pointing to Hippolyte. “Monsieur is a celebrated painter; his name must be known to you despite your indifference to the arts.”

The old man saw the intent in suppressing the name and bowed. “Certainly,” he said. “I heard much about his pictures at the last Salon. Talent has immense privileges,” he added, noticing the artist’s red ribbon. “That distinction, which we must earn at the cost of our blood and long service, you win in your youth; but all glory is of the same kindred,” he said, laying his hand on his Cross of Saint-Louis.

Hippolyte murmured thanks and fell silent, content to admire the beautiful girl’s head. Adelaide’s face, luminous, made him forget the misery of the dwelling. He answered sparingly, his attention divided: soul attending the girl, ear following the conversation. Hope poured out a thousand visions of happiness; he refused to analyze the evening’s incidents.

After a while the Baroness and her daughter began to play cards with the old gentleman. The Chevalier, faithful to his shadow role, stood behind his friend’s chair, watching and answering mute inquiries with nods and gestures.

“Du Halga, I always lose,” said the gentleman.

“You discard badly,” replied the Baronne.

“For three months now I have never won a single game,” he said.

“Have you the aces?” asked the old lady.

“Yes, one more to mark,” he answered.

“Shall I come and advise you?” said Adelaide.

“No, no. Stay where I can see you. By Gad, it would be losing too much not to have you to look at!”

At last the game was over. The gentleman pulled out his purse and, throwing two louis-d’or on the table with temper, “Forty francs,” he exclaimed, “the exact sum.—Deuce take it! It is eleven o’clock.”

“It is eleven o’clock,” repeated the silent figure, looking at the painter.

Hippolyte thought it time to retire. He found a few commonplace remarks to make, took leave, and went away wholly possessed by the first raptures of true love, without attempting to analyze the evening’s little incidents.

The next day he felt an ardent desire to see Adelaide again. He might have appeared in the morning but waited until the afternoon. Blushing like a girl, he shyly asked Mademoiselle Leseigneur for the portrait of the Baron.

“But come in,” said Adelaide, who had heard him come down from the studio.

He entered tongue-tied; happiness dulled his wit. To see Adelaide, to hear the rustle of her skirt after longing all morning, made trifles precious. The old lady was absent. Adelaide brought a chair to reach the portrait; finding she could not without stepping on a chest of drawers, she turned to Hippolyte and, blushing, said, “I am not tall enough. Will you get it down?”

Her modesty was the true motive; he read it and returned a look of intelligence—the sweetest language of love. Adelaide cast down her eyes. Hippolyte, almost timid, took down the picture, examined it in the window light, and went away saying only, “I will return it soon.”

Both felt a storm of agitation, as if a stone flung into a deep lake had set ripples in their hearts.

Back in his studio, the easel awaited a fresh canvas; he worked with the ardor artists throw into whims until dinner, then returned that evening and stayed from nine till eleven. The same topics, arrivals, and the same game, the same loss by the old man; only Hippolyte, a little bolder, ventured to chat with the young girl.

A week passed, and their feelings underwent the slow, delightful transformations that build understanding. Every day Adelaide’s greeting grew more intimate, her voice more eager, her manner more familiar. They laughed and talked with the simplicity of children newly made friends, as if they had known one another for years. Schinner wished to be taught piquet; being a novice, he blundered and lost almost every game. Without a word of love the lovers knew they were everything to each other. Hippolyte enjoyed his power over his gentle friend; Adelaide, timid and devoted, allowed many concessions, playing small caprices like a spoilt child. Familiarity with the old Count waned: she perceived the painter’s melancholy from his abrupt tone when the old man unceremoniously kissed her hands or throat.

Mademoiselle Leseigneur began to expect an account of the painter’s actions; she scolded him for absences so effectually that he gave up other friends and stayed in the little apartment that enchanted him. Adelaide’s jealousy flared when she heard he sometimes visited Parisian drawing-rooms late; she argued passionately that such a life was bad for his health and could not be loving. Driven by passion and a girl’s exactions, he lived exclusively with her.

Never was there a purer or more ardent love. Both trusted and were delicate; their passion grew without sacrifices. A spontaneous affinity made their union close. Two months after their first meeting their lives had become one: in the morning Adelaide could tell by footsteps that “He is there.” When Hippolyte went home to his mother at dinner he always looked in on his neighbors; in the evening he flew back with lover’s punctuality. Adelaide enjoyed realizing the ideal she naturally dreamed of.

The old gentleman came less often; Hippolyte, once jealous, had taken his place at the green table and shared his ill-luck. Yet sometimes, amid happiness, thought of Madame de Rouville’s desperate poverty haunted him. “Strange! twenty francs every evening?” he asked himself, and dared not confess his odious suspicions.

He spent two months on the portrait; when finished, varnished, and framed, he deemed it one of his best works. Madame de Rouville had not spoken of it again. Was this indifference or pride? He would not account for the silence. Joyfully plotting with Adelaide, he planned to hang it when the Baroness was out. One day, while her mother walked in the Tuileries, Adelaide went up to his studio to see the portrait in its light. She stood speechless in ecstatic contemplation. The painter, uneasy, leaned forward; she held out her hand, unable to speak, and two tears fell. Hippolyte kissed her hand; for a minute they stood mute, longing to confess love and not daring. They hung the picture in its place. Hippolyte dined that evening with the Baroness, who, overcome and in tears, embraced him.

That evening the old émigré, the Baron de Rouville’s comrade, paid a visit to announce he had just been promoted to vice-admiral; his land travels had been counted as naval campaigns. Seeing the portrait, he shook the painter’s hand and exclaimed, “By Gad! though my old hulk does not deserve to be perpetuated, I would gladly give five hundred pistoles to see myself as like as that is to my dear old Rouville.”

At this hint the Baroness looked at her young friend and smiled with sudden gratitude. Hippolyte suspected the admiral meant to offer the price for both portraits while paying for his own. His pride as an artist, and jealousy, took offense; he replied, “Monsieur, if I were a portrait-painter I should not have done this one.” The admiral bit his lip and sat down to cards.

Hippolyte stayed near Adelaide, who proposed a dozen hands of piquet. As he played he noticed an unexpected eagerness in Madame de Rouville to win and finger the old gentleman’s gold. Evil suspicions troubled him. Could the Baroness be living by gambling? Was she playing to pay a debt, to meet the rent? Why would this rich old man frequent a fourth-story room where he lost about forty francs each evening? Why had he been so familiar with Adelaide and then given up the rights he had acquired?

These reflections led him to watch the old man and the Baroness more closely. Their sidelong glances at Adelaide displeased him. “Am I being duped?” was his last, horrible thought. He resolved to stay after the departure of the two old men to confirm or dispel his suspicions. He drew out his purse to pay and, carried away by thought, laid it on the table and fell into a brief reverie; then, ashamed of his silence, he rose, answered some commonplace question, and went close to the Baroness to examine her withered features as she talked.

He left, racked by doubts. A few steps down, he turned back to fetch the forgotten purse. “I left my purse here!” he said to Adelaide.

“No,” she answered, reddening.

“I thought it was there,” he said, pointing to the card-table. Not finding it, shamed for Adelaide and the Baroness, he looked at them with blank amazement that made them laugh; he checked himself, felt his waistcoat, and said, “I must have made a mistake. I have it somewhere no doubt.”

In the purse were fifteen louis d’or and some small change. The theft seemed flagrant and denied with effrontery; Hippolyte no longer felt doubt about his neighbors’ morals. He stood on the stairs, legs shaking, dizzy, in a cold sweat, unable to walk, struggling with the shock of his destroyed hopes. He recalled trifling observations that now corroborated his frightful suspicions and opened his eyes to the character and life of these two women.

Had they waited until the portrait was given them before robbing him? If so, the theft was more odious. He remembered Adelaide’s seeming curiosity at the worn silk netting—might she have been counting coins in his purse while making innocent jests to watch for a moment to steal? He briefly fancied other motives—perhaps the admiral hesitated to marry Adelaide—but checked himself with the thought that if the Baroness hoped to make him marry her daughter, they would not have robbed him.

Clinging to his illusion and the love already rooted, he tried to find an accident: “The purse must have fallen on the floor,” he told himself. He searched himself hastily but did not find it. His memory retraced the truth: he distinctly saw the purse on the green cloth. Even so, he excused Adelaide, thinking misfortune might explain a degrading action; there must be some secret. He refused to admit that that proud face could be a lie.

That night he did not sleep. The heartache from the loss of unrealized bliss made rapid inroads. To lose the future we dreamed of is a keener pang than losing known happiness. His spirit plunged into a shoreless sea; such partial death can lead, in delicate natures, to ruin. Hippolyte wandered under the Tuileries’ shade, absorbed and forgetful.

There he met François Souchet, a school-fellow about to set out for Italy. “Why, Hippolyte, what ails you?” asked the sculptor.

“I am most unhappy,” replied Hippolyte gravely.

“Nothing but a love affair can cause you grief. Money, glory, respect—you lack nothing.”

Led into confidences, Hippolyte confessed his love. When Souchet heard “Rue de Suresnes, a young girl on the fourth floor,” he laughed and began to recall what all the young men knew: the mother’s appearance and the air that betrayed her station. “Do you not know her by the way she holds her bag?” he said. Several acquaintances joined; jokes and mockery, lively but painful, followed. Hippolyte’s reticence made him suffer as his heart’s secret was handled so carelessly. He pretended contradiction and asked for proofs, but the jesting resumed. “Come at four o’clock and study their walk,” Souchet advised. Torn by conflicting feelings, Hippolyte left, still convinced Adelaide must be superior to such accusations and filled with remorse for suspecting her. He thought of playing the good genius who rescues a woman abandoned by slander—romantic to a highly-strung soul.

He sat in his studio, gazed at his picture without working, tears in his eyes, brush in hand but not touching. Night fell; roused by darkness, he went downstairs, met the old admiral, looked darkly at him as he bowed, and fled. He had planned to see the ladies, but the admiral’s presence froze him. For the hundredth time he wondered what interest brought this prodigal, with eighty thousand francs a year, to a fourth story where he lost forty francs every evening; he imagined motives.

In the next days Hippolyte threw himself into his work to conquer passion by idea and composition. Study consoled him, though memories of Adelaide persisted. One evening, leaving his studio, he saw the door of the ladies’ rooms half open and Adelaide standing in the window recess. The door and staircase made it impossible not to be seen. He bowed coldly, with a glance of supreme indifference; yet thinking of the pain such coldness would inflict, he shuddered inwardly. That week of disdain would have been terrible; perhaps the purse had been found and she had sought him nightly. Ashamed of resisting his heart for a week, he called that evening on Madame de Rouville.

All his suspicions vanished at the sight of Adelaide, pale and thin. “Good heavens! what is the matter?” he asked, after greeting the Baroness.

Adelaide made no reply, but gave him a look of deep melancholy that pained him. “You must have thought us indifferent?” said the Baroness.

“I was wrong,” he replied. “There are forms of pain we know not how to confide to any one.”

“The sincerity and strength of friendship are not to be measured by time,” the Baroness observed sadly.

“What ails you?” he asked Adelaide.

“Oh, nothing,” said the Baroness, explaining Adelaide had sat up late finishing some woman’s work and would not listen when told a day more or less did not matter.

Hippolyte, blushing with shame at his suspicions, ascribed the loss of his purse to some accident. The evening was delicious to both. Adelaide could read his thoughts; though he could not confess his wrongs, he had come back more in love and affectionate, seeking tacit forgiveness. Yet at Madame de Rouville’s suggestion to play—“Let us have our little game; my old friend Kergarouet will not let me off”—his fears revived. He sat at the card-table; Adelaide said he needed a partner because he did not know piquet.

Throughout the game the Baroness and her daughter exchanged looks of intelligence, alarming Hippolyte more because he was winning. At last a final hand left the lovers in the old lady’s debt. Reaching for some money he saw before him a purse Adelaide had slipped in front of him without his noticing it; the poor child held the old one in her hand and was, to keep her countenance, looking into it for the money to pay her mother. The sight made the blood rush to his heart; he was near fainting.

The substituted purse contained his fifteen gold louis. It was worked with gilt beads; rings and tassels betrayed Adelaide’s taste and the small hoard she had spent on it. It was an impossible, delicate way to show the painter that his gift could only be repaid by some proof of affection.

Overcome, Hippolyte turned to Adelaide and her mother, saw their trembling pleasure, felt mean and foolish; tears rose. He sprang up, clasped Adelaide, pressed her to his heart, and stole a kiss; then, with the simple heartiness of an artist, “I ask for her for my wife!” he exclaimed, looking to the Baroness.

Adelaide looked half-wrathful, and Madame de Rouville, somewhat astonished, considered her reply when a bell rang. The old vice-admiral entered, followed by his shadow and Madame Schinner. Alarmed by calumnies against Adelaide, Hippolyte’s mother had inquired and reported them to the admiral, who declared he would crop all the scoundrels’ ears for them.

Prompted by his wrath, he told Madame Schinner his secret of losing intentionally at cards, because the Baroness’s pride left him no means but these ingenious ones to help her.

After Madame Schinner had paid respects to Madame de Rouville, the Baroness looked at the Comte de Kergarouet, the Chevalier du Halga—friend of the departed Comtesse de Kergarouet—Hippolyte, and Adelaide, and said with a gracious, heartfelt tone, “So we are a family party this evening.”

PARIS, May 1832

Produced by John Bickers, and Dagny

PIERRETTE

By Honore De Balzac

Translated by Katharine Prescott Wormeley

                             DEDICATION

  To Mademoiselle Anna Hanska:

  Dear Child,--You, the joy of the household, you, whose pink or
  white pelerine flutters in summer among the groves of
  Wierzschovnia like a will-o’-the-wisp, followed by the tender eyes
  of your father and your mother,--how can I dedicate to _you_ a
  story full of melancholy? And yet, ought not sorrows to be spoken
  of to a young girl idolized as you are, since the day may come
  when your sweet hands will be called to minister to them? It is
  difficult, Anna, to find in the history of our manners and morals
  a subject that is worthy of your eyes, that no choice has been
  left me; but perhaps you will be made to feel how fortunate your
  fate is when you read the story sent to you by
                                                    Your old friend,
                                                         De Balzac.

PIERRETTE

I. THE LORRAINS

At dawn of an October day in 1827 a sixteen-year-old youth, dressed in what modern phrase calls proletary, stood in a small square of Lower Provins. At that hour he could study the houses around the oblong space unnoticed. The mills by the river already whirred; their wheels, echoed from the Upper Town, intensified the silence so that the wheels of a distant diligence could be heard a league off. The two long sides of the square, separated by an avenue of lindens, were built in the simple style of bourgeois life. No signs of commerce were visible; the porte-cochères of the rich were few and seldom used, except that of Monsieur Martener, a physician with a cabriolet. Some fronts were covered by vines or roses climbing to second-story windows, scenting the air. One end of the square opens on the main street, the gardens reaching the river bank; the other opens onto a street parallel to the main road.

At that quieter end the youth recognized the house he sought: a front of white stone grooved as courses, closed gray blinds, slender iron balconies with yellow-painted rosettes. Above the ground and first floors rose three dormer windows in a slate roof; on the peak of the central one was a new weather-vane of a hunter shooting a hare. Three stone steps led to the door. A leaden pipe discharged sink-water into a gutter on one side, indicating the kitchen; on the other were two windows closely shuttered with heart-shaped openings to admit light, those of the dining-room. Vent-holes to the cellar were closed by painted iron shutters cut in open-work. Everything was new. This restored house, fresh against the older facades, suggested the paltry taste and self-satisfaction of a retired petty shopkeeper.

The young man inspected these details with a pleasure tinged with sadness; his eyes roved from the kitchen to the roof. The rosy glow of the rising sun fell on a calico curtain at one garret window; the others lacked that luxury, and his face brightened. He stepped back, leaned against a linden, and sang in the drawling tone of western France the Breton ditty, a song often sung to newly married couples:

  “We’ve come to wish you happiness in marriage,
    To m’sieur your husband
    As well as to you:

  “You have just been bound, madam’ la mariee,
    With bonds of gold
    That only death unbinds:

  “You will go no more to balls or gay assemblies;
    You must stay at home
    While we shall go.

  “Have you thought well how you are pledged to be
    True to your spouse,
    And love him like yourself?

  “Receive these flowers our hands do now present you;
    Alas! your fleeting honors
    Will fade as they.”

This native air, tender and melancholy, would awaken a Breton maiden’s memories, picturing the manners and feelings of her noble old land. As he finished the first couplet, the singer, never taking his eyes from the attic curtain, saw no sign of life. While he sang the second, the curtain stirred. At “Receive these flowers,” a face appeared; a white hand cautiously opened the casement, and a girl made a sign with her head as he closed with the melancholy thought, “Alas! your fleeting honors will fade as they.”

To her the young workman suddenly produced a yellow flower, common in Brittany and sometimes found in La Brie—the furze, or broom.

“Is it really you, Brigaut?” she said in a low voice.

“Yes, Pierrette, yes. I am in Paris. I have started to make my way; but I’m ready to settle here, near you.”

A window on the first floor beneath Pierrette’s attic creaked. The girl showed terror and whispered: “Run away!”

The lad jumped like a frightened frog to a bend in the street where a mill projected as the square opens into the main thoroughfare; but despite his agility his hob-nailed shoes echoed on the stones, easily distinguished from the mill’s music, and no doubt heard by the person who opened the window.

That person was a woman — an old maid. No man would have torn himself from a morning nap to listen to a minstrel in a jacket; only a maid wakes to songs of love. She opened her blinds with the furtive motion of a bat, looked about, saw nothing, and only heard the flying footfalls. Few sights are more dreadful than the matutinal apparition of an ugly old maid at her window.
