“Not very well,” she replied shortly. “You woke me last night out of my first sleep, and I was wakeful for the rest of the night.” Sitting down, she added, “Gentlemen, the milk is getting cold.”

Stupefied at being so ill-naturedly received by his landlady—whom he half expected to apologize—yet frightened at the prospect of confrontation, the poor vicar sat in silence. His natural inclination was to avoid quarrels; reason urged him not to submit to discourtesy. Torn, he examined the broad green lines on the oilcloth the landlady always left on the table at breakfast, oblivious to its ragged edges. The priests sat opposite in cane-seated arm-chairs; Mademoiselle Gamard presided in a high chair on casters near the dining-room stove. The room and the salon were on the ground floor beneath Birotteau’s salon and bedroom.

When his coffee, duly sugared, arrived, the grim silence chilled him. He dared not look at Troubert’s dried features or the old maid’s threatening visage; he turned instead to the plethoric pug on a cushion near the stove—the most important but least troublesome person in the household since he had ceased to bark. Birotteau, fond of harmless chatter he thought aided digestion, addressed the dog: “Well, my pretty, are you waiting for your coffee?” The pug blinked and closed his eyes peevishly.

The vicar’s loquacity, usually trusted to start conversation with Mademoiselle Gamard, failed him now; forced to strain his mind for beguiling topics in recent mornings, he felt tongue-tied. He summoned courage to remark, “This coffee is excellent,” and then, looking at a scrap of sky between Saint-Gatien’s buttresses, added, “It will be finer today than yesterday.” That remark earned Mademoiselle Gamard’s most gracious look for Troubert and a terrible severity for Birotteau, who by then was looking at his plate.

Mademoiselle Sophie Gamard presented the very elegiac nature of an old maid. To understand her influence in the drama and on Birotteau’s life, it helps to summarize what makes an Old Maid as she embodied it. Some beings consume without producing; moral philosophy and political economy condemn such uselessness. Few old maids willingly enter this rank; society, especially in France, casts a strong stigma on a woman who has no one willing to share life’s blessings or ills. Unmarried women reach a period when they are judged for their rejection; if ugly, goodness should compensate; if handsome, some serious cause is suspected; if they chose celibacy for independence, mothers and men condemn their disloyalty to womanly devotion. To renounce womanhood is to abjure its poetry and forfeit consolations rightly due to mothers.

Moreover, generous sentiments and the refinements of taste develop only by exercise. Remaining unmarried, a woman may become void of meaning; selfish and cold, she provokes repulsion. The world’s judgment, harsh though it may be, presses upon such women and their faces show the distress. A life without the expression of happiness that adorns married women yields sharpness and peevishness; suffering breeds bitterness. Before an old maid blames herself for isolation she blames others; reproach can become a desire for revenge.

Because they did not exercise the feminine impulse to please, many old maids have little elegance or taste. They are used to choosing what serves themselves at the sacrifice of pleasing others; they end by feeling and suffering this difference. Jealousy is indelible in the female breast. An old maid’s soul is jealous yet void; she knows only the miserable side of the passion society allows women. Thwarted in all hopes and forced to deny their natures, they endure inward torment. Their glance becomes oblique from fear and shame rather than modesty; they never forgive society—or themselves—for their false position.

These sad truths were visible in Mademoiselle Gamard’s dulled gray eyes and the dark circles beneath them; all the wrinkles on her face ran in straight lines. Scattered hairs, once brown, grew on her chin. Thin lips scarcely covered long but white teeth; her complexion was dark and her hair, once black, had turned gray from frightful headaches that made her wear a false front poorly fitted to conceal the junction. Her gown—silk in summer, merino in winter—was always brown and rather tight on her angular figure. A limp collar exposed a red, ribbed neck. Her origin as the daughter of a wood-merchant explained some defects. She might once have been plump, but no trace remained. An aquiline nose proclaimed despotism of nature, a flat forehead narrowness of mind. Her movements had an odd abruptness; she held herself erect and walked as if her joints were consolidating, advancing in a solid block like a statue. When in good humour she would tell of chances to have married and of timely discoveries of her lovers’ lack of means—unwittingly proving her worldly prudence over her heart.

This typical old maid was framed by the varnished-paper Turkish landscapes that decorated the dining-room. The salon, the “yellow salon,” was consistently yellow—curtains, furniture, walls—gilt mirror, crystal candlesticks and clock. No one had seen Mademoiselle Gamard’s private room; conjecture imagined it full of odds and ends and keepsakes dear to the hearts of old maids.

Lacking the natural activity women are given, Mademoiselle Gamard spent her energies on petty intrigues, provincial cabals and self-seeking schemes—the usual occupation of old maids. Birotteau had developed in Sophie Gamard the only feeling possible for her: hatred. That passion, latent under provincial calm, would become intense because spent on petty things in a narrow sphere. Birotteau, one of those predestined to suffer because they cannot avoid hurts they do not see, became an object of her malice.

“Yes, it will be a fine day,” replied Troubert, after a pause, outwardly conforming to politeness.

Frightened at how long he had sat without speaking—he had, for the first time, taken coffee without conversation—Birotteau left the dining-room with his heart squeezed as if in a vise and walked the box-edged garden paths that formed a star. Turning after the first round, he saw Mademoiselle Gamard and Troubert standing stock-still on the threshold—Troubert with folded arms and the immobility of a tomb statue; she leaning against the blind door. Both seemed to be watching him and counting his steps. Nothing is more disquieting to a timid creature than to feel itself the focus of close examination, and if those eyes are full of hatred, the suffering is martydom.

Birotteau fancied he was blocking their path. Driven by fear and a desire not to inconvenience them, he left the garden and went to the church, no longer thinking of his canonry, absorbed by the old maid’s disheartening tyranny. Saint-Gatien offered occupation—funerals, a marriage, baptisms—and he forgot his troubles. When his stomach reminded him of dinner, he checked his watch and saw, alarmed, that it was some minutes after four. Mademoiselle Gamard’s punctuality was known; he hurried back.

Passing the kitchen door he saw that the first course had been removed. In the dining-room the old maid said with sour rebuke and joy at being able to blame him: “It is half-past four, Monsieur Birotteau. You know we are not to wait for you.” He noticed the gauze over the clock had been moved; the hands had been set ahead of the cathedral clock. He could make no remark: any protest would have invited one of those fierce, eloquent expositions in which Mademoiselle Gamard excelled. The thousand petty annoyances a servant will make her master or a woman her husband endure were instinctively divined and used against Birotteau. The way she delighted in plotting against his domestic comfort bore marks of a profoundly malignant genius, yet she contrived always, as far as could be seen, to be in the right.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Eight days after the opening events of this history, shifts in household relations made Abbé Birotteau perceive a plot long growing against him.

At first Mademoiselle Gamard’s hostility had been covert and the vicar could ignore it; after the candlestick and the altered clock he could no longer doubt her animus. He fell into despair, imagining her thin, clawlike fingers everywhere. Gamard, delighted by revenge, hovered above him like a bird of prey. She had long conceived a plan the simple priest could not foresee, and now executed it with the petty ingenuity of solitary people who, deprived of great passions, expend themselves on meticulous outward devotion.

These small torments prevented Birotteau, effusive and craving pity, from confiding in friends—another cruelty. His timidity made him dread seeming ridiculous by troubling anyone with such pettiness. Yet these petty matters were his life—he preferred the absence of strong feeling and found happiness in routine. His paradise turned to hell; his dread of confronting Gamard grew, and secret distress harmed his health. One morning, while putting on his mottled blue stockings, he noticed his calves had shrunk. Terrified, he resolved to ask Abbé Troubert to intervene officially between him and the landlady.

In Troubert’s bare, cheerless room the vicar, ashamed to bother a man so occupied, after many internal agonies explained his case. The canon listened coldly, suppressing an occasional smile. He blamed Birotteau’s failure to notice on his preoccupation with elevated thoughts, remarked that recluses once thought little of food and lodging, and suggested modern priests retire into the solitude of their hearts. He said he might arbitrate because his friendship with Gamard had always observed Church limits, but justice required hearing both sides. Troubert professed to see no change in Gamard, attributing temper fluctuations to pulmonary suffering, and urged patience; if Birotteau stayed longer he would come to esteem her. Birotteau left confounded.

Unable to consult anyone, he judged Gamard alone and fancied that leaving her house for a few days might extinguish her dislike. He resolved to spend a week at Madame de Listomère’s country house, the Alouette, a habit he often followed. Ironically, this was exactly what Gamard wished; only patient endurance could have foiled her plan. The vicar, incapable of divining this, was doomed to fall.

Ten days after his arrival at the Alouette, while breakfasting, he was told Monsieur Caron wished to speak with him. Caron was Gamard’s lawyer. Agitated and surprised, Birotteau learned Caron had been sent to ask how long he intended to remain. The original agreement had not foreseen a long absence and might lead to contest; Gamard, anxious to avoid dispute, had sent Caron to learn his plans. The abbé asked for time to seek advice.

Back at table, Birotteau described his trouble. The country-house company, bored with monotony, eagerly took Gamard’s side. Madame de Listomère supposed Troubert wanted Birotteau’s apartment; Monsieur de Listomère urged resistance. An old landowner, Monsieur de Bourbonne, suggested something more serious: Gamard intended to turn Birotteau out, and people like her risked nothing in such games. De Bourbonne, thin, shrewd, experienced in weighing words and actions, had a reputation that made him feared in local affairs.

Mademoiselle Salomon de Villenoix arrived from Tours with news that altered everything. She reported that the vicar-general was very ill and the archbishop had provisionally delegated his powers to Abbé Troubert, so the canonry would depend on him. At a recent gathering the Abbé Poirel had spoken of Birotteau’s annoyances to Gamard in a way that cast blame on the vicar. Madame de Listomère asked whether he preferred to be made canon or to continue living with Gamard; the company unanimously chose the canon. She suggested that by leaving Gamard’s house he might secure the canonry—“one good turn deserves another.” All applauded except her nephew, the Baron, who joked about wishing to see a fight.

Despite their support, forces were unequal: the city’s best society had less power than Gamard backed by Troubert. Acting on Madame de Listomère’s advice, a servant fetched Caron back. De Bourbonne counselled caution and delay, but passion prevailed. In a short conference Caron urged Birotteau to sign a paper declaring his relinquishment of domicile. Confused and overwhelmed, Birotteau read and mechanically signed it: he declared he left Gamard’s house of his own free will and that he had been properly fed and lodged. Caron asked where to send the abbé’s belongings; Birotteau said Madame de Listomère. De Bourbonne, suspicious, requested to see the paper and noted there must be prior documents between Birotteau and Gamard. The fatal deed, the vicar said, was in his library.

The helplessness of this innocent man touched Madame de Listomère and Mademoiselle Salomon, who soothed him like mothers consoling a child. They promised lodging and counsel, urged play to distract him, and spoke of asking Troubert for help. Dazzled by the prospect of living with Madame de Listomère, he temporarily forgot his ruined happiness. But that night the reality returned: thoughts of moving, of scattered books and furniture, and the end of long habits overwhelmed him. He could not reconcile one sweet first year in Gamard’s house with the cruel second; the canonry seemed a poor compensation. He clung to Mademoiselle Salomon’s friendship, but dared not wholly trust new attachments.

The narrator digresses to note that among spinsters there are heroic kinds—those who sacrifice themselves nobly. Mademoiselle Salomon belonged to this class: once beloved and young, she had tended a lover who went mad, refusing to call him insane; she had devoted five years to his mechanical well‑being, and after his death settled in Tours to do good. Simple, frank, and regular in features, she attached herself to the feeble, making her warmly interested in Birotteau.

Mademoiselle de Villenoix returned Birotteau to Tours the next morning and left him at the cathedral quay to go to the Cloister, bent on preserving the canonry and overseeing the removal of his furniture. At the house he found his old apartment occupied: Marianne told him Troubert was installed in his rooms. The sight of Troubert, comfortably seated in Chapeloud’s library and armchair, confirmed the depth of the conspiracy. Birotteau realized with sinking dread that Troubert intended to possess Chapeloud’s and his spoils. Facing Troubert’s sardonic glance, he felt struck dumb.

Troubert coolly told him Gamard had informed him of Birotteau’s departure and that he had been installed out of necessity; if the furniture belonged to Birotteau, Gamard’s scrupulous honesty guaranteed its return; if he wished to return, Troubert would cede the apartment. The words were terrible. Birotteau ran to find Gamard and met her on the staircase. Bowing despite her derisive smile, he protested that she should have allowed him to pack his books and furniture. She interrupted, asking if he had read his deed, and then, with the triumphant clarity of hatred, reminded him of the clause: by agreement, if he left her house his furniture would indemnify her for the difference in board between what he paid and what the late Abbé Chapeloud had paid. With Poirel’s appointment as canon, Gamard exulted in the arrangement. At these words Birotteau faintly bowed and fled, fearing a complete breakdown if he stayed.

He found at Madame de Listomère’s house his linen, clothing, and papers packed in a trunk, and sank, hiding his face in his hands. Poirel had been made canon; Birotteau had no home, means, or furniture. Mademoiselle Salomon, unaware of the deed’s terms, believed his distress due to Poirel’s nomination and, persuaded by the porter, took him in her carriage. Back at the Alouette, friends tended him until evening, when he recounted the morning’s events.

De Bourbonne asked to see the deed. Birotteau produced it. De Bourbonne read the clause providing that, because of an eight‑hundred‑franc yearly difference between Chapeloud’s board and Gamard’s charge, and because Birotteau could not pay full price, he had accepted sums lent by Gamard and, as indemnity, engaged to leave her all household property at death, or to transfer it should he voluntarily give up the apartment. De Bourbonne cursed the agreement and declared Gamard armed with claws.

Birotteau had never imagined anything would separate him from the house where he hoped to live and die. He had not remembered the clause, which he had not discussed; in his eagerness to enter the house he would have signed any document. Madame de Listomère, in hot indignation, declared she had made him sign and must restore his lost happiness. De Bourbonne advised law: the deed might constitute fraud. The Baron and others urged litigation. De Bourbonne privately warned that many now friendly would soon desert him, and if Birotteau wished peace he should resign the vicariate and retire to a distant parish where Troubert could not pursue him. This prospect horrified the priest—leaving Tours was for him like death; celibates replace feelings with habits, and Birotteau needed his routine: his walks, salons, and games.

Word quickly spread that Madame la Baronne de Listomère had invited Birotteau to stay. The baroness’s reception implicitly denied Gamard’s assertions and wounded Gamard deeply. To understand Gamard’s resources, one must see the network that served Troubert. Accompanied by Troubert, Gamard made evening rounds to houses where circles of old maids, a few men swayed by servants’ gossip, and other busybodies met. These women, ever sifting words and scrutinizing neighbors, kept exact accounts of fortunes and actions, retailing scandal and shaping local opinion. Spread through town like capillary fibres, they absorbed household secrets and transmitted them mechanically to Troubert. They formed a kind of informal police—dumb but perpetually talking—whose gossip could have terrible effects when it touched important interests. The quarrel became the absorbing passion of these salons; beneath it lay a class sentiment—a small‑town people‑versus‑senate struggle.

Yet these petty passions engaged their participants with an intensity equal to great affairs. Troubert’s hours, like those of a gambler or statesman, were heavy with anxious thought. The narrator asks, in passing, to consider how certain passions prompt virtues that widen a life beyond its selfish center.

Madame de Listomère returned to Tours and took Birotteau to her lawyer. The lawyer, whose practice lay among the devout, did not think the case easy. Friends who had believed justice certain had delayed bringing the suit until returning to town, and Gamard’s allies had taken the initiative, spreading the affair to Birotteau’s damage. According to the deed, Gamard had the law on her side; in equity, judges and respectable laymen might view Birotteau as having derogated from his formerly peaceable and mild character. Gamard had lent him money to pay Chapeloud’s legacy duties without a receipt; Birotteau, not of age or character to sign with full understanding, left Gamard’s house after two years while Chapeloud and Troubert had been there twelve and fifteen years respectively; the suit might appear like ingratitude. The lawyer refused to undertake it and besought Madame de Listomère to avoid embroiling herself.

That evening Birotteau told friends of the lawyer’s refusal. De Bourbonne advised continued caution and suggested only a radical lawyer might take the case. The Baron, inflamed, insisted Troubert’s involvement made it a public matter; valuable pictures and furniture had been implicated, and Birotteau could not have intended to give away such wealth for two years of board. He proposed getting Monsieur Salmon, an expert from the Museum, to appraise the paintings and furniture.

Two days later the suit began. Choosing the radical lawyer both helped and harmed Birotteau: opponents of government and critics of religion seized on the case, and the town buzzed. The Museum expert appraised Valentin’s Virgin and Lebrun’s Christ at eleven thousand francs; the bookshelves and Gothic furniture, fashionable in Paris, were worth at least twelve thousand. The full appraisal exceeded thirty‑six thousand francs. Clearly, Birotteau could not have intended to give Gamard such a sum under the deed; legally, he had equitable grounds to amend the agreement; if refused, Gamard’s conduct might constitute intentional fraud. The radical lawyer served a harsh writ on Gamard, a document of skillful legal argument backed by precedent and code citations; it so clearly condemned the old maid that many copies were maliciously distributed through the town.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
A few days after hostilities began between Birotteau and the old maid, the Baron de Listomere, who expected promotion to corvette captain, received a letter warning he might be put on the retired list. He went to Paris and to the minister, who laughed at his fears; but the next day, despite the minister’s assurances, the baron made inquiries. By an indiscretion a secretary showed him a document confirming the news, awaiting only the director’s signature.

The baron hurried to an uncle, a deputy who could see the minister at the Chamber, and begged him to learn the minister’s intentions in a matter that threatened his future. Waiting anxiously in his uncle’s carriage, he was told on the way home: the minister had first accused him of leading Radicals in Tours and of objectionable politics; but the real trouble, his uncle learned, was ecclesiastical. The baron had spoken slightingly of Abbé Troubert, the vicar-general, an influential man representing the Jesuits in the province. His uncle warned him to make peace with Troubert at once, saying such priests were men with whom one must live in harmony; Troubert was likely to be raised to a bishopric and could even harm the uncle’s prospects.

These words explained to the baron Troubert’s secret activities that Birotteau had often wondered about: “I can’t think what he does with himself—sitting up all night.” Troubert’s position, skill, and female network in Tours had made him the Congregation’s proconsul there; archbishop, general, prefect—all were under his covert influence. The Baron de Listomere decided on his course. “I shall take care not to get another round shot below my water-line,” he told his uncle.

Three days later the baron, returning hurriedly, told his aunt the family must abandon support of “that fool of a Birotteau.” He detained Monsieur de Bourbonne as the old man was leaving a rubber of whist; Bourbonne's clear-sightedness seemed essential to navigate the reefs the Listomeres faced. The baron’s return and disquiet hinted he had met a check in his campaign against Gamard and Troubert. He revealed Troubert’s hidden power.

“I knew that,” said Bourbonne.

“Then why did you not warn us?” the baroness cried.

“Forget that I knew it, and I will forget that you did,” Bourbonne replied. “If we do not keep this secret now we shall be thought his accomplices. Pretend to be duped; but watch your steps. I warned you, but you would not understand me.”

Abandoning Birotteau was taken for granted by the three. “To beat a retreat with the honors of war has always been the triumph of the ablest generals,” said Bourbonne. “Bow to Troubert; if his hatred is less than his vanity you may win him; if you bow too low he will walk over you. Make believe you intend to leave the service, and you’ll escape him. Send away Birotteau, madame, and you will set things right with Mademoiselle Gamard. Ask Troubert, when you meet him at the archbishop’s, if he plays whist; he will say yes. Invite him to your salon; you are a woman and can win a priest. When the baron is promoted and his uncle made a peer, and Troubert a bishop, you can make Birotteau a canon if you choose. Meantime yield—but yield gracefully, with a slight menace. Your family can give him as much as he can give you. As for you, sailor, keep your deep-sea line about you.”

“Poor Birotteau?” said the baroness.

“Get rid of him at once,” Bourbonne replied. “If some clever Radical gets that empty head, he may cause trouble. The court might give a verdict in his favor, and Troubert must fear that. I have said my say.”

The next morning the baroness took Birotteau aside and, not without embarrassment, asked him to withdraw his lawsuit against Mademoiselle Gamard and to leave her house. As she spoke he turned pale. She explained, briefly and without disguising the gravity, the wide ramifications of the affair, making him see the net woven by revenge and the capacity and power of Troubert, whose hatred of Chapeloud now vented itself in seizing Chapeloud’s property and persecuting his friend. Birotteau clasped his hands and wept, listening as one at the edge of a precipice. The baroness ended: family duties must be considered before friendship; yield, and she would ensure he lacked nothing, arranging matters through Monsieur de Bourbonne to save appearances. “Grant me the right to abandon you,” she begged.

The bewildered abbé cried, “Chapeloud was right when he said that if Troubert could drag him by the feet out of his grave he would do it!” Yet he yielded. “I trust myself to you—I am but the stubble of the streets,” he said, using a local word for a pathetic bit of straw. He asked only for Chapeloud’s portrait, painted for him, and the baroness promised to see Mademoiselle Gamard herself and to consult the archbishop.

Birotteau left dismayed; Troubert now seemed to him a pyramid whose hands were in Paris and elbows in the cloister. The news of Birotteau’s removal from the Listomere house seemed mysterious: the baroness told people the nephew meant to marry and leave the navy, and wanted the vicar’s apartment. The Listomeres’ apparent neutrality implied recognition of the Congregation’s occult power. But the lawsuit still awaited judgment; their opponents yielded while threatening.

Then Mademoiselle Gamard took cold leaving the cathedral and was soon dangerously ill. The town burst into commiseration: “Mademoiselle Gamard’s sensitive nature could not bear the scandal; in spite of the justice of her cause she was likely to die of grief—Birotteau has killed his benefactress.” Such talk spread through the female conclave and the town.

Madame de Listomere went to pay her promised visit but the old maid was too ill to see her. She asked to speak to the vicar-general. Troubert, gratified to receive in Chapeloud’s library the woman who had hitherto ignored him, kept her waiting before admitting her. No courtier could have put more dissimulation into negotiating personal interests than the baroness and the priest did now.

Bourbonne had coached the baroness: “Don’t forget your cue. You are a mediator, not an interested party. Troubert is a mediator. Weigh your words; if he strokes his chin you have him.” Their conversation was a duel of implied meanings. Madame de Listomere began by expressing regret at Birotteau’s lawsuit and her desire to settle it.

“The harm is done, madame,” Troubert said gravely. “The pious Mademoiselle Gamard is dying.” (He intended to place the guilt on the baroness.)

“On hearing of her illness I entreated Monsieur Birotteau to relinquish his claims; I have brought the document intending to give it to that excellent woman,” she replied. (She meant to avoid compromising herself.) After a tense interchange of civilities and veiled thrusts about religion, politics, and the paintings, she laid the deed of relinquishment on the table and said she had come to reconcile two Christians.

“Your nephew has been to Paris, I believe,” Troubert said. “He returns tonight; the minister does not want him to leave the service.” “He has done us the service of getting a proper estimate on the value of those paintings,” Troubert added, eyeing the pictures. Madame de Listomere offered frames for Saint-Gatien if they were to be given to the chapel. He replied they did not belong to him. She handed him the relinquishment, saying it ended all discussion and that she had some compassion for Birotteau; he had asked, as the price of renunciation, for Chapeloud’s portrait. Troubert looked fixedly at her and went down to ask Mademoiselle Gamard.

He soon returned. “Madame,” he said, “I bring you the words of a dying woman. ‘The Abbé Chapeloud was so true a friend...that I cannot consent to part with his picture.’ As for me, if it were mine I would not yield it. My feelings to my late friend were so faithful that I should feel my right above others’.”

“That’s no need to quarrel over a bad picture,” the baroness said, offering to have a copy made and courteously inviting him to play whist at her house. Troubert, stroked his chin—caught by vanity—and promised he would oblige her for her sake.

Madame de Listomere went home hoping the archbishop would complete the peace. But Mademoiselle Gamard died the next day. Her will left everything to Abbé Troubert; the fortune was appraised at three hundred thousand francs. He sent the Listomeres invitations to the services and funeral; the baroness insisted they go. “We must go,” she said. Bourbonne added that the baron must go to the cemetery; the lieutenant, unluckily, had not left Tours.

The services were performed with unusual ecclesiastical magnificence. Only Birotteau wept, kneeling in a side chapel; he believed himself guilty and sincerely prayed for the deceased, lamenting that he had not obtained her forgiveness. Troubert followed the body to the grave and delivered a discourse that, by eloquence, enlarged the old maid’s life to monumental proportions. In the peroration he spoke of her life devoted to God and of Providence withdrawing her from trials; his tone suggested she rested innocent. After the sermon, in a gesture that betrayed more joy than grief, he sprinkled the coffin with holy water.

The next morning Mademoiselle Salomon came to tell Madame de Listomere that “our poor Abbé Birotteau has just received a frightful blow: he is appointed curate of Saint-Symphorien.” Saint-Symphorien lay beyond the bridge, a suburb three miles away—an exile in plain sight of the town. The parsonage was cold and damp, the parish too poor, and the removal seemed an infamous plot.

To end the history: five months later Troubert was made Bishop of Troyes; Madame de Listomere died, leaving an annuity of fifteen hundred francs to Abbé Birotteau. The day her will’s dispositions were announced, Monseigneur Hyacinthe, Bishop of Troyes, about to leave Tours for his diocese, delayed when he received the news. Furious at being foiled by the baroness—whom he had lately received while she had secretly aided a man he counted as an enemy—Troubert again threatened the baron’s career and jeopardized his uncle’s peerage. He made one of those soft, vengeful priestly speeches in the archbishop’s salon. The Baron de Listomere went to him the next day and must have submitted to hard conditions, for his later conduct showed entire submission to the Jesuit’s will.

The new bishop deeded Mademoiselle Gamard’s house to the cathedral chapter, gave Chapeloud’s books and bookcases to the seminary, and presented the two disputed pictures to the Chapel of the Virgin—but he kept Chapeloud’s portrait. No one could explain this near-total renunciation of Gamard’s bequest; Bourbonne supposed the bishop had secretly kept invested monies to support his rank in Paris. Only the night before Troubert’s departure did Bourbonne unearth the hidden reason: the baron had contested Madame de Listomere’s legacy to Birotteau on grounds of undue influence. The deathblow to the feeblest victim was complete.

A few days after the case was brought the baron was promoted to captain. As ecclesiastical discipline, the curate of Saint-Symphorien—Birotteau—was suspended; his superiors judged him guilty. The murderer of Sophie Gamard was also a swindler; had Troubert kept the property openly, it would have been harder to censure Birotteau.

When Monseigneur Hyacinthe drove along the quay Saint-Symphorien in a post-chaise on his way to Paris, poor Birotteau was placed in an armchair in the sun on a terrace above the road. Pale and haggard, smitten by the archbishop, he was the skeleton of the man who less than a year earlier had rolled contentedly along the cloister. The bishop cast one look of pity and contempt, then went his way.

There is no doubt Troubert might once have been a Hildebrand or an Alexander VI. In our era the Church is no longer the political power it once was; celibacy concentrates a man’s faculties on a single passion—egotism—which makes celibates either useless or mischievous. We live when governments tend to make Man for Society rather than Society for Man. There is a perpetual struggle between the Individual and a social system that uses him while he tries to use it. In earlier times man was first father, then lived for clan or city; later, for caste or religion, and his range of interests widened. Today his life attaches to a vast country; perhaps someday his family may be the whole universe.

Will such moral cosmopolitanism—Christian Rome’s hope of Brotherhood—prove only a sublime error? It is natural to believe in such a noble vision, but human nature lacks such divine proportions. Souls vast enough to hold a world of feeling belong to great exceptions. Physiologists who say as the brain enlarges the heart narrows are mistaken. The apparent egotism of those who bear a science, a nation, or a code is a highest passion; it is the maternity of the masses. To create peoples and ideas they must unite in their mighty brains the tenderness of woman and the force of God. The histories of Innocent III, Peter the Great, and other great leaders show the same vast thought of which Troubert was a provincial representative.

DEDICATION

To Monsieur Charles Nodier, member of the French Academy.

My dear Nodier, here is a book of deeds hidden from the action of the laws behind the closed doors of domestic life; in these affairs the finger of God, often called chance, supplies the place of human justice, and the moral, though sometimes pointed by a scoffer, remains striking and instructive.

Such deeds teach much about Family and Maternity. We may yet understand, perhaps too late, the effects of the diminution of paternal authority. That authority, which formerly ceased only at the father’s death, was the sole human tribunal before which domestic crimes could be arraigned; kings sometimes aided its execution. However good and tender a mother may be, she cannot perform the patriarchal function any more than a woman can take a king’s place on the throne. Here I have perhaps more plainly shown how essential to European society is the indissoluble marriage bond, how fatal feminine weakness can be, and how dangerous selfish interests are when indulged without restraint. May a society that worships wealth shudder to see the impotence of the law in dealing with a system that deifies success and forgives every means of attaining it. May it return to the Catholic religion for purification of its masses and an education other than that of a lay university.

In the “Scenes from Military Life” many noble self-devotions will be set forth; allow me to point out the depraving effect of war’s necessities on minds who then act at home as if on the battlefield.

You have cast a sagacious glance over our time; your philosophy shines in many bitter reflections. You have better than most perceived the havoc wrought in our minds by the existence of four distinct political systems. I cannot more fitly dedicate this history than under your protection. Your name may defend my work against the criticisms sure to follow it—for where is the patient who keeps silence when the surgeon lifts the dressing from his wound?

To the pleasure of dedicating this Scene to you I add the pride of making known your friendship for one who here subscribes himself

Your sincere admirer,
De Balzac
Paris, November, 1842.

THE TWO BROTHERS

CHAPTER I

In 1792 the people of Issoudun spoke of Doctor Rouget as consummately malignant. Rumor held he made his wife miserable, though she was the neighborhood beauty. Whether shallow or not, the wife’s character remained private; the doctor was “not pleasant to deal with,” and townsmen treated him civilly. His wife, a demoiselle Descoings, was frail as a girl—said to be why he married her—and bore a son, then, ten years later, a daughter, Agathe, whose birth surprised even the doctor.

These facts matter, for without them Rouget would seem a monster, while in truth he embodied an evil sheltered under the axiom that “men should have strength of character,” a phrase that has caused much female misery.

The elder Descoings, Agathe’s parents-in-law, were commission merchants in the wool trade, buying for manufacturers and selling for producers, growing rich and miserly. Their son Descoings disliked Issoudun, set up as a grocer in Paris’s rue Saint-Honoré, and by love, patience, and some parental money married his predecessor’s widow, Madame Bixiou.

In 1792 Descoings seemed to prosper. The old Descoings retired and bought forfeited estates. Doctor Rouget, foreseeing his wife’s death, sent Agathe to Paris to live with his brother-in-law: he judged that his child might win the affection of his childless sister and brother. Agathe, the handsomest maiden of Issoudun, did not resemble her parents. Her birth had driven a wedge between Doctor Rouget and his friend Monsieur Lousteau; Madame Rouget spent much time with Madame Hochon, Lousteau’s sister and Agathe’s godmother, who later told Issoudun what it knew of Madame Rouget.

When Rouget told his wife he would send Agathe to Paris, her first words were, “I shall never see my daughter again.” She grew yellow with grief; tongues said the doctor was killing her by inches. The foolish behavior of her son Jean-Jacques, who owed her little attention or respect, added to her misery. Jean-Jacques resembled his father in the worst ways; the doctor himself was unsatisfactory at best.

Agathe’s arrival did not bring happiness to her uncle. In the same period Descoings was imprisoned on a hint from Robespierre. He had expressed doubts of the famine to customers, and citoyenne Duplay, Robespierre’s housekeeper, denounced him for “accaparation.” His wife’s ill-judged efforts to obtain his release only worsened matters. She appealed to officials who either refused to meddle or forgot promises. A little sugar or a cordial to citoyenne Duplay might have saved Descoings. The lesson: relying on honest men can be as futile as trusting scoundrels; one must often rely on oneself. Descoings perished on the scaffold with André Chénier. His death produced more sensation then than Chénier’s; only thirty years later was it clear France lost more by Chénier’s death.

That neighborhood shop, near Robespierre’s lodging, seemed cursed; César Birotteau later failed there as well. During visits Bridau paid the widow, he was struck by Agathe’s calm, innocent beauty and married her with her father’s consent. Doctor Rouget, eager to secure his design, hurried to Paris to draw the marriage contract to his advantage. Bridau’s ardent and disinterested love gave carte blanche to the perfidious doctor, who used his son-in-law’s blindness to family machinations, as this history will show.

Madame Rouget, or rather the doctor, inherited the elder Descoings’ property; within two years both had died, and after his wife’s death in 1799 Rouget acquired vineyards, farms, and iron-works. Jean-Jacques proved incapable of action; the father destined him for proprietorship and let him grow in wealth and silliness. After 1799 town cipherers put at least thirty thousand francs’ income to the doctor’s credit. From his wife’s death he led a debauched life, kept within his house; this man of “strength of character” died in 1805. Jean-Jacques remained a bachelor for reasons partly his father’s fault, as will appear.

It is worth asking the result of the secret vengeance the doctor took on a daughter he did not recognize as his own, though legitimately his. Agathe bore a strong likeness to the doctor’s mother; resemblances sometimes skip generations. In turn, Agathe’s eldest child, who resembled her, possessed the moral qualities of his grandfather, Doctor Rouget.

Agathe’s beauty was pure and placid—an oval face, clear complexion, golden hair, composed mouth, long lashes, tender dark-blue eyes. Bridau’s portrait of her hangs in his atelier. She was the ideal provincial housekeeper, pious though not sanctimonious, with an education given by the Church. In ordinary terms she was an accomplished wife, yet her ignorance of life opened the way to misfortunes; the Roman epitaph “She did needlework and kept the house” fitted her.

Under the Consulate Bridau adored Napoleon and in 1804 became head of a department at the ministry of the interior with a salary of twelve thousand francs and handsome emoluments. From 1804 to 1808 the Bridaus lived in a handsome suite on the Quai Voltaire, near the ministry and the Tuileries. A cook and footman were their only servants. Agathe, early afoot, went to market with her cook and prepared breakfast; Bridau never went to the ministry before eleven. When together, she prepared his breakfast, watched him off at the window, cleared the table, dressed, played with the children, and later sat silent, knitting by his writing table when he worked late. Sometimes they attended the theatre or used a ministerial box; Agathe enjoyed the occasional restaurant as any provincial new to Paris. Bridau received friends Thursdays and gave an annual Shrove-Tuesday ball.

Their conjugal life had but three events: the birth of two children three years apart and Bridau’s death in 1808, killed by overwork just as Napoleon was about to appoint him director-general, count, and councillor of state. Napoleon, impressed by Bridau’s devotion, was about to reward him when the effort to finish a work before the Emperor’s departure caused an inflammatory fever that killed him. Touched by a devotion that received none of the brilliant recognitions given to soldiers, Napoleon planned an order to requite civil services, the Order of the Reunion, worn with a blue ribbon, though Providence thwarted that profanation. After Bridau’s death the Emperor inquired into his widow’s circumstances: both sons received scholarships at the Imperial Lyceum paid from the Emperor’s privy purse, and Madame Bridau was given a pension of four thousand francs.

From her marriage to her husband’s death Agathe had had no communication with Issoudun. Her mother died as she was about to bear her youngest son, and when her father died, the coronation of the Emperor kept Bridau busy and Agathe unwilling to leave him. Her brother Jean-Jacques had not written. Once a year she received a letter from her godmother Madame Hochon, to which she replied with commonplaces and paid little heed to the pious advice therein.

Some time before Doctor Rouget’s death Madame Hochon warned Agathe she would get nothing from her father’s estate unless she gave a power of attorney to Monsieur Hochon. Agathe was reluctant to harass her brother. Whether because Bridau thought the spoliation legal or because he shared his wife’s magnanimity, he would not listen to Roguin, his notary, who urged him to use his ministerial position to contest the deeds by which the father had deprived his daughter. Thus husband and wife tacitly sanctioned the transfer. Roguin, however, forced Bridau to reflect that if he died first Agathe would be left without property. Reviewing their finances, Bridau found that between 1793 and 1805 they had used nearly thirty thousand of the fifty thousand francs that old Rouget had given to Agathe at her marriage. He invested the remaining twenty thousand in public funds then quoted at forty, from which Agathe received about two thousand francs a year. As a widow, she could live suitably on an income of six thousand francs. With provincial good sense she planned to change residence, dismiss the footman, and keep only a cook; but her intimate friend Madame Descoings, insisting on being considered her aunt, sold her own establishment and came to live with Agathe, taking Bridau’s study as her bedroom.

The two widows pooled their revenues, possessing jointly about twelve thousand francs a year. Such simple, “natural” measures often deserve attention, for fools excuse themselves by calling wrongs natural. Behold the serpent amid flowers.

In 1809 Madame Descoings, who never told her age, was sixty-five. Once called a beauty, she had kept her looks by constitution; medium height, plump, fresh, with fine shoulders and a rather rosy complexion, her blond hair showed no gray despite her husband’s fate. She loved good cheer, the theatre, and a secret vice—lottery shares. Like women lucky enough to stay young, she spent on dress and was the pleasantest of companions, never opposing anyone and giving and taking a jest. If she did not marry again, it was no doubt the times: during the Empire marrying men found young women too easily.

Madame Descoings often cheered Madame Bridau with theatre and drives, prepared excellent dinners, and even tried to marry her to her own son by her first husband, Bixiou. To do this she revealed a family secret carefully kept by her, her late husband, and their notary: her son Bixiou, thirty-five and a widowed major who later perished at Lützen, had a son—a grandson she secretly saw and claimed as the child of her first wife. That boy, educated with Agathe’s sons at the Imperial Lyceum on a half-scholarship, proved clever, witty, and gifted as a draughtsman and designer.

Agathe, who lived only for her children, declined to remarry out of fidelity to her husband and good sense. But being a good mother is harder than being a good wife; a widow must both love and command her children. Few women are firm enough to perform that double duty. Thus Agathe, despite many virtues, was the innocent cause of grave unhappiness. Through her lack of intelligence and blind confidence, she fell victim to Madame Descoings, who nursed a combination of three numbers in a lottery and used the joint household money to pay stakes, hoping at last to win and enrich her grandson, Agathe, and the little Bridaus.

When the debts reached ten thousand francs she increased her stakes; failing to win, the debt rolled up. At twenty thousand francs she lost her head, still hoping for a trey. She attempted to mortgage her property to help, but Roguin, the notary, showed that the late Doctor Rouget had secured her a life-interest only by disinheriting Madame Descoings in favor of his son Jean-Jacques. No money-lender would advance twenty thousand francs to a sixty-six-year-old on an annuity of about four thousand when ten percent could be obtained elsewhere. One morning Madame Descoings fell at Agathe’s feet and, sobbing, confessed the state of things. Agathe reproached her not; she dismissed the footman and cook, sold all but the bare necessities of her furniture, sold three-fourths of her government funds, paid the debts, and bade farewell to her appartement.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
One of the worst corners in Paris was the section of the rue Mazarin between the rue Guenegard and the rue de Seine, behind the Institute. The high gray walls of the college and Cardinal Mazarin’s library cast chill shadows there; the sun seldom shone and the north wind blew. The poor ruined widow, Madame Bridau, took a third-floor lodging in a shallow house at that damp, dark corner. Opposite were the Institute buildings with the studios of young artists—rapins—then still there. From Madame Bridau’s new windows one could peer into those melancholy barred cages. Looking north the view closed on the dome of the Institute; up the street the only movement was a file of hackney-coaches. She planted boxes of earth at the windows, cultivating illicit aerial gardens whose vegetables improved the air. The staircase wound round upon itself; the third floor was the last. Three windows opened to three rooms—dining-room, a small salon, and a chamber on one side of the landing; on the other a little kitchen and two single rooms; above, an immense garret without partitions. She chose this lodging because it cost only four hundred francs a year on a nine-year lease, because it was near her sons’ school, and because she was used to the quarter.

The interior matched the outside. The dining-room, hung with yellow paper patterned with small green flowers and floored with unglazed tiles, contained only a table, two sideboards, and six chairs brought from the other apartment. The salon had an Aubusson carpet given to Bridau when the ministry was refurnished, and a common mahogany sofa with Egyptian heads covered in green silk. Over it hung an amateur pastel portrait of Bridau that drew the eye: the noble, obscure brow, serene yet proud eyes, prudent lips, and frank smile suggested a man of duty—“Justum et tenacem,” as the Emperor had said. On the opposite wall, over a card-table, flashed a picture of the Emperor in brilliant colors by Vernet, Napoleon riding rapidly, attended by his escort.

Agathe had given herself two large birdcages—canaries and Java sparrows—and adopted this childish fancy since her husband’s loss. After three months the widowed chamber had become a litter: cats domiciled on the sofa; canaries, occasionally let loose, left droppings on the furniture. She scattered millet and chickweed and put tidbits for the cats in broken saucers. Garments lay everywhere. Everything of Bridau’s was scrupulously preserved: his desk implements were kept with the care one might give a knight’s armor; she had wrapped and sealed the last pen he used and marked it so. The cup from which he drank his last draught stood on the fireplace. After his death no trace of coquetry or ordinary self-care remained in the young widow of thirty-five. Parted from the only man she had known and loved—one who had never caused her unhappiness—she seemed to have renounced her womanhood. Some people, through love, transfer the self to another; when that other dies, no life of their own remains.

Agathe now lived only for her children and was infinitely sad at the privations their ruin would bring. From the move to the rue Mazarin a melancholy took her face. She hoped a little in the Emperor; he could do no more than he already was doing—giving three hundred francs a year to each child from his privy purse, besides scholarships.

The brilliant Descoings occupied an apartment on the second floor, similar to her niece’s above. She had assigned Madame Bridau three thousand francs out of her annuity; Roguin, the notary, arranged it, but it would take seven years of slow repayment to make good the loss. The Descoingses, reduced to an income of twelve hundred francs, lived modestly with her niece. They employed a woman-of-all-work for mornings only; Madame Descoings liked to cook. In evenings a few old friends—persons at the ministry who owed their places to Bridau—came for cards with the two widows. Madame Descoings still cherished her trey, declaring it obstinate about turning up; she hoped by one stroke to repay the loan she’d made upon her niece. She was dear to the Bridaus partly from a feeling of wrong she had done them and partly because her niece never reproached her under privation. So Philippe and Joseph were cosseted; the old gambler of the Imperial Lottery cooked them nice dinners with sweets. Later, they could extract small sums from her—one for pencils and paper, the elder for tennis-shoes and marbles. Madame Descoings’s passion forced her to be content with fifty francs a month for domestic expenses so she could gamble with the rest.

Madame Bridau, by motherly love, kept her expenses to the same sum. As penance for former overconfidence she cut off little enjoyments. Timid and distrusting, she exaggerated risk into virtue. She feared the Emperor might die or forget them; she shuddered at the risk her children ran of being left without means. Unable to understand Roguin’s assurance that in seven years Descoings’s assignment would restore the sold sum, she trusted neither notary, aunt, nor government; she trusted only herself and practiced privation. By laying aside three thousand francs every year from her pension she fancied she would have thirty thousand in ten years, yielding fifteen hundred a year to her children. At thirty-six she might live twenty years more; if she kept to the plan she could leave each child enough for bare necessities.

Thus the two widows passed from hollow opulence to voluntary poverty—one through a vice, the other through the purest virtue. These petty details matter: the view into student dens below, the tumult of the rapins, the need to look up at the sky to escape the damp street, the presence of that portrait full of soul, the sight of rich old colors in the calm home, the mother’s preference for her elder child and opposition to the younger’s tastes—these facts prefaced this history and perhaps explain Joseph Bridau, one of the greatest painters of the modern French school.

Philippe, the elder, strikingly resembled his mother. Blond and blue-eyed, he had a daring look taken for intrepidity and courage. Old Claparon, who had entered the ministry with Bridau and was one of the whist players, tapped Philippe’s cheek and prophesied he’d “stand to his guns.” Stimulated, the boy assumed a resolute manner, became adroit at bodily exercises; fights at the Lyceum taught endurance and contempt for pain, laying the foundation of military valor. He acquired a distaste for study; public education failed to develop mind and body together.

Agathe believed physical resemblance implied moral likeness; she confidently expected delicacy of feeling combined with manly vigor. Philippe was fifteen when the family moved to rue Mazarin; his winning ways confirmed maternal belief. Joseph, three years younger, resembled his father but only in defective ways. His thick black hair was always disorderly; Philippe’s was neat. By some fatality Joseph could not keep his clothes clean; dress him in new clothes and he made them look old. The elder took care of his things out of vanity. Unconsciously Agathe scolded Joseph and held up his brother as example; she never treated them alike. Fetching them from school she worried especially about Joseph. Such trifles drove her into maternal preference.

No one among the ordinary circle—Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, or even the Abbé Loraux—noticed Joseph’s faculty for observation. Absorbed in his own tastes, the future colorist paid no attention to himself; during childhood this torpor alarmed his father. His face, which later gained originality, wore a sullen expression. The features were pinched, contracted by the close attention Joseph paid to his surroundings. Philippe flattered his mother with clever sayings and lively answers, prompting hope. Joseph was taciturn, a dreamer. The mother hoped great things of Philippe and expected nothing of Joseph.

Joseph’s leaning to art began simply. During Easter holidays of 1812, returning from a walk in the Tuileries with his brother and Madame Descoings, he saw a pupil drawing a caricature of a professor on the wall of the Institute and stopped in admiration. The next day he stood at the window watching pupils enter by the rue Mazarin door; then slipped into the long courtyard with statues, busts, half-finished marbles, plasters, and baked clays; he gazed feverishly—his instinct awakened, his vocation stirred. He entered a ground-floor room with a half-open door where a dozen young men drew from a statue; they began to make fun of him.

“Hi! little one,” cried the first, scattering crumbs.

They mocked his ugliness. For a quarter hour Joseph bore the teasing in Chaudet’s atelier. But his persistency and expression struck the pupils; when asked what he wanted, Joseph said he wished to learn to draw. They encouraged him. Won by friendliness, he said he was Madame Bridau’s son.

“Oh! if you are Madame Bridau’s son,” they cried, “you will certainly be a great man!” They teased and tested him—one pupil set up a “telegraph trial,” stretching Joseph’s arms and daring him to hold them so. Joseph, at thirteen, stood motionless for five minutes amid shouts; at ten minutes sweat stood on his brow. Then a bald-headed, pale, sickly man entered—Chaudet. He lowered Joseph’s arms, patted him, and asked what brought him. Hearing Joseph wanted to be an artist and was Madame Bridau’s son, Chaudet, struck by recollection of Bridau’s service, welcomed him. He gave him a board, paper, and chalks, sent a pupil for cakes and sugar-plums, and petted the child. The scene—rough, warm-hearted, and real—impressed Joseph. Chaudet, for whom the Emperor’s protection earlier opened prospects later cut short by premature death, appeared like a vision. Joseph told his mother nothing, but he spent two hours every Sunday and Thursday in Chaudet’s atelier. Madame Descoings, who humored the boy’s fancies, supplied pencils, red chalks, prints, and paper. At school he sketched masters, drew comrades, and showed surprising assiduity in drawing-class. Lemire, his drawing-master, reported Joseph’s faculty to Madame Bridau. She, like a true provincial with little knowledge of art, was terrified. When Lemire left, she wept.

“What is to become of me!” she cried to Madame Descoings. “Joseph, whom I meant to make a government clerk—protected by his father’s memory at the ministry—wants to be a painter, a vagabond! I always knew that child would give me nothing but trouble.”

Madame Descoings confessed she had encouraged Joseph’s passion, taking him to the Salon where his interest in pictures was, she declared, miraculous.

“If he understands painting at thirteen,” she said, “your Joseph will be a man of genius.”

“Yes; and see what genius did for his father—killed him with overwork at forty!” Agathe answered.

As Joseph entered his fourteenth year, Agathe, against Madame Descoings’s entreaties, went to Chaudet and requested he cease to “debauch” her son. She found the sculptor in a blue smock modeling his last statue, working with spasmodic jerks born of passion. He received the widow roughly at first but, moved by Bridau’s memory and by devotion to craftsmanship, he burst into holy wrath at her plea.

“I was under obligations to your deceased husband; I wished to help his son, to watch his first steps in the noblest of careers,” he cried. “A great artist is a king—happier, independent; he reigns in the world of fancy. Your son has a glorious future. Faculties like his are rare; they are disclosed at his age in beings like Giottos, Raphaels, Titians. In my opinion he will make a better painter than sculptor. If I had such a son, I should be as happy as the Emperor is to have given himself the King of Rome. You are mistress of his fate. Make him a miserable quill-driver, tie him to a desk, and you’ve murdered him! But a true vocation is stronger than obstacles. Vocation means a call—God’s election. You will make your child unhappy, that’s all.” He flung the clay aside and dismissed the day’s work.

Agathe, alarmed by the sight of a nude woman in a corner, left horrified. Chaudet told his pupils she was not to bring the little Bridau any more; they jeered as she closed the door. When the students learned she opposed the Institute, their happiness turned to the project of getting Joseph among them. He often slipped into Regnauld the painter’s studio where he was encouraged to paint. When the widow complained the bargain was not kept, Chaudet’s pupils insisted they were not Chaudet and composed a long mocking song about her.

That evening Agathe refused cards and sat drowned in tears.

“What is the matter, Madame Bridau?” asked old Claparon.

“She thinks her boy will have to beg his bread because he has the bump of painting,” Madame Descoings said. The men offered their usual advice: let the boy follow his bent; if not a genius, he could later be placed in an office. Desroches spoke of keeping a son hard at work to make a man of him; Du Bruel warned against letting a boy choose a profession at his age. Claparon and others counselled resignation to Joseph’s tastes. Their unanimity only increased Agathe’s anxieties. She feared the atelier’s naked women; Madame Descoings joked about “good fires.”

Soon after, the disasters of the retreat from Moscow became known; Napoleon returned to Paris to organize troops. Philippe, tired of school, wanted to serve the Emperor; he saw a review at the Tuileries—the last Napoleon ever held—and became infatuated with soldier’s life. Military splendor and uniforms seduced him. Without his mother’s knowledge he wrote a petition to the Emperor asking to enter the army. Within twenty-four hours the Emperor sent him to the Imperial Lyceum at Saint-Cyr; six months later, in November 1813, he was appointed sub-lieutenant in a cavalry regiment. He spent that winter in cantonments, learned to ride, and was dispatched to the front. During the campaign in France he was made lieutenant after an outpost affair where his bravery saved his colonel’s life. The Emperor named him captain at La Fere-Champenoise and took him on his staff. Inspired, Philippe won the cross at Montereau, witnessed Napoleon’s farewell at Fontainebleau, refused to serve the Bourbons, and returned in July 1814 to find his mother ruined.

Joseph’s scholarship was withdrawn after the holidays; Madame Bridau vainly entreated that it be inscribed on the ministry rolls. Joseph, more devoted than ever to painting, begged to go to Regnauld and promised to earn his living, saying he was advanced enough in the second class to do without rhetoric. Philippe, a captain at nineteen and decorated, who had served the Emperor as aide-de-camp in two battles, flattered the mother immensely. Coarse and blustering, without merit beyond cavalry bravery, he was to her a man of genius; whereas Joseph, puny, sickly, unkempt, and dreaming an artist’s glory, would only worry her.

The winter of 1814–1815 favored Joseph. Secretly encouraged by Madame Descoings and Bixiou, a pupil of Gros, he worked in that celebrated atelier, forming an intimacy with Schinner. The return from Elba came; Captain Bridau joined the Emperor at Lyons, accompanied him to the Tuileries, and commanded a squadron in the Guard’s dragoons. After Waterloo—where he was slightly wounded and took the officer’s Legion of Honor—he was near Marshal Davoust at Saint-Denis and, through Davoust’s intercession, retained his cross and rank though placed on half-pay.

Joseph, anxious about his future, studied with ardor through these turbulent times, sometimes to the point of illness.

“It is the smell of the paints,” Agathe said to Madame Descoings. “He ought to give up a business so injurious to his health.”

Agathe’s anxieties remained for her lieutenant-colonel son. When he returned in 1816 reduced from a nine-thousand-franc commander’s salary to half-pay, she fitted attic rooms for him and spent her savings. Philippe belonged to the faithful Bonapartes of café Lemblin; he acquired the habits and airs of a half-pay officer, exaggerated them, spurned the Bourbons, and refused infantry opportunities. To his mother he seemed noble. “The father himself could have done no more,” she said.

Philippe’s half-pay sufficed; he cost little at home, whereas Joseph’s expenses were paid by the widows. From then Agathe’s preference for Philippe was openly shown. Up to that time it had been secret; but the colonel’s persecution for the Emperor and his wound drew forth maternal tenderness. “He is unfortunate” explained everything to her. Joseph, simple in the artist’s way and brought up to admire his big brother, was not hurt by maternal preference and shared his worship of the hero who had carried Napoleon’s orders. How could he doubt the superiority of the brother he had seen in green and gold, commanding on the Champ de Mars?

Agathe, notwithstanding preference, was an excellent mother. She loved Joseph, though she could not understand him. Joseph adored his mother; Philippe let her adore him. Toward her the dragoon softened his military roughness yet never concealed contempt for Joseph—expressed in a patronizing, almost friendly way. He nicknamed the weak, shrunken seventeen-year-old “Cub.” Philippe’s patronizing manners would have wounded anyone less indifferent than Joseph, who believed soldiers hid goodness beneath brutal exteriors. Joseph did not yet know that soldiers of genius can be gentle and courteous; all genius is alike.

“Poor boy!” said Philippe to his mother, “we mustn’t plague him; let him do as he likes.” To Agathe, the colonel’s contempt seemed fraternal affection. “Philippe will always love and protect his brother,” she thought.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
In 1816 Joseph obtained his mother’s permission to convert the garret adjoining his attic into an atelier; Madame Descoings contributed money for his necessities. The two widows treated painting as a trade, but Joseph arranged the humble studio. Madame Descoings persuaded the owner to add a skylight; Joseph painted the hall chocolate and hung sketches. Agathe reluctantly installed an iron stove so her son could work at home without abandoning Gros’s or Schinner’s studios.

The constitutional party, backed by half-pay officers and Bonapartists, stirred emeutes around the Chamber of Deputies; conspiracies were rumored. Philippe, involved in these affairs, was arrested and released for lack of proof; the minister of war restored him to the active list, ending his half-pay. France seemed unsafe; Agathe feared provocateurs. Philippe spent time in disaffected cafés, drinking and picking up bad habits, while Agathe lived in dread. The Grecian sages still visited Madame Bridau nightly. Claparon, retained at the ministry though cautious, whispered Moniteur news. Desroches, retired after old Du Bruel, still fought for his pension. Their friends urged Agathe to send Philippe abroad.

“They talk of conspiracies, and your son will be certain to fall a victim,” they warned.

“Philippe is cut from the wood the Emperor made into marshals,” Du Bruel replied; “he mustn’t give up his profession. Let him serve in the East, in India—”

“Think of his health,” Agathe answered.

“Why doesn’t he get some place, some business?” Desroches asked. “There are private offices to be had.” Agathe insisted Philippe was a soldier and wouldn’t want anything else. Du Bruel suggested royal favor could make him lieutenant-colonel, though Madame Descoings doubted his prospects without noble birth.

To please his mother, Philippe took up the notion of joining General Lallemand in the United States and helping found the Champ d’Asile, one of the most disastrous swindles run under the name of a national subscription. Agathe gave ten thousand francs and saw him off at Havre. By the end of 1817 she lived on the six hundred francs left and, by prudent investment, secured seven percent on savings. Joseph emulated her frugality: dressed plainly, denied himself comforts, and lived on bread, milk, and Brie; only Madame Descoings and Bixiou showed sympathy.

Joseph later recalled how the coming summer saved him the cost of charcoal. Now a colorist as capable as Gros, he consulted his master only occasionally. He was already planning a revolt against classical traditions, aiming to free art to represent Nature — a struggle that began with his first Salon exhibition and never ceased.

1818 was terrible for the family. Roguin, Madame Descoings’s notary, absconded with seven years’ withheld annuity funds. Three days later a bill of exchange for a thousand francs arrived from New York: Philippe had lost everything in the Champ d’Asile. The letter told of debts contracted and comrades who had indorsed for him. Agathe, blaming herself, wept. The two women faced selling silver, linen, and furniture. Joseph, alarmed, told Gérard, who secured an order for two royal copies of Louis XVIII’s portrait at five hundred francs each and supplied Joseph with materials. Joseph painted four panels in ten days, sold them, and brought his mother the thousand francs to meet Philippe’s draft. Then a letter announced Philippe’s imminent return on a packet whose captain had trusted him for passage; he would need a thousand francs at Havre.

Joseph promised his mother the money after finishing the copies. Agathe, ecstatically grateful, kissed him and called him “his father all over.” Madame Bridau rushed to Havre and watched beyond the round tower daily for the packet. The sight of home softened Philippe, but his misfortunes in Texas and New York had brought out his worst traits: he had grown brutal, selfish, rude, given to drink and smoking, and imagined himself persecuted. Such men either live by honest virtues or treat every want as necessity; Philippe had drifted to the latter, becoming cunning and self-centered while keeping an air of frank soldierly simplicity. His figure was stouter, face bronzed, speech terse and decisive; exposed to hardship he had become a man who makes himself feared. To his mother he remained a hero; in truth he had become, bluntly, a blackguard.

At Havre Agathe bought him clothes and could not at first restrain his indulgence. The scheme of conquering Texas with imperial remnants had failed from treachery and poor management; the Liberals had used the enterprise selfishly, abandoning devoted men. Agathe, a household manager with shrewd common sense, saw through the political chicane and recognized the subscription’s failure; she could not make her son see he had been duped rather than persecuted. During the costly week at Havre she could not persuade him to make terms with the royal government. She brought him back to Paris before her funds gave out.

On arrival, those awaiting them noted how much Agathe had aged. Philippe greeted Madame Descoings coolly. Joseph, now twenty-one and confident, was hurt by his brother’s contempt. At a family dinner the friends — Du Bruel, Claparon, Desroches, and the Abbe Loraux — observed the exile’s roughened manners and coarse voice. While Joseph prepared the card tables, the intimate friends asked Agathe what she intended for Philippe. She could not get him to serve the Bourbons; Du Bruel suggested other roles like writing plays, as his son had once done. Philippe, Bixiou, and young Desroches played ecarte. Madame Descoings, eager to celebrate the prodigal’s return, provided funds for play; Joseph secretly slipped twenty francs to Philippe.

The evening grew lively. Philippe, a poor player, ended owing fifty francs. He showed bad temper and quarreled with young Desroches and then with Desroches senior, who forbade further play though his son was right. Madame Descoings silenced her grandson’s barbed witticisms for fear of provoking the colonel. Philippe’s temper and gambling continued into the night.

The next morning the two widows, counting resources, resolved to economize: Madame Descoings proposed selling furniture and giving up apartments to save seven hundred francs a year and pay Philippe fifty francs monthly until he found something. They pooled incomes — about five thousand three hundred francs, four thousand from Madame Descoings as a life annuity — and had consumed their savings. Philippe promised to find a situation and asked only for board and lodging. Agathe kissed him; Madame Descoings slipped him a hundred francs to cover his losses.

Within ten days the furniture was sold and the household rearranged. Philippe’s habits hardened: he left after breakfast, returned at dinner, and spent evenings in cafés drinking brandy, reading newspapers, playing billiards at the Café Minerve, smoking many cigars, and gambling at night. He boasted of a regular life and, while often winning, returned home talking of smashing the ultras and the Bourbon bodyguard.

He dined often with army comrades who spoke freely of hopes based on plots and inventions like a submarine to restore the Emperor. He particularly liked an old dragoons’ captain, Giroudeau, whose company led Philippe into further dissipation and into seeing women as resources. Giroudeau, a bluff old roue, introduced Philippe to the theatre and to a young dancer, Florentine, and to another, Marie Godeschal — Mariette — who was beautiful, cold, and ambitious. Mariette, orphaned and poor, had a devoted brother who clerked in Derville’s law office; she resolved to use her beauty and talent to help him. Poorly dressed yet striking, she provoked Philippe’s passion. To Mariette, Philippe was a Guard staff-officer, and a liaison with him would elevate her above rivals. Florentine and Giroudeau encouraged a kind of morganatic connection between Philippe and Mariette.

Giroudeau promised to speak to his nephew Finot at a theatrical journal and to get Philippe a place on a newspaper. Philippe revealed his poverty, but Giroudeau reassured him: his nephew could put him on a paper or help him get patronage. Mariette, thinking Philippe would write for a newspaper and further her claims, accepted him.

The next day Philippe met Giroudeau at the subscription office where the veteran lived in a cramped room labeled “Subscription Office.” There they encountered Coloquinte, a one-armed man, and then climbed to Giroudeau’s nephew Finot’s attic, where Finot, cool and sardonic, eyed Philippe. Giroudeau praised the colonel; Finot mocked the Texan affair a little, then gave Philippe a plan: exploit his position as a victim of the Liberals and demand an account of the Champ d’Asile subscription. A new Opposition journal was starting; Finot could make Philippe its cashier with a salary of three thousand francs if Philippe found someone to go security for twenty thousand francs. “Threaten loudly,” Finot advised, “and we’ll silence you with a place.” Philippe was grateful and impressed by Finot’s worldly cleverness.

That night at cafés Philippe fulminated against the Liberals, declaring he would demand an accounting of the moneys collected. He went to Mariette to tell her of the expected post on a newspaper with ten thousand subscribers and how he would advance her claims.

Agathe and Madame Descoings waited anxiously for Philippe one night amid the political alarm following the assassination of the Duc de Berry. He returned with good news: he had been promised the cashiership of a new journal at three thousand francs on condition they could go security for twenty thousand francs by depositing their shares in the Funds as guarantee while drawing the interest. The friends urged Agathe to go security; she and Madame Descoings, overjoyed at the prospect of providing for Philippe, transferred her shares — representing thirteen hundred francs in interest — to secure him. Philippe promised to pay one hundred francs monthly for board and lodging and was acclaimed the best of sons. Those who had doubted him congratulated Agathe and admitted they had been unjust. Joseph, not to be outdone, resolved to pay for his own support and succeeded.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Three months later the colonel, who ate and drank enough for four, found fault with the household food and compelled the poor widows, on the ground of his pay, to spend much on their table, though he had not yet paid a penny. His mother and Madame Descoings, out of delicacy, would not remind him of his promise. The year passed without one of those coins Leon Gozlan so vigorously called “tigers with five claws” finding its way from Philippe’s pocket to the household purse. He quieted his conscience by seldom dining at home.

“Well, he is happy,” said his mother; “he is easy in mind; he has a place.”

Through the influence of a feuilleton edited by Vernou, a friend of Bixiou, Finot, and Giroudeau, Mariette appeared not at the Panorama-Dramatique but at the Porte-Saint-Martin, where she triumphed beside the famous Begrand. Among the directors was a rich and luxurious general in love with an actress, for whose sake he had become an impresario. This officer knew Philippe and Giroudeau. Mariette’s first appearance, heralded by Finot’s journal and by Philippe, was promptly arranged by the three officers; there seems to be solidarity among the passions in folly.

The mischievous Bixiou soon revealed to his grandmother and the devoted Agathe that Philippe, the cashier, the hero of heroes, was in love with Mariette, the celebrated ballet-dancer. The news was a thunder-clap to the two widows; Agathe’s religious principles taught her that women on the stage were brands in the burning; she and Madame Descoings thought such women dined off gold, drank pearls, and wasted fortunes.

“Now do you suppose,” said Joseph to his mother, “that my brother is such a fool as to spend his money on Mariette? Such women only ruin rich men.”

“They talk of engaging Mariette at the Opera,” said Bixiou. “Don’t be worried, Madame Bridau; the diplomatic body often comes to the Porte-Saint-Martin, and that handsome girl won’t stay long with your son. I heard an ambassador was madly in love with her. By the bye, another piece of news! Old Claparon is dead, and his son, who has become a banker, has ordered the cheapest kind of funeral for him. That fellow has no education; they wouldn’t behave like that in China.”

Philippe, prompted by mercenary motives, proposed marriage to Mariette; but she, knowing herself on the eve of an engagement at the Grand Opera, refused, either because she guessed the colonel’s motive or because she prized independence for her fortune. For the remainder of the year Philippe came home no more than twice a month. Where was he? Either at his office, the theatre, or with Mariette. No light as to his conduct reached the household of the rue Mazarin. Giroudeau, Finot, Bixiou, Vernou, Lousteau saw him leading a life of pleasure. Philippe shared the gay amusements of Tullia, a leading singer at the Opera, of Florentine, who took Mariette’s place at the Porte-Saint-Martin, of Florine and Matifat, Coralie and Camusot. After four o’clock, when he left his office, until midnight, he amused himself; some party had usually been arranged the night before—a good dinner, a card-party, a supper by some one of the set. Philippe was in his element.

This carnival, which lasted eighteen months, was not altogether without troubles. The beautiful Mariette no sooner appeared at the Opera in January, 1821, than she captured one of the most distinguished dukes of Louis XVIII’s court. Philippe tried to make head against the peer, and by April, compelled by his passion and despite some luck at cards, he dipped into the funds he was cashier of. By May he had taken eleven hundred francs. In that fatal month Mariette started for London to see what could be done with the lords while the temporary opera house in the Hotel Choiseul, rue Lepelletier, was being prepared.
