Major Mignonnet, lean and dignified, busied himself with steam-engine problems and lived modestly with Monsieur and Madame Carpentier. His gentle manners and scientific pursuits won town respect; he and Carpentier were often said to be “quite another thing” from Potel, Renard, Max, and the cafe Militaire habitués, who retained soldierly manners and the Empire’s defective morals.

When Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun, Max was excluded from society. He showed self-respect by not appearing at the club or complaining, though he was the town’s handsomest, best-dressed man, spent much money, and kept a horse—a rarity at Issoudun as Byron’s horse in Venice. We shall see how Max, poor and apparently without means, became the town’s dandy. His disgrace, earning bourgeois and religious contempt, connected with interests that brought Agathe and Joseph to Issoudun.

Judging by his audacious bearing and face, Max cared little for public opinion; he expected revenge and to lord it over those who condemned him. Although the bourgeoisie thought ill of him, the common people admired his courage, daring, and decision—his degradations were mostly unknown, even to the bourgeoisie. Max played a role like the blacksmith in the Fair Maid of Perth: champion of Bonapartism and Opposition, counted on in great occasions.

An incident illustrates his heroism. In 1819 a battalion commanded by royalist officers, fresh from the Maison Rouge, passed through Issoudun to Bourges. They lingered at the cafe Militaire—rendezvous of Bonapartists—where they asked for royalist papers. The fat landlady refused, offering the “Commerce” (the “Constitutionnel”). A captain tore it up, spat on it, and cried, “Bring dominos!” Word spread: “Let us tell Max!” Max entered the cafe with Major Potel and Captain Renard, followed by thirty young men. After a little comedy about the newspaper, Max, enraged, slapped the nearest officer and fought. They fought in the allée de Frapesle, three against three; Max killed his man. Major Potel wounded another who died in hospital; the third escaped. The battalion left that night. The affair spread through Berry and firmly established Max as a hero.

The Knights of Idleness, all young—the eldest not more than twenty-five—admired Max. Some envied his position. Under such a leader they accomplished great mischief. After May 1817, rarely a week passed without uproar. Max imposed conditions; statutes were drawn. These youths trained like pupils of Amoros—bold, agile, clever and strong as criminals. They practised roof-climbing, scaling houses, noiseless walking, mixing mortar, walling up doors, and collected ropes, ladders, tools, and disguises. They perfected invention and execution of pranks, achieving a Panurge-like genius for evil that provoked laughter while covering victims with ridicule and silencing complaints. Being sons of good families, they had household information facilitating outrages.

Sometimes they lay in ambush along the Grand’rue or the Basse rue, towns’ arteries. Crouched in wall angles at the hour households slept, they called along the town in tones of terror: “What’s the matter?” “What is it?” repeated cries woke citizens who hurried out in shirts and caps with lights, asking questions and showing queer faces.

A poor old bookbinder who believed in hobgoblins worked in a small basement shop. The Knights, disguised as devils, invaded at night, put him in his cutting-press, and left him shrieking as if among hell's souls. He roused the neighbors with tales of Lucifer and was nearly driven insane.

In a severe winter the Knights took down the collector of taxes’ chimney and rebuilt it overnight so noiselessly that they left no trace, but arranged the flue to send all smoke into the house. The collector suffered two months before discovering the cause and had to rebuild a new chimney.

Another time they put three trusses of hay dusted with brimstone and oiled paper down the chimney of a pious old woman friend of Madame Hochon. In the morning, lighting her fire, she thought she had started a volcano. Fire-engines came; the population rushed to help; Knights among the firemen deluged her house, frightening her as much with water as with the “fire.” She grew ill with fear.

To keep someone on guard and in terror, they wrote anonymous letters warning of an impending robbery, then stole softly around his house or under his windows, whistling as if to call each other.

A famous performance was sending letters to all heirs of a miserly old woman likely to leave large property, announcing her death and requesting they be present when the seals were affixed. Eighty persons arrived from neighboring towns in deep mourning—widows, sons, children, some in carrioles or carts. Imagine the scene between servants and arrivals and the notaries’ consultations; it created a sort of riot.

At last the sub-prefect waked to the intolerable state, made worse by the difficulty of discovering perpetrators. Suspicion fell on several youths; but the National Guard was a mere name, there was no garrison, and the lieutenant of police had only eight gendarmes—no patrols—so proof was impossible. The sub-prefect was made an object of mockery. He breakfasted on two fresh eggs, kept chickens, boiled eggs himself with watch in hand, boasting his egg-boiling skill. For two years he had boiled eggs successfully, earning many witticisms. Then, for a month, each night his eggs were taken and replaced by hard-boiled ones, ruining his reputation as the “sous-préfet à l’oeuf.” He lost his egg-boiling laurels and never suspected the Knights, who had planned instead. Max greased the sub-prefect’s stoves nightly with fetid oil, making the house unbearable. His wife, going to mass one morning, found her shawl glued inside by a tenacious substance and had to go without it. The sub-prefect finally requested transfer. His cowardly submissiveness helped establish the Knights’ weird and comic authority.

Beyond the rue des Minimes and the place Misere lay a quarter enclosed between an arm of the “Riviere forcee” and the ramparts, from the place d’Armes to the pottery market. This square was filled with poor houses crowded together, divided by streets so narrow two abreast could not pass. This quarter, a sort of cour des Miracles, housed poor people and lowly trades—hovels and “blind houses.” It has been, from earliest ages, an accursed quarter, haunt of evil-doers; one thoroughfare is called “the street of the Executioner.” For five centuries it’s been customary for the executioner to have a red door. The executioner of Chateauroux’s assistant is said to live there, seen rarely; vine-dressers alone keep intercourse with him, for he inherits the craft of curing wounds and fractures. In Issoudun’s heyday women wandered this quarter. It was home to second-hand sellers and old-clothes dealers whose wares infected the air; in short, the rendezvous of that apocryphal population found in such city parts, where two or three Jews sometimes gain ascendancy.

At a corner in the livelier half of the quarter existed, from 1815 to 1823 and perhaps later, a public-house kept by a woman called Mère Cognette. The house was well built of white stone with ashlar and cement infill, one storey with an attic; over the door hung a huge pine branch like a Florentine bronze. A blue poster showed, above the words “Good Beer of Mars,” a soldier pouring foam in an arched jet toward a very décolletée woman—an image to stir the imagination.

The ground floor was an immense hall serving as kitchen and dining-room, from whose beams provisions hung on huge nails. A winding staircase led to the upper storey; at the foot a door opened into a low, long room lit from a narrow, sunken provincial court like a chimney flue. Hidden by a shed and walls, this low room was where the Bad Boys held their plenary court. Ostensibly Pere Cognet boarded country folk on market-days; secretly he was landlord to the Knights. Pere Cognet, formerly a groom in a rich household, had married La Cognette, a cook in a good family. In the Rome suburb the town follows the Latin custom of placing a feminine termination on the husband’s name and giving it to the wife.

By uniting savings Pere Cognet and his spouse bought their house. La Cognette, about forty, tall and plump, with a Roxelane nose, swarthy skin, jet-black hair, lively brown eyes and a mirthful intelligence, was chosen by Max as the Order’s Leonarde for her character and cookery. Pere Cognet, about fifty-six, thick-set and under his wife’s rule—“he only sees things with a good eye,” she joked, being blind of the other—kept absolute silence about nightly gatherings. From 1816 to 1823 neither revealed what went on. Their devotion was absolute, but self-interest secured their affection and silence. Whenever the Knights knocked a certain way Pere Cognet would get up, light the fire and candles, open the door, and fetch a particular wine laid in for the Order; La Cognette prepared an excellent supper, eaten before or after expeditions planned the previous evening or day.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
CHAPTER VIII

While Joseph and Madame Bridau were journeying from Orléans to Issoudun, the Knights of Idleness played one of their best tricks. An old Spaniard, a former prisoner of war who stayed near Issoudun and did a small grain business, came early one morning to market and left his empty cart at the foot of the tower. Maxence, who had arrived for a rendezvous of the Knights at that spot, was soon asked in a whisper, “What are we to do to-night?”

“Here’s Père Fario’s cart,” he answered. “I nearly cracked my shins over it. Let us get it up on the embankment of the tower first, and we’ll decide afterwards.”

When Richard Cœur-de-Lion built the tower of Issoudun he raised it on the ruins of a basilica, itself above a Roman temple and a Celtic dun; these ruins form a mound of three ages. The tower stands on the apex of a cone with steep descents and is only approached by steps. To convey its height, compare it to the obelisk of Luxor on its pedestal: the tower’s pedestal toward the town was eighty feet high. In an hour the cart was taken off its wheels and hoisted, piece by piece, to the top of the embankment at the foot of the tower—a task like soldiers carrying artillery over a mountain pass. The cart was remounted on its wheels, and the Knights, hungry and thirsty by then, returned to Mère Cognette’s to laugh at the grimaces Fario would make when he came after his barrow in the morning.

The Knights did not play such capers nightly; three clever fellows could not invent three hundred and sixty-five pieces of mischief a year. Circumstances weren’t always propitious: sometimes the moon shone clear, or a prank had angered their betters; then one or another would refuse to join because a relation was involved. But if they were not at Mère Cognette’s every night, they met during the day for hunting, the autumn vintages, and winter skating. Among these twenty youths, some were nearer to Max and made him their idol. The two grandsons of Madame Hochon—François Hochon and Baruch Borniche—were his henchmen. Accepting family gossip about Lousteau’s left-handed parentage, they regarded Max as their cousin. Max lent them money, took them hunting, and exercised more influence over them than their grandfather did. Both were orphans kept under Monsieur Hochon’s guardianship for reasons to be explained later.

At that moment François and Baruch sat, one on each side of Max, at the middle of a table lit by four tallow candles. A dozen to fifteen bottles had just been drunk, for only eleven Knights were present. Baruch—his name showing that Calvinism still had hold in Issoudun—said to Max as the wine loosened tongues, “You are threatened in your stronghold.”

“What do you mean by that?” asked Max.

“My grandmother has had a letter from Madame Bridau, her goddaughter, saying she and her son are coming here. My grandmother’s getting two rooms ready.”

“What’s that to me?” Max asked, gulping his glass with a comic gesture.

Max was then thirty-four. A candle lit his soldierly face, brow, clear skin, ardent eyes, and black hair that bristled in those five black tongues called the “five points.” Despite contrasts, his face was sweet, with an outline like Raphael’s Madonnas and a well-cut mouth. When he laughed he showed thirty-two good teeth. About five feet six, he was well proportioned—neither too stout nor too thin. His hands were white and rather handsome, though his feet recalled the foot-soldier of the Empire. Max would have made a good general of division: broad shoulders and chest, movements betraying intelligence; born with grace and charm, the noble blood of his real father showed in him.

“Don’t you know, Max,” cried the son of a former surgeon-major named Goddet—now the best doctor in town—“that Madame Hochon’s goddaughter is the sister of Rouget? If she’s coming with her son, she might secure the property when he dies, and then—good-by to your harvest!”

Max frowned, watched the effect of the news around the table, and again asked, “What’s that to me?”

“But,” said François, “I should think that if old Rouget revoked his will—in case he made one in favor of the Rabouilleuse—”

Max cut him short. “I’ve stopped the mouths of people who dared meddle with you, my dear François,” he said; “and this is how you pay your debts? You use a contemptuous nickname for a woman to whom I am known to be attached.”

Max had never before spoken so of his relation with Mademoiselle Flore Brazier, the servant-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget, called the “Rabouilleuse” by respectable Madame Hochon. Everyone knew it was too ticklish a subject with Max to broach; no one did, unless Max began it, and he never had. To risk his anger was too dangerous, so even his best friends had not joked about Flore. When they spoke of his liaison before Major Potel and Captain Renard, Potel would say, “If he is the natural brother of Jean-Jacques Rouget where else would you have him live?” Captain Renard added, “The girl is worthless, and if Max lives with her where’s the harm?” After this snub François could not at once catch up his thread; he was less able when Max said gently, “Go on.”

“Faith, no!” cried François.

“You needn’t get angry, Max,” said young Goddet; “didn’t we agree to talk freely at Mère Cognette’s? Outside, we’d be mortal enemies if we remembered what’s said here. All the town calls Flore Brazier the Rabouilleuse; and if François let the nickname slip, is that a crime against the Order of Idleness?”

“No,” said Max, “but against our personal friendship. However, I thought better of it; I recollected we were in session, and that’s why I said ‘Go on.’”

A deep silence followed, so embarrassing that Max broke it. “I’ll go on for him—for all of you—and tell you what you are thinking. You think that Flore, the Rabouilleuse, La Brazier, the housekeeper of Père Rouget—you think that woman supplies all my wants since I returned to Issoudun. If I am able to throw three hundred francs a month to the dogs, treat you to suppers—as tonight—and lend money to all of you, you think I get the gold out of Mademoiselle Flore Brazier’s purse? Well, yes. Yes, Mademoiselle Brazier is aiming straight for the old man’s property.”

“She gets it from father to son,” observed Goddet.

“You think I intend to marry Flore when Père Rouget dies, and so this sister and her son, of whom I hear to-night for the first time, will endanger my future?”

“That’s just it,” cried François.

“That is what everyone thinks,” said Baruch.

“Don’t be uneasy, friends,” answered Max. “Forewarned is forearmed! Now then, I address the Knights of Idleness. If, to get rid of these Parisians I need the help of the Order, will you lend me a hand? Oh! within the limits we have marked out for our fooleries,” he added, seeing hesitation. “Do you suppose I want to kill them—poison them? Thank God I’m not an idiot. Besides, if the Bridaus succeed, and Flore has nothing but what she stands in, I should be satisfied; do you understand that? I love her enough to prefer her to Mademoiselle Fichet—if Mademoiselle Fichet would have me.”

Mademoiselle Fichet was the richest heiress in Issoudun, and her hand figured in the rumor of the younger Goddet’s passion for the mother. Frankness being prized, all the Knights rose as one: “You are a fine fellow, Max!” “We’ll stand by you!” “A fig for the Bridaus!” “Hurrah for Max!” and so forth. Thus eleven responses, acclamations, and toasts reflected their relaxed morality. It is easy to see Max’s reason for becoming their grand master: by leading the youths of the best families and doing them services, he created a support for himself to recover position later. He rose gracefully, waved his glass, and addressed them:

“As a mark of the ill-will I bear you, I wish you all a mistress equal to the beautiful Flore! As to this irruption of relations, I don’t feel present uneasiness; and as to the future, we’ll see what comes—”

“Don’t forget Fario’s cart!”

“Hang it! that’s safe enough!” said Goddet.

“Oh! I’ll engage to settle that business,” cried Max. “Be in the market-place early, all of you, and let me know when the old fellow goes for his cart.”

At half-past three in the morning the Knights slipped out silently to their homes, gliding close to the walls in list shoes. Max slowly returned to the place Saint-Jean, between the port Saint-Jean and port Vilatte—the quarter of the rich bourgeoisie. He had concealed his fears, but the news had struck home. His experience on the hulks at Cabrera had taught him a deep dissimulation. Above all, the forty thousand francs a year from Rouget’s landed property was the constituent element of Max’s passion for Flore Brazier. By his bearing one saw how much confidence she had given him in the financial future she expected via the old bachelor. Still, the arrival of legitimate heirs shook Max’s faith in Flore’s influence. Rouget’s savings, accumulated over seventeen years, still stood in his own name; even if the will Flore declared had been made in her favor were revoked, these savings could at least be secured by putting them in the name of Mademoiselle Brazier.

“That fool of a girl never told me, in all these seven years, a word about the sister and nephews!” cried Max, turning from the rue de la Marmouse into the rue l’Avenier. “Seven hundred and fifty thousand francs placed with different notaries at Bourges, Vierzon, and Châteauroux can’t be turned into money and put into the Funds in a week without everybody knowing in this gossiping place! The most important thing is to get rid of these relations; as soon as they are driven away we must hurry to secure the property. I must think it over.”

Tired, Max let himself into Père Rouget’s house with a pass-key and went to bed without noise, saying, “To-morrow my thoughts will be clear.”

Now it is necessary to relate why the sultana of the place Saint-Jean picked up the nickname “Rabouilleuse,” and how she became quasi-mistress of Jean-Jacques Rouget’s home.

As old Doctor Rouget, father of Jean-Jacques and Madame Bridau, advanced in years he perceived his son’s nonentity and harshly treated him, preparing him to submit to the yoke of the first tyranny. Coming home one day from his rounds, the malignant and vicious old man encountered a bewitching little girl at the edge of some fields along the avenue de Tivoli. Hearing the horse, the child sprang up from the brook, half naked, wearing a forlorn petticoat of coarse woollen strips, a rough paper tied as a hat, and tattered fichu and garments; yet beneath these rags was the loveliest head and charming figure. One companion wading in the water was a man who raised his head and said, “What are you about, Flore? While you are talking instead of catching, the creatures will get away.”

“Why have you come here from Vatan?” said the doctor.

“I am catching crabs for my uncle Brazier here.”

“Rabouiller” is a Berrichon word describing troubling the water to bring crabs to the surface; Flore Brazier held her rabouilloir with childlike grace.

“Has your uncle got permission to hunt crabs?”

“Hey! aren’t we all under a Republic that is one and indivisible?” cried the uncle.

“We are under a Directory,” said the doctor, “and I know of no law that allows a man to come from Vatan and fish in the territory of Issoudun. Have you got a mother, little one?”

“No, monsieur; my father’s in the asylum at Bourges. He went mad from a sunstroke in the fields.”

“How much do you earn?”

“Five sous a day while the season lasts; I catch them as far as the Braisne. In harvest I glean; in winter I spin.”

“You are about twelve years old?”

“Yes, monsieur.”

“Do you want to come with me? You shall be well fed and well dressed, and have some pretty shoes.”

“No, my niece will stay with me; I am responsible to God and man for her,” said Uncle Brazier, who had come up. He wore a peasant’s hat rotted by sun and rain, his face dark and sunken; his jacket patchwork, his trousers crash towelling.

“I am Doctor Rouget,” said that man; “as you are the guardian, bring her to my house in the place Saint-Jean. It will not be a bad day’s work for you nor for her.”

The doctor spurred his horse and returned to Issoudun. He had hardly sat down to dinner before his cook announced the citoyen and citoyenne Brazier.

“Sit down,” said the doctor to uncle and niece. Flore and her guardian, still barefoot, looked round the doctor’s dining-room with wonder; never had they seen such a place.

The house Rouget inherited from the Descoings family stands in the middle of place Saint-Jean, a long narrow square planted with a few sickly lindens. The houses here are better built than elsewhere; the Descoings house has three windows on the first storey and a porte-cochère leading to a courtyard and garden. Under the archway is a large hall lighted by two street windows, beyond which lies a kitchen and stair to the upper floor and attic. There is a wood-shed, wash-house, stable for two horses and a coach-house with lofts where the doctor’s servant slept.

The hall which the little peasant and her uncle admired is decorated with wooden carvings of Louis XV’s time and a handsome marble chimney-piece, over which Flore saw herself in a large gilded mirror. On the panelled walls hung pictures rescued from religious houses—works by great masters and copies prized for their frames and glass. The furniture showed luxury then little valued in Issoudun: a Boulle clock between silver candlesticks, carved oak armchairs covered with tapestry, and a rich console. But Rouget, his son, and the cook took little care: they spat upon a delicate hearth, the chandelier and ceiling bore black speckles from flies; brocatelle curtains were torn; a chest worth many thousand francs served as a sideboard.

“Here, Fanchette,” cried Rouget to his cook, “bring two glasses; give us some of the old wine.”

Fanchette, a big Berrichon countrywoman considered a better cook than even Mère Cognette, ran in with alacrity showing Rouget’s despotism and her own curiosity.

“What is an acre of vineyard worth in your parts?” asked the doctor, pouring a glass for Brazier.

“Three hundred francs in silver.”

“Well, then! leave your niece here as my servant; she shall have three hundred francs in wages, and, as you are her guardian, you can take them.”

“Every year?” exclaimed Brazier, eyes wide.

“I leave that to your conscience,” said the doctor. “She is an orphan; up to eighteen she has no right to what she earns.”

“Twelve to eighteen—that’s six acres of vineyard!” said the uncle. “Ay, she’s a pretty one, gentle as a lamb, well made and active, and obedient as a kitten. She were the light o’ my poor brother’s eyes.”

“I will pay a year in advance,” observed the doctor.

“Bless me! say two years, and I’ll leave her with you; she’ll be better off with you than with us; my wife beats her; there’s none but I to stand up for her, and the little saint of a creature is as innocent as a new-born babe.”

When he heard “innocent,” the doctor, struck, made a sign to the uncle and took him into the courtyard and garden, leaving the Rabouilleuse at the table with Fanchette and Jean-Jacques, who questioned her and to whom she naively related her meeting with the doctor.

“There now, my little darling, good-by,” said Uncle Brazier, kissing Flore; “you can say I’ve made your happiness leaving you with this kind and worthy father of the poor; you must obey him as you would me. Be a good girl and behave nicely and do everything he tells you.”

“Get the room over mine ready,” said the doctor to Fanchette. “Little Flore—I am sure she’s worthy of the name—will sleep there. To-morrow we’ll send for a shoemaker and dressmaker. Put another plate on the table; she shall keep us company.”

That evening all Issoudun talked of the sudden appearance of the little rabouilleuse in Doctor Rouget’s house; in that satirical region the nickname stuck to Mademoiselle Brazier before, during, and after her good fortune.

The doctor likely intended something for Flore similar in miniature to Louis XV’s conduct with Mademoiselle de Romans, but he was too late and too old. From twelve to fourteen the charming Rabouilleuse lived a life of unmixed happiness. Well dressed and often finer than Issoudun’s richest girls, she sported a gold watch and jewels given by the doctor to encourage studies, and had a master to teach her to read, write, and cipher. But the peasant life had instilled in Flore a deep repugnance to the bitter cup of knowledge, so the doctor stopped her education at that point. His intentions toward the child, whom he cleansed, clothed, and taught, were interpreted variously by the town’s gossip, which often produced fatal blunders in such communities.

“What do you suppose that old gorilla wants at his age with a little girl only fifteen years old?” society still said two years after Flore’s arrival.

“Ah! that’s true,” they answered. “His days of merry-making are long past.”

“My dear fellow, the doctor is disgusted at his son’s stupidity, and he persists in hating his daughter Agathe; he may intend to marry little Flore; suppose she gave him a fine, active boy like Max?” said one wise head.

“Bah! don’t talk nonsense! After such a life as Rouget and Lousteau led from 1770 to 1787, is it likely either would have children at sixty-five? The old villain has read the Scriptures and does as David did in his old age; that’s all.”

“They say Brazier, when drunk, boasts in Vatan that he cheated him,” cried one who always believed worst of people.

“Good heavens! neighbor; what won’t they say at Issoudun?”

From 1800 to 1805 the doctor enjoyed the pleasures of educating Flore without the annoyances Mademoiselle de Romans inflicts on Louis. The Rabouilleuse was envied by peasant girls within ten miles, although her conduct might be religiously reprehensible. Flore, born in 1787, grew up amid the saturnalias of 1793 and 1798, whose effects reached these country regions then deprived of priests and faith; marriage was little more than legal coupling, and revolutionary maxims left a deep impression. In 1802 Catholic worship was scarcely re-established; the Emperor found it difficult to obtain priests. In 1806 many parishes were still without clergy, so slowly had the dispersed clergy been gathered after the Revolution.

In 1802 therefore nothing was likely to reproach Flore Brazier unless it were conscience; and conscience was sure to be weaker than self-interest in Uncle Brazier’s ward. If, as everyone supposed, the cynical doctor was compelled by age to respect a child of fifteen, the Rabouilleuse was still considered very “wide awake,” a term much used locally. Some thought she could claim a certificate of innocence from the cessation of the doctor’s care in his last two years, during which he showed her less than coldness.

Old Rouget had killed too many people not to know when his end was near; and his notary, finding him on his death-bed, pressed him to make provision in favor of the young girl, then seventeen.

“So I do,” he said cynically; “my death sets her at liberty.”

This phrase revealed the nature of the old man. Cloaking his evil deeds with witty sayings, he obtained indulgence in a land that applauds wit—especially when tied to self-interest. In those words the notary read concentrated hatred of a man whose calculations had been balked by Nature, revenging himself upon the innocent object of impotent love. The doctor’s obstinate resolution to leave nothing to the Rabouilleuse—saying with a bitter smile when urged again, “Her beauty will make her rich enough!”—confirmed this opinion.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Jean-Jacques Rouget did not mourn his father, though Flore Brazier did. The old doctor had made his son extremely unhappy since Jean-Jacques came of age in 1791; but he had provided the peasant girl with the material comforts country folk prize. After the funeral, when Fanchette cleared the table and asked Flore what would become of her now that the doctor was dead, Jean-Jacques’s dull face suddenly showed feeling.

“Leave the room,” he said to Fanchette.

At seventeen Flore still held the delicacy and beauty that had attracted the doctor, though the embonpoint of a life without toil had begun to appear. Her bust and shoulders were developed; her face remained faultless. Jean-Jacques, eyes lighting for the first time, asked in a trembling voice if she felt at home.

“Yes, Monsieur Jean.”

He was about to declare himself but stopped, troubled by memories of the dead man and uncertain how far his father’s indiscretions had reached. Flore, unable to suspect his simplicity, waited; Jean-Jacques left her without finishing. It would be long before she understood him.

At thirty-seven, Jean-Jacques was still as timid and submissive to paternal discipline as a child of twelve. This timidity explains his whole life. When mental torpor and nervous timidity combine, a man can become a cipher; yet fine qualities sometimes hide behind such infirmity. In Jean-Jacques the sense of love outweighed his mental qualities; passion, stripped of romantic idealism, only increased his fear. He had never courted any woman in Issoudun: small stature, awkwardness, a vulgar flattened face, pallid skin, and large pale-green eyes made him seem old. A woman’s presence stultified him; desire petrified his speech. Paralyzed between passion and lack of ideas, he sought solitude.

The doctor, too late, saw the havoc of this temperament. He had wanted to get his son married but hesitated because a wife would manage the property; to avoid surrendering control he tried to instill avarice in Jean-Jacques, training him in mechanical habits and fixed ideas about investing revenues. He kept estates in order and long-leased to spare him management. But one thing escaped notice: timidity hid Jean-Jacques’s passionate love for the Rabouilleuse. Flore was the only woman in the bachelor’s presence, the light of his paternal home; he secretly watched her and rejoiced in his father’s education of her as making her easier to win. Passion that can reflect gives even ninnies a kind of intelligence.

The day after the funeral Flore, expecting a declaration, waited. Jean-Jacques lingered near her, glancing with passion but unable to speak. At last, during dessert, he awkwardly asked if she liked life there and whether she was honest. Flore, astonished and touched, answered readily. He asked if she was still the barefoot girl her uncle had brought; she blushed. Encouraged, Rouget stammered and could only say, “Are you the same?” She answered on her honor; he took her hand and, unable to say more, declared she could stay, hinting she should be mistress of the house.

This strange courtship lasted three weeks. One night Flore found Jean-Jacques crouched like a dog on the landing; she thought, “He loves me,” and, indulgent, did not mind. At another dessert Flore told him the truth about his father: the old man had been a joker and sometimes spiteful, but might not have meant harm to Jean-Jacques. Rouget, taking her hand, said, “If your father was nothing to you you can stay here with me, can’t you?” She admitted she would if he wished it. He confessed he had always loved her since she came barefoot. Flore made no direct answer at first; when silence became embarrassing, Jean-Jacques argued, “Wouldn’t it be better than returning to the fields?” She assented.

Yet he got no further. Men of his nature crave certainty; the effort to avow love costs too much and they quail. Ten months after his father’s death Jean-Jacques changed: his leaden face cleared and he breathed happiness. Flore insisted he take better care of himself and watched him go out. The town noticed the transformation.

“Have you heard?” people said. “Jean-Jacques inherits everything, even the Rabouilleuse. She’s sly! She’s very handsome and will make him marry her.” Some thought he ought to marry a woman of family; others praised Flore for devotion to the poor fellow. Thus Flore obtained management of the Rouget household—from father to son, as young Goddet had said. It is necessary to sketch that management for the edification of old bachelors.

Fanchette, the cook, alone protested that Flore should be queen over Jean-Jacques. She owned three hundred francs a year in the Funds and an annuity from the doctor; she could live without working and quit the house nine months after the funeral, on April 15, 1806. That date may indicate when Flore ceased to be an honest girl.

Foreseeing Fanchette’s leaving, Flore resolved to do without a servant. For six months she had studied Fanchette’s culinary operations. In choice living doctors rival bishops; the doctor, trained under Rouelle in Paris, had applied chemistry to cooking and improved provincial techniques. He taught subtleties—how to treat omelettes, use a “cagnard,” and prevent browned butter’s tartness—secrets honed in his kitchen.

Flore, a born fryer and roaster, soon surpassed Fanchette and became a cordon-bleu attentive to Jean-Jacques’s comfort. She rubbed up furniture, kept everything clean, managed the wash and mended linen with thrift. She learned family business, consulted the notary Monsieur Heron, and advised Jean-Jacques well. Two months before the doctor’s death Flore’s uncle Brazier had died after a fall, leaving a wine-shop where he had spent most of his time. Having also lost her father, she served her master with an orphan’s gratitude.

This period was paradise for poor Jean-Jacques. He acquired gentle habits of an animal, trained into monastic regularity. Flore woke him with breakfast ready; after a morning walk he read papers at three, spent much time at dinner, then went to bed early to save fire and candles—provincial economies that contribute to empty-mindedness.

For nine years their life was regular. Jean-Jacques lent at five percent on first mortgages, with release of the wife’s rights if married, never more than a third of a property’s value, and required notes for an extra two and a half percent—his father’s rules. Usury, that clog on peasant ambition, destroyed rural regions; Rouget’s seven and a half percent levy seemed reasonable and gave him investment choice; notaries reaped commissions.

During these nine years Flore obtained absolute control. At first she treated him familiarly; then, surpassing him in mind and force, she became in fact his superior and he the servant of his servant. He met this mastery halfway by letting her care for him as a son clings to a mother’s protection. Flore kept the house and managed all business; Jean-Jacques left everything to her so completely that life without her seemed impossible. She indulged his fancies and understood him thoroughly. Her brightness was his chief pleasure; when it clouded his state betrayed her power, which she sometimes exercised. Secure in her power, she did not think to make him marry her.

Towards the close of 1815 Flore, then twenty-seven, had reached full beauty: plump, fresh, white-skinned, the ideal “buxom housewife,” likened to a handsome barmaid with a higher type. In 1816 she saw Maxence Gilet and fell in love at first sight. Maxence, a penniless officer, saw more than a passing fancy in Flore once he learned of her and Jean-Jacques’s positions. He gladly took up abode at the Rougets’, recognizing the old bachelor’s weakness. Flore’s passion affected household affairs: she became sullen and gave Jean-Jacques flashes of temper like a wife suspecting infidelity. He asked why; her eyes were full of hatred and her voice contemptuous.

“You have neither heart nor soul!” she cried. “Sixteen years I have spent my youth in this house, and I have only just found out you have a stone there” (striking her breast). “For two months you have seen that brave captain, a victim of the Bourbons, cut out for a general and down in poverty, hunted into a hole where there’s no way to make a penny! He’s forced to sit on a stool all day in the mayor’s office to earn—what? Six hundred miserable francs—and here are you, with six hundred and fifty-nine thousand well invested, and sixty thousand francs’ income—thanks to me, who never spend more than three thousand a year—and you never think of offering him a home here, though the second floor is empty! You’d rather rats and mice run riot than put a human being there—and he’s a lad your father always allowed to be his own son! Do you want to know what you are? A fratricide! And I know why. You see I take an interest in him, and that provokes you. Well, yes! I do take an interest in him.”

Jean-Jacques stammered and could only plead ignorance of any such wish. She answered bitterly that there ought to be a man in the house for safety and reputation, and that Max could swallow three burglars as a flash. People might gossip; the proper story would be that his father commanded care of the poor Max. For nine years she had eaten his bread and accepted many offers of court; she had always replied she would not abandon the “poor innocent.” Jean-Jacques, terrified at the thought of losing her, promised to invite Maxence.

He did so politely, before witnesses. A reconciliation took place; but Jean-Jacques noticed signs that Flore’s affections had wholly shifted. For weeks she complained to tradespeople about Rouget’s alleged tyranny in inviting his natural brother. No one was deceived: they saw Flore as clever and artful. Old Rouget, however, found himself comfortable after Max became master of his house; he gained a companion who paid him attention without servility. Gilet talked, discussed politics, and sometimes walked with Rouget. After Max’s arrival Flore stopped cooking, saying it spoiled her hands. Mere Cognette, grand master of idleness, produced a relative, an old maid who had lost a curate master and promised devotion: an excellent cook whose fidelity would be rewarded with an annuity of three hundred francs after ten years. This Vedie, pitted by smallpox and ugly, took over the kitchen.

Flore now adopted the title Madame Brazier. She wore corsets, silk or fine woollen and cotton dresses, expensive neckerchiefs, embroidered caps, collars, lace ruffles and boots. She renewed her youth through richness and elegance, trimming a rough diamond into worth. She wished to honor Max. In 1817 she brought a horse, called English, from Bourges for the weary cavalry captain. Max had found in Issoudun an old lancer of the Imperial Guard, a Pole named Kouski, who, now poor, asked to quarter himself at Rouget’s as the captain’s servant. Max idolized Kouski, especially after the duel with three royalists. Thus from 1817 the household numbered five persons, three of whom were masters, and expenses rose to about eight thousand francs a year.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At the time Madame Bridau returned to Issoudun to claim—Maitre Desroches said—an endangered inheritance, Jean-Jacques Rouget had become almost semi‑vegetative. After Max’s instalment, Flore put the household on an almost episcopal footing. Rouget, exposed to better living and tempted by Vedie’s good cooking, did not grow fat but visibly weakened; digestion and chronic cough left him pale, with dark rings about his eyes. When neighbors asked, he always replied he had never been better. Thought mentally deficient, people did not notice his failing faculties. His love for Flore alone kept him alive; he existed for her, obeying her slightest look and watching her like a dog. As Madame Hochon remarked, at fifty‑seven he seemed older than her husband, who was an octogenarian.

Everyone supposed—and rightly—that Max’s apartment matched his charm. In six years the captain had improved the comfort and adorned it for himself and Flore. It was Issoudun luxury—colored tiles, somewhat elegant wallpapers, mahogany furniture, gilt mirrors, muslin curtains with red borders, a canopy bed, provincial draperies—comfort common in Paris but notable here. One unusual detail caused talk: rush‑matting on the stairs to muffle footsteps. Max came in at daybreak without waking anyone; Rouget did not suspect his guest was an accomplice in the Knights of Idleness’ nocturnal performances.

About eight the next morning Flore, in a cotton dressing‑gown with pink stripes, a lace cap, and furred slippers, softly opened Max’s door; seeing him asleep she stood beside the bed. He woke at once like a well‑trained soldier.

“Half‑past three,” she thought. “He must have a good constitution. I wonder what they did last night.”

“You are sleepy; I’ll go away,” she said.

“No, stay; there’s something serious going on.”

“Were you up to mischief last night?”

“Ah, bah! It concerns you and me and that old fool. You never told me he had a family! Well, his family are coming—coming here—no doubt to turn us out.”

“Ah! I’ll shake him well,” said Flore.

“Mademoiselle Brazier,” said Max gravely, “things are too serious for giddiness. Send me my coffee; I’ll take it in bed and think over what we had better do. Come back at nine and we’ll talk. Meanwhile, act as if you’ve heard nothing.”

Frightened, Flore made his coffee; a quarter‑hour later Baruch burst in, crying to the grand master, “Fario is hunting for his barrow!” In five minutes Max was dressed and in the street; though he sauntered, he soon reached the tower embankment, where a crowd had gathered.

Fario was a withered, somewhat noble‑looking Spaniard, dry and phlegmatic, with piercing eyes. He swore his cart had been left at the foot of the embankment and now was missing; the horse stood harnessed thirty feet away. Max gravely went to inspect, the crowd following. Francois and Baruch kept the lower crowd back so there would be no one below when they reached the top.

When they climbed, they found no trace of the cart’s passage up the steep embankment; at the top it seemed impossible the barrow could have got there. Fario imagined witchcraft and lost his head. Max, taking advantage of Fario’s stupefaction, raised the cart by the shafts and flung it down, calling, “Look out there, below!” The crowd, persuaded by Francois, had retired; the cart dashed to pieces in a picturesque manner.

Fario raged, accusing someone of bringing it up, and then demanding recompense. Max, cooling him with a jest, said he would give him a magnificent cart for two hundred and fifty francs; Fario, mollified, accepted the bargain tone though he muttered threats of vengeance. Max, however, was annoyed by Fario’s hint that he would bring the cart up himself and by the Spaniard’s vindictive words. For five days Fario’s barrow was the talk of Issoudun; the story spread to neighboring departments. Because of Fario’s speech, Max and the Rabouilleuse were whispered about in Issoudun and openly discussed in Bourges, Vatan, Vierzon, and Chateauroux. Maxence Gilet knew how poisonous such comments could be.

“We can’t stop their tongues,” he said. “Ah! I did a foolish thing!”

“Max!” said Francois, taking his arm. “They are coming to‑night.”

“They! Who?”

“The Bridaus. My grandmother has had a letter from her goddaughter.”

“Listen, my boy,” said Max low. “I have been thinking deeply. Neither Flore nor I ought to seem opposed to the Bridaus. If these heirs are to be got rid of, it is for you Hochons to drive them out. Find out what sort of people they are. Tomorrow at Mere Cognette’s, after I’ve taken their measure, we can decide what to do, and how to set your grandfather against them.”

“The Spaniard found the flaw in Max’s armor,” Baruch told Francois as they walked to Monsieur Hochon’s and watched Max enter his own door.

While Max plotted, Flore, despite counsel, could not restrain her fury and burst upon the poor bachelor. When Jean‑Jacques incurred her anger, his little attentions and vulgar fondlings—his daily delights—were suppressed. Flore sent him, as children say, into disgrace. No more tender pet names—“my kitten,” “my old darling”—were heard. A cold, sharp, ironic “you” cut through the heart of the miserable old bachelor; the “you” was a declaration of war. Instead of helping him dress or anticipating his wishes—“You look as fresh as a rose!”—she left him to dress alone. If he called she answered from the foot of the stairs, “I can’t do everything at once; how can I look after your breakfast and wait upon you? Are not you big enough to dress yourself?”

“Oh, dear! what have I done to displease her?” he asked after a rebuff seeking shaving‑water.

“Vedie, take up the hot water,” cried Flore.

“Vedie!” exclaimed the poor man, stupefied. “Vedie, what is the matter with Madame this morning?”

Flore Brazier required her master, Vedie, Kouski, and Max to call her Madame. Vedie, pretending concern, warned Rouget that he ought to treasure Madame and that he might spoil his own life if he made her unhappy. Vedie left him cast down; he dropped into an armchair and stared into vacancy like the melancholy imbecile he was, and forgot to shave. These alternations of indulgence and severity had upon this feeble creature, whose life depended on his amorous fibre, the same morbid effect as sudden climatic changes produce upon the body. It was a moral pleurisy wearing him out like disease. Flore alone could thus affect him; to her and her alone he was as good as foolish.

“Well, haven’t you shaved yet?” she said, appearing at his door.

Her sudden presence made him start; from pale and cast down he blushed yet dared not complain.

“Your breakfast is waiting,” she added. “You can come down as you are, in dressing‑gown and slippers; for you’ll breakfast alone, I can tell you.”

To breakfast alone was his worst punishment; he loved to talk to her as he ate. At the foot of the stairs he was seized with a coughing fit; emotion excited his catarrh.

“Cough away!” said Flore in the kitchen, without caring if he heard. “Confound the old wretch! he is able enough to get over it without bothering others. If he coughs up his soul, it will only be after—”

Such were the amenities the Rabouilleuse addressed to Rouget when angry. The poor man sat in deep distress at a corner of the table and looked at his old furniture and pictures with a disconsolate air.

“You might at least have put on a cravat,” said Flore. “Do you think it pleasant for people to see such a neck as yours, redder and more wrinkled than a turkey’s?”

“But what have I done?” he asked, lifting his big light‑green eyes, full of tears, to her and trying to meet her hard countenance.

“What have you done?” she exclaimed. “As if you didn’t know! Your sister Agathe—who is as much your sister as I am sister of the tower of Issoudun, if one’s to believe your father, and who has no claim at all upon you—is coming from Paris with her son, a miserable two‑penny painter, to see you.”

“My sister and my nephews coming to Issoudun!” he said, bewildered.

“Oh, yes! play surprised; try to make me believe you didn’t send for them! Don’t fash yourself; we won’t trouble your Parisians—before they set their feet in this house we shall have shaken the dust off ours. Max and I will be gone, never to return. As for your will, I’ll tear it in quarters under your nose and to your very beard—do you hear? Leave your property to your family if you don’t think we are your family; and then see if you’ll be loved for yourself by a lot of people who have not seen you for thirty years—who in fact have never seen you! Is that the sort of sister who can take my place? A pinchbeck saint!”

“If that’s all, my little Flore,” said the old man, “I won’t receive my sister, or my nephews. I swear this is the first I heard of their coming. It is all got up by Madame Hochon—a sanctimonious old—”

Max, who had overheard, entered suddenly and said in a masterful tone, “What’s all this?”

“My good Max,” said the old man, glad for the soldier’s protection who by agreement with Flore always took his side, “I swear by all that is most sacred, I now hear this news for the first time. I never wrote to my sister; my father made me promise not to leave her any property; to leave it to the Church sooner than to her. Well, I won’t receive my sister Agathe or her sons—”

“Your father was wrong, my dear Jean‑Jacques, and Madame Brazier is still more wrong,” answered Max. “Your father no doubt had his reasons, but he is dead, and his hatred should die with him. Your sister is your sister, and your nephews your nephews. You owe it to yourself to welcome them, and you owe it to us as well. What would people say in Issoudun? Thunder! I’ve enough on my shoulders, without hearing people say we shut you up and don’t allow you a will of your own, or that we influence you against your relations and are trying to get hold of your property. The devil take me if I don’t pull up stakes if calumny is flung at me! the other is bad enough! Let’s eat our breakfast.”

Flore, now mild as a weasel, helped Vedie set the table. Old Rouget, full of admiration for Max, took him by both hands and led him to a window recess, saying low, “Ah! Max, if I had a son, I couldn’t love him better than I love you. Flore is right: you two are my real family. You are a man of honor, Max, and what you have just said is true.”

“You ought to receive and entertain your sister and her son, but not change your arrangements about property,” Max said. “In that way you will do right in the world’s eyes and yet keep your promise to your father.”

“Well! my dear loves!” cried Flore gayly, “the salmi is getting cold. Come, my old rat, here’s a wing for you,” she said, smiling on Jean‑Jacques.

At the words the long‑drawn face lost its cadaverous tints, a smile flickered on his lips; but he had another fit of coughing; joy excited as violent an emotion as punishment. Flore rose, pulled a little cashmere shawl from her shoulders, and tied it round his throat, exclaiming: “How silly to put yourself in such a way about nothing. There, you old goose, that will do you good; it has been next my heart—”

“What a good creature!” said Rouget to Max, while Flore fetched a black velvet cap for the nearly bald head.

“As good as she is beautiful,” answered Max, “but she is quick‑tempered, like all people who carry their hearts in their hands.”

This scene, repeated in variations, typifies how women across social ranks act when interest lifts them from obedience to grasping power. Between Flore Brazier and a duchess, differences are those of education and surroundings; the pouting of a fine lady is the same as the violence of a Rabouilleuse. At all levels, bitter sayings, ironical jests, cold contempt, hypocritical complaints, and false quarrels are as effective as gross outbursts.

Max began to relate, with humor, the Fario and barrow episode, making the old man laugh. Vedie and Kouski in the kitchen exploded, and Flore laughed convulsively. After breakfast, while Jean‑Jacques read the newspapers (they subscribed to the Constitutionnel and the Pandore), Max carried Flore to his quarters.

“Are you sure he has not made any other will since the one leaving property to you?”

“He hasn’t anything to write with,” she answered.

“He might have dictated it to some notary,” said Max; “we must look out for that. Therefore be cordial to the Bridaus, and at the same time turn those mortgages into money. The notaries will be glad to make the transfers; it is grist to their mill. The Funds are rising; we shall conquer Spain, deliver Ferdinand VII and the Cortez, and they will be above par. You and I could make a good thing by putting the old fellow’s seven hundred and fifty thousand francs into the Funds at eighty‑nine. Only you must try to get it done in your name; it will be so much secured anyhow.”

“A capital idea!” said Flore.

“And as there will be an income of fifty thousand francs from eight hundred and ninety thousand, we must make him borrow one hundred and forty thousand francs for two years, to be paid back in two instalments. In two years, we shall get one hundred thousand francs in Paris, and ninety thousand here, and risk nothing.”

“If it were not for you, my handsome Max, what would become of me now?” she said.

“Oh! tomorrow night at Mere Cognette’s, after I have seen the Parisians, I shall find a way to make the Hochons themselves get rid of them.”

“Ah! what a head you’ve got, my angel! You are a love of a man.”

The place Saint‑Jean stands at the centre of a long street called at the upper end rue Grand Narette and at the lower rue Petite Narette. The Grand Narette rises rapidly from place Saint‑Jean to the port Vilatte. The house of old Monsieur Hochon stands exactly opposite Jean‑Jacques Rouget’s. From Madame Hochon’s usual sitting‑room it was easy to see what went on at the Rouget household and vice versa, when curtains were drawn back or doors left open. The Hochon house resembled the Rouget house; doubtless the same architect built both.

Monsieur Hochon, formerly tax collector at Selles and born at Issoudun, had returned home and married the sister of the sub‑delegate, the gay Lousteau, exchanging his office at Selles for one at Issoudun. Having retired before 1787, he escaped the Revolution’s dangers, to whose principles, however, he now adhered like other “honest men” who howl with the winners. Hochon had the reputation of a miser; a telling episode shows him: at his daughter’s wedding the cook asked for twine to truss a turkey; the old man gave an end of string already used to tie a parcel and called after the cook, “Gritte, mind you give it back to me!”

Year by year Hochon grew more petty and penurious; at eighty‑five he belonged to men who stop mid‑conversation to pick up a pin, remarking as they stick it in their coat sleeve, “There’s the wife’s stipend.” He complained of cloth quality and how his coat had lasted ten years. Tall, gaunt, thin, sallow; saying little, reading little, doing nothing to fatigue himself, he enforced at home strict abstemious discipline, weighing and measuring food and drink for his family: his wife née Lousteau, his grandson Borniche with a sister Adolphine, heirs of old Borniche, and his other grandson Francois Hochon.

Hochon’s eldest son, taken by the 1813 draft as a “guard of honor,” was killed at Hanau. He had married early a rich woman to avoid conscription; after enrolment he wasted his substance, foreseeing his end. His wife, who followed the army at a distance, died at Strasburg in 1814, leaving debts Hochon refused to pay—answering creditors with an axiom: “Women are minors.”

The Hochon house, though large, was scantily furnished; on the second floor two rooms suited Madame Bridau and Joseph. Old Hochon repented having kept them with two beds, each with an old armchair of natural wood covered with needlework, and a walnut table with a wide‑mouthed water‑pitcher in a basin. He kept winter stores of apples, pears, medlars, and quinces on heaps of straw in these rooms where rats and mice ran riot, exhaling a mixed odor of fruit and vermin. Madame Hochon directed everything be cleaned; she fastened wallpaper with wafered patches and decorated windows with curtains pieced from old hoards. Denied a strip of drugget, she laid her bedside carpet for little Agathe—“Poor little thing!” she said—who was now over forty‑seven. Madame Hochon borrowed two night‑tables from a neighbor and boldly hired two chests of drawers with brass handles from a second‑hand dealer next to Mere Cognette. She had preserved two candlesticks carved in choice woods by her father, who had the “turning” mania; from 1770 to 1780 it was fashionable for rich people to learn a trade, and Monsieur Lousteau had been a turner. These candlesticks were ornamented with circlets from roots of rose, peach, and apricot trees. Madame Hochon actually risked using her relics. These preparations and sacrifices increased old Hochon’s anxiety; until now he had not believed the Bridaus’ arrival.

The morning of the Fario trick Madame Hochon said after breakfast, “I hope, Hochon, you will receive my goddaughter, Madame Bridau, properly.” Then, making sure her grandchildren were out of hearing, she added, “I am mistress of my own property; don’t oblige me to make up to Agathe in my will for any incivility on your part.”

“Do you think, madame,” answered Hochon mildly, “that at my age I don’t know forms of decent civility?”

“You know very well what I mean, you crafty old thing! Be friendly to our guests, and remember I love Agathe.”

“And you love Maxence Gilet also, who is getting the property away from your dear Agathe! Ah! you’ve warmed a viper in your bosom; but after all, the Rouget money is bound to go to a Lousteau.”

Having said this about Max and Agathe, Hochon turned to leave; but Madame Hochon, still erect and spare, wearing a round cap with ribbon knots, powdered hair, a taffeta petticoat of changeable colors like a pigeon’s breast, tight sleeves, and high‑heeled slippers, placed her snuff‑box on a little table and said, “Really, Monsieur Hochon, how can a man of your sense repeat absurdities which cost my poor friend her peace and Agathe the property she ought to have had from her father. Max Gilet is not the son of my brother, whom I often advised to save the money he paid for him. You know as well as I do that Madame Rouget was virtue itself—”

“And the daughter takes after her; for she strikes me as uncommonly stupid. After losing all her fortune, she brings her sons up so well that here is one in prison and likely to be indicted before the Court of Peers for a conspiracy worthy of Berton. As for the other, he is worse—a painter. If your protégés stay here till they have extricated that fool Rouget from Gilet and the Rabouilleuse, we shall eat a good deal more than half a measure of salt with them.”

“That’s enough, Monsieur Hochon; you had better wish they may not have two strings to their bow.”

Monsieur Hochon took his hat and his cane with an ivory knob and went away petrified by that speech; he had no idea his wife could show such resolution. Madame Hochon took her prayer‑book to read the service; her age prevented daily church attendance; she got there with difficulty on Sundays. Since receiving her goddaughter’s letter she had added a petition to her prayers, supplicating God to open Jean‑Jacques Rouget’s eyes, bless Agathe, and prosper the expedition she had drawn her into. Concealing the fact from her grandchildren, whom she accused of being “parpaillots,” she had asked the curate to say a mass for Agathe’s success during a neuvaine held by her granddaughter Adolphine Borniche, who thus made her prayers in church by proxy.

Adolphine, then eighteen, who for seven years had sewn at her grandmother’s side in that cold household of monotony and method, undertook the neuvaine hoping to inspire some feeling in Joseph Bridau, in whom she took an interest because of the monstrosities her grandfather attributed to the young Parisian.

All the old and sensible people of the town and fathers of families approved Madame Hochon’s conduct in receiving her goddaughter; their good wishes for her success matched the secret contempt for Maxence Gilet’s conduct. Thus news of Rouget’s sister and nephew raised two parties in Issoudun—the higher and older bourgeoisie, who offered good wishes and watched without assisting, and the Knights of Idleness and Max’s partisans, who were capable of high‑handed outrages against the Parisians.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Agathe and Joseph arrived at the Messageries-Royales office in the Place Misere at three o’clock. Though tired, Madame Bridau felt youth revive on seeing her native town; each step recalled girlish memories. News of the Parisians spread instantly; their arrival was known within ten minutes. Madame Hochon met her godchild at the door and kissed her like a daughter. After seventy-two years of a barren life, exhausted by the graves of three unhappy children, she cherished this renewed intimacy as if Agathe had never left, and took a deep interest in Bridau. Agathe was led triumphantly into the salon where Monsieur Hochon sat, cool as a tepid oven.

“Here is Monsieur Hochon; how does he seem to you?” his wife asked.

“Precisely the same as when I last saw him,” said Agathe.

“Ah! it is easy to see you come from Paris; you are so complimentary,” sneered the old man.

Introductions followed: Baruch Borniche, twenty-two; François Hochon, twenty-four; and little Adolphine, blushing and awkward, anxious not to look at Joseph Bridau, who in turn was scrutinized by the two young men and old Hochon. The miser thought, “He’s just out of the hospital; he’ll be as hungry as a convalescent.” The youths muttered, “What a head! what a brigand! we shall be kept busy!”

“This is my son, the painter; my good Joseph,” Agathe said, the emphasis betraying a mother’s heart, her thoughts really in the Luxembourg prison.

“He looks ill,” said Madame Hochon; “he is not at all like you.”

“No, madame,” Joseph answered with the blunt candor of an artist; “I am like my father, and very ugly at that.”

Madame Hochon pressed Agathe’s hand as if to say, “Ah! my child; I understand now why you prefer your good-for-nothing Philippe.”

“I never saw your father, my dear boy,” she added aloud; “it is enough to make me love you that you are your mother’s son. Besides, you have talent, so the late Madame Descoings used to write to me; she was the only one in late years who told me much about you.”

“Talent!” exclaimed Joseph, “not yet; but with time and patience I may win fame and fortune.”

“By painting?” Monsieur Hochon sneered.

“Come, Adolphine,” said Madame Hochon, “go and see about dinner.”

“Mother,” said Joseph, “I will attend to the trunks.”

“Hochon,” the grandmother said to François, “show the rooms to Monsieur Bridau.”

It was half past three and dinner at four; Baruch rushed into town to tell of the Bridau arrival, describe Agathe’s dress, and above all paint Joseph’s haggard, determined face—like a brigand’s. That evening Joseph was the talk of every household.

“That sister of Rouget must have seen a monkey before her son was born,” said one; “he’s the image of a baboon.”

“He has the face of a brigand and the eyes of a basilisk.”

“All artists are like that.”

“They are spiteful as monkeys.”

“I have just seen Monsieur Beaussier, and he says he would not like to meet him in a dark wood; he saw him in the diligence.”

“He has hollows over the eyes like a horse, and he laughs like a maniac.”

“The fellow looks capable of anything; perhaps he ruined his fine brother. Poor Madame Bridau doesn’t seem happy with him.”

“Suppose we take advantage of his being here, and have our portraits painted?”

Curiosity flared. Those entitled to visit the Hochons resolved to call that night and examine the Parisians. Their arrival in the stagnant town was like dropping a beam among frogs.

After stowing trunks into the attic chambers, which he inspected, Joseph noted the silent house—the walls, staircase, and woodwork utterly plain and damp, furnished only with necessities. He felt the sharp transition from poetic Paris to arid province; coming downstairs and seeing Monsieur Hochon slicing bread for each person, he understood Molière’s Harpagon.

“We should have done better to go to an inn,” he thought.

Dinner confirmed his fears. A watery soup showed quantity favored over quality; bouilli was ceremoniously garnished with parsley and held the table’s center. Vegetables were served separately and counted as items of the repast. The bouilli’s place was accompanied by hard-boiled eggs on sorrel, a salad dressed with nut-oil, and little cups of custard flavored with burnt oats standing in for vanilla; butter, radishes, pickled gherkins, and horseradish completed the array. Madame Hochon approved, nodding contentedly, and her husband shrugged as if to say, “See the extravagances you force me to commit!”

After Monsieur Hochon slivered the bouilli into slices about as thick as a dancing-shoe sole, he replaced it with three pigeons. The wine was the 1811 country vintage. Adolphine had decorated the table ends with flowers.

“At Rome as the Romans do,” thought the artist, and began to eat—like a man who had breakfasted at Vierzon on an execrable coffee. When Joseph, having eaten all his bread, asked for more, Monsieur Hochon slowly searched his pockets for a key, unlocked a cupboard, broke off a section of a twelve-pound loaf, cut a round, divided it, laid the pieces on a plate, and passed it across the table with the silence of an old soldier meeting death calmly. Joseph took the half-slice and understood that asking for more was forbidden. No one was surprised. Conversation went on. Agathe learned that her birthhouse had been bought by the Borniches; she wished to see it.

“No doubt,” said her godmother, “the Borniches will be here this evening; we shall have half the town—who want to examine you,” she added, turning to Joseph, “and they will all invite you.”

Gritte, the sixty-year-old servant, brought dessert—the famous ripe cheese of Touraine and Berry, with mouldy discolorations like vine-leaves; she placed nuts and time-honored biscuits beside them.

“Well, Gritte, the fruit?” asked Madame Hochon.

“But, madame, there is none rotten,” answered Gritte.

Joseph laughed aloud as among his atelier comrades; he perceived that parsimony in eating only fruits near to rotting had become settled habit.

“Bah! we can eat them all the same,” he exclaimed.

“Monsieur Hochon, pray get some,” said the old lady.

Monsieur Hochon, much incensed, fetched peaches, pears, and Saint Catherine plums.

“Adolphine, go and gather some grapes,” Madame Hochon ordered her granddaughter.

Joseph looked at the two young men as if to say: “Is it to such fare you owe your healthy faces?” Baruch smiled; he and his cousin had been moderate and frequented Mère Cognette’s for feasts. Just before dinner, Baruch had been told the grand master convoked the Order at midnight for a magnificent supper and a great enterprise. The feast of welcome at the Hochons explained the nocturnal repasts of two young fellows blessed with appetites who never missed them.

“We will take the liqueur in the salon,” said Madame Hochon, rising and motioning Joseph to give her his arm. As they went out before the others she whispered to him:

“Eh! my poor boy; this dinner won’t give you indigestion; I had hard work to get it for you. It is always Lent here; you will get enough just to keep life in you, and no more. So you must bear it patiently.”

The old woman’s kindness pleased Joseph.

“I have lived fifty years with that man, without ever hearing half-a-dozen gold pieces chink in my purse,” she went on. “Oh! if I did not hope that you might save your property, I would never have brought you and your mother into my prison.”

“But how can you survive it?” cried Joseph naively.

“Ah, you may well ask!” she said. “I pray.”

Joseph quivered at the words, which raised her in his estimation; he stepped back to look into her face. “Let me paint your portrait,” he said.

“No, no,” she answered, “I am too weary of life to wish to remain here on canvas.”

Gayly uttering the sad thing, she opened a closet and brought out a flask of ratafia she made from a nuns’ recipe, the same source for Issoudun’s celebrated cake; Monsieur de Rivière ordered quantities yearly for the Seraglio.

Adolphine held a lacquer tray of little engraved glasses; as her mother filled and handed them around, Agathe remarked, “It seems as though my father’s turn were coming round!” This immutable provincial custom recalled her youth.

“Hochon will go to his club presently to read the papers, and we shall have a little time to ourselves,” said the old lady quietly.

Ten minutes later three women and Joseph were alone in the salon, where the floor was only swept, and the worsted-work designs and plain, dismal furniture seemed exactly as when Agathe left. Monarchy, Revolution, Empire, and Restoration had left no trace in this room.

“Ah! my godmother, compared with your life, mine has been cruelly tried,” exclaimed Madame Bridau, surprised to find even a canary she knew stuffed on the mantel among the old clock, brass brackets, and silver candlesticks.

“My child,” said the old lady, “trials are in the heart. The greater the need for resignation, the harder the struggle with ourselves. But don’t speak of me, let us talk of your affairs. You are directly in front of the enemy,” she added, pointing to the Rouget windows.

“They are sitting down to dinner,” said Adolphine.

The young girl, destined for a cloister, looked constantly out the window, hoping to hear more about the enormities imputed to Maxence Gilet, the Rabouilleuse, and Jean-Jacques. The old lady told her granddaughter to leave her and Agathe alone until visitors arrived. “We shall have ten or twelve batches of inquisitive folk here tonight,” she said.

Madame Hochon then recounted Maxence’s influence over Jean-Jacques Rouget and the Rabouilleuse’s doings, with all the town’s embroidered comments. Adolphine announced the approach of the Borniche, Beaussier, Lousteau-Prangin, Fichet, Goddet-Hérau families—fourteen persons in all.

“You now see, my dear child,” said the old lady, “that it will not be easy to get this property out of the wolf’s jaws—”

“It seems so difficult—with a scoundrel such as you represent him, and a daring woman like that crab-girl—as to be actually impossible,” Joseph remarked. “We should have to stay a year in Issoudun to counteract their influence and overthrow their dominion over my uncle. Money isn’t worth such a struggle—not to speak of the meannesses to which we should have to stoop. My mother has only two weeks’ leave; her place is permanent and must not be risked. As for me, in October I have an important work Schinner has obtained for me from a peer of France; my future fortune is in my brushes.”

Madame Hochon was amazed; though relatively superior to the town, she did not believe in painting. She glanced at her goddaughter and pressed her hand.

“This Maxence is the second volume of Philippe,” whispered Joseph to his mother, “only cleverer and better behaved. Well, madame,” he added aloud, “we won’t trouble Monsieur Hochon by staying long.”

“Ah! you are young; you know nothing of the world,” said the old lady. “A couple of weeks, if judicious, may produce great results; listen to my advice, and act accordingly.”

“Oh! willingly,” said Joseph. “I know I have an amazing incapacity for domestic statesmanship: for example, I’m sure I don’t know what Desroches himself would tell us to do if my uncle declines to see us.”

Mesdames Borniche, Goddet-Hérau, Beaussier, Lousteau-Prangin and Fichet, with their husbands, entered the room.

When the fourteen were seated and compliments over, Madame Hochon presented Agathe and Joseph. Joseph sat in his armchair all evening, slyly studying the sixty faces which, from five until half-past nine, posed for him gratis, as he later told his mother. Such behavior before Issoudun’s aristocracy did not change its opinion of him: everyone went home ruffled by his sarcastic glances, uneasy under his smiles, and even frightened at his sinister face, which a class unable to recognize genius found alarming.

After ten, when the household slept, Madame Hochon kept her goddaughter in her chamber until midnight. Secure from interruption, the two women exchanged sorrows. As Agathe listened to the last echoes of a soul that had missed its destiny and felt the sufferings of a generous heart whose charity could never be exercised, she perceived the immensity of the desert in which this noble, unrecognized soul had been wasted, and knew that she, with city life’s small joys, was not the most unhappy.

“You who are so pious,” she said, “explain my shortcomings; tell me what God punishes in me.”

“He is preparing us, my child,” answered the old woman, “for the striking of the last hour.”

At midnight the Knights of Idleness collected one by one like shadows under the Boulevard Baron's trees, whispering.

“What are we going to do?” each asked on arriving.

“I think,” said François, “Max means merely to give us a supper.”

“No; matters are serious for him and the Rabouilleuse: he has concocted some scheme against the Parisians.”

“It would be a good joke to drive them away.”

“My grandfather,” said Baruch, “is terribly alarmed at having two extra mouths to feed, and would seize any pretext—”

“Well, comrades!” cried Max softly, now appearing, “why star-gazing? the planets don’t distil kirschwasser. Come, let us go to Mère Cognette’s!”

“To Mère Cognette’s! To Mère Cognette’s!” they all cried.

The cry rang through the town like troops rushing; silence followed. The next day many asked, “Did you hear those frightful cries last night about one? I thought there was a fire.”

A supper worthy of La Cognette brightened the faces of twenty-two guests; the whole Order was present. At two, as they began to siroter, Max rose to speak:

“My dear fellows! the honor of your grand master was grossly attacked this morning after our memorable joke with Fario’s cart—attacked by a vile peddler, and what is more, a Spaniard (oh, Cabrera!); and I have resolved to make the scoundrel feel my vengeance—always, of course, within our rules. After reflecting, I have a trick worth execution—a famous trick that will drive him crazy. While avenging the insult, we shall feed the sacred animals of the Egyptians—little beasts unjustly persecuted. Thus good is the child of evil. I order you all, under pain of displeasing your humble grand master, to procure clandestinely, each, twenty rats, male or female. Collect your contingent within three days. Keep the rodents without food so they are ravenous. I will accept house-mice and field-mice as rats. Twenty-two times twenty gives four hundred; four hundred let loose in the old Capuchin church where Fario stores his grain will eat a not insignificant quantity! Be lively; Fario delivers most grain soon, and I am determined that Spaniard shall find a terrible deficit. I have not the merit of this invention,” Max added, seeing admiration; “render to Caesar that which is Caesar’s. My scheme reproduces Samson’s foxes; but Samson was an incendiary, no philanthropist; we, like Brahmins, protect a persecuted race. Mademoiselle Flore Brazier has set mouse-traps, and Kouski hunts field-mice. I have spoken.”

“I know,” said Goddet, “where to find an animal worth forty rats himself alone.”

“What’s that?”

“A squirrel.”

“I offer a little monkey,” said a younger member, “he’ll make himself drunk on wheat.”

“Bad!” exclaimed Max, “it would show who put the beasts there.”

“But we might each catch a pigeon some night,” said young Beaussier, “taking them from different farms; if we put them through a hole in the roof, they’ll attract thousands.”

So for the next week, Fario’s storehouse became the order of the night. “Recollect; people rise early in Saint-Paterne. Also, none of you go there without turning the soles of your list shoes backward. Knight Beaussier, the inventor of pigeons, is made director. I shall leave my imprint on the sacks of wheat. Gentlemen, you are appointed to the commissariat of the Army of Rats. If you find a watchman sleeping in the church, you must make him drunk—and cleverly—so he is far from the Rodents’ Orgy.”

“You don’t say anything about the Parisians?” Goddet asked.

“Oh!” exclaimed Max, “I want time to study them. Meantime, I offer my best shotgun—the one the Emperor gave me, a treasure from Versailles—to whoever finds a way to play the Bridaus a trick that will get them into difficulties with Madame and Monsieur Hochon, so those worthy people will send them off, or the Parisians will be forced to go of their own accord—without injuring the venerable ancestors of my two friends here, Baruch and François.”

“All right! I’ll think of it,” said Goddet, who coveted the gun.

“If the inventor of the trick doesn’t want the gun, he shall have my horse,” added Max.

After that night twenty brains were set to work to lay a plot against Agathe and her son following Max’s programme. But only the devil or chance could help them succeed; the conditions made it well-nigh impossible.

The next morning Agathe and Joseph came down before the second breakfast at ten. In Hochon’s household first breakfast meant a cup of milk and a slice of bread and butter taken in bed or when rising. While waiting for Madame Hochon to finish her minute toilette, Joseph noticed Jean-Jacques Rouget planted squarely at his door across the street. He pointed him out; Agathe could hardly recognize her brother, so altered he was.

“That is your brother,” said Adolphine.

“What an idiot he looks like!” exclaimed Joseph.

Agathe clasped her hands and looked to heaven. “What a state they have driven him to! Good God! can that be a man only fifty-seven?”

She looked closely and saw Flore Brazier standing behind him, hair dressed, snowy shoulders and a dazzling bosom through a gauze neckerchief trimmed with lace; her grenadine dress had leg-of-mutton sleeves fastened at the wrists with bracelets. A gold chain rippled over her bosom as she leaned forward to give Jean-Jacques his black silk cap; the scene was evidently studied.

“Hey!” cried Joseph, “there’s a fine woman, and a rare one! She’s made, as they say, to paint. What flesh-tints! Oh, the lovely tones! what surface! what curves! Ah, those shoulders! She’s a magnificent caryatid. What a model she would have been for one of Titian’s Venuses!”

Adolphine and Madame Hochon thought him talking Greek; Agathe signed behind his back that she was used to such jargon.

“So you think a creature who is depriving you of your property handsome?” said Madame Hochon.

“That doesn’t prevent her from being a splendid model—just plump enough not to spoil the hips and contour—”

“My son, you are not in your studio,” said Agathe. “Adolphine is here.”

“Ah, true! I did wrong. But you must remember that since leaving Paris I’ve seen nothing but ugly women—”

“My dear godmother,” Agathe said hastily, “how shall I meet my brother if that creature is always with him?”

“Bah!” said Joseph. “I’ll go and see him myself. I don’t think him such an idiot now I find he has the sense to rejoice his eyes with a Titian’s Venus.”
