“If he were not an idiot,” said Monsieur Hochon, who had come in, “he would have married long ago and had children; and then you would have no chance at the property. It is an ill wind that blows no good.”

“Your son’s idea is very good,” said Madame Hochon; “he ought to pay the first visit. He can make his uncle understand that if you call there he must be alone.”

“That will affront Mademoiselle Brazier,” said old Hochon. “No, no, madame; swallow the pill. If you can’t get the whole property, secure a small legacy.”

The Hochons were not clever enough to match Max. In the middle of breakfast Kouski brought a letter from Monsieur Rouget to his sister, Madame Bridau. Madame Hochon made her husband read it aloud:

My dear Sister,—I learn from strangers of your arrival in Issoudun. I can guess the reason which made you prefer Monsieur and Madame Hochon’s house to mine; but if you will come to see me you shall be received as you ought. I should certainly pay you the first visit if my health did not compel me to keep the house; for which I offer my affectionate regrets. I shall be delighted to see my nephew, whom I invite to dine with me tomorrow—young men are less sensitive than women about the company. It will please me if Messrs. Baruch Borniche and François Hochon will accompany him.

Your affectionate brother,

J.-J. Rouget.

“Say we are at breakfast, but Madame Bridau will send an answer presently, and the invitations are all accepted,” said Monsieur Hochon to the servant.

The old man put a finger to his lips for silence. When the street-door closed, Monsieur Hochon, little suspecting the intimacy between his grandsons and Max, threw one of his slyest looks at his wife and Agathe, remarking, “He is just as capable of writing that note as I am of giving away twenty-five louis; it is the soldier who corresponds with us!”

“What does that portend?” asked Madame Hochon. “Well, never mind; we will answer him. As for you, monsieur,” she added to Joseph, “you must dine there; but if—”

The old lady was stopped by a look from her husband. Knowing her affection for Agathe, old Hochon feared she might leave some legacy to her goddaughter if Agathe lost the Rouget property. Though fifteen years older, the miser hoped to inherit her fortune and eventually control their property. That hope was fixed. Madame Hochon knew threatening him with her will was the best way to get concessions. Monsieur Hochon now sided with his guests. An enormous fortune was at stake; with a sense of social justice he wished it to go to natural heirs rather than pillaged by outsiders. The sooner the matter was decided, the sooner he would rid himself of the guests. The struggle between interlopers and heirs had become a fact, and Monsieur Hochon’s keen intelligence, lulled by provincial monotony, was roused. Madame Hochon was pleasantly surprised to find such an able auxiliary on the Bridau side.

Towards midday the brains of Monsieur and Madame Hochon, Agathe, and Joseph—Joseph amazed at the old people’s scrupulous choice of words—produced the following answer, concocted for Max and Flore:

My dear Brother,—If I have stayed away from Issoudun, and kept up no intercourse with any one, not even with you, the fault lies not merely with the strange and false ideas my father conceived about me, but with the joys and sorrows of my life in Paris; for if God made me a happy wife, he has also deeply afflicted me as a mother. You are aware that my son, your nephew Philippe, lies under accusation of a capital offence for his devotion to the Emperor. Therefore you can hardly be surprised if a widow, compelled to take a humble situation in a lottery-office for a living, should come to seek consolation among those among whom she was born.

The profession adopted by the son who accompanies me is one that requires great talent, many sacrifices, and prolonged studies before any results can be obtained. Glory for an artist precedes fortune; is not that to say that Joseph, though he may bring honor to the family, will still be poor? Your sister, my dear Jean-Jacques, would have borne in silence the penalties of paternal injustice, but you will pardon a mother for reminding you that you have two nephews; one who carried the Emperor’s orders at Montereau and served in the Guard at Waterloo, now in prison for his devotion; the other, from his thirteenth year, has been impelled by natural gifts to enter a difficult though glorious career.

I thank you for your letter, my dear brother, with heartfelt warmth, for my own sake and Joseph’s, who will certainly accept your invitation. Illness excuses everything, and I shall therefore go to see you in your own house. A sister is always at home with a brother, whatever life he has adopted.

I embrace you tenderly.

Agathe Rouget

“There’s the matter started. Now, when you see him,” said Monsieur Hochon to Agathe, “you must speak plainly to him about his nephews.”

Gritte returned ten minutes later to report, as is provincial custom, all she had seen and heard.

“Since yesterday Madame has had the whole house cleaned up, which she left—”

“Whom do you mean by Madame?” asked old Hochon.

“That’s what they call the Rabouilleuse,” answered Gritte. “She left the salon and Monsieur Rouget’s part of the house in a pitiable state; but since yesterday the rooms have been made like before Monsieur Maxence moved in. You can see your face on the floors. La Vedie told me Kouski rode off at five and came back at nine with provisions. It’s going to be a grand dinner—a dinner fit for the archbishop of Bourges! There’s a fine bustle; Monsieur pokes into everything. He says, ‘I want to do honor to my nephew,’ and he is delighted to find his sister so amiable. Oh! she had on such a dress! Two diamonds in her ears—Vedie says three thousand francs apiece; such lace! rings and bracelets! you’d think she was a shrine; and a silk dress like an altar cloth. She had little black satin slippers and stockings marvels—flowers in silk and openwork—and a lovely little apron Vedie says cost more than two years’ wages.”

“Well done! We shall have to dress up,” laughed the artist.

“What do you think of all this, Monsieur Hochon?” said the old lady after Gritte left.

Madame Hochon made Agathe observe her husband, who sat with his head in his hands, elbows on chair-arms, plunged in thought.

“You have to do with a Maître Bonin!” said the old man at last. “With your ideas, young man,” he added to Joseph, “you haven’t force enough to struggle with a practised scoundrel like Maxence Gilet. No matter what I say, you will commit some folly. But tell me everything you see, hear, and do to-night. Go, and God be with you! Try to get alone with your uncle. If, despite your genius, you can’t manage it, that in itself will throw light on their scheme. But if you do get a moment alone, out of earshot, damn it, pull the wool from his eyes about the situation those two have put him in, and plead your mother’s cause.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
At four o’clock Joseph crossed the avenue of weak lindens from the Rouget house to the Hochon house. Kouski announced him. The table was set in the large hall; Joseph, who could easily distinguish his uncle, went up, kissed him, and bowed to Flore and Max.

“We have not seen each other since I came into the world, my dear uncle,” the painter gayly said.

“You are very welcome, my friend,” said the old man, looking at his nephew in a dull way.

“Madame,” Joseph told Flore with the frank vivacity of an artist, “this morning I envied my uncle the pleasure of admiring you every day.”

“Isn’t she beautiful?” the old man murmured; his dim eyes brightened.

“Beautiful enough to be the model of a great painter.”

“Nephew,” Rouget was nudged by Flore, “this is Monsieur Maxence Gilet; he served the Emperor, like your brother, in the Imperial Guard.”

Joseph rose and bowed to Max.

“You’re of the dragoons, I believe?” Max said. “I was only a dust-trotter.”

“On foot or horseback,” Flore put in, “you both risked your skins.”

Joseph and Max inspected one another. Max, a dandy with an eager face and the gray eyes of his father, struck the painter as an energetic fellow; Joseph’s artist’s eye calculated the household as a triangle: a dull old uncle, a handsome soldier, and a captivating young woman.

At that moment Baruch and François entered.

“Have you seen the tower of Issoudun?” Flore asked Joseph. “No? Then, if you’d like a walk before dinner in an hour, we’ll show you the great curiosity.”

“Gladly,” said Joseph, incapable of perceiving any impropriety.

While Flore went to put on her bonnet, Joseph jumped up to look at the pictures.

“Ah! you have pictures, indeed, uncle!” he cried at one that struck him.

“They came to us from the Descoings, who bought them during the Revolution, when the convents and churches in Berry were dismantled,” Rouget answered.

Joseph was lost in admiration. “Magnificent! That fellow didn’t spare his canvas. Better and better—” he murmured.

“There are seven or eight very large ones up in the garret, kept on account of the frames,” Max said.

“Let me see them!” cried Joseph; Max took him upstairs.

Joseph returned wildly enthusiastic. Max whispered to the Rabouilleuse, who drew the old man to a window embrasure where Joseph heard her say, “Your nephew is a painter; you don’t care for those pictures; be kind, and give them to him.”

“It seems,” said Jean-Jacques, leaning on Flore’s arm to reach Joseph by an Albano, “—it seems that you are a painter—”

“Only a ‘rapin,’” said Joseph.

“What may that be?” asked Flore.

“A beginner,” he replied.

“Well,” Jean-Jacques continued, “if these pictures can be of any use to you in your business, I give them to you,—but without the frames. Oh! the frames are gilt, and besides, they are very funny; I will put—”

“Well done, uncle!” cried Joseph, enchanted; “I’ll make you copies of the same dimensions, which you can put into the frames.”

“But that will take your time, and you will want canvas and colors,” said Flore. “You will have to spend money. Come, Père Rouget, offer your nephew a hundred francs for each copy; here are twenty-seven pictures, and I think there are eleven very big ones in the garret which ought to cost double,—call the whole four thousand francs. Oh, yes,” she added to Joseph, “your uncle can well afford to pay you four thousand francs for the copies, since he keeps the frames—but bless me! you’ll want frames; and they say frames cost more than pictures; there’s more gold on them. Answer, monsieur.” She shook the old man’s arm. “Hein? It isn’t dear; your nephew will take four thousand francs for new pictures in place of the old ones.”

“Well, nephew, I will pay you four thousand francs for the copies—”

“No, no!” said the honest Joseph; “four thousand francs and the pictures, that’s too much; the pictures, don’t you see, are valuable—”

“Accept, simpleton!” said Flore; “he is your uncle, you know.”

“Very good, I accept,” Joseph said, bewildered; for he had recognized a Perugino.

The artist beamed as he went out with the Rabouilleuse on his arm, which suited Maxence’s plans. Neither Flore, nor Rouget, nor Max, nor anyone in Issoudun knew the true value of the pictures, and Max reckoned he had gained Flore’s favor cheaply by parading her through the town leaning on the arm of his master’s nephew. People flocked to their doors to see the crab-girl’s triumph; the sensation helped Max; the incident of the pictures and the four thousand francs spread. Their dinner, with Lousteau, one of the court judges, and the Mayor, was splendid and lasted five hours. By nine o’clock at dessert Joseph, seated opposite his uncle and between Flore and Max, had fraternized with the soldier and taken him for a good fellow. Joseph returned home at eleven somewhat tipsy. Rouget was carried to bed dead-drunk; he had soaked up the wine like sand.

“Well,” Max said to Flore when alone, “isn’t this better than making faces at them? The Bridaus are well received, given presents, smothered with attentions; they will sing our praises and leave us in peace. Tomorrow you and I and Kouski will take down the pictures and send them over to the painter; we’ll put the frames in the garret and cover the walls with one of those varnished papers representing scenes from Telemachus.”

“Oh, that will be much prettier!” said Flore.

Next morning Joseph did not wake till midday. From bed he saw the pictures, brought in while he slept, leaning against the opposite wall. While he examined them anew, recognizing each masterpiece and searching for signatures, his mother had gone to see and thank her brother, urged by old Hochon, who, having heard of the painter’s follies the night before, despaired of the Bridau cause.

“Your adversaries have the cunning of foxes,” he said to Agathe. “In all my days I never saw a man carry things with such a high hand as that soldier; they say war educates young men! Joseph has let himself be fooled. They shut his mouth with wine, those miserable pictures, and four thousand francs! Your artist hasn’t cost Maxence much!”

The long-headed old man instructed Madame Bridau about a line of conduct: enter into Maxence’s ideas, cajole Flore, set up intimacy, and thus obtain a few moments’ interview with Jean-Jacques alone. Madame Bridau was warmly received by her brother, to whom Flore had taught her lesson. The old man was ill from his excesses. Agathe, under the circumstances, could scarcely at once speak of family matters; Max thought it proper and magnanimous to leave brother and sister alone. The calculation worked: poor Agathe found her brother so ill she would not deprive him of Madame Brazier’s care.

“Besides,” she said to the old bachelor, “I wish to know a person to whom I am grateful for the happiness of my brother.”

These words pleased the old man, who rang for Madame Flore. Flore, nearby, showed servile attentions and tenderness to her master, fussing over him like a new bride, and the poor creature received it with feeling.

“We owe you much gratitude, mademoiselle,” said Agathe, “for the attachment you have long shown my brother, and for the way you watch over his happiness.”

“That is true, my dear Agathe,” said the old man; “she has taught me what happiness is; she is a woman of excellent qualities.”

“And therefore, my dear brother, you ought to have recompensed Mademoiselle by making her your wife. Yes! I am too sincere in my religion not to wish to see you obey the precepts of the church. You would each be more tranquil in mind if you were not at variance with morality and the laws. I have come here, dear brother, to ask for help in my affliction; but do not suppose that we wish to make any remonstrance as to the manner in which you may dispose of your property—”

“Madame,” said Flore, “we know how unjust your father was to you. Monsieur here can tell you,” she went on, looking at Jean-Jacques, “that the only quarrels we have ever had were about you. I have always told him that he owes you part of the fortune he received from his father, and your father, my benefactor,—for he was my benefactor.” She added tears: “I shall ever remember him! But your brother, madame, has listened to reason.”

“Yes,” said the old man, “when I make my will you shall not be forgotten.”

“Don’t talk of these things, my dear brother; you do not yet know my nature.”

After such a beginning, it is easy to imagine how the visit went on. Rouget invited his sister to dinner on the next day but one.

During these three days the Knights of Idleness conducted their usual mischiefs: they captured an enormous quantity of rats and mice, kept them half-famished, and one night let four hundred and thirty-six into a grain storage, including breeding mothers. They also put a dozen pigeons into the old church roof. These animals revelled while the watchman, enticed away by drink, took no care of his master’s property.

Madame Bridau believed, contrary to old Hochon, that her brother had yet made no will; she intended to ask about Mademoiselle Brazier as soon as she could take a walk with him alone—a hope Flore and Maxence constantly dangled and deceived.

Meantime the Knights searched for a way to put the Parisians to flight, finding none practicable.

At the end of a week—half the time the Parisians were to stay—the Bridaus had made no progress. “Your lawyer does not understand the provinces,” old Hochon told Madame Bridau. “What you have come to do can’t be done in two weeks, nor in two years; you ought never to leave your brother, but live here and try to give him some ideas of religion. You cannot countermine the fortifications of Flore and Maxence without getting a priest to sap them. That is my advice, and it is high time to set about it.”

“You certainly have very singular ideas about the clergy,” said Madame Hochon to her husband.

“Bah!” the old man exclaimed, “that’s just like you pious women.”

“God would never bless an enterprise undertaken in a sacrilegious spirit,” said Madame Bridau. “Use religion for such a purpose! Why, we should be more criminal than Flore.”

This conversation took place at breakfast with François and Baruch listening.

“Sacrilege!” exclaimed old Hochon. “If some good abbé, keen as I have known many, knew what a dilemma you are in, he would not think it sacrilege to bring your brother’s lost soul back to God, force him to send away the woman who causes the scandal (with a proper provision, of course), and show him how to set his conscience at rest by giving a few thousand francs a year to the seminary and leaving his property to the rightful heirs.”

The Hochon household, used to the old miser’s passive obedience, made no overt protest, though Baruch and François exchanged anxious glances about how dangerous such a scheme would be to Max’s interest.

“The fact is, madame,” said Baruch, “that if you want to secure your brother’s property, the only sure way will be to stay in Issoudun for the necessary length of time—”

“Mother,” said Joseph hastily, “you had better write to Desroches about all this. As for me, I ask nothing more than what my uncle has already given me.”

After recognizing the great value of his thirty-nine pictures, Joseph had carefully unnailed the canvases, covered them with paper, gummed the edges, and laid them one above another in an enormous wooden box, which he sent to Desroches by the carrier’s wagon, proposing to write him a letter by post. The precious freight had been sent off the night before.

“You are satisfied with a pretty poor bargain,” said Monsieur Hochon.

“I can easily get a hundred and fifty thousand francs for those pictures,” replied Joseph.

“Painter’s nonsense!” exclaimed old Hochon.

“Mother,” said Joseph, “I am going to write to Desroches and explain the state of things here. If he advises you to remain, you had better do so. As for your situation, we can always find another like it.”

“My dear Joseph,” said Madame Hochon, following him from the table, “I don’t know anything about your uncle’s pictures, but they ought to be good, judging by the places from which they came. If they are worth only forty thousand francs—a thousand apiece—tell no one. Though my grandsons are discreet, they might, without intending harm, speak of this windfall; it would be known all over Issoudun; and it is very important that our adversaries should not suspect it. You behave like a child!”

In fact, before evening many persons in Issoudun, including Max, had heard this estimate, which set off an eager search for old paintings and the appearance of many execrable daubs. Max repented having driven Rouget into giving away the pictures, and his rage against the heirs, after hearing old Hochon’s ecclesiastical scheme, increased. The influence of religion on such a feeble creature as Rouget was his greatest fear. The news decided Maxence to turn Rouget’s investments into money and to borrow on his landed property to buy into the Funds as soon as possible; but he considered it even more important to get rid of the Parisians at once.

Flore, by Max’s advice, pretended Monsieur was too feeble to take walks and ought to have a carriage. This pretext avoided inquiry when they went to Bourges, Vierzon, Châteauroux, Vatan, and other places where withdrawing investments obliged Max and Flore to be with Rouget. At the close of the week, all Issoudun was amazed that the old man had gone to Bourges to buy a carriage—a step the Knights regarded as favorable to the Rabouilleuse. Flore and Max selected a hideous “berlingot,” twenty-two years old, a sort of demi-fortune carriage. It only cost four hundred and fifty francs; Max bought a stout mare and repainted the carriage dark brown, buying a tolerable harness at a bargain. The town awaited Pere Rouget’s equipage; at its first appearance every household was on its doorstep.

The second time the old bachelor went out he drove to Bourges, went before a notary, and signed a power of attorney in favor of Maxence Gilet to carry out transfers. Flore reserved to herself the business of making Monsieur sell investments in Issoudun and its neighborhood. The principal notary in Bourges was requested by Rouget to get him a loan of one hundred and forty thousand francs on his landed estate. Nothing was known at Issoudun of these secret proceedings. Maxence, a good rider, went with his horse to Bourges and back between five in the morning and five in the afternoon. Flore never left the old bachelor. Rouget consented without objection to the actions Flore dictated; but he insisted that the investment in the Funds producing fifty thousand francs a year should stand in Flore’s name as holding a life-interest only, and in his as owner of the principal. His tenacity in this domestic dispute worried Max; he suspected the natural heirs had been on his mind.

Amid these movements, which Max concealed, he forgot the Spaniard and his granary. Fario returned to Issoudun to deliver his corn after various trips to raise the price of cereals. The morning after his arrival he noticed the church roof black with pigeons. He hurried to the storehouse, where he found half his grain devoured. Thousands of mice and rat marks showed further ruin. The church was a Noah’s ark. But anger turned the Spaniard white when he found the grain at the bottom sprouting from water introduced by tin tubes into the pile. Pigeons and rats might be instinct, but the wetting showed human hand.

Fario sat on the chapel steps with his head between his hands. After half an hour he spied the squirrel Goddet had sent him playing on a cross-beam. He rose, turned to his watchman with a calm face, made no complaint, but hired laborers to gather sound grain and spread the moist to dry, saving as much as possible; after estimating his losses at about three fifths, he attended to filling his orders. But his previous manipulations had raised the price of cereals, and he lost on the three fifths he had to buy to fill orders; his losses were really over half. The Spaniard, with no enemies, at once attributed this revenge to Gilet. He was convinced Maxence and others were the authors of the nocturnal mischief and intended to ruin him. It was a matter of over three thousand francs—nearly his whole capital since the peace. Driven by the desire for vengeance, the man displayed the persistence of a detective. Hiding at night in different parts of Issoudun, he soon acquired proof of the Knights’ proceedings: he saw them, counted them, watched their rendezvous, and knew of their suppers at Mère Cognette’s; he lay in wait, witnessed their deeds, and thus became well informed of their nocturnal habits.

In spite of Max’s journeys and preoccupations, he did not neglect his nightly employments—first, because he did not wish his comrades to suspect his operations with Père Rouget’s property; second, to keep the Knights in hand. They were convened for a prank to be talked of for years: poisoned meat was to be thrown to every watchdog in town and environs. Fario overheard them congratulating each other after a supper on the probable success of this massacre and laughing over the general mourning it would cause.

“It will make people forget Fario’s cart,” said Goddet.

Fario needed no further proof; his mind was made up.

After three weeks in Issoudun, Agathe and Madame Hochon were convinced of the old miser’s observation that it would take years to destroy Max and Flore’s influence. She had made no progress in Jean-Jacques’s confidence and was never left alone with him. On the other hand, Mademoiselle Brazier triumphed openly by taking Agathe to drive in the calèche, sitting beside her on the back seat while Monsieur Rouget and his nephew occupied the front. Mother and son impatiently awaited an answer to the confidential letter they had written to Desroches. The day before the night on which the dogs were to be poisoned, Joseph, nearly bored to death in Issoudun, received two letters: one from the great painter Schinner and the second from Desroches.

Schinner’s letter, from Beaumont-sur-Oise, informed Joseph he was recommended for a place at the chateau de Presles for the Comte de Serizy; Schinner was off to Italy; Joseph should take his place at the Salon if need be. Desroches’s letter gave practical advice.

Desroches wrote that Monsieur Hochon was a sensible old man and that Joseph’s mother should remain at Issoudun with Madame Hochon, paying a small board—say four hundred francs a year—to reimburse her hosts. Madame Bridau ought, he thought, to follow Monsieur Hochon’s counsel in everything; for Agathe’s scruples would ill suit negotiations with those who had none. Maxence was dangerous: the sort who made his vices serve his fortune. Joseph, whose nature made such sustained dissimulation impossible, should return to Paris; the business required continual attention, careful observation, servile civilities, discretion in speech, and a dissimulation wholly against an artist’s grain.

If they had said no will had been made, Desroches warned, be sure they had one; but wills can be revoked, and as long as Rouget lived he might be worked upon by remorse and religion. The inheritance would be the result of combat between the Church and the Rabouilleuse: there might come a time when religion gained the upper hand. For this reason Monsieur Hochon must keep an eye on the condition of the property: whether real estate was mortgaged, and if so, where and in whose name the proceeds were invested. It was easy to terrify an old man and stop spoliation at its outset. But Joseph’s actions may already have given the alarm and his adversaries may have already secured themselves.

“That is what I call an opinion in good shape,” exclaimed Monsieur Hochon, proud of being appreciated by a Parisian lawyer.

“Oh! Desroches is a famous fellow,” answered Joseph.

“It would be well to read that letter to the two women,” said the old man.

“There it is,” said Joseph, giving it to him; “as to me, I want to be off to-morrow; and I am now going to say good-by to my uncle.”

“Ah!” said Monsieur Hochon, “I see that Monsieur Desroches tells you in a postscript to burn the letter.”

“You can burn it after showing it to my mother,” said the painter.

Joseph dressed, crossed the little square, and called on his uncle, who was finishing breakfast. Max and Flore were at table.

“Don’t disturb yourself, my dear uncle; I have only come to say good-by.”

“You are going?” said Max, exchanging glances with Flore.

“Yes; I have work at the chateau of Monsieur de Serizy, and I am all the more glad because his arm is long enough to do a service to my poor brother in the Chamber of Peers.”

“Well, well, go and work,” said old Rouget, with a silly air. Joseph thought him extraordinarily changed within a few days. “Men must work—I am sorry you are going.”

“Oh! my mother will be here some time longer,” remarked Joseph.

Max made a movement with his lips which Flore observed, meaning: “They are going to try the plan Baruch warned me of.”

“I am very glad I came,” said Joseph, “for I have had the pleasure of making your acquaintance and you have enriched my studio—”

“Yes,” said Flore, “instead of enlightening your uncle on the value of his pictures, which is now estimated at over one hundred thousand francs, you have packed them off in a hurry to Paris. Poor dear man! he is no better than a baby! We have just been told of a little treasure at Bourges,—what did they call it? a Poussin,—which was in the choir of the cathedral before the Revolution and is now worth, all by itself, thirty thousand francs.”

“That was not right of you, my nephew,” said Jean-Jacques, at a sign from Max, which Joseph could not see.

“Come now, frankly,” said the soldier, laughing, “on your honor, what should you say those pictures were worth? You’ve made an easy haul out of your uncle! and right enough, too—uncles are made to be pillaged. Nature deprived me of uncles, but damn it, if I’d had any I should have shown them no mercy.”

“Did you know, monsieur,” asked Flore of Rouget, “what your pictures were worth? How much did you say, Monsieur Joseph?”

“Well,” answered the painter, red as a beetroot,—“the pictures are certainly worth something.”

“They say you estimated them to Monsieur Hochon at one hundred and fifty thousand francs,” said Flore; “is that true?”

“Yes,” said the painter, with childlike honesty.

“And did you intend,” said Flore to the old man, “to give a hundred and fifty thousand francs to your nephew?”

“Never, never!” cried Jean-Jacques, on whom Flore had fixed her eye.

“There is one way to settle all this,” said the painter, “and that is to return them to you, uncle.”

“No, no, keep them,” said the old man.

“I shall send them back to you,” said Joseph, wounded by Max and Flore’s offensive silence. “There is something in my brushes which will make my fortune, without owing anything to any one, even an uncle. My respects to you, mademoiselle; good-day, monsieur—”

Joseph crossed the square in that irritated, hurt haste artists know. The entire Hochon family were in the salon. Seeing Joseph gesticulate and talking to himself, they asked him what was the matter. The painter, as open as daylight, related before Baruch and François the scene; two hours later it was the town’s talk, embroidered by ears and imaginations. Some insisted the painter had been maltreated by Max; others that he had misbehaved to Flore and been turned out.

“What a child your son is!” said Hochon to Madame Bridau; “the booby is the dupe of a scene they have been keeping for the last day of his visit. Max and the Rabouilleuse have known the value of those pictures for the last two weeks—ever since he foolishly told it before my grandsons, who blurted it out to all the world. Your artist had better have taken himself off without taking leave.”

“My son has done right to return the pictures if they are really so valuable,” said Agathe.

“If they are worth, as he says, two hundred thousand francs,” said old Hochon, “it was folly to put himself in a way to be obliged to return them. You might have had that, at least, out of the property; whereas, as things are going now, you won’t get anything. And this scene with Joseph is almost a reason why your brother should refuse to see you again.”
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Between midnight and one, the Knights of Idleness began their nightly rounds distributing food to the dogs, an expedition that lasted until three when they supped at Cognette’s. At half-past four they crept home. As Max turned from the rue l’Avenier into the Grande rue, Fario, lurking in a recess, struck him in the heart, withdrew the blade and fled toward Vilatte, wiping the knife on his handkerchief. The Spaniard washed the handkerchief in the Riviere forcée and returned to his lodgings at Saint-Paterne, entering by an open window and going to bed; later a watchman found him asleep.

Max uttered a fearful cry as he fell. Lousteau-Prangin, the judge’s son and distant relation of the sub-delegate, and young Goddet of the lower Grande rue ran up, shouting, “They are killing Max! Help!” The town, used to such false alarms, remained drowsy. They found Max fainting and roused Monsieur Goddet, the surgeon. Max had recognized Fario; but when he revived among bystanders and felt the wound was not mortal, he seized the chance to make capital of the attack and whispered, “I think I recognized that cursed painter!”

Lousteau-Prangin ran to his father. Max was carried home by Cognette, young Goddet, and two others, while Mere Cognette and Monsieur Goddet walked beside the stretcher. Those who carried him glanced at Monsieur Hochon’s door and saw Hochon’s servant sweeping the steps. Gritte, like most provincial households, was early astir. The few words Max had spoken aroused Monsieur Goddet’s suspicions. He asked the woman if Monsieur Joseph Bridau was in bed.

“He went out at half-past four,” she answered. “He walked up and down his room all night.”

The answer caused exclamations of horror; Gritte, curious, came over and saw Max bleeding on a mattress. Artists can guess what had kept Joseph restless all night: he had dreaded the gossip at Issoudun, imagining the town would say he’d fleeced his uncle and been deceitful. Early at dawn he had gone to the poplar avenue to walk and calm himself, vowing never to return to Issoudun.

While Joseph comforted himself by promising to leave, Max prepared a theatrical outrage for his sensitive spirit. Monsieur Goddet, after probing the wound and finding that a small pocket-book had deflected the knife so the blow was serious but not mortal, did what country surgeons do to enhance their credit: he “did not answer for the patient’s life,” dressed him, gave a grave verdict to the Rabouilleuse, Jean-Jacques Rouget, Kouski, and the Vedie, then left the house. The Rabouilleuse wept over Max; Kouski and the Vedie declared the captain likely to die. Nearly two hundred people gathered about place Saint-Jean and the two Narettes.

“I sha’n’t be a month in bed; and I know who struck the blow,” Max whispered to Flore. “We’ll profit by it to get rid of the Parisians. I said I thought I recognized the painter; pretend I’m expected to die and try to have Joseph Bridau arrested. Let him taste prison for a couple of days and his mother will be off to Paris when she gets him out. Then we needn’t fear the priests they talk of setting on the old fool.”

When Flore came downstairs the crowd was ready to take the impression she meant to give. She went out in tears and, sobbing, related how the painter, “who had just the face for that sort of thing,” had been angry with Max the night before about some pictures he had “wormed out” of Père Rouget.

“That brigand—look at him!—thinks that if Max were dead his uncle would leave him his fortune,” she cried. “The old one told me Max is Doctor Rouget’s son!”

“Ah! he meant to do the deed before he left Issoudun; he chose his time, for he was going away today,” said one of the Knights of Idleness.

“Max hasn’t an enemy in Issoudun,” said another.

“Besides, Max recognized the painter,” said the Rabouilleuse.

“Where’s that cursed Parisian? Let us find him!” they cried.

“Find him? Why, he left Monsieur Hochon’s at daybreak.”

A Knight of Idleness ran to Monsieur Mouilleron. The crowd swelled; tumult threatened. Groups filled the Grande-Narette and formed before Saint-Jean; a mass assembled at porte Vilatte. Lousteau-Prangin, Monsieur Mouilleron the commissary of police, the lieutenant of gendarmes, and two men struggled through hedges of people whose cries prejudiced them against the Parisian, though he was unjustly accused—circumstances, however, pointed against him.

After conferring with Max, Monsieur Mouilleron sent the commissary and a sergeant with one gendarme to inspect the “theatre of the crime.” Messieurs Mouilleron and Lousteau-Prangin, with the gendarme lieutenant, crossed to the Hochon house now guarded by two gendarmes in the garden and two at the door. The crowd swelled; the whole town surged in the Grande rue.

Gritte rushed, terrified, to inform Monsieur Hochon: “Monsieur, we shall be pillaged! The town is in revolt; Monsieur Maxence Gilet has been assassinated; they say Monsieur Joseph did it!”

Monsieur Hochon, alarmed for his gold, dressed quickly, peered out, then bolted the house. He learned from Gritte that his guest had left at daybreak—after pacing his room all night—and not yet returned. Madame Hochon, already awake from the noise, heard the frightful tidings.

“He is innocent, of course,” she said.

“Before his innocence can be proved, the crowd may pillage us,” replied Hochon, white with fear.

“Where is Agathe?”

“Sound asleep.”

“So much the better,” said Madame Hochon. “A shock might kill the poor child.”

But Agathe woke half-dressed, disturbed by evasive answers and the turmoil. She found Madame Hochon pale, weeping at a salon window beside her husband.

“Courage, my child. God sends us our afflictions,” the old lady said. “Joseph is accused—”

“Of what?”

“Of a bad action he could never have committed,” Madame Hochon answered.

Seeing the lieutenant of gendarmes enter with the magistrates, Agathe fainted.

“There now!” said Monsieur Hochon. “Carry off Madame Bridau; women are only in the way at these times. Take her to her room and stay there, both of you. Sit down, gentlemen. The mistake to which we owe your visit will soon, I hope, be cleared up.”

“Even if it should be a mistake,” said Monsieur Mouilleron, “the crowd is so exasperated I fear for the accused’s safety. I should like to arrest him; that might pacify these people.”

“Who would have believed Monsieur Maxence Gilet inspired so much affection?” asked Lousteau-Prangin.

“One of my men says twelve hundred more are coming from the faubourg de Rome,” said the lieutenant; “they threaten to kill the assassin.”

“Where is your guest?” asked Mouilleron of Hochon.

“He went to walk in the country, I believe.”

“Call Gritte,” said the judge gravely. “The crime was committed not far from here.”

The three functionaries exchanged looks.

“I never liked that painter’s face,” said the lieutenant.

When Gritte appeared trembling, Lousteau-Prangin asked if she had seen Monsieur Joseph leave that morning. She confirmed it: “Just as I was getting up; he walked about all night, and was dressed when I came downstairs.” Lousteau ordered warrants fetched. Monsieur Hochon pleaded caution: Joseph may have been agitated for other reasons—he meant to return to Paris that day for business in which Gilet and Mademoiselle Brazier had questioned his honor. Mouilleron added that the quarrel over the pictures yesterday had been hot; with artists it can be a word and a blow. Lousteau declared no one in Issoudun had cause to kill Gilet. Hochon noted, “But what was Monsieur Gilet doing in the streets at four in the morning?”

A clamour rose from the other end of town, like thunder: “Here he is! Here he is—he’s arrested!” Joseph, returning quietly past the mill at Landrole to breakfast, had been seen at the place Misere. Two gendarmes ran in time to snatch him from the faubourg de Rome inhabitants, who had already pinioned him and threatened him with death.

“Give way! Give way!” cried the gendarmes as they formed a protective escort.

“You see, monsieur,” one who held the painter said, “it concerns our skin as well as yours. Innocent or guilty, we must protect you. The people adore Monsieur Gilet and want to take justice into their own hands. Didn’t we see them in 1830 dusting the jackets of the tax-gatherers?”

Joseph, pale, forced himself to go on. Amid hoots, insults, and stones, he was led from place Misere to place Saint-Jean; the gendarmes drew sabres, one was wounded, and Joseph received missiles on his legs, shoulders, and hat.

“We must disperse the crowd; I see but one way,” said the lieutenant to the magistrates. “Take Monsieur Bridau to the Palais accompanied by all of you; my gendarmes will make a circle. One can’t answer for anything before a furious crowd of six thousand.”

“You are right,” said Monsieur Hochon, trembling for his gold.

“Do you wish your friend’s house pillaged?” asked the lieutenant. “Could we beat back a crowd pushed from behind by an angry populace that knows nothing of legal forms?”

“That will do; let us go; we can explain later,” said Joseph, who had recovered composure.

“Give way, friends! He is arrested, and we are taking him to the Palais,” the lieutenant cried. “Respect the law!” said Mouilleron.

“Wouldn’t you prefer to see him guillotined?” jeered a gendarme at a group.

“Yes, yes, they shall guillotine him!” one madman shouted; the women echoed, “They are going to guillotine him!” By the time they reached the end of the Grande-Narette the crowd cried: “They are taking him to the guillotine! They found the knife upon him! That’s what Parisians are! He carries crime on his face!”

Though blood rushed to his head, Joseph walked from place Saint-Jean to the Palais with calm. In Monsieur Lousteau-Prangin’s private office he declared his innocence: he had been out past five, stood at half-past five before Saint-Cyr’s facade, talked with the sexton, crossed the vegetable market where women had assembled, passed place Misere, went as far as the mill of Landrole by the Pont aux Anes and watched the ducks five or six minutes—miller’s men must have seen him—and then followed the avenue to Tivoli where he spoke with the gardener. He urged the magistrates to verify these facts and begged not to be arrested, promising to remain in the office until cleared.

These sensible, confident statements impressed the magistrates.

“Yes, summon all those persons,” said Mouilleron; “but it is more than a day’s affair. Make up your mind to be imprisoned in the Palais.”

“Provided I can write to my mother to reassure her—oh, you can read the letter,” Joseph added.

This request was granted, and Joseph wrote to his mother assuring her the mistake could be rectified and that he expected to be free by evening or tomorrow.

Madame Bridau, suffering a nervous attack and medicated by Monsieur Goddet without relief, read the letter and was soothed into the usual depressed state. When Goddet returned, he found her regretting leaving Paris.

“How is Monsieur Gilet?” asked Madame Hochon of Goddet.

“His wound, though serious, is not mortal,” the doctor replied. “With a month’s nursing he will be all right. I left him writing to Monsieur Mouilleron to request Joseph’s release,” he added. “Oh! Max is a fine fellow. He then remembered something proving the assassin was not your son; the man wore list shoes, whereas it is certain Monsieur Joseph left in boots—”

“Ah! God forgive him the harm he has done me—”

A man had left Max, after dark, a typed note that read: “Captain Gilet ought not to let an innocent man suffer. He who struck the blow promises not to strike again if Monsieur Gilet will have Monsieur Joseph Bridau set at liberty,” without naming the attacker. After reading and burning this letter, Max wrote to Monsieur Mouilleron relating the list-shoe detail from Goddet and begged Joseph’s release and asked Mouilleron to visit so he might explain more.

By the time Max’s letter arrived, Lousteau-Prangin had verified Joseph’s account through the bell-ringer, market-women, washerwomen, and the miller’s men. Max’s note clinched Joseph’s innocence, and Monsieur Mouilleron himself escorted him back to the Hochons’. His mother greeted him with overflowing tenderness; the poor misunderstood son was grateful: he had found affection from misfortune.

“Oh,” said Monsieur Mouilleron with self-satisfaction, “I knew by your look before the crowd that you were innocent; but the only way to protect you was to arrest you as we did. You carried your head high.”

“I was thinking of something else,” replied Joseph. “An officer once told me he had been stopped in Dalmatia under similar circumstances returning from a walk. The recollection made me think of painting a revolt of 1793. Besides, I kept saying, Blackguard that I am! I deserve this for coming to look after an inheritance instead of painting in my studio.”

“If you allow me a word of advice,” said the procureur du roi, “take the postmaster’s carriage to-night and return to Paris by the diligence from Bourges.”

“That is my advice too,” said Monsieur Hochon, burning to see them go.

“My most earnest wish is to get away,” said Agathe, kissing Madame Hochon’s hand. “When shall I see you again?”

“Ah, my dear, never until we meet above. We have suffered enough here below,” the old woman answered in a low voice.

Shortly after, Monsieur Rouget surprised them with a farewell visit; Jean-Jacques came to bid his sister good-by and offer his calèche for the drive to Bourges. “Ah! your pictures have been a great evil to us,” said Agathe. “Keep them, my sister,” said Rouget, who still doubted their value. He let them kiss him and went away without manifesting feeling. Baruch, at a hint from his grandfather, had seen the postmaster. At eleven that night the two Parisians, ensconced in a wicker cabriolet drawn by one horse and a postilion, left Issoudun. Adolphine and Madame Hochon parted with tears; they alone regretted Joseph and Agathe.

“They are gone!” said François Hochon, going with the Rabouilleuse into Max’s bedroom.

“Well done! The trick succeeded,” answered Max, now tired and feverish.

“What did you say to old Mouilleron?” asked François.

“I told him I had given my assassin some cause to waylay me; that he was dangerous and likely, if I pursued the affair, to kill me before capture. Consequently, I begged Mouilleron and Prangin to make an ostensible active search but really to let the assassin go in peace, unless they wished to see me dead.”

“I do hope, Max,” said Flore, “that you will be quiet at night for some time to come.”

“At any rate, we are delivered from the Parisians!” cried Max. “The fellow who stabbed me had no idea what a service he was doing us.”

The next day the Parisians’ departure was celebrated as a provincial victory over Paris by nearly everyone in Issoudun, except the sober, staid inhabitants who shared Monsieur and Madame Hochon’s view. A few of Max’s friends spoke harshly of the Bridaus.

“Do those Parisians think we are all idiots?” cried one. “They came to fleece and have been shorn themselves,” said another. “They actually consulted a lawyer in Paris—what plan had they?” “A plan to get possession of old Rouget. But the Parisians were not clever enough; that lawyer can’t crow over us Berrichons!” “How abominable!” “That’s Paris for you!” “The Rabouilleuse knew they came to attack her, and she defended herself.” “She did gloriously right!”

To the townspeople the Bridaus were Parisians and foreigners; they preferred Max and Flore.

We can imagine the comfort with which Joseph and Agathe re-entered their lodging in the rue Mazarin. On the journey Joseph recovered spirits lost by arrest and a day’s confinement; but his mother could not be cheered. The Court of Peers was about to try the military conspirators, and that kept Agathe anxious. Philippe’s conduct, despite a clever defender recommended by Desroches, raised suspicions unfavorable to his character. To escape the trial’s twenty days, Joseph, after briefing Desroches fully on Issoudun, set off with Mistigris for the Comte de Serizy’s chateau.

Contemporary histories need not be repeated here: whether Philippe had played an agreed part or was truly an informer, he was condemned to five years’ surveillance by the police and ordered to leave Paris that day for Autun, the director-general’s choice for his exile. The punishment resembled parole with a town for a prison. Learning that the Comte de Serizy, one of the peers on the court-martial, was employing Joseph to decorate his chateau at Presles, Desroches sought an audience and found Monsieur de Serizy favorably disposed; they had met. Desroches explained the brothers’ financial plight, recalled their father’s services and neglect under the Restoration, and urged the minister to induce the director-general of police to change Philippe’s residence from Autun to Issoudun and to grant a dole of sixty francs a month to the former lieutenant-colonel, a matter of shame for the minister of war to deny.

“I will obtain all you ask of me, for I think it just,” replied the count.

Three days later Desroches, armed with authority, fetched Philippe from the Court of Peers’ prison and took him to his house in the rue de Béthisy. There the young barrister read Philippe a plain, unanswerable lecture in which lawyers reduce clients’ sentiment to simple, blunt terms. He humbled the former staff-officer by reproaching his reckless dissipations, his mother’s misfortunes, and the death of Madame Descoings, then explained Issoudun’s situation and probed Maxence Gilet and the Rabouilleuse’s characters. Philippe, who grasped such matters keenly, listened more to this part than to the reproaches.

“Under these circumstances,” Desroches continued, “you can repair some of the injury you have done—so far as possible; you cannot restore life to the poor mother you have nearly killed. But you alone can—”

“What can I do?” asked Philippe.

“I have obtained a change of residence for you from Autun to Issoudun.”

Philippe’s sunken, almost sinister face brightened.

“And,” Desroches went on, “you alone can recover your uncle’s inheritance; half may already be in Gilet’s hands. I suggest no plan; everything depends on local circumstances. You have to deal with a strong, astute man. The way he tried to recover the pictures he’d given to Joseph, and the audacity with which he laid the crime on your brother, shows he’s capable of anything. Be prudent; behave properly out of policy if not decency. Without telling Joseph, whose pride as an artist would revolt, I have sent the pictures to Monsieur Hochon, telling him to give them up to no one but you. By the way, Maxence Gilet is a brave man.”

“So much the better,” said Philippe; “I count on his courage for success; a coward would leave Issoudun.”

“Consult Monsieur Hochon,” urged Desroches. “You have your orders from the police; I have taken your place in the Orléans diligence for half-past seven tonight. I suppose your trunk is ready; come and dine.”

“I own nothing but what I have on my back,” said Philippe, opening his threadbare blue overcoat; “but I only need three things, which you must tell Giroudeau, the uncle of Finot, to send me—my sabre, my sword, and my pistols.”

“You need more than that,” said the lawyer, shuddering at his client’s appearance. “You will receive a quarterly stipend which will clothe you decently.”

“Bless me! are you here, Godeschal?” cried Philippe, recognizing in Desroches’s head-clerk the brother of Mariette.

“Yes, I have been with Monsieur Desroches for two months.”

“And he will stay with me till he gets business of his own,” said Desroches.

“How is Mariette?” asked Philippe, moved.

“She is getting ready for the opening of the new theatre.”

“It would cost her little trouble to get my sentence remitted,” said Philippe. “However, as she chooses!”

After a meagre dinner—Desroches boarded his head-clerk—the two lawyers put the political convict in the diligence and wished him good luck.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
On All-Souls’ day, November 2, Philippe Bridau registered at the Issoudun police commissary and, on an official’s advice, took rooms in rue l’Avenier. News that an officer banished for a recent conspiracy had come to town spread quickly, intensified by the fact he was brother to the painter who had been wrongly accused. Maxence Gilet, recovered from his wound, had turned Père Rouget’s mortgages into cash and placed the proceeds in the “grand-livre.” The 140,000-franc mortgage on Rouget’s property caused an uproar—provincial gossip traveled fast. Monsieur Hochon, anxious for the Bridaus and still smarting from the terror at his house possibly being ransacked, questioned old Monsieur Heron, the Bourges notary.

“If not for me,” Heron declared, “old Rouget would have kept the 50,000-franc income in Maxence Gilet’s name. I advised Mademoiselle Brazier to rely on the will, and cautioned Maxence and his mistress to let this sudden change be forgotten.” Hochon urged protection for the Bridaus.

Maxence and Flore, now secure, welcomed another nephew’s arrival; they knew they could have Rouget sign a power of attorney transferring funds to Max or Flore if danger threatened. If the will leaving Flore the principal were revoked, the 50,000-franc income and the newly raised 140,000-franc mortgage would be consolation.

The day after arriving, Philippe called on his uncle about ten in shabby clothes. Flore shivered at his repulsive appearance; Gilet felt the same chill that warns of a hidden enemy. Philippe’s ruined blue greatcoat was buttoned to the throat; his trousers were ragged, boots muddy, hat greasy, cane dull. His face had the gambler’s exhaustion—sallow skin, sunken cheeks, red-rimmed lids and a threatening brow; bald except for a fringe, his brilliant eyes were cold steel-blue. He introduced himself hoarsely as Philippe Bridau, a lieutenant-colonel ill-treated by the Bourbons, and complained he had to live on sixty francs a month by police order. He made a point of disdain for relying on his uncle.

“Good-morning, uncle,” he said, offering his hand; Rouget took it. He declared he would sooner cut off his hand than beg from his uncle after the way his mother and brother had been treated in Issoudun, but had felt it proper to pay respects. Flore invited him to breakfast; he declined, saying he had already eaten. He hinted he must safeguard the Bridaus’ honor, spoke curtly about pictures at Monsieur Hochon’s for identification, and left. The violence of his tone and the way he slammed the door shocked Flore and Max more than his looks.

Max and Flore watched him cross to the Hochons’. Gilet, though furious, kept his temper; he knew political prudence. Flore urged Rouget not to admit him again.

Later Philippe entered the Hochon house where curiosity was thick. Madame Hochon shuddered at the father of Agathe’s griefs and the supposed murderer of Madame Descoings; others felt unease. Philippe politely explained he had come to introduce himself and manage living on sixty francs. He spoke well, admitted past faults, and promised reformation. He praised Lousteau the journalist, which won the old lady’s favor. Madame Hochon advised him gently; he said prison had taught him much and he would live differently.

Outside, Philippe took Monsieur Hochon aside. In a whisper he warned that the town’s gossip could be ruinous and they must avoid being suspected of advising him publicly. Desroches had instructed Philippe to use Hochon’s counsel discreetly; they agreed to meet “accidentally” in open places when needed. Philippe asked for names of veterans in Issoudun who were not Maxence’s friends—he wanted allies. Hochon named Mignonnet, an artillery captain of the Guard, and Captain Carpentier, a cavalry officer who had replaced Gilet as mayoral clerk and befriended Mignonnet. They discussed Philippe’s need for work; a mutual insurance agency might pay fifty francs a month—enough.

Within a week Philippe bought on credit a decent suit, boots, gloves, and a hat. Giroudeau sent linen and weapons and a letter to Carpentier, who welcomed him and introduced him to Mignonnet, recommending him as a man of merit. Philippe narrated parts of the conspiracy—framed to win admiration. He presented the recent affair as a continuation of earlier army efforts against the Bourbons: a large northern operation and a Paris diversion. Betrayed, the leaders had seen the Paris plot reduced to a barrack affair; when the larger scheme failed Philippe said he broke links, letting only the smaller plot be exposed. His apparent misery and ruined costume served to minimize the conspiracy’s size in the authorities’ eyes. He professed to have played both sides, preserving favor with influential figures while waiting to join whichever party served his interests best. Carpentier and Mignonnet, impressed by the account and his seeming devotion, brought him into their circle.

They recommended him for the insurance post Hochon had mentioned, requiring only three hours a day. Philippe frequented their club; his manners and the officers’ praise earned him respect. Determined to seem sober and respectable, and to lull Gilet’s suspicions, he feigned simple-mindedness while quietly observing his uncle’s property and plotting. Hochon had given him details of Rouget’s wealth, which stirred his covetousness. He and Hochon agreed not to alarm Maxence; if Rouget were spirited off to Bourges the game would be lost.

Philippe dined once a week with Mignonnet, once with Carpentier, and every Thursday with Hochon; soon he had engagements most nights. He never spoke of his uncle or his brother and mother except in connection with their stay in Issoudun. The three officers, decorated men in town, walked together daily; Philippe wore a rosette and carried himself like a man of consequence. To Max’s friends Philippe’s demeanor and story made him seem a “sabreur,” a brave front of the old army.

“His conduct before the Court of Peers shows him either a dupe or a spy,” said Max; others defended him. Philippe, wishing to conceal details of his situation, lived in a secluded house in the faubourg Saint-Paterne with a garden; here he fenced with Carpentier, reviving his skills and learning new tricks. He practiced pistols with Mignonnet and Carpentier, ostensibly for sport but really to make Max believe he preferred pistols in a duel. When he met Gilet he waited for a bow and returned it with a cavalier touch to the hat, as an officer to a private. Gilet gave no sign of impatience and never spoke of Bridau at the Cognettes’, though the pranks of the Order of Idleness were temporarily suspended since Fario’s attack.

Philippe’s contempt for Gilet and Gilet’s restrained bearing became the talk of Issoudun. Some thought Philippe was setting a trap. Potel suggested Max might be baiting him; others speculated about what might grow from the enmity. Rumor set the two up as adversaries.

Philippe had investigated the circumstances of his brother’s arrest and Gilet’s and the Rabouilleuse’s past, and he was brought into contact with Fario the Spaniard. Finding Fario reliable, Philippe made him a confidant and promised him indemnity for past losses—Fario, a willing henchman, put his knowledge of the Knights of Idleness and his services at Philippe’s disposal. The town, abuzz with gossip, expected a deadly clash between the two men who intensely despised one another.

Toward the end of November, Philippe told Hochon he had discovered that Hochon’s grandsons Baruch and François were intimate with Maxence and had spied in his house, informing Max of what was said there. He claimed to have overheard them and learned from Fario of their nocturnal visits to drinking-shops in Maxence’s company; they owed Maxence over three thousand francs. Philippe suspected Max and the Rabouilleuse intended to secure the 50,000-franc income and then run away and marry. They must learn what was happening under Rouget’s roof, Philippe said, but he did not know how to proceed. Hochon promised to think.

Jean-Jacques Rouget suffered as never before since Philippe’s first visit. Flore, fearing some evil to Max, planned to leave Issoudun and marry Max in Paris after getting the Funds’ income transferred. Rouget refused to transfer, insisting Flore be sole heir—he believed she would abandon him if made rich. She tried cajolery and then coldness; Vedie found Rouget one morning with swollen eyes from weeping. For a week he breakfasted alone, perhaps on meager fare.

The day after Philippe’s talk with Hochon, he went to Rouget’s house again. Flore played tender and solicitous in his presence; Gilet stayed away. Philippe, discerning danger, judged it time to strike. He forced Flore to look him in the face and demanded to know if Rouget was free to walk alone with him. Flore stammered that he was. Philippe insisted Rouget accompany him on foot, implying that if he didn’t, the town’s claim that Rouget was under Flore’s dominion would be confirmed. Seeing Rouget hesitate, Philippe pretended to go; he caught Flore making a menacing gesture at her uncle, and told Rouget to meet him at the door in ten minutes or he would take other measures. He left for the Hochons’.

At nine that night, Hochon, Madame Hochon, Monsieur Heron the notary, and the grandchildren gathered. Hochon, enraged, denounced Baruch and François for their nocturnal life with Maxence and for having informed the Knights of Idleness; he accused them of betraying secrets for money and even of causing past crimes by transmitting plans. He read the guardianship accounts: the Borniche children’s net fortune was 70,000 francs, part of their mother’s dowry, but Hochon, as creditor to his daughter, controlled much of it. Baruch’s portion was twenty thousand francs. Hochon told them he would release their property to them but no longer tolerate their conduct; he discharged his guardianship and made their futures contingent on strict behavior. Baruch and François were stunned.

After Heron read the accounts, Hochon told Baruch he would be sent to Paris to study banking under Mongenod; François, who was in debt, would have only six hundred francs a year and would go to Poitiers to study law. Hochon gloated over his quick vengeance: he would watch them and test them by years of conduct. Having settled the legal business and obtained receipts, Heron was preparing to leave when Philippe arrived. Madame Hochon withdrew with her grandsons to see what effect the scene had produced.

Philippe and Hochon spoke in the window embrasure. Hochon had consulted Heron and learned that the security for the 50,000-franc income could not be sold without the owner’s signature or a power of attorney; Rouget had not given any power before a notary since his arrival. If Rouget attempted to give one in Issoudun they would know instantly; if he left town to sign, they would discover it through registration. Therefore, if Rouget left, they would follow him and learn his movements. Philippe reported seeing his uncle cross the street and detailed his last visit. He told Hochon that Fario and a man named Benjamin Bourdet—a soldier Philippe had helped in Vatan—were watching; they would follow Rouget secretly. “If they let him walk with you,” Hochon mused, “Maxence must believe he has found some means to win the game.” Philippe said Maxence was afraid of him but could not evade him; Fario had stirred public censure against Maxence and the gossip was now in motion.

When Philippe left, Flore hurried to Max’s room with a full account of his boldness. Max resolved that before resorting to fighting him they would play a bold card. He told Flore to send at once to Père Cognette for a horse and a char-à-banc; pack their things and go to Vatan pretending to stay, taking Vedie and the twenty thousand francs in Rouget’s drawer. If Philippe brought Rouget to Vatan, Flore must refuse to return unless Rouget signed a power of attorney. As soon as they obtained it, Max would slip off to Paris while she came back to Issoudun. When Jean-Jacques found her gone he would be desperate and likely follow; Max planned to give him a stern talking-to when he did.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
While the foregoing plot was progressing, Philippe walked arm in arm with his uncle along the boulevard Baron.

“The two great tacticians are coming to close quarters at last,” Monsieur Hochon thought as he watched the colonel march off with his uncle. Philippe, with his Paris-inflected phrasing, told Rouget bluntly that the girl he adored—Flore—was using him to secure his fortune for Max, whom she loved.

“I know that, Philippe, but I love her all the same,” the old man said.

“I’ve sworn, by my mother’s soul, to make your Rabouilleuse as supple as my glove,” Philippe answered. “I’ll kill Max as I would a dog—on one condition.”

“What is that?” Rouget asked stupidly.

“Don’t sign the power of attorney before the third of December; put them off till then. They want your signature to sell the fifty thousand a year in the Funds, to run off to Paris and pay for their wedding. Promise to defer it, and let me know the night before you mean to sign. Maxence won’t be your proxy unless he first kills me. If he kills me, you must accept him; if I kill him, you must take me in his place. I’ll make Flore love you; if she doesn’t, thunder! I’ll thrash her.”

“Oh! I never could allow that. A blow struck at Flore would break my heart,” Rouget cried.

“But it is the only way to govern women and horses. A man must make himself feared or loved,” Philippe insisted, and then greeted Mignonnet and Carpentier. Briefly explaining the situation, he concluded: “There are no two ways to save him: either Colonel Bridau must kill Captain Gilet, or Captain Gilet must kill Colonel Bridau. We celebrate the Emperor’s coronation the day after tomorrow; arrange the banquet seats so I sit opposite Gilet. You will be my seconds.”

Mignonnet and Carpentier agreed to help. Philippe urged his uncle repeatedly: promise to sign no paper before the third of December and he’d have Flore back and the old man happy. They walked and kept up Rouget’s spirits for hours. Philippe’s counsel was blunt: women must be made to fear a man who rules them. At home, Kouski opened the door in pretend tears—under Max’s orders—and announced that Madame had gone away and taken Vedie with her.

“Gone—away!” the old man cried, and sat down ruined by despair, searching the house and then sobbing.

Kouski said Flore had taken twenty thousand francs in gold from Rouget’s drawer, calling them wages for twenty-two years’ service. Rouget, frantic, blamed Philippe and begged Max to find Flore and bring her back. Gilet affected surprise and pretended ignorance of her departure; he had ordered Kouski not to open the door to anyone to maximize Rouget’s distress and pressure him to comply.

Maxence wandered town, avoided by many whom he had counted on. A general reaction had turned against him: the deeds of the Knights of Idleness and Joseph Bridau’s arrest, now explained, had disgraced Max in the opinion of the town. He met Captain Potel, who lamented that the Guard was being blackguarded and stripped of honor; Potel urged Max to attend the banquet and not be called a coward before Bridau. Max muttered that the power of attorney should not be in his name; he planned to have it in Baruch’s name, then transfer the property by his order. Cool and composed outwardly, Max was inwardly boiling but determined to have the money before he fought, resolved the income would not go to Philippe if he died.

When he entered the room where Rouget wept, Max feigned surprise at Flore’s departure and questioned Kouski. Kouski’s report—that Flore said Rouget had no affection for her and that she would never return—stung Rouget to the core. Max advised practical measures: serve dinner, then make inquiries; learn by dessert which road Flore had taken. The old man, relieved by Max’s pity, clung to him like a child to a savior.

That evening Kouski returned to announce Flore had driven toward Vatan. “She’s going back to her own people,” he said. Max suggested going to Vatan that night—bad road though it was—since Kouski knew how to drive; he proposed to saddle his horse and ride ahead. Philippe, already informed by Monsieur Hochon of Flore’s departure, rose from Mignonnet’s table and hurried toward Saint-Jean. At Rouget’s, Fario reported the stable activity.

“If they go toward Vatan,” Philippe said, “get me another horse and come with Benjamin to Mignonnet’s. I must know what Gilet does with my uncle.” Monsieur Hochon and Philippe speculated on whether Max, seeking to reconcile Rouget with Flore, would take the lead, or whether Max would go ahead to teach Flore a lesson. Philippe planned with Carpentier for any contingency.

Meanwhile, a lad brought a letter for Baruch Hochon. The two grandsons, chastened by the morning’s events, had promised to behave and repair past wrongs in hopes of their grandfather’s favor. Baruch read Max’s letter aloud: Max hoped Baruch would receive a power of attorney from Rouget at Vatan the next morning, expected to send him to Paris and to furnish the money for the trip, and likely to leave Issoudun on December third. Monsieur Hochon rejoiced: “The property of that old idiot is saved from the claws of the devil.” He instructed Baruch to accept the power of attorney, to stop at Orléans on the way to Paris, and to conceal his lodgings.

Suddenly Francois cried out: Rouget and Colonel Bridau were returning together in the caleche, with Benjamin and Carpentier following on horseback. Hochon hurried over and found Rouget in his bedroom writing a letter at Philippe’s dictation, threatening to revoke his will in Flore’s favor if she did not return: he would give his property to Philippe instead and declared that Gilet could no longer be his guest after Vatan.

“Captain Carpentier and I MET my uncle,” Philippe announced to Monsieur Hochon, emphasizing that his intervention had prevented Rouget’s being trapped into signing away his income. He believed his letter would bring Flore back that night. Hochon supported him: “Destroy your will, and Flore will be what she used to be.”

Carpentier took the letter and drove himself in the old caleche to Vatan, Benjamin riding with him, for Kouski had been sent there too—though the Pole had taken a hired horse to warn Max and Flore, and then disappeared. Philippe told Benjamin to take the Pole’s place in the household and move in; he would sleep in the room adjoining Gilet’s apartment if his uncle consented. Hochon freely predicted a jovial banquet.

When wheels were heard in the Grande-Narette about half-past eleven, Rouget’s agony turned to joy as he saw the carriage and two women entering the courtyard. Philippe helped Flore down with an iron touch, spoke to her with rough charm, and led her into Place Saint-Jean to set terms.

“My fine lady,” he began, “three days hence Maxence Gilet will be sent to the shades by this arm, or his will have taken me off guard. If I die, you’ll be mistress to my uncle; if I remain, you must keep him supplied with first-class happiness. If you don’t, I know girls in Paris prettier than you, seventeen years old, who would make him happy and serve my interests. Begin your attentions this very evening; if the old man is not gay tomorrow, I have one word to say to you; there’s but one way to kill a man without the law—that’s a duel— but I know three ways to get rid of a woman.”

Flore shook like one seized with fever. Old Rouget, disregarding Hochon’s objections, came out and clasped her hand, drew her into his room, and shut the door.

The next morning the town buzzed: “What will happen at the banquet between Max and Colonel Bridau?” Philippe told Vedie briefly: six hundred francs’ annuity or dismissal—enough to keep her neutral for a time. Knowing Max’s life to be in danger, Flore grew more affectionate to Rouget than before; self-interested devotion is often more agreeable than truth. She appeared at breakfast the following day on Rouget’s arm, tears in her eyes upon seeing Philippe’s severe and sinister face in Max’s place.

“She can’t endure the idea of your fighting Maxence,” old Rouget said.

“I have not the slightest desire to kill Gilet,” Philippe replied. “He need only go to America; I’d be the first to outfit him and wish him a safe voyage. It’s better to kick his legs about in a free country than have them rot in a pine box. But perhaps you think he’s a good shot; if so, he might kill me—it’s on the cards.”

“Will you let me speak to him?” Flore implored, with humble submission.

“Certainly; he can come here and pack up his things. I will stay with my uncle; I shall not leave him.” Flore sent Vedie to the hotel to ask Max to come, but Philippe interrupted: send his things to the hotel. Vedie returned with Max’s formal refusal; he asked Mademoiselle Brazier to send his belongings. Philippe told Flore to take Vedie with her, for appearances’ sake, and said that if she wasn’t back by midday she must give a power of attorney to Rouget to attend to his affairs.

Max would not be moved. Routed in public and too proud to retreat, he refused to go to America; he would not take Flore without her money, yet he would not evidently reveal his heart. He insisted on killing Philippe to regain his lost honor. “We have committed a monstrous folly,” he said. “We ought to have gone to Paris. I thought that fellow was a fire-eater, but I’ll not be a coward. He’s lowered me in town; I cannot get back what I lost unless I kill him. I have buried nine already.” Despite Philippe’s superiority in brute strength, Max trusted his own skill with weapons and thought he could run Philippe through.

Flore shut herself in to cry; all day gossip ran wild. The duel between Philippe and Maxence seemed inevitable.

“Philippe has managed this thing well from the beginning,” Mignonnet told Hochon. “I would never have believed that big, easy-going fellow would have succeeded as he has. The two have rolled together like a couple of thunder-clouds.”

Carpentier praised Philippe’s conduct before the Court of Peers; Renard and Potel argued about Max’s conduct and whether the Guard was disgraced. Some townsfolk lamented that Max might be trying to lay hands on fifty thousand francs a year; Potel flared at the suggestion. Across Issoudun, households prayed for Colonel Bridau.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Towards four o’clock the following day, the officers of the old army at Issoudun and its environs strolled about the place du Marché, in front of Lacroix’s eating-house, waiting for Colonel Philippe Bridau. The banquet for the coronation was to begin at five. Various groups discussed Max’s disgrace and his dismissal from old Rouget’s house; the officers were to dine at Lacroix’s, and the common soldiers planned a meeting at a nearby wine-shop. Among the officers, Potel and Renard alone defended Max.

“Is it any of our business what takes place among the old man’s heirs?” said Renard.

“Max is weak with women,” Potel remarked.

“There’ll be sabres unsheathed before long,” said an old sub-lieutenant.

“A folly that doesn’t succeed becomes a crime,” Mignonnet observed dryly.

Max joined the old soldiers and was received in significant silence. Potel and Renard took his arms and walked with him, conversing. Presently Philippe appeared in full dress; his air contrasted with the forced gratitude Max showed his supporters. Bridau’s hand was grasped by Mignonnet, Carpentier, and others. This welcome, so unlike the one Max received, dispelled the last feeling of cowardice or prudence awakened in him by Flore’s entreaties and tendernesses.

“We shall fight,” he said to Renard, “and to the death. Therefore don’t talk to me any more; let me play my part well.”

The three Bonapartists returned to the officers’ group. Max bowed first to Bridau, who returned it; their glance was frigid.

“Come, gentlemen, let us take our seats,” said Potel.

“And drink to the health of the Little Corporal, who is now in the paradise of heroes,” cried Renard.

The company entered Lacroix’s long dining-hall. At Philippe’s request the two adversaries were seated opposite each other. Some young men of the town, among them Knights of Idleness, lingered outside, anxious to see what might happen. They deplored the crisis, though each considered a duel inevitable.

Throughout the meal both antagonists displayed vigilance beneath apparent joviality. At dessert Philippe rose and proposed the first toast.

“To the Grand Army!” he cried.

“To the Grand Army!” repeated every voice. Eleven private soldiers, including Benjamin and Kouski, entered and repeated the toast. The old soldiers stood behind the officers.

“I claim the second toast,” said Mignonnet. “Let us drink to those who attempted to restore his son!”

Everyone present, except Maxence Gilet, bowed to Philippe and raised their glasses. Max rose and, amid expectancy, bowed ironically to Philippe.

“May we all meet again at this time next year,” said Max.

His voice, though level, was charged; some muttered “It’s coming!” and Kouski whispered predictions. Tension lingered, but the two spoke in tones of ordinary conversation; there was nothing theatrical except the profound stillness.

“Look here, you others!” Philippe said to the soldiers; “our affairs don’t concern the bourgeoisie—no word, therefore. It is for the Old Guard only.”

“They’ll obey orders, colonel,” Renard replied.

The dessert passed; libations were plentiful. The antagonists and their four seconds arranged that such a duel, with its stakes of fortune and reputation, should not appear an ordinary brawl. Both behaved like gentlemen, calming the anxious crowd. At ten that night the two adversaries agreed on sabres; the rendezvous was behind the chancel of the Capuchins’ church at eight o’clock next morning. Goddet, former army surgeon, would be present. The seconds agreed the combat would last only ten minutes, whatever occurred.

At eleven that night Monsieur and Madame Hochon surprised Colonel Bridau in his rooms. Madame Hochon, tearful, implored him to say his prayers before leaving the house and presented him a relic: a tooth of Sainte Solange mounted on black velvet. She urged him to wear it.

“Will it protect me from a sabre-thrust?” asked Philippe.

“Yes,” she replied.

“Then I have no right to wear that accoutrement any more than if it were a cuirass,” cried Philippe.

The old lady kissed his forehead and gave thirty francs—her all—to Benjamin, asking him to sew the relic into Philippe’s trousers. Benjamin complied, not from belief in its virtue but because the pay and the commission constrained him. Madame Hochon left confident that Sainte Solange would protect him.

At eight the next morning, December third, with cloudy weather, Max, his seconds, and the Pole arrived on the small meadow around the apse of the Capuchins’ church. There Philippe and his seconds waited, Benjamin among them. Potel and Mignonnet paced off twenty-four feet; attendants drew lines with a spade at each end: no man was allowed to retreat beyond his line under pain of dishonor. Each was to stand at his line and advance when the seconds gave the word.

“Do we take off our coats?” asked Philippe coldly.

“Of course,” answered Maxence, adopting a bully’s manner.

They stripped to shirts; their muscles hardly trembled despite the cold. Goddet, the seconds, and two soldiers admired their composure.

Just as the signal was given, Max caught sight of Fario’s sinister face peering through a hole in the church roof. Those eyes, full of hatred and revenge, for a moment dazzled him. Philippe adopted the inside guard that marks a duellist of the first rank. Max sensed inferiority.

“Ah! the lascar!” he thought; “he’s an expert; I’m lost!”
