He attempted a moulinet, twirling his sabre to bewilder Philippe and disarm him; but at the first encounter he felt the colonel’s wrist was iron and spring-like. Philippe’s parries were cool and precise. Between two men of such calibre, victory depends on a flash of calculation and a single false movement. At a bad parry by Max the colonel sent the sabre spinning from his hand.

“Pick it up,” he said; “I am not the man to kill a disarmed enemy.”

There was something atrocious in the grandeur of these words; they seemed to show such conscious superiority that the onlookers took them for calculation. When Max resumed position, he had lost coolness and confronted again Philippe’s raised guard. He resolved to redeem his shame by a bold stroke; taking his sabre in both hands he rushed furiously, determined to kill or be killed. Philippe received a cut slashing his forehead and part of his face, but with a terrible oblique moulinet he cleft Max’s head. These two savage blows ended the combat in the ninth minute. Fario came down to gloat over his enemy in convulsions; Philippe was carried back to his uncle’s house.

Thus perished a man who might have achieved great deeds in different circumstances; nature had given him courage, self-possession, and political sagacity, but not the nobility of conduct needed for greatness. He was not much regretted, given the perfidy by which his adversary—worse in character—had brought him into disrepute. His death ended the exploits of the Order of Idleness, to Issoudun’s relief. Philippe had little to fear as the duel’s circumstances were spread with praise for the bravery of both combatants.

“But they had better both have been killed,” remarked Monsieur Mouilleron; “it would have been a good riddance for the Government.”

Flore Brazier’s situation would have been embarrassing but for the condition into which she fell after Max’s death. A brain fever set in, combined with dangerous inflammation from her escapade to Vatan. If she had had ordinary health she might have fled the house where Max’s bed lay and where his murderer suffered; instead she hovered between life and death for three months, attended by Goddet, who also attended Philippe.

As soon as Philippe could write he sent letters.

To Monsieur Desroches he reported he had “killed the most venomous of the two reptiles,” though his own head had been split by a sabre. He noted that the other “viper” remained and that he must come to terms with her because his uncle clung to her. An illness had kept Flore in bed, which he hoped Providence would use to call her soul if she repented. By his account Goddet—“a worthy soul”—and others had been won to his side; Goddet preached resignation and religion to Flore and had promised to keep her in bed for three months. He had bought over the cook, who spread tales suggesting Max would have ill-treated Flore. “All goes well so far,” he wrote; “my uncle, by old Hochon’s advice, has destroyed his will.”

To Monsieur Giroudeau, care of Mademoiselle Florentine, he requested a girl—Cesarine, if available—or some ballet-girl who could appear as the respectable daughter of a brave soldier and stand ready to come at his call; if she answered his purpose he would give her fifty thousand francs on his uncle’s death. He told of his duel and hinted at lively times to come.

Agathe, informed by Madame Hochon, rushed to Issoudun and was given Philippe’s former room. Her maternal tenderness revived as she listened to town praises for her son.

“After all, my child,” said Madame Hochon, “youth must have its fling. The dissipations of a soldier under the Empire must, of course, be greater than those of young men looked after by their fathers. Thanks to your son, Issoudun now breathes and sleeps in peace. Philippe has come to his senses rather late; he told us frankly that those three months in the Luxembourg sobered him. Monsieur Hochon is delighted with his conduct here; everyone thinks highly of it. If he can be kept away from the temptations of Paris, he will end by being a comfort to you.”

Philippe played the saint to his mother because he needed her. He did not wish to resort to Cesarine unless he continued to be an object of horror to Mademoiselle Brazier. He recognized Flore was essential to his uncle’s life and preferred to use her rather than bring in a dancer who might marry the old man. He conceived the idea of making his uncle marry Flore, thus legitimizing her position and keeping her—and the property—within reach. He asked his mother to visit Flore and treat her in a sisterly way.

“I must confess, my dear mother,” he said in an affected tone before Monsieur and Madame Hochon, “that my uncle’s way of life is not becoming; he could, however, make Mademoiselle Brazier respected by the community if he chose. Wouldn’t it be far better for her to be Madame Rouget than the servant-mistress of an old bachelor? She had better obtain a definite right to his property by a marriage contract. If you, or Monsieur Hochon, or some good priest would speak to both parties, you might put a stop to the scandal which offends decent people.”

The next day Agathe and Madame Hochon appeared at Flore’s bedside and repeated Philippe’s words. In Issoudun the colonel was praised as a man of noble character, particularly because of his conduct toward Flore. For a month Flore heard Goddet, Madame Hochon, Agathe, and Rouget all urging a marriage. When, attracted by the idea of becoming Madame Rouget, she grew eager to recover so the ceremony could occur, it was not difficult to make her understand that she would be unwelcome in the Rouget family if she intended to turn Philippe out.

“Besides,” the doctor whispered, “you really owe him this good fortune. Max would never have allowed you to marry old Rouget. And, if you have children, you can revenge Max, for that will disinherit the Bridaus.”

Two months after the duel, in February 1823, the sick woman, urged by those about her and implored by Rouget, consented to receive Philippe; the sight of his scars moved her to tears, and his softened manner calmed her. By Philippe’s wish they were left alone.

“My dear child,” he said, “it is I who, from the start, have advised your marriage with my uncle; if you consent, it will take place as soon as you are recovered.”

“So they tell me,” she replied.

“Circumstances have compelled me to give you pain; it is natural therefore that I should wish to do you all the good I can. Wealth, respect, and a family position are worth more than what you have lost. You wouldn’t have been that fellow’s wife long after my uncle’s death, for I happen to know, through friends of his, that he intended to get rid of you. Come, my dear, let us understand each other and live happily. You shall be my aunt and nothing more. You will take care that my uncle does not forget me in his will; on my side, you shall see how well I will have you treated in the marriage contract. Keep calm, think it over, and we will talk later. All sensible people, indeed the whole town, urge you to put an end to your illegal position; no one will blame you for receiving me. It is well understood in the world that interests go before feelings. By the day of your marriage you will be handsomer than ever. The pallor of illness has given you an air of distinction, and on my honor, if my uncle did not love you so madly, you should be the wife of Colonel Bridau.”

Philippe left after dropping into Flore’s mind the idea of revenge through children—a hint that stirred her. In this scene Philippe echoed, in miniature, the manipulative speech of a schemer: personal calculation masked as sentiment has formidable power over the heart. Nature herself works by self-interest.

At the beginning of April 1823, Rouget’s hall hosted a splendid dinner to celebrate the signing of the marriage contract between Mademoiselle Flore Brazier and the old bachelor. Guests included Monsieur Heron, the witnesses, Mignonnet, Carpentier, Hochon, Goddet, the mayor, the curate, Agathe Bridau, Madame Hochon and her friend Madame Borniche. The bride was much impressed by the protection offered by Philippe and the two ladies. Flore was dazzling. The curate, who had been instructing her for the last fortnight, was to allow her first communion the following day. The local journals praised the marriage as a revival of religion and a moral correction; the “Journal du Cher” and the “Journal de l’Indre” printed accounts that lauded Philippe’s supposed disinterestedness.

By the contract Rouget secured to Flore a dower of one hundred thousand francs and a life annuity of thirty thousand.

After the wedding, Agathe returned to Paris triumphant and told Joseph and Desroches the “good news.”

“Your son Philippe is too wily a man not to keep his paw on that inheritance,” said Desroches.

“You, and Joseph too, will always be unjust to that poor boy,” said Madame Bridau. She defended her son’s conduct as statesmanlike, a reaction to idle powers misapplied.

“Why do you suppose that he will not succeed by honest means?” she demanded.

“You will see!” exclaimed Desroches. “Fortunate or unfortunate, Philippe will remain the man of the Rue Mazarin, the murderer of Madame Descoings, the domestic thief. But don’t worry yourself; he will manage to appear honest to the world.”

The morning after the marriage, Philippe took Madame Rouget aside.

“My dear aunt,” he said, “you now belong to the family. Thanks to me, the law has tied the knot. Now, no nonsense. I intend that you and I should play above board. I know the tricks you will try against me; and I shall watch you like a duenna. You will never go out of this house except on my arm; and you will never leave me. As to what passes within the house, damn it, you’ll find me like a spider in the middle of his web. Here is something,” he continued, showing her a letter, “which will prove to you that I could, while you were lying ill upstairs, have turned you out of doors without a penny. Read it.”

The letter was from the theatrical set—Mariette and friends—detailing a plan to provide a trained actress to entrap Rouget and secure the property for Philippe. It described the girl’s arts and the social circle prepared to exploit any fortune, and suggested the pleasures awaiting Philippe in Paris. The letter shook Madame Rouget; terror and dependency betrayed her.

“I trust you,” he said, fixing her with terrible meaning, “as you see; but I expect some return. I have made you my aunt intending to marry you some day. You are worth more to me than Esther in managing my uncle. In a year from now we must be in Paris; you will amuse yourself much better there than here; it is a perpetual carnival. I shall return to the army and become a general, and you will be a great lady. There’s our future; now work for it. But I must have a pledge to bind this agreement. You are to give me, within a month from now, a power of attorney from my uncle, which you must obtain under pretence of relieving him of the fatigues of business. Also, a month later, I must have a special power to transfer the income in the Funds. When that stands in my name, you and I have an equal interest in marrying each other. There it all is, my beautiful aunt, as plain as day. Between you and me there must be no ambiguity. I can marry my aunt at the end of a year’s widowhood; but I could not marry a disgraced girl.”

He left without waiting for an answer. Vedie, clearing the table, found her mistress pale and sweating despite the season. Flore felt as if she had fallen to the bottom of a precipice; the future seemed black and full of monstrous shapes. The damp chill of vaults, an instinctive fear of the man, oppressed her; yet a voice told her she deserved to have him for her master. She was helpless.

Flore Brazier had had a room of her own in Rouget’s house; now Madame Rouget belonged to her husband and was deprived of the freedom of a servant-mistress. In the horrible situation she discovered the hope of having a child, but soon saw that impossible under Philippe’s constant watch. Benjamin was Philippe’s innocent spy; Vedie trembled under him. Flore felt deserted and utterly helpless; she feared death. Without knowing how he might manage it, she felt certain that if he suspected pregnancy her doom would be sealed. The sound of his voice, the veiled glitter of his gambler’s eye, the slightest movement of the soldier—politeness cloaking brutality—made her shudder. The power of attorney he demanded he obtained as soon as he wanted it; Flore fell under his dominion as France had fallen under Napoleon.

Like a butterfly caught in wax, Rouget rapidly dissipated his remaining strength. In his decay the nephew remained as cold and impassible as diplomats in convulsed times.

Philippe, now skeptical of Napoleon II., wrote to the minister of war through intermediaries to offer his services to the King. He explained that Napoleon was gone; having been faithful, he was now free to serve the monarch. He asked for consideration and reinstatement at his rank, professing entire submission and assuring the minister of his fidelity. Accompanying the letter were requests for permission to go to Paris on urgent family business and endorsements from the mayor, the sub-prefect, and the commissary of police of Issoudun, praising Philippe’s conduct and citing newspaper articles about Rouget’s marriage.

Two weeks later, Philippe received permission and a letter informing him that by order of the King he was, as a preliminary favor, reinstated lieutenant-colonel in the royal army.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Lieutenant-Colonel Bridau returned to Paris with his aunt and the helpless Rouget. Three days after their arrival he escorted them to the Treasury, where Jean-Jacques assigned his income to Philippe. Exhausted and plunged by their nephew into the dissipations of actresses, journalists, artists, and equivocal women, the elderly Rouget found excitements that soon killed him. At Giroudeau’s instigation, Lolotte, a handsome Opera ballet-girl, became the amiable assassin of the old man. Rouget died after a splendid supper at Florentine’s; Lolotte blamed a slice of pâté de foie gras, and the Strasburg masterpiece—incapable of defending himself—was accounted to have died of indigestion.

Madame Rouget thrived amid this décolleté society; Philippe put her in charge of Mariette, whose vigilance kept the widow from committing any real folly. In October 1823 Philippe returned to Issoudun with a power of attorney from his aunt to liquidate his uncle’s estate; by March 1824 he was back in Paris with sixteen hundred thousand francs—the net proceeds, not counting the pictures still with Monsieur Hochon. On Hochon’s assurances of solvency, Philippe put the whole into Mongenod and Sons at six percent, with three months’ notice for withdrawal.

Philippe invited his mother to his marriage—witnessed by Giroudeau, Finot, Nathan, and Bixiou—and by contract the widow Rouget secured to him her million-franc portion should she die childless. No invitations were sent; Philippe had designs. He lodged his wife in an appartement in the rue Saint-Georges, bought furnished from Lolotte. Madame Bridau the younger thought it delightful; her husband rarely set foot in it. Unknown to her, Philippe purchased a magnificent hotel in the rue de Clichy for 250,000 francs, paying 150,000 down and taking two years for the rest, pouring two years’ income into renovations and furnishings. The restored pictures from Issoudun, then valued at 300,000 francs, shone there.

The accession of Charles X brought favor to the family of the Duc de Chaulieu; believing itself secure, the elder branch encouraged former Imperial soldiers. Philippe, who had likely made valuable revelations about the conspiracies of 1820 and 1822, was appointed lieutenant-colonel in the regiment of the Duc de Maufrigneuse, who felt bound to protect the man from whom he had taken Mariette. A corps-de-ballet connection helped; private councils sought a tinge of liberalism around the Dauphin, and Philippe—now a sort of equerry to the Duc—was presented to the Dauphin and Dauphine. Liking brisk, soldierly characters with past fidelity, they took to him; Philippe played the part, turning the spurious liberalism to his profit by securing appointment as aide-de-camp to a marshal who stood well at court.

In January 1827 Philippe, promoted in the Royal Guard as lieutenant-colonel under the Duc de Maufrigneuse, solicited ennoblement. Under the Restoration, nobility rewarded “roturiers” serving in the Guard. Colonel Bridau bought the estate of Brambourg and asked to entail it under the title of count; through high-society intimacies the favor was granted. Appearing with the luxury of horses, carriages, and livery, he adopted the airs of a great lord. Gazetted as Comte de Brambourg, he frequented the house of the Comte de Soulanges and, insatiable and backed by mistresses of influential men, sought to be one of the Dauphin’s aides-de-camp. He told the Dauphin that an “old soldier, wounded on many a battle-field and who knew real warfare, might, on occasion, be serviceable to Monseigneur,” and, adopting the tones of sycophancy required at court, became exactly what the position demanded—respectable in manner while concealing his origins.

Philippe intended to marry Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges and become a general commanding a Guard regiment. To keep him quiet they made him Commander of the Legion of Honor and of Saint Louis. On rainy evenings, as Agathe and Joseph returned home, they met Philippe in full uniform—bedizened with orders, reclining in a coupe—who patronizingly greeted them. Madame Bridau explained his absence by the obligations of his elevated position; Joseph resented the mud he threw in their faces.

The Duc de Maufrigneuse warned Philippe that to marry Amelie de Soulanges he must be free; Philippe, with theatrical gesture, replied he had the “melancholy certainty of losing” his wife, who had taken to drinking and would die within a week—by then he would inherit his uncle’s million. He claimed thirty thousand francs a year in five per cents and forty thousand from entailed land; if Monsieur de Soulanges got a marshal’s baton, Philippe hoped to become general and peer of France.

After the Salon of 1823 a leading painter obtained the management of a lottery-office near the Markets for Agathe; she soon exchanged it for an office in the rue de Seine in the same house as Joseph’s atelier. Thus the widow hired an agent and ceased to be an expense to her son. Yet as late as 1828 she doubted Joseph’s fame, which was hotly contested: the bourgeoisie—who pay for art—were reluctant to support a genius in question. Supported by Gros and Gérard, Joseph received decoration after the Salon of 1827 but few orders; his imaginative fancies produced inequality used by critics to deny his talent. His friends counseled grand projects—Schinner declared he needed “a whole cathedral to decorate” to silence critics.

Such talk alarmed Agathe and confirmed her long judgment about her sons. Facts supported her provincial view: Philippe, now a great man, had outgrown his early errors; Joseph, whom she had seen work too often, seemed no more advanced in 1828 than in 1816. Poor Joseph owed money, bowed by debt; Agathe felt he had chosen a worthless career, while Philippe’s public display—uniform, plumes, pelisse, crosses—impressed her as the fulfillment of maternal dreams. On a day of public ceremony she felt proud of Philippe and forgot that the lottery-office enabling her to live came through Joseph.

One day Agathe noticed Joseph more worried than usual by his color-man’s bill and resolved to free him from debts. Though cursing his profession in her heart, lacking money of her own, she relied on Philippe’s good heart and purse; for three years she had expected his visits, imagining that if she appealed he would bring an enormous sum. She wrote to him, reminding him of his neglect and asking him to remember Joseph, suggesting that with his wealth he could spare some thousands. Philippe’s reply, brought two days later to the atelier, was a curt rebuke: “A man does not marry a Mademoiselle Amelie de Soulanges without the purse of Fortunatus, if under the name of Comte de Brambourg he hides that of — Your son, Philippe Bridau.” Agathe fell half-fainting; the letter dropped. Joseph, who had been working, found her and sent for Horace Bianchon. When told of the letter, Agathe asked a confessor, Abbé Loraux, who gently rebuked her for having misled even him: she had loved the wrong son, making him her glory, misunderstanding the true worth of Joseph. The priest urged her to love Joseph now and pray for him.

Joseph, who had gone out to borrow money to pay pressing debts, returned to find his mother questioning if he would forgive her. She confessed, “I have never loved you as you deserved.” Joseph protested with pain and affection; Agathe kissed him and gave him a tenderness earlier reserved only for Philippe. For two weeks of struggle, each expression of feeling seemed a lifetime’s worth. D’Arthez, Michel Chrestien, Fulgence Ridal, Pierre Grassou, and Bianchon kept Joseph company; Joseph divined that his mother wished to see Philippe before she died. He begged Bixiou—who frequented the circles where Philippe still appeared—to persuade his shameless brother to play, out of pity, a little comedy for their mother’s sake.

Bixiou undertook the mission. Philippe laughed when told, refusing to go: the “only service the poor woman can render me is to die as soon as she can,” he said, adding cynically that the less his family was seen, the better for his position. When Bixiou reported this, Joseph was chilled. The abbé, sitting by the bed, had told Agathe, “Alas! my child, you have never had but one son.” Those words confirmed her.

After a final delirium in which she murmured, “Whom does Philippe take after?” Agathe died twenty hours later. Joseph followed his mother to the grave alone: Philippe had gone on business to Orléans; in truth he had been driven from Paris by the momentary anger of Joseph, who had written immediately after Agathe’s last sigh: “Monster! my poor mother has died of the shock your letter caused her. Wear mourning, but pretend illness; I will not suffer her assassin to stand at my side before her coffin. Joseph B.” Joseph could not paint for grief, yet his friends vowed to stay with him.

Two weeks after Agathe’s funeral a letter arrived for Joseph from an old woman upstairs: Comtesse Flore de Brambourg. The missive—signed by “Comtesse Flore de Brambourg”—begged help; the writer said she had been eleven days at the Hotel de Brambourg without aid, living in a garret, unable to pay rent, and threatened with expulsion; she accused Philippe of desiring her death and mentioned threats he had made. Joseph and his friends, suspecting foul play behind Philippe’s serenity, summoned the woman who had brought the letter: Madame Gruget, a shabby nurse who told a sad story of Flore’s decline.

Moved, Joseph supplied ten francs and, with Bianchon, Bixiou, and Desroches, went to see Flore’s attic. They found a hideous spectacle: a woman green and thin as one drowned, nearly hairless, bound with a handkerchief, eyeringed and exhausted. Flore, in tears, declared Philippe guilty—“Philippe killed Max ... and now he has killed me. God uses him as a scourge!” Bianchon sought to treat her; Desroches planned to document her condition and summon Philippe to restore the conjugal home, since he had not publicly accused her of adultery, so her conjugal rights remained. Bianchon hurried to Desplein for an operation; Flore was removed to Dubois’s private hospital and three weeks later the Hospital Gazette reported one of modern surgery’s boldest operations on “F. B.” The patient died—more from exhaustion and starvation than from treatment.

On the day news spread that Flore had died, the Comte de Brambourg went, in deep mourning, to inform the Comte de Soulanges of his loss and to put forth his claims. Fashion whispered that the Comte de Soulanges might marry his daughter to a parvenu of merit soon to be made brigadier-general and given command of a Royal Guard regiment. De Marsay told Eugène de Rastignac this at the Rocher de Cancale, where Bixiou was present. Bixiou vowed to prevent it.

Among many old friends Philippe had refused to recognize were those who could not revenge themselves; but he had insulted abilities like Bixiou’s, who never forgave. Once, at the Rocher de Cancale, after Philippe had snubbed him—“You can come and see me when you are made a minister”—Bixiou devised a practical joke to expose him. Disguised as a secularized priest with green spectacles, he went to the Hotel de Soulanges and, received by the count, related in a feigned voice the whole scandalous narrative—Agathe’s death, old Rouget’s, Madame Descoings’, the theft of a newspaper, and Philippe’s early morals—citing secret particulars confided by Bianchon. He advised Soulanges to question Philippe’s former comrades before giving his daughter in marriage.

Three months later the Comte de Brambourg gave a supper to du Tillet, Nucingen, Eugène de Rastignac, Maxime de Trailles, and Henri de Marsay. When they condoled about his rupture with Soulanges, he parried with nonchalance. He boasted of future millions—“I shall have ten millions two years from now”—and spoke confidently of finance; du Tillet and Nucingen, bankers central to political affairs, exchanged a sly glance. They were positioned to manipulate the Bourse.

The opportunity came in July 1830. Du Tillet and Nucingen had helped Philippe make 1,500,000 francs; he trusted them. Yet Philippe, devoted to the Restoration and contemptuous of “civilians,” misjudged the political tide and speculated on a rise; the bankers, sure of a revolution, played against him for a fall. They encouraged his hopes while plotting to undermine him, and Philippe fought as though his millions depended on the struggle. An order sent him to Saint-Cloud with the Duc de Maufrigneuse likely saved his life that day; had he been on the boulevards he might have been killed by attackers led by his friend Giroudeau. A month later little remained of his immense fortune except his house, furniture, estates, and the Issoudun pictures. He had trusted in the Restoration and kept faith with the elder branch until 1834. Jealous of Giroudeau’s colonelcy, he re-entered service; in 1835 he obtained command of a regiment in Algeria, where he stayed three dangerous years, hoping for a general’s epaulets. But Giroudeau’s influence continually balked him. Hard and brutal, Philippe exceeded ordinary severity and was hated despite a Murat-like bravery.

In early 1839, during a retreat before superior forces, he made a rash dash against the Arabs with a single company, rejoined the main body after fierce fighting, and, surrounded, cried “Your colonel! to me! a colonel of the Empire!” His men, seeing him cut off, were unwilling to risk themselves to rescue him. Philippe met a terrible death: hacked to pieces by scimitars, his head cut off.

Joseph, who about this time had married—through Comte de Serizy—to the daughter of a millionaire farmer, inherited his brother’s Paris house and the entailed estate of Brambourg. Had Philippe foreseen this, he might have varied the entail. Joseph’s chief pleasure in the inheritance was the fine collection of paintings from Issoudun; he now has an income of sixty thousand francs, and his father-in-law continues to add to the household. Though Joseph paints magnificent pictures and serves artists well, he is not a member of the Institute; as result of a clause in the entail he is now Comte de Brambourg, a fact that often makes him roar with laughter among his atelier friends.

ADDENDUM

The following personages appear in other stories of the Human Comedy; the list notes some of the works in which they recur.

- Horace Bianchon: appears in Father Goriot; The Atheist’s Mass; César Birotteau; The Commission in Lunacy; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; The Secrets of a Princess; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; A Study of Woman; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Honorine; The Seamy Side of History; The Magic Skin; A Second Home; A Prince of Bohemia; Letters of Two Brides; The Muse of the Department; The Imaginary Mistress; The Middle Classes; Cousin Betty. Additionally narrated Another Study of Woman; La Grande Bretèche.

- César Birotteau: César Birotteau; At the Sign of the Cat and Racket.

- Jean-Jacques Bixiou: The Purse; The Government Clerks; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Firm of Nucingen; The Muse of the Department; Cousin Betty; The Member for Arcis; Beatrix; A Man of Business; Gaudissart II.; The Unconscious Humorists; Cousin Pons.

- Comte de Brambourg (title of Philippe Bridau, later Joseph): The Unconscious Humorists.

- Philippe Bridau: Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life.

- Joseph Bridau: The Purse; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Start in Life; Modeste Mignon; Another Study of Woman; Pierre Grassou; Letters of Two Brides; Cousin Betty; The Member for Arcis.

- Jean François du Bruel: The Government Clerks; A Start in Life; A Prince of Bohemia; The Middle Classes; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; A Daughter of Eve.

- Mme du Bruel (Claudine Chaffaroux): A Prince of Bohemia; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Letters of Two Brides; The Middle Classes.

- Madame Cabirolle: A Start in Life; Agathe-Florentine Cabirolle: A Start in Life; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris.

- Camusot: A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; Cousin Pons; The Muse of the Department; César Birotteau; At the Sign of the Cat and Racket.

- Jean-Jérôme-Séverin Cardot: A Start in Life; Lost Illusions; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; At the Sign of the Cat and Racket; César Birotteau.

- Henri, Duc de Chaulieu: Letters of Two Brides; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; The Thirteen.

- Michel Chrestien: A Distinguished Provincial at Paris; The Secrets of a Princess.

- Charles Claparon: César Birotteau; Melmoth Reconciled; The Firm of Nucingen; A Man of Business; The Middle Classes.

- Coloquinte: A Distinguished Provincial at Paris.

- Mademoiselle Coralie: A Start in Life; A Distinguished Provincial at Paris.

- Desplein: The Atheist’s Mass; Cousin Pons; Lost Illusions; The Thirteen; The Government Clerks; Pierrette; The Seamy Side of History; Modeste Mignon; Scenes from a Courtesan’s Life; Honorine.

DEDICATION

To Madame la Duchesse de Castries.

THE ILLUSTRIOUS GAUDISSART

CHAPTER I

The commercial traveller, a personage unknown to antiquity, is one of the striking figures created by the manners and customs of our present epoch. May he not, in some conceivable order of things, be destined to mark for coming philosophers the great transition which welds a period of material enterprise to the period of intellectual strength? Our century will bind the realm of isolated power, abounding in creative genius, to the realm of universal but levelling might—equalizing products, spreading them among the masses, and being itself controlled by the principle of unity—the final expression of all societies. Do we not find the dead level of barbarism succeeding the saturnalia of popular thought and the last struggles of civilizations which accumulated the treasures of the world in one direction?

The commercial traveller! Is he not to the realm of ideas what our stage-coaches are to men and things? He is their vehicle; he sets them going, carries them along, rubs them up with one another. He takes from the luminous centre a handful of light, and scatters it broadcast among the drowsy populations of the duller regions. This human pyrotechnic is a scholar without learning, a juggler hoaxed by himself, an unbelieving priest of mysteries and dogmas which he expounds all the better for his want of faith. Curious being! He has seen everything, known everything, and is up in all the ways of the world. Soaked in the vices of Paris, he affects to be the fellow-well-met of the provinces. He is the link which connects the village with the capital; though essentially neither Parisian nor provincial—he is a traveller. He sees nothing to the core: men and places he knows by their names; as for things, he looks merely at their surface, and he has his little tape-line with which to measure them. His glance shoots over all things and penetrates none. He occupies himself with a great deal, yet nothing occupies him.

Jester and jolly fellow, he keeps on good terms with all political opinions, and is patriotic to the bottom of his soul. A capital mimic, he knows how to turn on the smiles of persuasion, satisfaction, and good-nature, or drop them for the normal expression of his natural man. He is compelled to be an observer of a certain sort in the interests of his trade. He must probe men with a glance and guess their habits, wants, and above all their solvency. To economize time he must come to quick decisions as to his chances of success—a practice that makes him more or less a man of judgment; on the strength of which he sets up as a judge of theatres, and discourses about those of Paris and the provinces.

He knows all the good and bad haunts in France, “de actu et visu.” He can pilot you, on occasion, to vice or virtue with equal assurance. Blest with the eloquence of a hot-water spigot turned on at will, he can check or let run, without floundering, the collection of phrases which he keeps on tap, and which produce upon his victims the effect of a moral shower-bath. Loquacious as a cricket, he smokes, drinks, wears a profusion of trinkets, overawes the common people, passes for a lord in the villages, and never permits himself to be “stumped,”—a slang expression all his own. He knows how to slap his pockets at the right time, and make his money jingle if he thinks the servants of the second-class houses which he wants to enter (always eminently suspicious) are likely to take him for a thief. Activity is not the least surprising quality of this human machine. Not the hawk swooping upon its prey, not the stag doubling before the huntsman and the hounds, nor the hounds themselves catching scent of the game, can be compared with him for the rapidity of his dart when he spies a “commission,” for the agility with which he trips up a rival and gets ahead of him, for the keenness of his scent as he noses a customer and discovers the sport where he can get off his wares.

How many great qualities must such a man possess! You will find in all countries many such diplomats of low degree; consummate negotiators arguing in the interests of calico, jewels, frippery, wines; often displaying more true diplomacy than ambassadors themselves, who, for the most part, know only the forms of it. No one in France can doubt the powers of the commercial traveller; that intrepid soul who dares all, and boldly brings the genius of civilization and the modern inventions of Paris into a struggle with the plain commonsense of remote villages, and the ignorant and boorish treadmill of provincial ways. Can we ever forget the skilful manoeuvres by which he worms himself into the minds of the populace, bringing a volume of words to bear upon the refractory, reminding us of the indefatigable worker in marbles whose file eats slowly into a block of porphyry? Would you seek to know the utmost power of language, or the strongest pressure that a phrase can bring to bear against rebellious lucre, against the miserly proprietor squatting in the recesses of his country lair?—listen to one of these great ambassadors of Parisian industry as he revolves and works and sucks like an intelligent piston of the steam-engine called Speculation.

“Monsieur,” said a wise political economist, the director-cashier-manager and secretary-general of a celebrated fire-insurance company, “out of every five hundred thousand francs of policies to be renewed in the provinces, not more than fifty thousand are paid up voluntarily. The other four hundred and fifty thousand are got in by the activity of our agents, who go about among those who are in arrears and worry them with stories of horrible incendiaries until they are driven to sign the new policies. Thus you see that eloquence, the labial flux, is nine tenths of the ways and means of our business.”

To talk, to make people listen to you—that is seduction in itself. A nation that has two Chambers, a woman who lends both ears, are soon lost. Eve and her serpent are the everlasting myth of an hourly fact which began, and may end, with the world itself.

“A conversation of two hours ought to capture your man,” said a retired lawyer.

Let us walk round the commercial traveller, and look at him well. Don’t forget his overcoat, olive green, nor his cloak with its morocco collar, nor the striped blue cotton shirt. In this queer figure—so original that we cannot rub it out—how many divers personalities we come across! In the first place, what an acrobat, what a circus, what a battery, all in one, is the man himself, his vocation, and his tongue! Intrepid mariner, he plunges in, armed with a few phrases, to catch five or six thousand francs in the frozen seas, in the domain of the red Indians who inhabit the interior of France. The provincial fish will not rise to harpoons and torches; it can only be taken with seines and nets and gentlest persuasions. The traveller’s business is to extract the gold in country caches by a purely intellectual operation, and to extract it pleasantly and without pain. Can you think without a shudder of the flood of phrases which, day by day, renewed each dawn, leaps in cascades the length and breadth of sunny France?

You know the species; let us now take a look at the individual.

There lives in Paris an incomparable commercial traveller, the paragon of his race, a man who possesses in the highest degree all the qualifications necessary to the nature of his success. His speech is vitriol and likewise glue—glue to catch and entangle his victim and make him sticky and easy to grip; vitriol to dissolve hard heads, close fists, and closer calculations. His line was once the hat; but his talents and the art with which he snared the wariest provincial had brought him such commercial celebrity that all vendors of the “article Paris” paid court to him, and humbly begged that he would deign to take their commissions.

Thus, when he returned to Paris in the intervals of his triumphant progress through France, he lived a life of perpetual festivity in the shape of weddings and suppers. When he was in the provinces, the correspondents in the smaller towns made much of him; in Paris, the great houses feted and caressed him. Welcomed, flattered, and fed wherever he went, it came to pass that to breakfast or to dine alone was a novelty, an event. He lived the life of a sovereign, or, better still, of a journalist; in fact, he was the perambulating “feuilleton” of Parisian commerce.

His name was Gaudissart; and his renown, his vogue, the flatteries showered upon him, were such as to win for him the surname of Illustrious. Wherever the fellow went—behind a counter or before a bar, into a salon or to the top of a stage-coach, up to a garret or to dine with a banker—everyone said, the moment they saw him, “Ah! here comes the illustrious Gaudissart!” No name was ever so in keeping with the style, the manners, the countenance, the voice, the language, of any man. All things smiled upon our traveller, and the traveller smiled back in return. “Similia similibus”—he believed in homoeopathy. Puns, horse-laugh, monkish face, skin of a friar, true Rabelaisian exterior, clothing, body, mind, and features, all pulled together to put a devil-may-care jollity into every inch of his person. Free-handed and easy-going, he might be recognized at once as the favourite of grisettes, the man who jumps lightly to the top of a stage-coach, gives a hand to the timid lady who fears to step down, jokes with the postillion about his neckerchief and contrives to sell him a cap, smiles at the maid and catches her round the waist or by the heart; gurgles at dinner like a bottle of wine and pretends to draw the cork by sounding a filip on his distended cheek; plays a tune with his knife on the champagne glasses without breaking them, and says to the company, “Let me see you do _that_”; chaffs the timid traveller, contradicts the knowing one, lords it over a dinner-table and manages to get the titbits for himself. A strong fellow, nevertheless, he can throw aside all this nonsense and mean business when he flings away the stump of his cigar and says, with a glance at some town, “I’ll go and see what those people have got in their stomachs.”

When buckled down to his work he became the slyest and cleverest of diplomats. All things to all men, he knew how to accost a banker like a capitalist, a magistrate like a functionary, a royalist with pious and monarchical sentiments, a bourgeois as one of themselves. In short, wherever he was he was just what he ought to be; he left Gaudissart at the door when he went in, and picked him up when he came out.

Until 1830 the illustrious Gaudissart was faithful to the article Paris. In his close relation to the caprices of humanity, the varied paths of commerce had enabled him to observe the windings of the heart of man. He had learned the secret of persuasive eloquence, the knack of loosening the tightest purse-strings, the art of rousing desire in the souls of husbands, wives, children, and servants; and what is more, he knew how to satisfy it. No one had greater faculty than he for inveigling a merchant by the charms of a bargain, and disappearing at the instant when desire had reached its crisis. Full of gratitude to the hat-making trade, he always declared that it was his efforts in behalf of the exterior of the human head which had enabled him to understand its interior: he had capped and crowned so many people, he was always flinging himself at their heads, etc. His jokes about hats and heads were irrepressible, though perhaps not dazzling.

Nevertheless, after August and October, 1830, he abandoned the hat trade and the article Paris, and tore himself from things mechanical and visible to mount into the higher spheres of Parisian speculation. “He forsook,” to use his own words, “matter for mind; manufactured products for the infinitely purer elaborations of human intelligence.” This requires some explanation.

The general upset of 1830 brought to birth, as everybody knows, a number of old ideas which clever speculators tried to pass off in new bodies. After 1830 ideas became property. A writer, too wise to publish his writings, once remarked that “more ideas are stolen than pocket-handkerchiefs.” Perhaps in course of time we may have an Exchange for thought; in fact, even now ideas, good or bad, have their consols, are bought up, imported, exported, sold, and quoted like stocks. If ideas are not on hand ready for sale, speculators try to pass off words in their stead, and actually live upon them as a bird lives on the seeds of his millet. Pray do not laugh; a word is worth quite as much as an idea in a land where the ticket on a sack is of more importance than the contents. Have we not seen libraries working off the word “picturesque” when literature would have cut the throat of the word “fantastic”? Fiscal genius has guessed the proper tax on intellect; it has accurately estimated the profits of advertising; it has registered a prospectus of the quantity and exact value of the property, weighing its thought at the intellectual Stamp Office in the Rue de la Paix.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Having become an article of commerce, intellect and its products must obey the laws that bind other manufactured interests. Thus ideas, conceived by idle Parisians—who nevertheless fight many a moral battle over champagne and pheasants—are handed at birth from brain to commercial travellers employed to spread them “urbi et orbi,” seasoned with advertisement and prospectus, by which they catch in their rat-trap the departmental rodent called subscriber, sometimes stockholder, occasionally corresponding member or patron, but invariably fool.

“I am a fool!” many a poor country proprietor has said when, caught by the prospect of being the first to launch a new idea, he finds that he has launched his thousand or twelve hundred francs into a gulf.

“Subscribers are fools who never can be brought to understand that to go ahead in the intellectual world they must start with more money than they need for the tour of Europe,” say the speculators.

Consequently there is endless warfare between the recalcitrant public, which refuses to pay Parisian imposts, and the tax-gatherer who, living by his receipt of custom, lards the public with new ideas, turns it on the spit of lively projects, roasts it with prospectuses, basting with flattery, and finally gobbles it up with some toothsome sauce. Moreover, since 1830 what honors and emoluments have been scattered throughout France to stimulate the zeal and self-love of the “progressive and intelligent masses”! Titles, medals, diplomas, a sort of legion of honor for martyrs, have followed each other rapidly. Speculators in the manufactured products of the intellect have developed a spice, a ginger, all their own. From this have come premiums, forestalled dividends, and that conscription of noted names levied without the knowledge of the unfortunate writers who bear them, who thus find themselves associated with more enterprises than there are days in the year; the law takes no account of the theft of a patronymic. Worse is the rape of ideas which these caterers for the public mind, like slave-merchants, tear from the parental brain before they are matured, and drag half-clothed before the eyes of their sultan—the terrible public—which, if they don’t amuse it, will cut off their heads by curtailing the ingots and emptying their pockets.

This madness of our epoch reacted upon the illustrious Gaudissart, and here follows how it happened. A life-insurance company, having been told of his irresistible eloquence, offered him an unheard-of commission, which he accepted. The bargain concluded, our traveller was put in training by the secretary-general, who schooled him in the business: its dark holes, its dialect, the particular public to be gullible, impromptu replies, and unanswerable arguments—sharpening the file of the tongue which was about to operate upon the life of France.

The puppet amply rewarded the pains bestowed upon him. The heads of the company boasted of the illustrious Gaudissart so loudly that the financial managers of two then-prominent newspapers—since defunct—were seized with the idea of employing him to get subscribers. The proprietors of the Globe, an organ of Saint-Simonism, and the Movement, a republican journal, each invited him to a conference and proposed paying ten francs a head for every subscriber if he brought in a thousand, but only five francs if he got five hundred. The cause of political journalism did not interfere with his pre-accepted life-insurance work; the bargain was struck. Gaudissart even demanded indemnity from the Saint-Simonians for eight days spent studying their doctrines, asserting a prodigious effort of memory and intellect was necessary to get to the bottom of that “article,” though he asked nothing from the republicans. He inclined to republican ideas and had already dipped into conspiracies; he had been arrested and released for want of proof; and, as he told the newspaper proprietors, he had lately grown a mustache and needed only a hat of certain shape and a pair of spurs to represent the Republic suitably.

CHAPTER II

For a week this commanding genius went every morning to be Saint-Simonized at the Globe, and every afternoon to the life-insurance company, where he learned financial diplomacy. His aptitude and memory were prodigious; by April 15, the usual opening of the spring campaign, he could set out. Two large commercial houses, alarmed at declining business, begged him not to desert the article Paris and seduced him with large offers. The king of travellers yielded; enormous premiums and old friendships swayed him.

“Listen, my little Jenny,” he said in a hackney-coach to a pretty florist.

All truly great men delight in allowing themselves to be tyrannized over by a feeble being, and Gaudissart had found his tyrant in Jenny. He was bringing her home at eleven from the Gymnase, where he had taken her in full dress to a first-tier box.

“On my return, Jenny, I shall refurnish your room in superior style. That big Matilda, who pesters you with comparisons and her India shawls, won’t have a word to say then. I consecrate to the adornment of your room all the ‘Children’ I shall get in the provinces.”

“That’s a pretty thing to say! Monster of a man! Do you dare to talk to me of your children? Do you suppose I am going to stand that sort of thing?”

“Oh, what a goose you are, my Jenny! That’s only a figure of speech in our business.”

“A fine business, then!”

“Well, but listen; if you talk all the time you’ll always be in the right.”

“I mean to be. You take things easy!”

“You don’t let me finish. I have taken under my protection a superlative idea—a newspaper written for children. In our profession, when travellers have caught, say, ten subscribers to the ‘Children’s Journal,’ they say, ‘I’ve got ten Children,’ just as I say when I get ten subscriptions to the ‘Movement,’ ‘I’ve got ten Movements.’ Now don’t you see?”

“That’s all right. Are you going into politics? If you do you’ll get into Saint-Pélagie, and I shall have to trot down there after you. Oh! if one only knew what one puts one’s foot into when we love a man, by my word of honor we would let you alone to take care of yourselves, you men! However, if you are going away to-morrow we won’t talk of disagreeable things—that would be silly.”

The coach stopped before a pretty fourth-story house in the Rue d’Artois, the abode of Mademoiselle Jenny Courand, reputed privately married to the illustrious Gaudissart, a rumor he did not deny. To maintain her supremacy, Jenny required innumerable attentions and threatened to dismiss him for the slightest omission. She ordered him to write from every town and render a minute account of his proceedings.

“How many ‘Children’ will it take to furnish my chamber?” she asked, throwing off her shawl by a good fire.

“I get five sous for each subscriber.”

“Delightful! And is it with five sous that you expect to make me rich? Perhaps you are like the Wandering Jew with your pockets full of money.”

“But, Jenny, I shall get a thousand ‘Children.’ Think that children have never had a newspaper to themselves before. But what a fool I am to try to explain matters to you—you can’t understand such things.”

“Can’t I? Then tell me—tell me, Gaudissart, if I’m such a goose why do you love me?”

“Just because you are a goose—a sublime goose! Listen, Jenny. I am going to undertake the Globe, the Movement, the Children, the insurance business, and some of my old article Paris; instead of earning a miserable eight thousand a year, I’ll bring back twenty thousand at least from each trip.”

“Unlace me, Gaudissart, and do it right; don’t tighten me.”

“Yes, truly,” said the traveller complacently. “I shall become a shareholder in the newspapers, like Finot, one of my friends, the son of a hatter, who now has thirty thousand francs income, and is going to make himself a peer of France. When one thinks of that little Popinot—ah, mon Dieu! I forgot to tell you that Monsieur Popinot was named minister of commerce yesterday. Why shouldn’t I be ambitious too? Ha! ha! I could easily pick up the jargon of those fellows who talk in the chamber, and bluster with the rest of them. Now, listen to me:—

“Gentlemen,” he said, standing behind a chair, “the Press is neither a tool nor an article of barter: it is, under its political aspects, an institution. We are bound, as legislators, to consider all things politically, and therefore we must examine the Press and ask if it is useful or noxious, if it should be encouraged or taxed or free. These are serious questions. I feel that I do not waste the time of this Chamber by examining this article—the Press—and explaining to you its qualities. We are on the verge of an abyss. Undoubtedly the laws have not the nap they ought to have—Hein?” he said, looking at Jenny. “All orators put France on the verge of an abyss. Don’t I know their dodges? I’m up to all the tricks of all the trades. Do you know why? Because I was born with a caul; my mother has got it, but I’ll give it to you. You’ll see! I shall soon be in the government.”

“You!”

“Why shouldn’t I be the Baron Gaudissart, peer of France? Haven’t they twice elected Monsieur Popinot as deputy? He dines with Louis-Philippe. There’s Finot; he is going to be, they say, a member of the Council. Suppose they send me as ambassador to London? I tell you I’d nonplus those English! No man ever got the better of Gaudissart, and nobody ever will. Yes, I say it! No one ever outwitted me, and no one can—in any walk of life, politics or impolitics. But, for the time being, I must give myself wholly to the capitalists; to the Globe, the Movement, the Children, and my article Paris.”

“You will be brought up with a round turn, you and your newspapers. I’ll bet you won’t get further than Poitiers before the police will nab you.”

“What will you bet?”

“A shawl.”

“Done! If I lose that shawl I’ll go back to the article Paris and the hat business. But as for getting the better of Gaudissart—never! never!”

And the illustrious traveller threw himself into a Napoleonic attitude. Gaudissart was thirty-eight, of medium height, stout and fat like men who roll about in stage-coaches, with a round face, ruddy cheeks, and regular features of the type sculptors adopt as models for Abundance, Law, Force, Commerce. His stomach swelled in a pear shape; he caught Jenny up and kissed her.

“Hold your tongue, young woman!” he said. “What do you know about Saint-Simonism, Fourierism, heroic enterprise, or woman’s freedom? I’ll tell you what they are—ten francs for each subscription, Madame Gaudissart.”

“On my word of honor, you are going crazy, Gaudissart.”

“More and more crazy about you,” he replied, flinging his hat upon the sofa.

The next morning, after a glorious breakfast with Jenny, Gaudissart departed on horseback to work the chief towns of his district, assigned by his various enterprises. After forty-five days beating the country between Paris and Blois, he remained two weeks at Blois to write correspondence and make short visits. The night before leaving Blois for Tours he indited a letter to Mademoiselle Jenny Courand which proves their tie and his triumphs: he boasted of lodging two millions between Paris and Blois, selling many Ternaux shawls at Orléans, lamented that the Movement did not move the republicans, recounted making a hundred Globes, and rejoiced at getting two thousand Children subscriptions. He described cajoling a dynastic diner into subscribing to the Children’s Journal rather than quarrel, and promised to do Amboise in two days and “topple” Tours.

CHAPTER III

Five days later Gaudissart started from the Hotel des Faisans in Tours and went to Vouvray, a rich district where the public mind seemed susceptible. Trotting along the embankment, he admired the landscape, little dreaming his commercial infallibility was about to perish in Vouvray.

A note on the Tourangian mind: stamped by Rabelais’ spirit, Touraine’s people are polished, witty, poetic, voluptuous, yet their impulses subside quickly. The softness of the atmosphere, ease of life, and jovial manners smother sentiment of art and enervate will. At home the Tourangian sits like an Indian on his mat or a Turk on his divan, employing wit in laughing at neighbors and making merry all his days. Touraine is like Rabelais’ Abbaye of Theleme—good cheer reigns.

The illustrious Gaudissart encountered in Vouvray one of those indigenous jesters whose wit is perfection. The Tourangians especially hated and villified Saint-Simonian doctrines, their hatred taking the form of superb disdain and witty maliciousness. For his sins, after arriving and consulting the landlord Mitouflet, Gaudissart betook himself to the town’s merry-maker, a former dyer named Monsieur Vernier, influential among the small proprietors, a leader of gossip and squabbles.

“Monsieur Vernier,” Gaudissart began, “I come to solicit your aid. I am sent into the provinces on an enterprise of the utmost importance, undertaken by bankers who—”

“Who mean to win our tricks,” said Vernier, used to travellers.

“Precisely,” replied Gaudissart. “But with your fine tact, Monsieur, you must be aware we can’t win tricks unless it is their interest to play at cards. I am no longer a common traveller. The most distinguished bankers in Paris take part in this affair; the most respectable houses are concerned, and their interests guarantee—” and Gaudissart drew forth his string of phrases.

Vernier let him go the length of his tether, listening with apparent interest which completely deceived him. But after “guarantee” Vernier ceased attention and plotted to play him a malicious trick: to fetch the town’s supposed banker—Margaritis, a half-insane man—and have Gaudissart deal with him.

At the head of the Valley Coquette lived Margaritis, of Italian origin, half-insane, married, childless, cared for by a devoted wife. He carried two long-bladed knives, sometimes threatened his wife, and insisted on keeping a sack of flour and two puncheons of wine in the cellar. He made incessant demands for newspapers and, for seven years, read the same old Indre-et-Loire without perceiving the repetition. When company came he often sat silent, then at ten o’clock on a certain clock he would rise with mechanical precision and say sternly, “Go away!” He had lucid intervals when he advised on wine sales; at times he became annoying, ransacking closets and stealing delicacies. Once in five years his being told “the good-man does not hear anything” drove him to frenzy, knife flourishing.

The evening before Gaudissart reached Vouvray, Madame Margaritis had found it hard to deceive her husband, who insisted on watching his puncheons; she had begged neighbor Pierre Champlain to roll two puncheons into their cellar to quiet him. Vernier told his wife he would take Benoîte Fontanieu as witness to the scene, to spread the joke.

Vernier escorted Gaudissart, who believed he had made a conquest of both ladies with wit and humor, to La Fuye, the modest house of Margaritis. The salon opened to the field; Margaritis, seated in a yellow Utrecht-velvet arm-chair, did not stir. Madame Vernier introduced the traveller and bade him “put away your mathematics and talk.” Margaritis rose and invited Gaudissart to sit.

“Monsieur has doubtless been in business?” Gaudissart began.

“Public business,” answered Margaritis. “I pacificated Calabria under Murat.”

“In that case we shall quickly understand each other,” said Gaudissart.

Margaritis’s peculiarities—pear-shaped bald head, sunken eyes, thin nose, long chin—gave him a look between a retired professor and a rag-picker. Gaudissart, turning his watch-key with a rotatory click that kept the lunatic quiet, launched into his patter about life insurance. Time, he said, is the most precious social wealth; to economize time is to become wealthy. The bankers and capitalists who had seen the waste of rising men's time had “capitalized” future prospects, cashing talents by discounting time, representing in money the products developed by time—the moral qualities and talents as living forces. He explained with literal examples: suppose a painter has a glorious future; the company will recognize and insure his intellectual capital, paying his family in case of death; the painter pays a small annual premium, three percent, to secure his family and thus free himself of anxieties distracting him from success. Insurance, Gaudissart called it, is the exchequer of beneficence, a mutual insurance against poverty, the cashing of talent.

“That is usury!” cried Margaritis.

Gaudissart switched tack and offered wine; Margaritis poured Vouvray of 1819 and claimed it as Head of Vouvray, a wine for kings. The lunatic delighted in pointing out the etymology: capital, caput—head—the Head of Vouvray. He even offered to sell his puncheons.

Gaudissart returned to his argument: the insured man, once recognized and holding company paper as security, could borrow on it at the Bourse and among bankers; civilization denies bread to the men who give it luxury, he declared, and insurance bridges the gulf. The company also offered annuities on a sliding scale superior to tontines. Margaritis urged another glass; Gaudissart praised the wine.

When Gaudissart turned to the Globe and Saint-Simonian doctrine, Margaritis fell into position and professed comprehension, though answering with local touches: “Twice; on foot.” Gaudissart praised the paper as full of life, science, and as something to make time pass agreeably in the country. It cost eighty francs; Margaritis would not pay but offered seven francs for the Children’s Journal and consented to push the Globe’s interests if Gaudissart took his wine, bargaining humorously over the price. The two men strolled among the vines; the three ladies and Vernier watched, laughing at the spectacle of the urbane traveller and the lunatic discussing, gesticulating, and walking with vehemence.

Finally Gaudissart sat at a card-table and wrote an order for delivery of the two casks. Margaritis read it, counted out seven francs for the Children’s Journal, and gave them to the traveller. Gaudissart, a Norman who required reciprocal agreements, demanded Margaritis sign a bond to deliver the two puncheons. Margaritis did. Gaudissart left humming triumphantly.

CHAPTER V

At the Soleil d’Or Gaudissart conversed with landlord Mitouflet, an old soldier, about Margaritis. The host, who never laughed at a jest but took it with a soldier’s gravity, hinted that Margaritis was a lunatic.

“May be; the good-man knows a deal. He knows too much for other folks,” said Mitouflet.

Gaudissart, enraged when Vernier’s prank was revealed, marched to the dyer’s salon where Vernier was laughing with friends. “Monsieur,” he began, “you are a scoundrel and a blackguard; under pain of being thought a turn-key—you will give me satisfaction for the insult you dared to offer me in sending me to a lunatic! Do you hear me, Monsieur Vernier, dyer?”

Vernier, delighted at the audience, replied that it was right to make fun of a man who wanted them to feather his nest by subscribing to a paper preaching a new religion that denied inheritance. “Père Margaritis said things a great deal more sensible,” he added.

Gaudissart flew at Vernier to slap him but the crowd interposed; his wig fell on Mademoiselle Clara Vernier’s head. He declared he would be at the Soleil d’Or until morning, ready to show what giving satisfaction meant; he had “fought in July.” Vernier answered he would “fight in Vouvray,” and make the traveller stay longer than he imagined.

For the first time the prince of travellers did not dine jovially. The town was in a ferment over the duel. Madame Fontanieu and the assistant-mayor implored Mitouflet to arrange things to avoid a violent death. He took it upon himself. In the evening he brought pens and paper to Gaudissart.

“If you are going to fight to-morrow,” Mitouflet said, “you had better make some settlement of your affairs; perhaps you have letters to write. Are you a good swordsman? I have foils.” They put on foils and masks; Mitouflet’s experience won him sixty-two passes. He advised pistols, suggesting they be loaded to fire wide so both could retire with honor. Gaudissart grumbled about possibly killing the man; Mitouflet calmed him.

Next morning the adversaries met by the bridge of La Cise. Vernier nearly shot a cow; they embraced after firing in the air. “Your joke was rough, but it was a good one,” said Gaudissart; Vernier announced twenty subscriptions to the Children’s Journal. “Why shouldn’t we all breakfast together?” said Gaudissart. Men who fight are often those who come to good understanding.

Back at the inn Gaudissart wanted to send a summons to Margaritis for delivery of the two casks. “But he has not got them,” said Vernier. Gaudissart demanded indemnity and considered himself outwitted if he failed to pursue the claim. Madame Margaritis, alarmed, brought thirty francs to the traveller; Gaudissart accepted it and left Vouvray content with the settlement, though recognizing the region’s resistance to new ideas.

On returning from his trip through the southern departments, Gaudissart rode in a diligence and explained the enigmas of life to a young man as if to an infant. Passing Vouvray the young man exclaimed at the fine site; Gaudissart, patronizing, replied, “Yes, Monsieur, but not habitable on account of the people. You get into duels every day. Why, it is not three months since I fought one just there,” pointing to the bridge, “with a damned dyer; but I made an end of him—he bit the dust!”
