A zigzagging stove pipe, a shelf with plaster models—Niobe, Venus, hands, écorchés yellowed by smoke—pictures, frames without paintings, and paintings without frames gave it the typical studio jumble of poverty and richness, care and neglect. The atelier, with its remnants of ancient costumes and armor, had something mysteriously grand: genius and death, Diana beside a skull—beauty and destruction, pseudo and reality—colors glowing in shadow. It was the symbol of an artist’s mind.

One bright July day, two sunbeams traced gold lines across the dusty studio. A dozen easels rose like masts; several young girls animated the scene with varied expressions, attitudes, and dresses. Strong shadows from the green serge curtains arranged for each easel produced piquant chiaroscuro. The group of girls was the prettiest picture in the room.

A fair young girl, simply dressed, sat somewhat apart, working with evident courage and an air of dread; she was the prettiest, most modest, and apparently the least rich. The pupils formed two distinct groups or cliques—two minds—contrasting social origins even where rank might seem forgotten.

One group, daughters of bankers, notaries, and merchants, was lively, rich, and aware of the aristocrats’ cutting slights. The other was a patrician circle, led by Mademoiselle Amélie Thirion, daughter of one of the King’s ushers: vain, silly, proud of court connections, always late and elaborately dressed. In the banking group, girls were spirited and talkative; in the aristocratic circle, the girls had elegant manners but little simplicity, their faces polite rather than candid. Yet among them all, childlike heads sometimes appeared—pure faces with half-open lips and innocent smiles—when the class assembled like a group of angels on a cloud.

On this day Servin had not appeared—he had been mostly in another studio finishing a picture for the Exposition. Suddenly Amélie Thirion spoke in a low urgent tone to her neighbor. A great silence fell; both coteries fell still, curious. Amélie rose, took an easel near Ginevra Piombo, carried it away from the noble group, and placed it close to a partition that separated the studio from the attic’s extreme end, where broken casts and the winter wood were kept. She rolled aside stool, colors, even the Prudhon copy Ginevra had been working on. The patrician move produced murmurs: Amélie’s coup d’état had the air of an insult.

The commercial party chattered: “What will Mademoiselle Piombo say?” “She’s not one to complain, but she’ll remember it.” “They ought not to add to Ginevra’s sorrow; her father has resigned.” Matilde Roguin of the bankers advised modesty—Ginevra’s character was unpredictable. The girls debated vengeance and consequence.

Footsteps on the narrow stair announced someone. Absolute silence. To grasp Amélie’s ostracism, one must recall the date: late July 1815. The second Bourbon Restoration had split friendships again; families and children shared monarchical fever. Ginevra Piombo loved Napoleon passionately; her father, Baron di Piombo, had devotedly aided the return from Elba and suffered for it. The aristocratic studio girls were ardent royalists; their hatred for Bonapartists was intense. Ginevra, one of Servin’s earliest pupils, had long occupied the spot Amélie displaced; the move was both insult and artistic pain—artists prize certain places where they work. Moreover, jealousy fueled the Right clique: Ginevra, admired by Servin, excelled and obtained an ascendency like Bonaparte’s among soldiers.

On Ginevra’s entrance she was met with profound silence. Of all the pupils she was the handsomest, tallest, and best formed; her carriage had noble grace. Her face radiated intelligence and Corsican enthusiasm tempered by calm, with long hair, black eyes and lashes, and a certain proud forehead that betrayed Corsican moral instincts. Her simplicity and Lombard beauty made her captivating; her only flaw was that her mature beauty made her look older than the rest. She had refused marriage to care for her parents, and painting substituted for passions and distractions.

“You are very silent today,” she said, greeting Laure, the young girl painting apart, with a gentle praise. Laure and Ginevra exchanged affection. Ginevra moved to her easel and, seemingly absorbed, adjusted her sleeves and palette. The banking group watched; the Thirion camp shot sidelong glances. Matilde noted Ginevra’s indifference and wondered if she’d noticed the insult.

At that moment Ginevra abruptly walked to the partition and painfully raised the green serge shade. Through a slight opening she peered into the adjacent space—a closet lighted by a skylight—and what she saw staggered her. In the little bed beyond lay the figure of an officer of the Imperial Guard; the Imperial eagle was visible. Servin was hiding a proscribed man. She recognized the regular breathing of a sleeping man—she had heard it the night before—and now felt the huge responsibility of guarding him. She resolved to remain in that spot to prevent any betrayal: “Better that I should be here to prevent some luckless accident,” she thought.

Her seeming indifference to Amélie’s insult was thus explained: she was preoccupied with discovering who lay hidden beyond the partition. The girls, puzzled by her silence, interpreted it variously—some applauded her supposed grandeur of soul. The banking circle, inspired, contrived to humiliate the aristocrats with biting sarcasm until Madame Servin’s arrival broke the contest.

Madame Servin entered, and with a look at the door where Ginevra sat, and obvious uneasiness she announced, “Monsieur Servin cannot come today.” She complimented the girls and reached Ginevra with concealed anxiety. The soldier’s breathing could be heard; Madame Servin pretended not to notice—deliberate deafness that Ginevra perceived. The hidden man shifted on his pallet.

Ginevra then began to hum a Corsican canzonetta softly to cover the sound; her singing, so out of character, surprised everyone and later became evidence to some of jealousy. Madame Servin left; the session ended without incident. Ginevra let the others depart but gave signs of wanting to be left alone, casting impatient glances as pupils lingered.

Amélie, cruel toward the girl who excelled her, suspected Ginevra’s preoccupation concealed a purpose. She returned stealthily to the studio after fetching a misplaced bag, found Ginevra once more on her precarious perch near the partition and coughed to attract attention. Ginevra started, blushed, pretended the change in shade required her position, and descended. She left, retaining the image of a man’s head like Girodet’s Endymion.

For two days Ginevra thought: “Who can he be? He is not Marshal Ney—” On the third day, despite haste, she found Amélie already there. They watched one another long and coldly. Amélie had glimpsed a handsome head through the crevice but not the uniform. Suddenly Servin arrived early.

“Mademoiselle Ginevra,” he said after a glance, “why have you placed yourself there? The light is bad. Come nearer and pull down that curtain a little.” He sat near Laure and began his usual round of scolding, flattering, and jesting. Yet Ginevra did not obey; she stayed by the partition and sketched, in sepia, the hidden man’s head. A work done under emotion bore a distinct stamp—her passion lent her an intuition that, in that instant, seemed supernatural. She worked amid a tremor she mistook for fear, glancing furtively to hide the sketch.

But she failed to see Amélie’s eyeglass trained full upon the drawing in her large portfolio. Recognizing the portrait of the mysterious man, Amélie darted forward; Ginevra hastily thrust the sheet into her portfolio. The prisoner stirred—perhaps awakened by the noise—Ginevra knocked over her stool, laughed incoherently, and by a practiced trick she threw the portrait into her portfolio before Amélie could seize it. Surrounding the easel, Servin praised the copy she was making; all were fooled except Amélie, who slipped behind companions to open the portfolio where she had seen the sketch. Ginevra took it up and placed it before her; they stared silently at one another.

“Come, mesdemoiselles, take your places,” said Servin. “If you wish to do as well as Mademoiselle di Piombo, you mustn’t be always talking fashions and balls.”

When they were seated, Servin sat beside Ginevra.

“Was it not better that I should be the one to discover the mystery rather than the others?” she whispered.

“Yes,” replied Servin, “you are one of us, a patriot; but even if you were not, I should have confided the matter to you.”

They understood one another, and Ginevra no longer hesitated to ask, “Who is he?”

“An intimate friend of Labédoyère,” Servin answered—“one who helped unite the 7th regiment with the grenadiers of Elba. He was a major in the Imperial Guard and was at Waterloo.”

“Why not have burned his uniform and shako and given him citizen’s clothes?” she asked.

“He will have them to-night,” said Servin. “My father-in-law rescued him from Labédoyère’s captors and brought him here by night a week ago. I hoped to keep him out of sight in this corner, the only safe spot.”

“If I can be useful, employ me,” offered Ginevra. “I know the Maréchal de Fétre.”

“We’ll see,” Servin answered.

Their conversation had lasted too long to escape the other girls. Servin then moved around again, giving such long lessons that he stayed past the usual hour. He called after Amélie—who had been spying—reminding her to get her bag. The girl returned feigning surprise; Servin’s remark deepened her conviction of some mystery. She noisily went down the stair, slammed the Servins’ door to seem gone, then softly returned and stationed herself outside the studio door, resolved to spy further.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
When the painter and Ginevra thought themselves alone, Servin rapped oddly on the garret door, which opened on its rusty hinges. Ginevra saw a tall, well-made young man in an Imperial uniform; one arm was in a sling and his pallor betrayed suffering. He recoiled at the sight of an unknown woman.

Amelie, unable to look into the room with the door closed and afraid to remain, slipped away silently after hearing the garret open.

“Fear nothing,” said the painter to the officer. “Mademoiselle is the daughter of a faithful friend of the Emperor, the Baron di Piombo.”

At once the soldier had no doubt of Ginevra’s patriotism.

“You are wounded,” she said.

“Oh! it is nothing, mademoiselle,” he replied; “the wound is healing.”

From the street came the cry of the vendors: “Condemned to death!” They all shuddered, and the soldier heard a name that turned him pale.

“Labedoyere!” he cried, falling on a stool.

They sat silently. Drops stood on the young man’s livid forehead; he seized his hair in despair and rested his elbow on Ginevra’s easel.

“After all,” he said, rising abruptly, “Labedoyere and I knew what we were doing. We were certain of the fate awaiting us, whether triumph or defeat. He dies for the Cause, and here am I hiding myself!”

He rushed to the studio door; Ginevra reached it first and barred his way.

“Can you restore the Emperor?” she said. “Do you expect to raise that giant who could not maintain himself?”

“But what can I do?” the young man asked the two friends fate had sent him. “I have no relation in the world. Labedoyere was my protector and friend; without him I am alone. Tomorrow I myself may be condemned; my only fortune was my pay. I spent my last penny to come here and try to snatch Labedoyere from his fate; death is, therefore, a necessity for me. When a man decides to die he ought to know how to sell his life to the executioner. I was thinking just now that the life of an honest man is worth that of two traitors, and the blow of a dagger well placed may give immortality.”

This outburst alarmed Servin and even Ginevra, who understood the young man’s nature. She admired his handsome face and sweet voice, which did not lose its fervor in anger. Then she tried to soothe him.

“Monsieur,” she said, “as for your pecuniary distress, permit me to offer my savings. My father is rich; I am his only child; he loves me, and I am sure he will never blame me. Have no scruple in accepting my offer; our property comes from the Emperor; we do not own a penny that is not the result of his munificence. Is it not gratitude to assist his faithful soldiers? Take what you need as indifferently as I offer it. It is only money!” she added with contempt. “Now, as for friends—those you shall have.”

She raised her head proudly and her eyes shone.

“The head which falls tomorrow before a dozen muskets will save yours,” she went on. “Wait till the storm is over; you can then escape and take service in foreign countries if you are not forgotten here; or in the French army, if you are.”

There is always a delicacy in consolation from a woman, something maternal, foreseeing, and complete. When words of hope and peace are spoken with graceful gesture by a beautiful benefactress, a young man does not resist. The prisoner breathed in love through all his senses. A rosy tinge colored his white cheeks; his eyes lost some of their dulled sadness and he said, in a peculiar tone:—

“You are an angel of goodness—But Labedoyere!” he added. “Oh, Labedoyere!”

At that cry they looked at one another in silence, each understanding the others’ thought. They were no longer mere acquaintances: in those moments the links of friendship knit them as if for years.

“Dear friend,” said Servin, “can you save him?”

“I can avenge him.”

Ginevra quivered. Though the stranger was handsome, what touched her more was the pity born of a woman’s heart for suffering that is not ignoble; but a cry of vengeance, a proscribed soul devoted to Napoleon, Corsican generosity—this stirred her deeply. She looked at the officer with respectful emotion that shook his heart. For the first time a man had produced a keen sensation in her. At once her sympathy deepened into something more: she put the stranger’s soul on a par with his noble beauty and the proportions she admired as an artist. Led by curiosity to pity, from pity to powerful interest, she felt such profound sensations she was uneasy at staying.

“Until tomorrow, then,” she said, giving him a gentle parting smile.

Seeing that smile illuminated her features anew; the stranger forgot all else for a moment.

“Tomorrow,” he said sadly; “but tomorrow, Labedoyere—”

Ginevra put a finger to her lips and looked at him, as if saying: “Be calm, be prudent.”

The young man cried in his own language: “Ah! Dio! che non vorrei vivere dopo averla veduta?—who would not wish to live after seeing her?”

The peculiar accent made Ginevra quiver.

“Are you Corsican?” she cried, returning toward him with a beating heart.

“I was born in Corsica,” he replied; “but I was brought, while very young, to Genoa, and as soon as old enough for military service I enlisted.”

The young man’s beauty, his attachment to the Emperor, his wound and misfortunes—all blended into a new, delightful sentiment in Ginevra. This persecuted man was a child of Corsica; he spoke its cherished language. She stood motionless, held by a magical sensation; before her eyes was a living portrait to which every feeling lent vivid color. At Servin’s invitation the officer sat on a divan; the painter, after removing the sling, undid the bandages to dress the wound. Ginevra shuddered at a long, broad sabre gash across his forearm and a moan escaped her. The stranger raised his head and smiled at her. Servin lifted the lint and touched the lacerated flesh with delicate care; the wounded man’s pale, sickly face expressed, as he looked at the girl, more pleasure than suffering. An artist might have admired the contrasts: white linen and bared arm against the red and blue uniform.

A soft half-light pervaded the studio; a parting evening ray lit where the soldier sat, bathing his blanched face, black hair, and clothes. To Ginevra’s Italian imagination it was an omen. He seemed a celestial messenger, speaking her language and unconsciously conjuring childhood memories; in her heart dawned a feeling as fresh and pure as her innocence. For a short moment she was motionless and dreamy, then blushed, exchanged a soft glance with the wounded man, and fled with his vision before her eyes.

The next day, though not a class day, Ginevra came to the studio and the prisoner sat beside her easel. Servin, with a sketch to finish, played mentor as the two talked chiefly in Corsican. The soldier related the sufferings of the retreat from Moscow; at nineteen he had made the passage of the Beresins, and was almost the last of his regiment. He described with fiery words the disaster of Waterloo. His voice was music to the Italian girl. Brought up as a Corsican, Ginevra was, in some sense, a child of Nature; falseness was unknown to her; she gave herself up without reserve to her impressions, showing them without the coquetry of Parisian girls. That day she often sat with palette and brushes in her hands without touching a color, eyes fixed on the officer, lips slightly parted, listening as if painting a stroke never painted. She was not surprised at the softness in his eyes; she felt her own despite a resolve to remain stern. After pauses she painted diligently without raising her head, because he sat near and watched. The first time he sat by her in silence she said, after a long pause:—

“Does it amuse you to see me paint?”

That day she learned his name was Luigi. They agreed that on class days, when they could not see each other, any important political event would be signaled by Ginevra singing certain Corsican melodies.

The following day Mademoiselle Thirion informed the class, under pledge of secrecy, that Ginevra di Piombo had a lover, a young man who came during lesson hours and concealed himself in the garret.

“You, who take her part,” she said to Mademoiselle Roguin, “watch her carefully, and you will see how she spends her time.”

Ginevra was observed with malicious attention. They listened to her songs and watched her glances. At times, when she supposed herself unseen, a dozen pairs of eyes were furtively upon her. Enlightened thus, the girls interpreted truly the emotions crossing the beautiful Italian’s features—her gestures, the tones in which she hummed, and the attention with which she listened to sounds only she could hear through the partition.

By the end of a week, Laure was the only one of Servin’s fifteen pupils who had resisted peeking at Luigi through the crevice; she still defended her friend. Mademoiselle Roguin tried to make her wait on the staircase after class to prove the intimacy by entering the studio and surprising them, but Laure refused to condescend to such espionage, and thus became the object of reprobation.

Soon Mademoiselle Thirion declared it improper to attend classes with a painter whose opinions were tainted with patriotism and Bonapartism (then synonymous), and she ceased to attend. Though she forgot Ginevra, the harm she planted bore fruit. Little by little the other young girls told their mothers of the strange events at the studio. One day Matilde Roguin did not come; the next another was missing, and so on, until the last three or four left. Ginevra and Laure were the sole occupants of the deserted studio for several days.

Ginevra did not notice the falling off nor ask the cause. As soon as she had invented means of communicating with Luigi she lived in the studio in a delightful solitude, thinking only of the officer and the dangers that threatened him. Though a sincere admirer of noble characters who never betray their political faiths, she urged Luigi to recognize royal authority to be released and remain in France. He refused. If passions are born and nourished under romantic causes, never were so many such circumstances conjoined to unite two young souls. Their friendship progressed in a month more than a society friendship would in ten years. Adversity tested and revealed character. Ginevra studied Luigi and soon esteemed him. Older than Luigi, she found charm in being courted by a youth so grand, so tried by fate—a youth who combined manly experience with adolescent graces. Luigi felt unspeakable pleasure in being apparently protected by a woman of twenty-five. Was it not proof of love? The union of gentleness and pride, strength and tenderness in Ginevra was irresistible, and he was subjugated. In short, they loved each other so profoundly there was no need to deny or proclaim it.

One evening Ginevra heard the accustomed signal. Luigi scratched with a pin on the woodwork, making no more noise than a spider fastening its thread. The signal meant he wished to come out.

Ginevra glanced around, and not seeing Laure, opened the door; but Luigi caught sight of the pupil and abruptly retired. Surprised, Ginevra saw Laure and said as she went to her easel:—

“You are staying late, my dear. That head seems finished; you only want a highlight—see! on that knot of hair.”

“You would do me a great kindness,” said Laure in a trembling voice, “if you would give this copy a few touches; then I could carry away something to remind me of you.”

“Willingly,” said Ginevra, painting a few strokes. “But I thought it was a long way from your home, and it is late.”

“Oh! Ginevra, I am going away, never to return,” cried the poor girl sadly.

“You mean to leave Monsieur Servin!” exclaimed Ginevra, less affected than she might have been a month earlier.

“Haven’t you noticed, Ginevra, that for some days past you and I have been alone in the studio?”

“True,” said Ginevra, as if struck by recollection. “Are all those young ladies ill, or going to be married, or are their fathers on duty at court?”

“They have left Monsieur Servin,” replied Laure.

“Why?”

“On your account, Ginevra.”

“My account!” repeated the Corsican, springing up, brow threatening and eyes flashing.

“Oh! don’t be angry, my kind Ginevra,” cried Laure in distress. “My mother insists on my leaving. The young ladies say you have some intrigue, and that Monsieur Servin allows the young man you love to stay in the dark attic. I never believed these calumnies nor told my mother. But last night Madame Roguin met her at a ball and asked if she still sent me here. When my mother answered yes, Madame Roguin told her the falsehoods. Mamma scolded me severely; she said I must have known all, and that I failed in proper confidence between mother and daughter. Oh! my dear Ginevra! I, who took you for my model, how grieved I am that I can’t be your companion.”

“We shall meet again in life; girls marry—” said Ginevra.

“When they are rich,” sighed Laure.

“Come and see me; my father has a fortune—”

“Ginevra,” Laure added tenderly, “Madame Roguin and Laure’s mother are coming to see Monsieur Servin tomorrow to reproach him; hadn’t you better warn him?”

A thunderbolt could not have surprised Ginevra more.

“What matter is it to them?” she asked naively.

“Everybody thinks it very wrong. Mamma says it is immoral.”

“And you, Laure, what do you say?”

Laure looked up and their thoughts joined. She could no longer hold back tears; she flung herself on Ginevra’s breast and sobbed. At this moment Servin entered.

“Mademoiselle Ginevra,” he cried with enthusiasm, “I have finished my picture! It is now being varnished. What have you been doing meanwhile? Where are the young ladies; are they on holiday or in the country?”

Laure dried her tears, bowed to Monsieur Servin, and went away.

“The studio has been deserted for some days,” replied Ginevra, “and the young ladies are not coming back.”

“Pooh!” said the artist.

“Oh! don’t laugh,” said Ginevra. “Listen: I am the involuntary cause of the loss of your reputation—”

The artist smiled and interrupted:—

“My reputation? Why, in a few days my picture will make it at the Exposition.”

“That relates to your talent,” replied the girl. “I speak of your morality. Those young ladies have told their mothers that Luigi was shut up here, and that you lent yourself— to—our love.”

“There is some truth in that, mademoiselle,” replied the professor. “The mothers of those young ladies are foolish; had they come to me I should have explained. But I don’t care a straw! Life is short.”

And the painter snapped his fingers above his head. Luigi, who had heard part of the conversation, came in.

“You have lost all your scholars,” he cried. “I have ruined you!”

The artist took Luigi’s hand and Ginevra’s and joined them.

“Marry one another, my children,” he said with fatherly kindness.

They dropped their eyes; their silence was the first avowal of love.

“You will surely be happy,” said Servin. “There is nothing to equal the happiness of two beings like yourselves when bound together in love.”

Luigi pressed his protector’s hand and presently, with emotion, said:—

“To you I owe it all.”

“Be happy! I bless and wed you,” said the painter with comic unction, laying his hands on the lovers’ heads.

This jest ended the strained feeling. All three looked at one another and laughed. Ginevra pressed Luigi’s hand with the simple ardor of her native habits.

“Ah ca, my dear children,” resumed Servin, “you think all will go right now, but you are much mistaken.”

The lovers looked astonished.

“Don’t be anxious. I’m the only one your romance will harm. But Madame Servin is a little straitlaced; I don’t see how to settle it with her.”

“Heavens! and I forgot to tell you,” exclaimed Ginevra, “that Madame Roguin and Laure’s mother are coming here tomorrow to—”

“I understand,” said the painter.

“But you can easily justify yourself,” continued the girl, proudly. “Monsieur Luigi,” she added to him with an arch look, “will no longer object to entering the royal service. Well then,” after his smile, “tomorrow morning I will send a petition to a most influential man at the War ministry—a man who will refuse nothing to the daughter of the Baron di Piombo. We shall obtain a ‘tacit’ pardon for Captain Luigi, for, of course, they will not allow him the rank of major. And then,” she added to Servin, “you can confound the mothers of my charitable companions by telling them the truth.”

“You are an angel!” cried Servin.

While this passed at the studio, Ginevra’s parents grew anxious at her delay.

“It is six o’clock, and Ginevra not yet home!” cried Bartolomeo.

“She was never so late before,” said his wife.

The two old people looked at one another with unusual anxiety. Too restless to remain, Bartolomeo paced the salon with an active step for a man over seventy-seven. Thanks to a robust constitution he had changed little since arriving in Paris; despite his tall figure he walked erect. His white sparse hair left uncovered a broad protuberant skull suggesting character and firmness. His face, seamed with deep wrinkles, had taken with age a nobler expression, preserving pallid tones that inspire veneration. The ardor of his passions lived in the fire of his eyes; his still-not-wholly-white eyebrows retained mobility. His head’s aspect was stern but conveyed that Piombo had a right to be so. His kindness and gentleness were known only to his wife and daughter. In functions, or before strangers, he never laid aside the majesty time had impressed; the habit of frowning with heavy eyebrows and giving his eyes a Napoleonic fixity made his manner icy.

During his political life he had been so generally feared he seemed unsocial. The life, morals, and fidelity of Piombo made him obnoxious to courtiers. Despite delicate missions entrusted to him which would have been lucrative to others, he possessed an income of not more than thirty thousand francs from an investment in the Grand Livre. Considering cheap government securities under the Empire and Napoleon’s liberality toward faithful servants, the Baron di Piombo must have been a man of stern integrity. He owed his barony to the Emperor’s need to give him a title before sending him on missions to foreign courts.

Bartolomeo always professed hatred for the traitors Napoleon surrounded himself with. It is told that he took three steps to the door of the Emperor’s cabinet after advising him to get rid of three men in France on the eve of the 1814 campaign. After the second Bourbon return Bartolomeo ceased wearing the Legion of Honor. No man better represented those old Republicans, incorruptible friends to the Empire, relics of two energetic governments. Though he displeased courtiers, he had Darus, Drouots, and Carnots as friends. As for other politicians, he cared not a whiff of his cigar’s smoke for them, especially since Waterloo.

Bartolomeo had bought, for the moderate sum Madame Mère had paid him for his Corsican estates, the old Portenduere mansion, in which he made no changes. Lodged usually at government expense, he did not occupy it until after Fontainebleau. Following simple habits, the baron and his wife gave no heed to outward splendor; their furniture was what they bought with the mansion. The grand apartments, lofty, sombre, and bare, the wide mirrors in blackened gilded frames, and Louis XIV–period furniture suited Bartolomeo and his wife, personages worthy of antiquity.

Under the Empire and during the Hundred Days, while exercising well-rewarded functions, the old Corsican maintained a great establishment, more to honor his office than to shine himself. His and his wife’s lives were so frugal and tranquil that their modest fortune sufficed. To them their daughter Ginevra was more precious than all wealth. When in May, 1814, the Baron di Piombo resigned, dismissed his servants, and closed his stable, Ginevra, quiet and unpretending like her parents, saw nothing to regret. Like all great souls, she found luxury in strength of feeling and happiness in quiet and work. These three loved one another too well for externals to matter.

Often, especially after Napoleon’s second fall, Bartolomeo and his wife passed delightful evenings with their daughter, listening while she sang and played. They took vast secret pleasure in her slightest word; their eyes followed her with tender anxiety; they heard her step in the court-yard. Like lovers, the three often sat silently together, understanding better than by speech the eloquence of their souls. This profound sentiment animated their every thought. If memories of Napoleon’s benefits or public events distracted them, they could always return to this source without affecting their community of thought, for Ginevra shared their political passions. What more natural than the ardor with which they found refuge in their only child?

Until now public life had absorbed Bartolomeo’s energy; after leaving office he felt the need to cast it into the last sentiment remaining. Apart from parentage, there may have been another reason for their intense reciprocal love: it was undivided love. Ginevra’s whole heart belonged to her father, as his belonged to her; and if we are bound by defects more than virtues, Ginevra echoed her father’s youthful passions. This was the sole imperfection of their triple life. Ginevra was born unyielding, vindictive, and passionate, like her father.

The Corsican delighted in developing these savage sentiments in his daughter, as a lion teaches lion-cubs to spring at prey. But this apprenticeship to vengeance having no outlet in family life, the child turned the principle against her father; as a child she forgave him nothing, and he yielded. Piombo saw nothing more than childish nonsense in these quarrels, but Ginevra acquired a habit of ruling her parents. Amid the tempests he loved to excite, a look or word of tenderness sufficed to pacify them, and often they were nearest a kiss when threatening vehemently.

Nevertheless, for five years Ginevra, grown wiser, avoided such scenes. Her faithfulness, devotion, love filling her every thought, and admirable good sense overcame her temper. Yet a great evil resulted from her training; she lived with her parents on an equality always dangerous.

Piombo and his wife, without education, allowed Ginevra to study as she pleased. Following youthful caprices she studied many things for a time, abandoning each train of thought until painting proved her dominant passion. Ginevra would have made a noble woman had her mother guided her studies and harmonized her gifts; her defects came from the fatal education the old Corsican delighted in giving her.

After pacing and ringing the bell, a servant entered.

“Go and meet Mademoiselle Ginevra,” said his master.

“I always regret our carriage on her account,” remarked the baroness.

“She said she did not want one,” replied Piombo, looking at his wife, who, used to obedience, lowered her eyes and said no more.

Already a septuagenarian, tall, withered, pale, and wrinkled, the baroness resembled those old women Schnetz paints in Italian genre scenes. She was habitually silent, sometimes seeming another Mrs. Shandy; occasionally a word or look betrayed that her feelings retained youthful vigor. Her dress, devoid of coquetry, was often in bad taste. She usually sat passive, buried in a low sofa like a Sultana Valide, awaiting or admiring her Ginevra. All was well with her if Ginevra was happy. Her hair was white, and a few strands only showed above her wrinkled forehead and hollow cheeks.

“It is now fifteen days,” she said, “since Ginevra made a practice of being late.”

“Jean is so slow!” cried the impatient old man, buttoning his blue coat and seizing his hat, which he dashed on as he took his cane and departed.

“You will not get far,” said his wife, calling after him.

As she spoke the porte-cochere opened and shut, and the old mother heard Ginevra’s steps in the court-yard. Bartolomeo almost instantly reappeared, carrying his daughter, who struggled in his arms.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
“Here she is, my Ginevra, Ginevrettina, Ginevrola, mia Ginevra bella!” cried the old man.

“Oh, father, you hurt me!”

Ginevra was at once set down respectfully. She nodded to her mother, who had been frightened by the cry, as if to say, “Don’t be alarmed; it was only a trick to get away.”

The pale, wan face of the baroness regained color and even gaiety. Piombo rubbed his hands violently—his token of joy; when his facial muscles relaxed, every wrinkle on his forehead became kindly. The two old people looked like a drooping plant refreshed by a little water.

“Now, to dinner! to dinner!” cried the baron, offering his large hand to his daughter, whom he called “Signora Piombellina”—another sign of good humor, to which Ginevra replied with a smile.

“Ah ca!” said Piombo as they left the table. “Your mother tells me you have stayed at the studio much longer than usual. It seems painting is more to you than your parents—”

“Oh, father!”

“Ginevra is preparing some surprise for us, I think,” said the mother.

“A picture of your own! will you bring us that?” cried the Corsican, clapping his hands.

“Yes, I am very much occupied at the studio,” replied Ginevra slowly.

“What is the matter, Ginevra? You are turning pale!” cried her mother.

“No!” exclaimed the girl with resolution, “no! it shall never be said that Ginevra Piombo acted a lie.”

Piombo and his wife looked at her in astonishment.

“I love a young man,” she added, her voice full of feeling, lowering her eyelids as if to veil the fire of her eyes.

“Is he a prince?” asked her father ironically.

“No, father,” she said gently, “he is a young man without fortune.”

“Is he very handsome?”

“He is very unfortunate.”

“What is he?”

“Labedoyere’s comrade; he was proscribed, without refuge; Servin concealed him, and—”

“Servin is a good fellow,” cried Piombo, “but you, my daughter, you do wrong to love any man except your father.”

“It does not depend on me to love, or not to love,” replied Ginevra.

“I flattered myself,” continued her father, “that my Ginevra would be faithful to me until I died; and that my love and that of her mother would suffice her till then; I did not expect our tenderness would find a rival in her soul, and—”

“Did I ever reproach you for your fanaticism for Napoleon?” said Ginevra. “Have you never loved any one but me? Did you not leave me for months when you went on missions? I bore your absence courageously. Life has necessities to which we must all submit.”

“Ginevra!”

“No, you don’t love me for myself; your reproaches betray intolerable egotism.”

“You dare blame your father’s love!” exclaimed Piombo, eyes flashing.

“Father, I don’t blame you,” replied Ginevra, gentler than her trembling mother expected. “You have grounds for your egotism, as I have for my love. Heaven is my witness that no girl has fulfilled her duty to her parents better than I. I have never felt anything but love and happiness where others see obligation. It is now fifteen years that I have never left your wing, and it has been a dear pleasure to charm your life. But is it ingratitude to desire a husband who will protect me hereafter?”

“What! do you reckon benefits with your father, Ginevra?” said Piombo in a dangerous tone.

A dreadful pause followed. Bartolomeo at last broke the silence in a heart-rending tone:—

“Oh! stay with us! stay with your father, your old father! I cannot have you love another man. Ginevra, you will not have long to await your liberty.”

“But, father, remember I need not leave you; we shall be two to love you; you will learn the man to whose care you bequeath me. You will be doubly cherished—by him who is my other self, by me who am all his.”

“Oh! Ginevra, Ginevra!” cried the Corsican, clenching his fists; “why did you not marry when Napoleon brought me to accept the idea? Why did you not take the counts and dukes he presented?”

“They loved me to order,” she said. “Besides, they would have made me live with them, and I did not wish to leave you alone.”

“You don’t wish to leave me alone,” said Piombo, “and yet you marry!—that is leaving me alone. I know you, my daughter; in that case, you would cease to love us. Elisa,” he added, looking at his wife who was motionless as if stupefied, “we have no longer a daughter; she wishes to marry.”

The old man sat down, raised his hands to heaven as if invoking Divine power; then bowed himself over as if weighed down with sorrow.

Ginevra saw his agitation, and the restraint with which he checked his anger touched her to the heart; she had expected a violent crisis; she had not armed her soul against paternal gentleness.

“Father,” she said tenderly, “no, you shall never be abandoned by your Ginevra. But love her a little for her own sake. If you knew how he loves me! Ah! He would never make me unhappy!”

“Comparisons already!” cried Piombo, terrible. “No, I can never endure the idea of your marriage. If he loved you as you deserve he would kill me; if he did not love you, I should put a dagger through him.”

His hands and lips trembled, his body trembled, but his eyes flashed lightnings. Ginevra alone matched his look, her eyes flaming; the daughter was worthy of the sire.

“Oh! to love you! What man is worthy of such a life?” Piombo continued. “To love you as a father is paradise on earth; who is there worthy to be your husband?”

“He,” said Ginevra; “he of whom I am not worthy.”

“He?” repeated Piombo mechanically; “who is he?”

“He whom I love.”

“How can he know you enough to love you?”

“Father,” said Ginevra with impatience, “whether he loves me or not, if I love him—”

“You love him?” cried Piombo.

Ginevra bent her head softly.

“You love him more than you love us?”

“The two feelings cannot be compared,” she replied.

“Is one stronger than the other?”

“I think it is,” said Ginevra.

“You shall not marry him,” cried the Corsican, his voice shaking the window-panes.

“I shall marry him,” replied Ginevra, tranquilly.

“Oh, God!” cried the mother, “how will this quarrel end? Santa Virgina! place thyself between them!”

The baron, who had been striding, now sat; an icy sternness darkened his face; he looked fixedly at his daughter, and said in a weak gentle voice:—

“Ginevra, no! you will not marry him. Oh! say nothing more to-night—let me think the contrary. Do you wish to see your father on his knees, his white hairs prostrate before you? I supplicate you—”

“Ginevra Piombo does not pass her word and break it,” she replied. “I am your daughter.”

“She is right,” said the baroness. “We are sent into the world to marry.”

“Do you encourage her in disobedience?” said the baron to his wife, who, terrified, now changed to marble.

“Refusing to obey an unjust order is not disobedience,” said Ginevra.

“No order can be unjust from the lips of your father, my daughter. Why do you judge my action? The repugnance I feel is counsel from on high, sent perhaps to protect you from some great evil.”

“The only evil could be that he did not love me.”

“Always he!”

“Yes, always,” she answered. “He is my life, my good, my thought. Even if I obeyed you he would be ever in my soul. To forbid me to marry him is to make me hate you.”

“You love us not!” cried Piombo.

“Oh!” said Ginevra, shaking her head.

“Well, then forget him; be faithful to us. After we are gone—you understand?”

“Father, do you wish me to long for your death?” cried Ginevra.

“I shall outlive you. Children who do not honor their parents die early,” said the father, driven to exasperation.

“All the more reason why I should marry and be happy,” she replied.

This coolness and power of argument increased Piombo’s trouble; the blood rushed to his head, his face turned purple. Ginevra shuddered; she sprang like a bird on her father’s knee, threw her arms around his neck, and caressed his white hair, exclaiming tenderly:—

“Oh, yes, yes, let me die first! I could never survive you, my father, my kind father!”

“Oh! my Ginevra, my own Ginevra!” replied Piombo, whose anger melted under this caress like snow beneath the sun.

“It was time you ceased,” said the baroness, trembling.

“Poor mother!”

“Ah! Ginevretta! mia bella Ginevra!”

The father played with his daughter as though she were six. He amused himself by releasing the waving volume of her hair, by dandling her on his knee; there was something of madness in these expressions of his love. Presently she scolded while kissing him, and tried by jesting to obtain admission for Luigi; but her father, also jesting, refused. She sulked, coaxed again, and sulked until, by the end of the evening, she had impressed on her father both her love for Luigi and the idea of an approaching marriage.

The next day she said no more about her love; she was more caressing to her father than ever, and testified the utmost gratitude, as if to thank him for the consent he seemed to have given by silence. That evening she sang and played to him long, exclaiming now and then: “We want a man’s voice for this nocturne.” Ginevra was an Italian, and that says all.

At the end of a week her mother signed to her. Ginevra went; Elisa Piombo whispered:—

“I have persuaded your father to receive him.”

“Oh! mother, how happy you have made me!”

That day Ginevra had the joy of coming home on Luigi’s arm. The officer came out of hiding for the second time. Ginevra’s earnest appeals to the Duc de Feltre, then minister of war, had succeeded. Luigi’s name was replaced upon the roll of officers awaiting orders. This was the first great step. Warned by Ginevra of the difficulties he would meet with her father, the young man dared not express his fear of offending the old man. Courageous under adversity, brave on the battlefield, he trembled at the thought of entering Piombo’s salon. Ginevra felt him tremble; this emotion, the source of which lay in her, was to her another proof of love.

“How pale you are!” she said when they reached the house.

“Oh! Ginevra, if it concerned my life only—”

Though Bartolomeo had been notified of the presentation, he would not advance to meet him, but remained seated in his arm-chair; the sternness of his brow was awful.

“Father,” said Ginevra, “I bring you a person you will no doubt be pleased to see,—a soldier who fought beside the Emperor at Mont-Saint-Jean.”

The baron rose, cast a sidelong glance at Luigi, and said in a sardonic tone:—

“Monsieur is not decorated.”

“I no longer wear the Legion of honor,” replied Luigi timidly.

Mortified by her father’s incivility, Ginevra dragged forward a chair. The officer’s answer seemed to satisfy the old servant of Napoleon. Madame Piombo, seeing her husband’s eyebrows resume their natural position, said by way of conversation:

“Monsieur’s resemblance to a person we knew in Corsica, Nina Porta, is really surprising.”

“Nothing could be more natural,” replied the young man, on whom Piombo’s flaming eyes now rested. “Nina was my sister.”

“Are you Luigi Porta?” asked the old man.

“Yes.”

Bartolomeo rose, tottered, leaned against a chair and beckoned his wife. Elisa Piombo came. Then the two old people, each supporting the other, left the room, abandoning their daughter with a sort of horror.

Luigi, bewildered, looked at Ginevra, who had turned as white as marble, and stood gazing at the door through which her parents had disappeared. This departure and silence seemed so solemn that, for the first time, a feeling of fear entered her. She struck her hands together and said in a voice so shaken that only a lover could catch the words:—

“What misery in a word!”

“In the name of our love, what have I said?” asked Luigi.

“My father,” she replied, “never spoke to me of our deplorable history, and I was too young when we left Corsica to know anything.”

“Are we in vendetta?” asked Luigi, trembling.

“Yes.” The girl smiled sadly and bowed. Then raised her head with pride:—

“Oh, Luigi, our love must be pure and sincere to give me strength to tread the path I am about to enter. But it involves a happiness that will last throughout our lives, will it not?”

Luigi answered with a smile and pressed her hand.

Ginevra understood that true love could despise vulgar protestations at such a moment. His calm and restrained expression foreshadowed their strength.

The destiny of the pair was decided. Ginevra foresaw a cruel struggle, but the idea of abandoning Luigi vanished. She dragged him from her father’s house with desperate energy, and did not leave him until she saw him reach the lodging Servin had found.

By the time she returned, Ginevra had attained the serenity of firm resolution; no sign of uneasiness showed. She turned on her parents, whom she found about to dine, with a gentle glance devoid of hardihood. She saw her mother had been weeping; the redness of those withered eyelids shook her heart, but she hid her emotion. No one touched the dinner. All three rose from table without a word.

When Ginevra placed herself between her parents in the gloomy salon, Piombo tried to speak but his voice failed; he tried to walk but had no strength. He rang the bell.

“Pietro,” he said at last, “light the fire; I am cold.”

Ginevra trembled and watched him anxiously. The struggle within him must have been horrible; his face was distorted. Ginevra knew the peril, but did not flinch. Bartolomeo cast furtive glances at his daughter, as if fearing a character whose violence he had formed.

Between such natures all things were extreme. The certainty of impending change in father and daughter gave the baroness an expression of terror.

“Ginevra, you love the enemy of your family,” said Piombo at last, not daring to look at her.

“That is true,” she replied.

“You must choose between us. Our vendetta is a part of our being. Whoso does not share my vengeance is not a member of my family.”

“My choice is made,” replied Ginevra calmly.

His daughter’s tranquillity misled Bartolomeo.

“Oh! my dear child!” he cried, letting her see his eyes moistened with tears, the first and only tears he ever shed.

“I shall be his wife,” said Ginevra abruptly.

Bartolomeo seemed dazed but recovered his coolness and replied:—

“The marriage will not take place in my lifetime; I will never consent.”

Ginevra was silent.

“Ginevra,” continued the baron, “have you reflected that Luigi is the son of the man who killed your brother?”

“He was six when that crime was committed; he was therefore not guilty,” she replied.

“He is a Porta!” cried Bartolomeo.

“I have never shared that hatred,” said Ginevra eagerly. “You did not bring me up to think a Porta must be a monster. How could I know one you thought you had killed survived? Is it not natural you should yield your vendetta to my feelings?”

“A Porta!” repeated Piombo. “If his father had found you in your bed you would not be living now; he would have taken your life a hundred times.”

“It may be so,” she answered; “but his son has given me life, and more than life. To see Luigi is a happiness without which I cannot live. Luigi has revealed to me the world of sentiments. I may have seen faces more beautiful than his, but none has charmed me thus; I may have heard voices—no, never any so melodious! Luigi loves me; he will be my husband.”

“Never,” said Piombo. “I would rather see you in your coffin, Ginevra.”

The old Corsican rose and strode up and down, dropping sentences after pauses that betrayed his agitation.

“You think you can bend my will. Undeceive yourself. A Porta shall never be my son; that is my decree. Let there be no further question between us. I am Bartolomeo di Piombo; do you hear me, Ginevra?”

“Do you attach some mysterious meaning to those words?” she asked coldly.

“They mean that I have a dagger, and that I do not fear man’s justice. Corsicans explain themselves to God.”

“And I,” said the daughter rising, “am Ginevra Piombo, and I declare that within six months I shall be the wife of Luigi Porta. You are a tyrant, my father,” she added after a terrifying pause.

Bartolomeo clenched his fists and struck them on the marble mantel.

“Ah! we are in Paris!” he muttered.

Then he was silent, crossed his arms, bowed his head, and said nothing more that evening.

After once declaring her will, Ginevra affected inconceivable coolness. She opened the piano and sang, played nocturnes and scherzos with grace and sentiment displaying perfect freedom, thus triumphing over her father, whose dark face showed no softening. The old man was cruelly hurt by this tacit insult; he gathered bitter fruits of the training he had given his daughter. Respect is a barrier that spares grief to parents and remorse to children.

The next day when Ginevra sought to leave at her usual hour she found the gates closed. She said nothing but managed to inform Luigi of her father’s severity. A chambermaid, who could neither read nor write, carried letters between the lovers. For five days they corresponded thus, thanks to the youth’s invention.

Father and daughter seldom spoke. Both nursed hatred in their hearts; they suffered proudly and in silence. Each tried to break their ties but without success. No gentle thought brightened Piombo’s face at sight of his Ginevra. The girl had something savage in her eye when she looked at him; reproach sat on that innocent brow; she gave herself to happy thoughts, yet at times remorse dulled her eyes. It was not difficult to believe she could never enjoy peacefully any happiness that caused her parents sorrow.

With Bartolomeo, as with his daughter, hesitations caused by native goodness yielded to pride and the rancor of their Corsican nature. They encouraged each other in anger and closed their eyes to the future, perhaps flattering themselves the other would yield.

At last, on Ginevra’s birthday, her mother, despairing at the growing estrangement, attempted to reconcile them by recalling family memories. They were all three sitting in Bartolomeo’s study. Ginevra guessed her mother’s aim by her hesitation and smiled sadly.

A servant announced two notaries with witnesses. Bartolomeo looked fixedly at these persons whose cold formal faces grated on souls so strained. The old man turned to his daughter uneasy. He saw on her face a triumphant smile that made him expect a shock, but affected to maintain deceitful indifference as he gazed at the notaries. They sat after his gesture.

“Monsieur is, no doubt, M. le Baron di Piombo?” began the oldest.

Bartolomeo bowed. The notary glanced at Ginevra with a sly look, took out his snuff-box, and slowly began:—

“Monsieur, I am Monsieur Roguin, your daughter’s notary, and we have come—my colleague and I—to fulfil the intentions of the law and—put an end to the divisions which—appear—to exist—between yourself and Mademoiselle, your daughter—on the subject—of—her—marriage with Monsieur Luigi Porta.”

This pedantic delivery probably amused Roguin. He paused and looked at Bartolomeo with the familiar air of a business lawyer. Notaries feign interest and wear a mask of benevolence that irritated Bartolomeo so he had to collect all his reason to prevent throwing Roguin through the window. Anger ran through his wrinkles causing the notary to think, “I’ve produced an effect.”

“But,” Roguin continued in honeyed tones, “monsieur le baron, on such occasions our duties are preceded by—efforts at—conciliation—Deign to listen. It is in evidence that Mademoiselle Ginevra di Piombo—attains this very day—the age at which the law allows a respectful summons before marriage despite parental non-consent. Now—it is usual in honorable families—to avoid publicizing differences—and not to injure the future of a young couple—for whenever a young lady has recourse to respectful summons she exhibits determination too marked to allow a father or mother,” here he turned to the baroness, “hoping she will follow their wishes—Paternal resistance being null—by reason of that fact—and also from law—it is customary—for every sensible man—after a final remonstrance—to leave her at liberty—”

Roguin stopped, seeing he might talk for two hours without answer; he felt a singular emotion at the man before him. An extraordinary change had taken place on Piombo’s face; his wrinkles contracted into narrow lines giving him an indescribable cruelty; he cast the look of a tiger. The baroness was mute and passive. Ginevra, calm and resolute, waited silently; she knew the notary’s voice more potent than hers and seemed determined to say nothing. When Roguin ceased, the scene was so terrifying the witnesses trembled. The notaries looked at each other, consulted, and walked to the window.

“Did you ever meet people born into the world like that?” Roguin asked his colleague.

“You can’t get anything out of him,” replied the younger. “In your place read the summons. That old fellow isn’t a comfortable person; he’s furious, and you’ll gain nothing arguing with him.”

Roguin then read a stamped paper containing the respectful summons; after which he asked Bartolomeo his answer.

“Are there laws in France which destroy paternal authority?” demanded the Corsican.

“Monsieur—” said Roguin.

“Which tear a daughter from her father?”

“Monsieur—”

“Which deprive an old man of his last consolation?”

“Monsieur, your daughter only belongs to you if—”

“And kill him?”

“Monsieur, permit me—”

There is nothing more horrible than the cool precise reasoning of notaries amid passionate scenes.

The forms about him seemed to Piombo escaped from hell; his repressed rage knew no bounds as the calm fluted “permit me.” Suddenly he sprang to a dagger hanging above the fireplace and rushed toward his daughter. The younger notary and a witness threw themselves before Ginevra; Piombo knocked them down, his face on fire and eyes casting flames. When Ginevra saw him approach she looked at him with triumph and slowly knelt.

“No, no! I cannot!” he cried, flinging away the weapon, which buried itself in the wainscot.

“Well, then! have mercy! have pity!” she said. “You hesitate to be my death and refuse me life! Oh! father, never have I loved you as I do at this moment; give me Luigi! I ask your consent upon my knees: a daughter can humble herself before her father. Luigi, give me my Luigi, or I die!”

The violent excitement that suffocated her stopped her words; her convulsive movements showed she lay between life and death. Bartolomeo roughly pushed her away.

“Go,” he said. “The wife of Luigi Porta cannot be a Piombo. I have no daughter. I have not the strength to curse you, but I cast you off; you have no father. My Ginevra Piombo is buried here,” he said deeply, pressing his heart. “Go, leave my house, unhappy girl,” he added after a pause. He took her by the arm to the gate and silently put her out.

“Luigi!” cried Ginevra entering her lover’s humble lodging, “my Luigi, we have no other fortune than our love.”

“Then am I richer than the kings of the earth!” he cried.

“My father and my mother have cast me off,” she said in deepest sadness.

“I will love you in place of them.”

“Then let us be happy—we WILL be happy!” she cried with a gayety that had something dreadful.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
The day after Ginevra was driven from her father’s house she went to Madame Servin for asylum and protection until the legal period for marrying Luigi had passed.

There began for her a rough schooling in trouble. Madame Servin received her coldly, annoyed at the scandal Ginevra had brought upon her husband, and politely warned that she could not expect future help. Too proud to beg and stunned by this selfishness, Ginevra took a room in the lodging-house nearest Luigi’s. The Portas’ son spent his days at his future wife’s feet; his youthful devotion and pure words dispelled the banished daughter’s despair. The future he painted was so fair that she finally smiled again, though her father’s severity remained in her mind.

One morning the lodging-house servant brought trunks and packages containing linens, clothes, and household necessities. Ginevra recognized her mother’s foresight, and among the gifts found a purse in which the baroness had put Ginevra’s money, adding her own savings. A letter implored her to renounce the fatal marriage if possible; it had cost her mother much to send these things. She begged Ginevra not to think her cruel if she were henceforth forced to abandon her to want, and though she feared she could no longer assist her, she blessed and prayed for her happiness should she persist. Tears had effaced some words.

“Oh, mother!” cried Ginevra, deeply moved. She felt the impulse to return home, to throw herself at her father’s feet and see her mother. But Luigi entered; at the sight of him her filial impulse vanished. Her tears stopped; she lacked the courage to abandon the loving and unfortunate youth. To be the sole hope of a noble being, to love him and then desert him—such a sacrifice young hearts cannot make. Ginevra had the generosity to bury her own grief.

The marriage day arrived. Ginevra had no friend with her. While she dressed, Luigi fetched the witnesses: a cavalry sergeant under lifelong obligation to him and a master-mason who owned their future apartment. Each brought a friend; the five men, simple and unaccustomed to ceremony, wore ordinary clothes. Nothing about them suggested the usual joy of a bridal procession.

Ginevra’s own dress was simple, yet her beauty was so noble that the witnesses’ greetings faltered; they bowed with respect and admired her in silence. That reserve chilled the party. Joy breaks forth freely only among equals; thus chance made all dull and grave around the bridal pair. Outwardly, nothing reflected the happiness within their hearts.

Because the church and mayor’s office were close, Luigi and Ginevra walked, followed by the witnesses. At the mayor’s hall they found other wedding parties waiting. Ginevra sat beside Luigi on a long bench; their witnesses stood. Nearby two brides, dressed in white and surrounded by joyous families, reflected the usual gaiety of marriage: fathers, mothers, and relatives busy and happy. As she watched, Ginevra’s heart swelled; she pressed Luigi’s arm. A tear rolled down the Corsican’s face; never had he seemed to understand the joys she sacrificed for him. Love poured down its light upon their hearts; amid the festive crowd they felt alone with each other while the witnesses conversed on indifferent matters.

Ginevra, superstitious as an Italian, read an omen in the contrast and felt terror. Then the clerk called Monsieur Luigi da Porta and Mademoiselle Ginevra di Piombo. The celebrity of the bride’s name drew attention and surprise that the wedding was not more sumptuous. A murmur noted the absence of her parents; her father’s wrath seemed present. The clerk announced that the father and mother protested.

“On both sides?” asked the mayor.

“The groom is an orphan,” replied the clerk.

“Where are the witnesses?”

“Here,” said the clerk, pointing to the four men.

“But if the parents protest—” began the mayor.

“The respectful summons has been duly served,” replied the clerk, producing the annexed papers.

This bureaucratic decision contained the whole story: the hatreds of the Portas and the Piombos and their passions were inscribed in the civil record. Ginevra trembled and found refuge only in Luigi’s eyes amid the cold waste around her. The mayor assumed a stern air; the clerk looked on with malicious curiosity. Deprived of adornments, the ceremony became a simple act, great only in thought.

After a few questions and signatures with the witnesses, Luigi and Ginevra were joined in civil matrimony. They walked back through lines of joyous relations who did not belong to them, for whom the only interest in the couple was the delay their ceremony caused to others. In the mayor’s court-yard, under the open sky, a sigh escaped Ginevra.

“Can a lifetime of devotion and love suffice to prove my gratitude for your courage and tenderness, my Ginevra?” said Luigi.

She forgot her sufferings at his tears. “Why should others come between us?” she answered, delighting Luigi. A sense of accomplishment lightened them as they made their way to a dark chapel in a corner of the building for the religious rite. An old priest married them amid other grander ceremonies going on nearby. Altars glowed, orange blossoms crowned the Virgin, and the church was full of pomp; yet when it came to hold the satin yoke above Luigi and Ginevra, the acolytes were absent and two witnesses performed the task. The priest’s hurried homily contained an indirect reproach to Ginevra for her parents’ absence; after uniting them before God, he left them.

“God bless them!” said Vergniaud, the sergeant, to the mason at the church porch. “No two creatures were ever more fitted for one another. The parents are foolish. I don’t know a braver soldier than Colonel Luigi.”

This blessing, the only one bestowed on their marriage-day, soothed Ginevra’s heart. They parted with firm handshakes; Luigi thanked his landlord. “Adieu, ‘mon brave,’” he said to the sergeant.

“I am now and ever at your service, colonel—soul, body, horses, and carriages; all that is mine is yours.”

“How he loves you!” said Ginevra.

Luigi hurried his bride to their modest apartment and, when the door closed, took her in his arms. “Oh, my Ginevra! for now you are mine, here is our true wedding. Here all things will smile upon us.” They toured three rooms: a salon-dining room, a bedchamber, and a study Luigi had arranged for her, stocked with easels, colors, lay figures, casts, pictures, and portfolios—an artist’s paraphernalia.

“So here I am to work!” she said with childlike happiness. She inspected hangings and furniture, thanking Luigi for a little magnificence. A bookcase held favorite books; a piano filled a corner. “You have good taste,” she said, caressing his hand.

They entered the nuptial chamber, fresh and white. Ginevra examined each piece of furniture with the minute care of a bride inspecting her treasures. “We begin by ruining ourselves,” she said half-joking.

“True! for all my back pay is there,” replied Luigi. “I have mortgaged it to a worthy fellow named Gigonnet.”

“Why did you do so?” she asked reproachfully, though secretly pleased. “Do you believe I should be less happy in a garret? But it is all charming—and it is ours!”

Above the three rooms, under the roof, were Luigi’s study, a kitchen, and a servant’s room. The windows looked onto a high wall and a gloomy court-yard, but to the lovers their hidden nest was paradise. In that vast Parisian house they were like two pearls in their shell; to others it might seem a prison.

The first days were given to love. Work came slowly; they could not resist passion. Luigi lay for hours at his wife’s feet admiring every detail of her beauty; Ginevra caressed his hair, never weary of his “belta folgorante.” They delighted in each other’s manners and graces. They played like children with trifles that always led back to love, ceasing only to dream. An air sung by Ginevra reproduced to their souls the lights and shadows of their passion. They roamed the country together, finding love in flowers, sky, and sunsets; each day differed, and their love grew because it was true. They had tested each other and recognized that their souls promised inexhaustible joys.

Their love was artless: long conversations, unfinished speeches, deep silences, ardor. They comprehended love in its many moods; for them what seemed monotonous to others was endlessly varied and enchanting.

Soon prudence drew them from Eden: they had to work to live. Ginevra, skilled at imitating old paintings, began copying and found customers among picture-dealers. Luigi, whose training had been military, sought employment in Paris with difficulty. At last, weary and unwilling to let the burden fall solely on Ginevra, he used his excellent handwriting to copy papers for lawyers and notaries. His frankness and situation interested many; soon he had more work than he could handle and needed assistants. The business grew gradually, and combined with sales of Ginevra’s pictures, gave them a competence of which they were proud—for it was earned by their industry.

This was the happiest period of their lives. Days flowed full of occupation and love. At night, after laboring, they met in Ginevra’s studio where music refreshed them. She never complained; appearing to Luigi with a smile, each bore a ruling thought: Ginevra worked for Luigi, Luigi for Ginevra.

Sometimes, in his absence, Ginevra imagined the happiness they might have had if her parents had accepted them; the thought of remorse cast her into melancholy. She pictured her old father alone or her mother weeping secretly to hide her tears from the inexorable Piombo. These visions pursued her like a presentiment.

She celebrated their anniversary by giving Luigi a portrait he had long desired—his Ginevra painted by herself. It was her finest work: not only a likeness, but the glow of her beauty, the purity of her feelings, and the happiness of love captured as if by magic—a votive offering to their felicity.

Another year of ease and comfort passed. The simple history of their life could be told in three words: They were happy. No event of consequence disturbed their contentment.
################################################
############# MIDDLE OF THE STORY. #############
################################################
Early in 1819 the picture-dealers asked Ginevra for something besides copies; competition made selling difficult. Madame Porta now saw she had erred in neglecting “genre” painting, which might already have won Ginevra a reputation. She tried portrait-painting, but met many artists more in need of money. Luigi and Ginevra had saved a little, so they were not yet anxious.

Toward the end of winter Luigi worked without intermission. He, too, fought competitors. Payment for writing had fallen so much that he could no longer employ help; he had to work longer himself for the same pay. His wife had finished several pictures of merit, but dealers bought mostly for reputation; her paintings had little chance. Ginevra offered them almost nothing, without success.

Household danger loomed. Their souls floated on an ocean of happiness; love overwhelmed them with its treasures, while poverty rose like a skeleton amid their joy. Yet they hid their anxieties. When Ginevra felt like weeping at Luigi’s worn, suffering face, she redoubled her caresses; Luigi, concealing his forebodings, expressed tenderer love. They exalted feeling to compensate; words, joys, and caresses grew frenzied. They feared the future. What feeling can equal a passion that may be destroyed by death or want on the morrow? Each felt the need to deceive the other, clinging ardently to any hope.

One night Ginevra woke and missed Luigi. A faint light on the opposite courtyard wall showed him writing in his study; he now waited till she slept to go to his garret and write. Four o’clock struck. She pretended to sleep. When Luigi returned, exhausted, she looked sadly at his worn face where toil and care had begun to write wrinkles.

“It is for me he spends his nights,” she thought, weeping—then resolved to imitate him. That day, using a letter of recommendation from Elie Magus, one of her dealers, she got an order for coloring lithographs. She painted by day and colored engravings at night, sharing Luigi’s clandestine labor. They entered their nuptial bed only to deceive each other; both feigned sleep, and each left the bed as soon as the other seemed asleep.

One night Luigi, burning with feverish fatigue, opened his garret casement for air and saw the reflection of Ginevra’s lamp on the opposite wall. He went down softly and surprised her coloring engravings.

“Oh, Ginevra!” he cried.

She rose convulsively and blushed.

“Could I sleep while you wore yourself out?” she said.

“But to me alone belongs the right to work in this way,” he answered.

“Could I be idle,” she asked with tears, “when every mouthful we eat costs a drop of your blood? I should die if I could not add my efforts to yours. All should be in common between us: pains and pleasures.”

“She is cold!” Luigi cried in despair. “Wrap your shawl closer, my Ginevra; the night is damp.”

They went to the window; she leaned on his breast, he held her round the waist, and together in deep silence they gazed at the sky brightening toward dawn. Clouds moved quickly; the East grew luminous.

“See!” said Ginevra. “It is an omen. We shall be happy.”

“Yes, in heaven,” Luigi replied with a bitter smile. “Oh, Ginevra! you who deserved all the treasures on earth—”

“I have your heart,” she said joyfully.

“Ah! I complain no more!” he answered, drawing her close and covering the face growing thin with kisses that comforted him.

“What silence!” Ginevra murmured. “I take pleasure in sitting up. The majesty of Night awes and inspires. All sleep, I wake.”

“Oh, my Ginevra,” he cried, “it is not to-night alone I feel how delicately moulded is your soul. But see, dawn is here; come and sleep.”

“Yes,” she said, “if I do not sleep alone. I suffered too much the night I first discovered you waking while I slept.”

Their courage was briefly rewarded; but an event that usually crowns household happiness proved fatal. Ginevra had a son, “as beautiful as the day.” Motherhood doubled her strength. Luigi borrowed to meet confinement expenses. At first she did not feel the new burden; they surrendered to the joy of possessing a child. It was their last happiness.

Like swimmers uniting to breast a current, they struggled; sometimes apathy resembling the sleep before death overcame them. Soon they had to sell jewels. Poverty appeared—not hideous, but plainly clothed, almost tolerable; its voice neither terrifying nor raving, it made them lose habits of comfort and dried pride’s springs. Then, before they knew it, came want in all its horror, indifferent to rags, treading underfoot human sentiments.

Seven or eight months after little Bartolomeo’s birth, it would have been hard to see in the mother who nursed her sickly babe the original of the beautiful portrait — the sole remaining ornament. Without fire through a hard winter, Ginevra’s graceful outlines were destroyed; her cheeks grew white as porcelain, her eyes dulled as if life’s springs were drying. Watching her shrunken, discolored child, her only suffering was his misery; Luigi had no courage to smile on his son.

“I have wandered over Paris,” he said one day. “I know no one; can I ask strangers? Vergniaud, my old sergeant, is concerned in a conspiracy and imprisoned; besides, he has lent all he could. As for our landlord, he has not asked rent in over a year.”

“But we are not in want,” replied Ginevra gently, affecting calm.

“Every hour brings new difficulty,” Luigi answered in terror.

Another day Luigi sold Ginevra’s pictures, her portrait, and the few household articles they could spare for a miserable sum, prolonging their agony. In these days of wretchedness Ginevra showed the sublimity of her nature and her resignation.

Stoically she bore misery’s blows; her strong soul upheld her; she worked with unfaltering hand beside her dying son, performed household duties with marvelous activity, and sufficed for all. She was even happy when she saw Luigi’s surprised smile at the cleanliness she produced in their one poor room.

“Dear, I kept this bit of bread for you,” she said one evening when he returned, worn out.

“And you?”

“I? I have dined, dear Luigi; I want nothing more.”

The tender look on her beseeching face urged him more than words to take the food she had denied herself.

Luigi kissed her with one of those kisses of despair given in 1793 between friends mounting a scaffold. In such moments two beings see each other heart to heart. Comprehending suddenly that his wife was starving, Luigi was seized by the fever consuming her. He shuddered and went out, pretending business called, for he would rather have drunk poison than secure life by eating the last morsel of bread left in their home.

He wandered wildly about Paris amid flaunting luxury, hurried past windows where gold glittered. At last he resolved to sell himself as a substitute for military service, hoping the sacrifice would save Ginevra and that her father would take her home during his absence.

He went to an agent and found an old officer of the Imperial guard. “It’s two days since I have eaten,” he said in a slow, weak voice. “My wife is dying of hunger and has never complained; she will die smiling, I think. For God’s sake, comrade,” he added bitterly, “buy me in advance; I am robust; I am no longer in the service, and I—”

The officer gave Luigi a sum on account. The wretched man laughed convulsively as he grasped the gold and ran breathless home, crying, “Ginevra! Oh, my Ginevra!”

It was almost night when he reached their room. He entered softly, fearing to shock his wife, whom he had left so weak. The last rays of the sun faded from Ginevra’s face as she sat sleeping in her chair, holding her child on her breast.

“Wake, my dear one,” he said, not seeing the infant, who at that moment shone with a strange pallor.

Hearing his voice, the poor mother opened her eyes, met Luigi’s look, and smiled; but Luigi cried out in horror; he scarcely recognized his wife, half mad. With a savage gesture he showed her the gold. Ginevra laughed mechanically, then cried in a dreadful voice, “The child, Luigi, he is cold!”

She looked at her son and swooned. The little Bartolomeo was dead. Luigi took his wife in his arms, the child still clasped to her; after laying her on the bed he went out to seek help.

“Oh! my God!” he cried as he met his landlord on the stairs. “I have gold, gold, and my child has died of hunger, and his mother is dying, too! Help me!”

He returned like one distraught to his wife, leaving the mason and neighbors to gather a few things for needs they had been too proud to reveal.

Luigi had cast his gold upon the floor and knelt by the bed. Ginevra, delirious, murmured, “Father! take care of my son, who bears your name.”

“Oh, my angel! be calm,” Luigi said, kissing her; “our good days are coming back.”

“My Luigi,” she said, looking at him with extraordinary attention, “listen. I feel that I am dying. My death is natural; I suffered too much; besides, such happiness as mine must be paid for. Yes, Luigi, be comforted. I have been so happy that if I lived again I would accept our fate. I am a bad mother; I regret you more than my child—My child!” she added hollowly.

Two tears escaped her dying eyes, and suddenly she pressed the little body she could not warm.

“Give my hair to my father, in memory of his Ginevra,” she said. “Tell him I have never blamed him.”

Her head fell on her husband’s arm.

“No, you cannot die!” cried Luigi. “The doctor is coming. We have food. Your father will take you home. Prosperity is here. Stay with us, angel!”

Her faithful heart, so full of love, grew cold. Ginevra turned her eyes to him she loved, though she was conscious of nothing else. Confused images passed before her; memory of earth faded. She knew Luigi was there and clasped his hand tightly as if to hold herself from some precipice.

“Dear,” she said at last, “you are cold; I will warm you.”

She tried to put his hand upon her heart, but died.

Two doctors, a priest, and neighbors came into the room, bringing what might save the poor couple and calm despair. They made some noise entering; afterwards an awful silence filled the room.

While that scene took place, Bartolomeo Piombo and his wife sat in their antique chairs at the corners of a vast fireplace in their salon. A friend had just left; the chair he had occupied remained between the two Corsicans. The clock told midnight. The old couple had lost the ability to sleep; they sat silent, like persons in their dotage, gazing about at things they did not see. Their deserted salon, filled with memories, was feebly lit by a single expiring lamp. Without the hearth’s spark they might soon be in total darkness.

Piombo cast glances at the empty chair—glances full of thoughts, remorse crowding upon one another— for the seat was Ginevra’s. Elisa Piombo watched expressions cross her husband’s pallid face; though long used to divining his feelings, tonight they seemed threatening and melancholy, making his soul incomprehensible even to her.

Would Bartolomeo yield at last to the memories that chair awakened? Had he been shocked to see a stranger occupy it, used for the first time since his daughter left? Had the hour of his mercy struck—the hour Elisa had vainly prayed and waited for?

These thoughts shook the mother. For an instant her husband’s countenance became so terrible she trembled at having used this simple means to bring up Ginevra’s name. The wintry night blew snow sharply against the blinds; the old woman dropped her head to hide tears. Suddenly a sigh burst from the old man; he seemed crushed. She risked speaking—for the second time in three years—of his daughter.

“Ginevra may be cold,” she said softly.

Piombo quivered.

“She may be hungry,” she continued.

The old man dropped a tear.

“Perhaps she has a child and cannot suckle it; her milk is dried up!” said the mother in despair.

“Let her come! let her come to me!” cried Piombo. “Oh! my precious child, thou hast conquered me.”

The mother rose as if to fetch her daughter. At that instant the door opened noisily, and a man, his face no longer human, stood before them.
